> - **Topic:** Classification of Composite Materials Based on Matrices and Reinforcements: Polymer Matrix Composites, Metal Matrix Composites, Ceramic Matrix Composites, Plastic and Rubber Matrix Composites, Smart Composites, Carbon-Carbon Composites, Intermetallic Composites, Laminated Composites, Fibre Reinforced Composites, Laminar Composites, Particulate Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Material (KTU Definition):** A heterogeneous material composed of two or more distinct phases — a **matrix phase** (the continuous binder) and a **reinforcement phase** (the discontinuous, stronger load-bearing constituent) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material whose **properties are superior to those of the individual constituents acting alone**.

The matrix serves three canonical roles:
1. **Binding the reinforcement** into a coherent structural shape.
2. **Transferring applied load** to the reinforcement through shear at the interface.
3. **Protecting the reinforcement** from environmental degradation (moisture, abrasion, chemical attack).

The reinforcement carries the bulk of the mechanical/thermal load and dictates the **anisotropic** directionality of properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Alloys:** In alloys, the phases are blended at the *atomic/molecular* level (homogeneous microstructure). In composites, the phases remain *macroscopically distinct* — you can physically see the fibre or particle boundary. This is the **composite–alloy dividing line** KTU examiners frequently test.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rebar in Concrete" Model

Think of a composite like **chocolate-chip cookie dough**:
- The **dough** = *matrix* (continuous, holds everything together, determines the basic shape and surface finish).
- The **chocolate chips** = *reinforcement* (discontinuous lumps that add strength, crunch, and flavor at specific locations).

You can also use the **concrete + steel rebar** analogy taught in every civil engineering class:
- **Concrete** = brittle, strong in compression, weak in tension → this is the *matrix*.
- **Steel rebar** = ductile, strong in tension → this is the *reinforcement*.
- The steel does the *tensile lifting*; the concrete does the *compressive crushing*. **Together**, they form reinforced concrete — far superior to either alone.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "MRS-MFC"**: When classifying any composite, always ask in this order:
> **M** → What is the **M**atrix?
> **R** → What is the **R**einforcement geometry (fibre, particle, laminar)?
> **S** → What **S**tructure does it form (continuous, laminated, sandwich)?
> **M** → What is the **M**anufacturing route?
> **F** → What are the **F**ailure modes?
> **C** → What **C**haracteristic applications?

---

## 1.3 Three Axes of Classification (the KTU taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes composite classification along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|-----------|
| **By Matrix Material** | Polymer, Metal, Ceramic, Plastic-Rubber, Carbon-Carbon, Intermetallic |
| **By Reinforcement Form** | Fibre (continuous/discontinuous/whisker), Particulate, Laminar |
| **By Structural Architecture** | Laminated, Sandwich, Hybrid, Smart (functional) |

A single engineering composite — for example, **glass-fibre reinforced epoxy** — simultaneously occupies one slot in *each* axis. KTU Part-B questions commonly ask students to "classify the given composite on all three axes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PECHT742 expects the student to be able to (a) identify which matrix dominates a given composite, (b) classify by reinforcement geometry, and (c) recognize special-purpose categories like Smart and Carbon-Carbon composites that **break the ordinary rules** (e.g., a C-C composite has *both* matrix and reinforcement made of carbon — a borderline case the examiner loves).

---

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering Practice

Different matrix–reinforcement combinations yield **wildly different service envelopes**. Choosing the wrong class is the difference between a flying airframe and a melted plastic part on a runway:

| Engineering Demand | Correct Composite Class |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Lightweight + corrosion-resistant body panels | **Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC)** |
| High-temperature turbine blade | **Ceramic Matrix Composite (CMC)** |
| Brake disc at 1000 °C | **Carbon-Carbon Composite** |
| Engine piston with wear resistance | **Metal Matrix Composite (MMC)** |
| Vibration damping in mountings | **Rubber Matrix Composite** |
| Shape-changing aircraft wing | **Smart Composite (Piezoelectric)** |

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Property Metrics (bolded for recall)

The following metrics recur in every composite classification problem:

- **Density (ρ)** — measured in **kg/m³** or **g/cc**.
- **Tensile Strength (σₜ)** — in **MPa** or **GPa**.
- **Young's Modulus (E)** — in **GPa**.
- **Specific Strength = σₜ / ρ** — units **kNm/kg**.
- **Specific Modulus = E / ρ** — units **MN·m/kg** or **GPa·cm³/g**.
- **Volume Fraction (Vƒ, Vₘ)** — dimensionless, $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- **Weight Fraction (Wf, Wm)** — dimensionless, $W_f + W_m = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical convention used throughout this module:** Subscript **f** denotes fibre/reinforcement; subscript **m** denotes matrix. Subscript **c** denotes the *composite* (effective) property. This triplet (f, m, c) appears in every Rule of Mixtures equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Axis-A: Classification Based on MATRIX Material

The matrix is the **continuous phase**. It determines the **upper service temperature**, **chemical resistance**, and **fabrication route**. The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following matrix classes:

### 2.1.1 Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)

- **Matrix family:** Thermosetting resins (epoxy, polyester, vinylester, phenolic) and thermoplastic resins (PEEK, PPS, nylon).
- **Reinforcement examples:** Glass fibre (GFRP), Carbon fibre (CFRP), Aramid fibre (Kevlar), Boron fibre.
- **Service temperature range:** typically **−50 °C to +250 °C** (thermosets) and **up to +400 °C** (high-temp thermoplastics).
- **Density:** **ρ ≈ 1.2 – 1.8 g/cc** — the lowest of all matrix families.
- **KTU advantages:** cheapest, easiest to fabricate (hand lay-up, filament winding, RTM, autoclave), excellent specific strength.
- **KTU limitations:** poor transverse properties, low max temperature, susceptible to UV/moisture.
- **Real-world applications:** Boeing 787 fuselage (53 % composites by weight), wind turbine blades, marine hulls, sports equipment.

### 2.1.2 Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)

- **Matrix family:** Aluminium, Magnesium, Titanium, Copper, Nickel alloys.
- **Reinforcement examples:** SiC particles, Al₂O₃ (alumina) particles, carbon fibres, boron fibres.
- **Service temperature:** typically **300 °C – 600 °C**.
- **Density:** **ρ ≈ 2.5 – 4.5 g/cc**.
- **KTU advantages:** Higher modulus and strength than base metal, retains properties at elevated temperature, ductile (does not shatter like PMC).
- **KTU limitations:** Higher processing temperature (requires liquid-state or solid-state routes like stir casting, squeeze casting, powder metallurgy), higher cost, harder to machine.
- **Real-world applications:** Toyota piston (Al–SiC), aerospace structural ribs, automotive drive shafts, brake rotors, electronic heat sinks.

### 2.1.3 Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)

- **Matrix family:** Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂, glass-ceramics.
- **Reinforcement examples:** SiC fibres, SiC whiskers, carbon fibres, alumina fibres.
- **Service temperature:** **800 °C – 1400 °C** — the highest of the three classic families.
- **Density:** **ρ ≈ 2.5 – 3.5 g/cc**.
- **KTU advantages:** Retains strength at temperatures where metals would creep/melt; oxidation resistant; exceptional wear resistance.
- **KTU limitations:** **Brittle**, expensive, very difficult to fabricate (requires hot isostatic pressing, chemical vapour infiltration).
- **Real-world applications:** Gas turbine shrouds, rocket nozzle throats, re-entry thermal protection, brake discs in high-performance cars.

### 2.1.4 Plastic and Rubber Matrix Composites

Often grouped separately because their matrix is a **synthetic polymer elastomer** rather than a structural thermoset:

- **Plastic matrices:** Polyethylene, polypropylene, PVC, ABS — used when impact resistance, chemical resistance, or low cost outweighs peak stiffness.
- **Rubber matrices:** Natural rubber, neoprene, silicone, polyurethane — used for damping, sealing, vibration isolation.
- **Reinforcement examples:** Carbon black (most common in tyres!), silica, short glass fibres, aramid pulp.
- **Key engineering insight:** Carbon-black-reinforced rubber is the *original* engineered composite (since 1904 in tyres) — yet it is rarely called a "composite" in industry.
- **Real-world applications:** Tyres, conveyor belts, bridge bearings, anti-seismic elastomeric pads, gaskets.

### 2.1.5 Carbon–Carbon Composites (C/C)

- **Special case where the matrix is ALSO carbon**: carbon fibres reinforcing a carbon (pyrolytic) matrix.
- **Fabrication:** Carbon fibres laid up → densified with carbon via CVD (chemical vapour deposition) or pitch impregnation → graphitized.
- **Service temperature:** **up to 2500 °C in inert atmosphere**.
- **Density:** **ρ ≈ 1.6 – 2.0 g/cc**.
- **KTU advantages:** Highest specific strength of *any* material above 1600 °C, exceptional thermal shock resistance.
- **KTU limitations:** **Oxidizes above 400 °C in air** unless protected by SiC/glass coatings; extremely expensive.
- **Real-world applications:** Space Shuttle nose cap, Formula-1 brake discs, rocket nozzle throats, military aircraft brake systems.

### 2.1.6 Intermetallic Matrix Composites

- **Matrix family:** Nickel aluminides (NiAl, Ni₃Al), Titanium aluminides (TiAl), iron aluminides (FeAl).
- **Reinforcement:** SiC, TiB₂, Al₂O₃, refractory metal wires (W, Mo).
- **Service temperature:** **600 °C – 1000 °C** — fills the gap between MMCs and CMCs.
- **Why they exist:** Pure intermetallics are strong but extremely brittle. Adding a ductile metallic reinforcement toughens them while preserving the high-temperature strength.
- **Real-world applications:** Advanced jet-engine turbine blades (under research), hypersonic vehicle skins.

### 2.1.7 Smart Composites

- **Definition:** Composites whose matrix or reinforcement is functionalised with **sensors, actuators, or shape-memory elements** that respond to external stimuli.
- **Smart constituents:** Piezoelectric ceramics (PZT, BaTiO₃), shape-memory alloys (Nitinol), magnetostrictive materials (Terfenol-D), optical fibres, carbon nanotubes.
- **Stimulus → Response pairings:**

| Stimulus | Smart Material | Response |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| Stress | Piezoelectric | Voltage |
| Voltage | Piezoelectric | Strain |
| Temperature | Shape-memory alloy | Shape change |
| Magnetic field | Magnetostrictive | Strain |
| Damage | Optical fibre | Light attenuation |

- **Real-world applications:** Adaptive helicopter rotor blades (vibration cancellation), self-healing polymer composites, morphing aircraft wings, structural health monitoring in bridges.

---

## 2.2 Axis-B: Classification Based on REINFORCEMENT Geometry

Reinforcements are categorised by their **shape and aspect ratio (l/d)**:

### 2.2.1 Fibre-Reinforced Composites (FRC)

- **Aspect ratio:** $\ell/d \geq 100$ for *continuous* fibres; lower for discontinuous.
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Continuous-fibre FRC:** Fibre runs unbroken through the entire composite → highest strength, but expensive and limited to simple shapes.
  - **Short-fibre (discontinuous) FRC:** Fibre length < critical length $\ell_c$ → lower efficiency but cheap, mouldable.
  - **Whisker-reinforced:** Single-crystal short fibres (SiC, Al₂O₃) with near-theoretical strength.
- **Examples:** CFRP, GFRP, Kevlar-epoxy, Al–Boron.

### 2.2.2 Particulate Composites

- **Aspect ratio:** $\approx 1$ (particles are equiaxed: spheres, cubes, flakes).
- **Examples:** Al–SiC particles (engine pistons), WC–Co (cemented carbides/cutting tools), tyre rubber + carbon black, concrete + gravel.
- **KTU key concept:** Particles strengthen the matrix mainly by **hindering dislocation motion** (in metallic matrices) or by **crack-pinning** (in ceramic matrices). They do not carry continuous load like fibres.

### 2.2.3 Laminar Composites

- **Reinforcement form:** Thin sheets or layers stacked together.
- **Examples:** Plywood (wood veneers), laminated safety glass (glass + PVB interlayer), explosion-clad steel–titanium plates, paper-laminate PCB substrates.
- **Key distinction from "Laminated Composites" below:** Here the *reinforcement itself is a sheet*. In the next category, the layered composite is formed by *stacking cured laminae* (plies).

---

## 2.3 Axis-C: Classification Based on STRUCTURAL Architecture

### 2.3.1 Laminated Composites

- Built by **stacking plies (laminae)** of unidirectional fibre-reinforced material with *different fibre orientations* in each ply.
- Each ply may have angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ (e.g., $[0°/45°/90°/−45°]$ in a "quasi-isotropic" layup).
- **Why angle-ply?** To balance strength in multiple directions — a unidirectional laminate is very strong along the fibre but weak across it.
- **Examples:** Aircraft wing skins, helicopter rotor blades, racing yacht hulls, pressure vessels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Laminar Composites** (the reinforcement form — sheets in the matrix) with **Laminated Composites** (the structural form — stacks of fibre plies). They are *different categories* in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 2.4 Master Formula Sheet (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume-fraction closure | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}$ | Weight fraction from volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density (Rule of Mixtures) | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Modulus along fibre (parallel ROM) | GPa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Modulus transverse to fibre (Inverse ROM) | 1/GPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Strength along fibre (iso-strain) | MPa |
| 7 | $\tau_c = \tau_f V_f + \tau_m V_m$ | Shear strength | MPa |
| 8 | $\alpha_c = \alpha_f V_f + \alpha_m V_m$ | Thermal expansion coefficient (parallel) | 1/K |
| 9 | $\ell_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fibre length | m |
| 10 | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_m - \sigma_m'}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m'}$ | Critical volume fraction (Kelly–Tyson) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_\ell = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta \ell / 2)}{\beta \ell / 2}$ | Fibre length efficiency factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \tau_i}{E_f d \, r}}$ | Shear-lag parameter | 1/m |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** A KTU Part-B question worth 14 marks will typically expect you to write **at minimum** formulas 3, 4, 5, and 9, plus define each term. Memorize the *set*, not individual formulas.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Matter

- **Formula 3 (ρ_c):** Determines weight savings in aerospace — every kg removed from a satellite saves roughly ₹5–10 lakh in launch cost.
- **Formula 4 vs Formula 5:** Explains the **anisotropy** of fibre composites (huge difference between along-fibre and across-fibre stiffness). Engineers use this difference deliberately.
- **Formula 9 (ℓ_c):** Tells the manufacturer whether a short fibre is being used efficiently. If actual fibre length $\ell < \ell_c$, the fibre will pull out before breaking — strength wasted.
- **Formulas 11–12 (Shear-lag):** Predicts how much of the fibre's strength is actually mobilised — the foundation of modern micromechanics codes (Digimat, e-Xstream).

---

## 2.6 The Three Axes in One Picture (KTU Mental Map)

```
                          COMPOSITE MATERIAL
                                  |
        __________________________|__________________________
        |                         |                        |
   MATRIX TYPE             REINFORCEMENT           STRUCTURE
        |                   GEOMETRY                   |
   _____|_____         ________|________         _______|_______
   |    |    |         |       |        |        |             |
  PMC  MMC  CMC      Fibre  Particulate  Laminar  Laminated   Smart
   |    |    |         |       |        |        |
Plast. C/C Inter       └── Continuous/Discontinuous
Rubber      Metallic       Whisker
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** from first principles — the single most important analytical tool KTU examiners test in Module 1.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Composite Density (ρ_c)

**Problem context:** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.45$ (volume fraction of glass fibres). Given $\rho_f = 2.55 \, \text{g/cc}$ (glass) and $\rho_m = 1.20 \, \text{g/cc}$ (cured epoxy), find $\rho_c$.

### Starting Premise

A composite volume $V_c$ is divided into two non-overlapping sub-volumes: fibre volume $V_f V_c$ and matrix volume $V_m V_c$.

$$
V_c = V_f V_c + V_m V_c
$$

Dividing both sides by $V_c$:

$$
1 = V_f + V_m
$$

### Mass-Weighted Step

Mass = density × volume:

$$
m_c = m_f + m_m
$$

$$
\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

### Applying Volume-Fraction Closure

Since $V_f + V_m = 1$, the volumes $V_f$ and $V_m$ here are actually **dimensionless fractions** (they are often written simply as $V_f, V_m$ with implicit unit volume). The composite density becomes:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

### Numerical Substitution

$$
\rho_c = (2.55)(0.45) + (1.20)(0.55)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.1475 + 0.6600
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = 1.8075 \;\text{g/cc} \;\approx\; 1.81 \;\text{g/cc}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

The composite is **heavier than epoxy alone** but **lighter than glass alone**, lying between them as the rule dictates. The 45 % fibre fraction dominates the density because $\rho_f > \rho_m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Modulus along the Fibre (Iso-Strain / Voigt Model)

**Problem context:** Same composite, with $E_f = 72 \, \text{GPa}$ (E-glass), $E_m = 3.5 \, \text{GPa}$ (cured epoxy), $V_f = 0.45$. Find $E_c$ along the fibre direction.

### Starting Assumption — Iso-Strain

When a load is applied **parallel** to the fibres, both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (they are bonded and deform together):

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon
$$

### Force Equilibrium

The total load on the composite cross-section equals the sum of loads carried by each phase:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

Stress = force / area, so:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

Using $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ and dividing by $A_c$:

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \varepsilon \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

The area ratios equal the volume fractions (for uniform cross-section):

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

### Numerical Substitution

$$
E_c = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)
$$

$$
E_c = 32.40 + 1.925
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c = 34.325 \;\text{GPa} \;\approx\; 34.3 \;\text{GPa}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

Even though the epoxy is **20× weaker** than the glass fibre, the fibre dominates the composite stiffness — a **10× improvement** over pure epoxy. This is the iso-strain case (Voigt upper bound).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Young's Modulus Transverse to Fibres (Iso-Stress / Reuss Model)

**Problem context:** Same composite loaded **perpendicular** to the fibres.

### Starting Assumption — Iso-Stress

When loaded transversely, both phases experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ but **different strains**:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma
$$

### Strain Compatibility

Total displacement = sum of phase displacements. Dividing by total length and using $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$:

$$
\varepsilon_c \cdot L_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot L_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot L_m
$$

Dividing by $L_c$ and substituting strain = stress/modulus:

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot V_m
$$

Dividing both sides by $\sigma$:

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

$$
E_c = \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)^{-1}
$$

### Numerical Substitution

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{0.45}{72} + \frac{0.55}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = 0.00625 + 0.15714
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = 0.16339 \;\text{GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c \approx 6.12 \;\text{GPa}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation — The Anisotropy Pay-Off

Compare the two directions:
- **Along fibres:** $E_c \approx 34.3$ GPa (10× epoxy)
- **Transverse to fibres:** $E_c \approx 6.12$ GPa (only ~1.75× epoxy)

This **5.6× anisotropy ratio** is exactly why engineers angle-ply laminates (rotate fibre directions in successive plies) — to recover stiffness in all directions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fibre Length $\ell_c$

**Problem context:** A carbon fibre of diameter $d = 8 \, \mu\text{m}$ and tensile strength $\sigma_f = 4.0 \, \text{GPa}$ is embedded in an epoxy matrix with interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 40 \, \text{MPa}$. Find the minimum fibre length for full strength mobilisation.

### Force Balance on a Single Fibre

A short fibre is loaded by **shear stress** along its cylindrical interface (the matrix "drags" the fibre into tension).

The tensile load carried by the fibre must be balanced by the shear force from the matrix:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}
$$

> **Why $\ell / 2$?** Because shear stress acts on *both* ends of the fibre, building up from zero at each end to a maximum in the middle. The effective load transfer length is $\ell/2$ per end.

### Solving for $\ell$

$$
\sigma_f \cdot d = 2 \tau_i \cdot \ell
$$

$$
\ell = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \ell_c
$$

### Numerical Substitution

$$
\ell_c = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa})(8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa})}
$$

$$
\ell_c = \frac{32 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N/m}}{80 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N/m}^2}
$$

$$
\ell_c = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ell_c = 0.4 \, \text{mm} \;=\; 400 \,\mu\text{m}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

- Fibres **shorter than 400 μm** will *pull out* of the matrix before they fracture — strength wasted.
- Fibres **longer than 400 μm** (typically continuous carbon fibres at 5–10 mm) operate at full $\sigma_f$.
- This is why **chopped-fibre composites** (with $\ell \approx 1$–$5$ mm) are designed with the fibre length comfortably above $\ell_c$, but discontinuous whiskers (SiC) are only used when the matrix is so strong that $\sigma_f$ itself is small.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Critical Volume Fraction $V_{f,crit}$ (Kelly–Tyson Equation)

The composite fails when either phase reaches its failure strain. There is a *minimum* fibre volume fraction below which adding fibres actually *weakens* the matrix.

### Composite Strength at Fibre Failure

$$
\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_{m}' (1 - V_f)
$$

where $\sigma_{m}'$ is the matrix stress at the *fibre-failure strain* (not the matrix ultimate strain — this is the critical subtlety).

### Setting Composite Strength Equal to Unreinforced Matrix Strength

We require $\sigma_{cu} \geq \sigma_{mu}$ (the matrix alone):

$$
\sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_{m}' (1 - V_f) \geq \sigma_{mu}
$$

Solving for $V_f$:

$$
V_f \geq \frac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_{m}'}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{m}'} = V_{f,crit}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{f,crit} = \frac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_{m}'}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{m}'}}
$$

### Numerical Example

Given: $\sigma_{fu} = 4.0$ GPa (C-fibre), $\sigma_{mu} = 80$ MPa (epoxy), $\sigma_{m}' = 50$ MPa (epoxy stress at fibre-failure strain).

$$
V_{f,crit} = \frac{80 - 50}{4000 - 50} = \frac{30}{3950}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{f,crit} \approx 0.0076 \;=\; 0.76\%}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

Only **0.76 %** fibre volume fraction is needed to exceed the unreinforced matrix strength — but real composites use 30–65 % because higher $V_f$ dramatically improves stiffness and ultimate strength. The critical value is a *lower bound*, not an optimum.

---

## 3.6 Master Comparison Table — Iso-Strain vs Iso-Stress vs Experimental

| Property | Iso-Strain (Voigt) | Iso-Stress (Reuss) | Real Composite |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Assumed strain field | Uniform | Non-uniform | Approximately uniform along fibres |
| Loads carried | Fibre-dominant | Matrix-dominant | In-between |
| Modulus value | **Upper bound** | **Lower bound** | $E_R \leq E_{\text{real}} \leq E_V$ |
| Use case | Fibre direction | Transverse direction | Off-axis loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trivia:** The expressions for $E_c$ derived here are mathematically identical to the **Voigt (1889)** and **Reuss (1929)** bounds originally derived for **polycrystalline metals**. Hill (1952) later proved they are rigorous upper and lower bounds for *any* composite. This is why KTU examiners sometimes credit "Voigt–Reuss bounds" instead of "Rule of Mixtures".

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Specific Strength of GFRP vs Steel (Typical Exam Problem)

**Given:**
- GFRP composite: $\rho_c = 1.81$ g/cc, $\sigma_c = 1200$ MPa (along fibre).
- Mild steel: $\rho = 7.85$ g/cc, $\sigma = 450$ MPa.

**Specific Strength:**
$$
\text{SS}_{\text{GFRP}} = \frac{1200}{1.81} = 663 \;\text{kNm/kg}
$$

$$
\text{SS}_{\text{Steel}} = \frac{450}{7.85} = 57.3 \;\text{kNm/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\text{SS}_{\text{GFRP}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{Steel}}} \approx 11.6 \times}
$$

**Interpretation:** A GFRP component can be **11.6 times lighter** than a steel component carrying the same tensile load — the foundational reason for composites in aerospace.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COMPOSITE MATERIAL] --> B[BY MATRIX PHASE]
    A --> C[BY REINFORCEMENT PHASE]
    A --> D[BY STRUCTURAL FORM]

    B --> B1[Polymer Matrix - PMC]
    B --> B2[Metal Matrix - MMC]
    B --> B3[Ceramic Matrix - CMC]
    B --> B4[Plastic-Rubber Matrix]
    B --> B5[Carbon-Carbon Matrix]
    B --> B6[Intermetallic Matrix]
    B --> B7[Smart Functional Matrix]

    C --> C1[Fibre Reinforcement]
    C --> C2[Particulate Reinforcement]
    C --> C3[Laminar Reinforcement]

    C1 --> C1A[Continuous Fibre]
    C1 --> C1B[Discontinuous Fibre]
    C1 --> C1C[Whisker]

    D --> D1[Laminated Composite]
    D --> D2[Sandwich Composite]
    D --> D3[Hybrid Composite]
    D --> D4[Smart Composite]

    B1 --> E1[Example: GFRP - Wind Turbine]
    B2 --> E2[Example: Al-SiC Piston]
    B3 --> E3[Example: SiC-SiC Turbine]
    B4 --> E4[Example: Tyre Carbon Black]
    B5 --> E5[Example: F1 Brake Disc]
    B6 --> E6[Example: NiAl-TiB2 Blade]
    B7 --> E7[Example: Piezo Helicopter Blade]

    C1A --> F1[Example: CFRP Airframe]
    C2 --> F2[Example: WC-Co Cutting Tool]
    C3 --> F3[Example: Plywood Sheet]

    D1 --> G1[Example: Angle Ply Wing Skin]
```

---

## 4.2 Reinforcement Geometry Comparison (Block Architecture Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[REINFORCEMENT SHAPE] --> R1[FIBRE<br/>l/d greater than 100]
    R0 --> R2[PARTICULATE<br/>l/d approx 1]
    R0 --> R3[LAMINAR<br/>sheet or flake]

    R1 --> R1A[Continuous<br/>Unidirectional]
    R1 --> R1B[Discontinuous<br/>Short or Chopped]
    R1 --> R1C[Whisker<br/>Single Crystal]

    R2 --> R2A[Spherical<br/>Particles]
    R2 --> R2B[Flake<br/>2D Platelet]

    R3 --> R3A[Stacked<br/>Veneers]
    R3 --> R3B[Clad<br/>Metal Foils]

    R1A --> USE1[High Strength along fibre axis]
    R1B --> USE2[Moderate strength, cheap to mould]
    R1C --> USE3[Near theoretical strength]

    R2A --> USE4[Isotropic strengthening<br/>Dislocation blocking]
    R2B --> USE5[2D barrier to crack growth]

    R3A --> USE6[Balanced planar properties]
    R3B --> USE7[Corrosion resistance at surface]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Smart Composite

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[SMART COMPOSITE SYSTEM] --> M[MATRIX PHASE<br/>Epoxy or PEEK]
    SC --> F[STRUCTURAL FIBRE<br/>Carbon or Glass]
    SC --> SN[SENSOR PHASE<br/>Piezoelectric or Optical Fibre]
    SC --> AC[ACTUATOR PHASE<br/>SMA or Piezo Stack]
    SC --> CT[CONTROL UNIT<br/>Microprocessor]

    SN --> CT
    CT --> AC
    AC --> M

    EXT[EXTERNAL STIMULUS<br/>Vibration, Heat, Load] --> SN
    CT --> OUT[CORRECTIVE RESPONSE<br/>Damping, Shape Change]

    M --- M1[Provides Structural Support]
    F --- F1[Carries Mechanical Load]
    SN --- S1[Detects Stimulus and Emits Signal]
    AC --- A1[Converts Signal to Mechanical Action]
```

---

## 4.4 Property Comparison Matrix (Engineering Selection Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Need What<br/>Primary Property?}
    Q -->|Lightweight + Low Cost| PMC[PMC Selection]
    Q -->|High Temperature + Tough| MMC[MMC Selection]
    Q -->|Ultra-High Temperature| CMC[CMC Selection]
    Q -->|Above 1600 C in Inert| CC[Carbon-Carbon Selection]
    Q -->|Vibration Damping| RMC[Rubber Matrix Selection]
    Q -->|Adaptive Response| SM[Smart Composite Selection]

    PMC --> PMC1[Service T less than 250 C]
    PMC --> PMC2[Density 1.2 to 1.8 g per cc]
    MMC --> MMC1[Service T up to 600 C]
    MMC --> MMC2[Density 2.5 to 4.5 g per cc]
    CMC --> CMC1[Service T up to 1400 C]
    CMC --> CMC2[Brittle but Refractory]
    CC --> CC1[Service T up to 2500 C Inert]
    CC --> CC2[Most Expensive Option]
```

---

## 4.5 Stress-Strain Behaviour Block Diagram (Composite vs Constituents)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Strain] --> B[Matrix Strain]
    A --> C[Fibre Strain]
    B --> D[Matrix Stress = Em times strain]
    C --> E[Fibre Stress = Ef times strain]
    D --> F[Weighted Sum Vf sigma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Composite Stress sigma c]
    G --> H[Composite Modulus E c]
```

---

## 4.6 Conceptual Layer Stack of a Laminated Composite

```
LAYER 1   0°   Fibres aligned with load direction
LAYER 2   45°  Off-axis fibres - takes shear loads
LAYER 3  -45°  Mirror of layer 2 - balances shear
LAYER 4   90°  Fibres transverse to load - takes perpendicular loads
LAYER 5   0°   Symmetry repeat
LAYER 6   90°  
LAYER 7  -45°  
LAYER 8   45°  
LAYER 9   0°   Mirrors layer 1 - SYMMETRIC LAMINATE
```

This [0/45/−45/90]_s sequence is the most common **quasi-isotropic layup** in aerospace. The "s" subscript denotes symmetry about the mid-plane.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**"Distinguish between Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) and Metal Matrix Composites (MMC) with respect to matrix type, service temperature, and one typical application each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~60 words):**

| Aspect | PMC | MMC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Matrix** | Thermoset (epoxy, polyester) or thermoplastic (PEEK) | Light metal (Al, Mg, Ti, Cu, Ni) |
| **Service temperature** | Up to 250 °C (thermoset), 400 °C (thermoplastic) | 300 – 600 °C |
| **Density** | 1.2 – 1.8 g/cc | 2.5 – 4.5 g/cc |
| **Typical application** | Wind turbine blade (GFRP) | Toyota engine piston (Al–SiC) |

**Key distinction:** PMC uses a **polymer** matrix and is limited by matrix softening; MMC uses a **metallic** matrix and retains properties at much higher temperatures, but is heavier and more expensive.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]
**"Define 'smart composite'. Give one example of a smart constituent and the stimulus-response pair it operates on."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~50 words):**

A **smart composite** is a material system containing a *structural matrix-reinforcement combination* integrated with **sensors, actuators, or functional phases** that allow it to *sense* an external stimulus and *respond* in a controlled manner.

**Example:** *Piezoelectric ceramic (PZT)* embedded in a carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate. When the structure is stressed (stimulus), the PZT generates a measurable voltage (response), enabling **structural health monitoring**.

---

## 5.2 PART-B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** *"Classify composite materials based on (i) matrix type and (ii) reinforcement geometry. For each classification, give one example and one characteristic property."*

**Model Answer:**

#### (i) Classification by Matrix Type

| Class | Example Composite | Characteristic Property |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC)** | Glass-fibre + epoxy | Low density (1.2–1.8 g/cc), high specific strength, max T ≈ 250 °C |
| **Metal Matrix Composite (MMC)** | Al–SiC particles | Retains strength at 300–600 °C, ductile (no brittle fracture) |
| **Ceramic Matrix Composite (CMC)** | SiC-fibre / SiC matrix | Service temperature up to 1400 °C, oxidation resistant, but brittle |
| **Plastic / Rubber Matrix** | Carbon-black + natural rubber | Excellent damping, low stiffness, used in tyres and bearings |
| **Carbon-Carbon (C/C)** | C-fibre / pyrolytic C matrix | Highest specific strength above 1600 °C in inert atmosphere |
| **Intermetallic Matrix** | NiAl–TiB₂ | Strength at 600–1000 °C, brittle base but toughened by reinforcement |
| **Smart Composite** | PZT + CFRP | Self-sensing and adaptive response |

#### (ii) Classification by Reinforcement Geometry

| Class | Example | Characteristic |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| **Fibre Reinforced (continuous)** | Unidirectional CFRP | Maximum strength along fibre direction |
| **Fibre Reinforced (discontinuous)** | Short-glass-fibre nylon | Isotropic strengthening, injection-mouldable |
| **Whisker Reinforced** | SiC-whisker / Al₂O₃ | Near-theoretical single-crystal strength |
| **Particulate Reinforced** | WC–Co (cemented carbide) | Isotropic strengthening, wear resistance |
| **Laminar Reinforced** | Plywood, laminated glass | Balanced in-plane properties |

**Examiners' Note:** A complete answer must list *all* three reinforcement geometries explicitly and give at least one matrix example. Marks are split roughly **3.5 + 3.5**.

---

### Question A(b) [7 marks]: *"Derive the Rule of Mixtures for (i) density of a composite and (ii) Young's modulus along the fibre direction. State the key assumption."*

**Model Answer:**

#### (i) Density ROM

Starting from conservation of mass:

$$
m_c = m_f + m_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad \rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

Using $V_f + V_m = 1$ (volume-fraction closure), and dividing by total volume:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

#### (ii) Modulus ROM along fibre

**Key assumption — Iso-strain:** Both fibre and matrix experience the *same strain* $\varepsilon$ when load is applied parallel to the fibres:

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon
$$

Force equilibrium on the cross-section:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

Substituting $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ and using $V_f = A_f / A_c$:

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c(\parallel) = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** for the composite modulus.

---

## 5.3 PART-B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** *"Explain with neat sketches the classification of composites based on reinforcement form. Compare the load-carrying mechanism of continuous-fibre, short-fibre, and particulate composites."*

**Model Answer:**

The reinforcement form classifies composites into three primary geometric categories:

**1. Fibre-reinforced composites** — Reinforcement has high aspect ratio $\ell/d \gg 1$. Sub-divided into:
- **Continuous-fibre:** Fibre runs unbroken across the entire composite. Load transfer via shear at the cylindrical interface. Highest strength efficiency. Example: unidirectional CFRP used in aircraft wing skins.
- **Discontinuous (short) fibre:** Fibre length $\ell < \ell_c$ or $\ell > \ell_c$. Load builds up from the fibre ends through interfacial shear. Example: injection-moulded glass-fibre nylon.
- **Whiskers:** Single-crystal short fibres with near-theoretical strength (no grain-boundary defects). Example: SiC-whisker-reinforced alumina cutting tools.

**2. Particulate-reinforced composites** — Reinforcement is approximately equiaxed ($\ell/d \approx 1$). The particles do *not* carry load continuously; instead, they **hinder dislocation motion** (in metallic matrices) or **pin cracks** (in ceramic matrices). Examples: Al–SiC pistons, WC–Co cutting tools.

**3. Laminar-reinforced composites** — Reinforcement is in *sheet* form. Examples: plywood (wood veneers bonded with adhesive), laminated safety glass (glass + PVB interlayer).

**Load-carrying comparison:**

| Form | Load Transfer Mechanism | Strength Efficiency |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Continuous fibre | Shear at cylindrical interface (full length) | Highest (~100 % of $\sigma_f$) |
| Short fibre ($\ell > \ell_c$) | Shear builds up to fibre midpoint | Moderate (60–90 %) |
| Particulate | Dislocation/crack interaction only | Lowest (10–40 %) |

---

### Question B(b) [7 marks]: *"For a unidirectional glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite, the following data are given: $V_f = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cc, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cc, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 2.0$ GPa, fibre diameter $d = 14 \mu m$, interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 35$ MPa. Calculate: (i) composite density, (ii) longitudinal modulus, (iii) transverse modulus, (iv) critical fibre length, (v) weight fraction of fibre."*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Composite Density — Rule of Mixtures**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.020 + 0.720 = \boxed{1.74 \;\text{g/cc}}
$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Modulus — Iso-Strain (Voigt) ROM**

$$
E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60)
$$

$$
E_{c,\parallel} = 28.8 + 2.1 = \boxed{30.9 \;\text{GPa}}
$$

**(iii) Transverse Modulus — Iso-Stress (Reuss) ROM**

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.40}{72} + \frac{0.60}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = 0.005556 + 0.171429 = 0.176985 \;\text{GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_{c,\perp} = \frac{1}{0.176985} = \boxed{5.65 \;\text{GPa}}
$$

**(iv) Critical Fibre Length**

$$
\ell_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^9)(14 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (35 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
\ell_c = \frac{28\,000}{70\,000\,000} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ell_c = 0.40 \;\text{mm} \;=\; 400 \,\mu\text{m}}
$$

**(v) Weight Fraction of Fibre**

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c} = \frac{(2.55)(0.40)}{1.74} = \frac{1.020}{1.74}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_f = 0.586 \;\approx\; 58.6\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite the fibre being only 40 % by volume, it constitutes 58.6 % by weight because glass is denser than epoxy. This is the *single most common mistake KTU students make* — confusing volume and weight fractions.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Laminar" with "Laminated"** — Laminar composites have *sheet reinforcements* in a matrix (e.g., plywood). Laminated composites are *stacks of cured fibre plies* at different angles. Different categories in the syllabus. (−2 marks)
>
> 2. **Mixing up $V_f$ and $W_f$** — Volume fraction is dimensionless and sums to 1. Weight fraction sums to 1 too, but its value differs whenever $\rho_f \neq \rho_m$. (−1 mark)
>
> 3. **Writing "iso-stress" for the longitudinal modulus** — The Voigt model (parallel) is iso-strain; the Reuss model (series) is iso-stress. Many students reverse them. (−2 marks)
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the key assumption in derivations** — "Iso-strain" or "Iso-stress" must be written explicitly before any ROM derivation. Examiners award 1 mark purely for stating the assumption. (−1 mark)
>
> 5. **Carbon-Carbon composites under air** — Students often forget that C/C composites **oxidize catastrophically above ~400 °C in air**, despite surviving 2500 °C in inert atmosphere. Always quote the atmosphere! (−1 mark)
>
> 6. **Smart composites without stimulus-response pair** — Just naming a smart material is incomplete. Always write: *Stimulus → Material → Response*. (−1 mark)

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Print This Before the Exam**

- [x] **Composite = Matrix (continuous) + Reinforcement (discontinuous).** Both phases must be *macroscopically distinct* — otherwise it is an alloy.
- [x] **Three classification axes:** Matrix type, Reinforcement geometry, Structural form.
- [x] **Seven matrix classes in syllabus:** PMC, MMC, CMC, Plastic-Rubber, C/C, Intermetallic, Smart.
- [x] **Three reinforcement geometries:** Fibre (continuous/discontinuous/whisker), Particulate, Laminar.
- [x] **Service temperature ladder (low → high):** PMC (250 °C) → MMC (600 °C) → CMC (1400 °C) → C/C (2500 °C inert only).
- [x] **Density ladder (light → heavy):** PMC (1.4) → CMC (3.0) → MMC (3.5) → C/C (1.8) — note C/C is *lighter than aluminium* despite being the most temperature-resistant.
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures — Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures — Modulus parallel:** $E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt / Iso-strain).
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures — Modulus transverse:** $E_{c,\perp} = (V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m)^{-1}$ (Reuss / Iso-stress).
- [x] **Critical fibre length:** $\ell_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$. Below this, fibre pulls out — strength wasted.
- [x] **Volume-fraction closure:** $V_f + V_m = 1$. Always.
- [x] **Weight-fraction conversion:** $W_f = \rho_f V_f / \rho_c$.
- [x] **Specific strength = σ/ρ.** GFRP beats steel by ~10× — the entire reason composites exist in aerospace.
- [x] **Voigt upper bound, Reuss lower bound** — real composite modulus always lies between them.
- [x] **C/C oxidises above 400 °C in air** — must be coated with SiC for high-temperature air use.
- [x] **Carbon-black-reinforced rubber** is the *oldest engineered composite* — predates modern composites by decades.
- [x] **Smart composites need stimulus → material → response triplets**, not just material names.
- [x] **Laminar ≠ Laminated.** Laminar = sheet reinforcement in matrix. Laminated = stack of fibre plies at varied angles.
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures is exact only at iso-strain**; the transverse ROM is a *lower bound*, not exact.
- [x] **Anisotropy ratio** $E_\parallel / E_\perp$ is typically 5–10 for unidirectional fibre composites — drives the need for angle-ply laminates.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PECHT742 Composite Materials**
> *KTU 2024 Scheme | Prepared per KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Protocol*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Traditional and Novel Approaches: Process Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TRADITIONAL AND NOVEL APPROACHES: PROCESS FUNDAMENTALS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Composite manufacturing processes* are defined as the controlled sequences of material placement, impregnation, consolidation, and curing operations used to convert separate reinforcement and matrix constituents into a unified, load-bearing structural component. The **Process Fundamentals** govern the thermodynamics, rheology, and kinetics that ultimately determine the fiber volume fraction $(V_f)$, void content, and final mechanical performance of the laminate.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Kitchen"

Imagine you are making a layered sandwich in two different kitchens:

1. **Traditional Kitchen (Open Mould / Hand Lay-up):** You spread peanut butter manually on bread slices, layer them by hand, and press down. It is slow, labor-intensive, but flexible. Air bubbles may be trapped — just like voids in a hand-laid laminate.

2. **Novel Kitchen (Automated / Additive):** A robotic chef precisely deposits each ingredient layer-by-layer under a sealed vacuum chamber, applies uniform heat, and cures the sandwich in a pressurized oven (autoclave). The result is consistent, low-void, high-strength.

The two kitchens represent **Traditional Approaches** (contact moulding, manual lay-up, low-pressure wet processes) and **Novel Approaches** (Out-of-Autoclave, Additive Manufacturing, Automated Fiber Placement). Both aim for the same goal — a strong, void-free composite — but differ in **process control, repeatability, and scalability**.

### 1.2 Classification of Composite Manufacturing Processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Three master families per KTU 2024 PECHT742 syllabus:**

| Family | Sub-Category | Pressure Class | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Mould (Traditional)** | Contact moulding | Atmospheric | Hand lay-up, Spray-up |
| **Closed Mould (Traditional)** | Liquid Composite Moulding (LCM) | Low pressure (< 1 MPa) | RTM, VARTM, RIFT |
| **Closed Mould (Traditional)** | Compression / Injection | Medium pressure (1–20 MPa) | SMC, BMC, Injection moulding |
| **Continuous (Traditional)** | Filament processes | Atmospheric / Vacuum | Filament winding, Pultrusion |
| **Prepreg-based (Novel / Premium)** | Out-of-Autoclave & Autoclave | High pressure (0.1–1.4 MPa) | Autoclave, OoA, VBO |
| **Automated Lay-up (Novel)** | Mechanized deposition | Variable | AFP, ATL |
| **Additive Manufacturing (Novel)** | 3D printing | Minimal | FFF, SLA, SLS, Contour Crafting |

### 1.3 Critical Process Parameters (Highlighted)

- **Curing Temperature** $(T_{cure})$ — governs crosslink density.
- **Curing Pressure** $(P_{cure})$ — typically **0.1 MPa to 1.4 MPa**.
- **Viscosity Window** $(\eta_{min})$ — resin flow phase; usually **100–500 mPa·s**.
- **Fiber Volume Fraction** $(V_f)$ — target **0.50–0.65** for structural laminates.
- **Void Content** $(V_v)$ — must be **< 1 %** for aerospace; **< 5 %** for industrial.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Traditional Process Fundamentals

### 2.1.1 Hand Lay-Up (Open Contact Moulding)

**Why it exists:** Lowest capital investment; suitable for large structures (boat hulls, wind turbine nacelle covers).

**How it works — Step logic:**
1. Mold release agent applied to single-sided mould.
2. Gelcoat (optional) sprayed for surface finish.
3. Reinforcement (chopped strand mat / woven roving) manually placed.
4. Liquid thermoset resin (polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy) brushed/rolled.
5. Compaction rollers squeeze out air and excess resin.
6. Ambient or heated cure (room temperature to **80 °C**).
7. Demould after gel time $\tau_{gel}$.

**Engineering Reality:** $V_f$ is limited to **0.20–0.35**; high void content (3–5 %) is common due to manual consolidation.

### 2.1.2 Spray-Up

- Chopped fibers (length 10–40 mm) and resin sprayed simultaneously via dual-nozzle gun.
- Compaction by hand rollers.
- Used for low-load items: shower trays, chemical tanks.

### 2.1.3 Filament Winding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Filament winding* is a continuous, automated process where resin-impregnated rovings are wound over a rotating mandrel at controlled winding angles $(\pm \theta)$ to produce axisymmetric parts such as pressure vessels, pipes, and rocket motor casings.

**Tow tension** $T$ and **winding angle** $\alpha$ are the two master control variables.
**Tensile modulus** along a fiber at angle $\alpha$ (Classical Laminate Theory simplification):

$$
E_\alpha = \left[ \frac{\cos^4\alpha}{E_1} + \frac{\sin^4\alpha}{E_2} + \sin^2\alpha\cos^2\alpha\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right) \right]^{-1}
$$

### 2.1.4 Pultrusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Pultrusion* is a continuous, steady-state process where dry reinforcement is *pulled* through a resin bath, then through a heated die where curing occurs, producing constant-cross-section profiles (rods, beams, ladders).

**Pull force** $F_{pull}$ required to overcome die-wall friction and viscous drag:

$$
F_{pull} = \mu \, P_{die} \, A_{contact} + \tau_{wall} \, A_{wall}
$$

where $\mu$ is the Coulomb friction coefficient, $P_{die}$ is the die back-pressure, and $\tau_{wall}$ is the resin shear stress.

### 2.1.5 Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM)

- Dry preform placed in matched metal mould.
- Mould closed; resin injected under **0.1–0.7 MPa**.
- Cure in mould at **80–180 °C**.
- Produces $V_f$ of **0.45–0.55** with void content **< 1 %**.

### 2.1.6 Compression Moulding — SMC/BMC

- **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)** — chopped fibers + filled resin in sheet form.
- Placed in heated press, moulds at **100–160 °C** under **5–20 MPa**.
- Cycle time 1–5 minutes — high-volume automotive.

## 2.2 Novel / Advanced Process Fundamentals

### 2.2.1 Autoclave Processing

- Stacked prepregs vacuum-bagged, then pressurized at **0.3–1.4 MPa** with concurrent heating (typically **120–180 °C** for epoxy, **175–200 °C** for BMI).
- Gold standard for aerospace: void content **< 0.5 %**.
- Cost driver: autoclave capital + long cycles (4–10 h).

### 2.2.2 Out-of-Autoclave (OoA) / VARTM / VBO

- Same prepreg concept as autoclave but uses only vacuum (≤ **0.1 MPa**) consolidation.
- Requires low-void, breathable prepregs with tailored resin flow.

### 2.2.3 Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) & Automated Tape Laying (ATL)

- Robotic heads place prepreg tows (AFP) or tapes (ATL) with **in-situ** consolidation using heat rollers or lasers.
- Throughput advantages for large surfaces (fuselage skins, wing skins).

### 2.2.4 Additive Manufacturing (AM) of Composites

- **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF):** thermoplastic/short-fiber filament extruded layer by layer.
- **SLA/DLP:** photopolymer resin reinforced with nano-clays or short fibers.
- **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS):** powdered thermoplastic/carbon composites.
- **Continuous Fiber Reinforced AM:** Markforged-style, prints continuous carbon fiber within thermoplastic matrix.

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations constitute the **must-know analytical toolkit** for PECHT742 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | $V_f$, $V_m$, $V_v$ — fiber/matrix/void volume fractions | Dimensionless | Laminate composition check |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{N \cdot A_f}{h \cdot W}$ | $N$ — number of plies; $A_f$ — ply cross-section; $h$ — laminate thickness; $W$ — width | Dimensionless | Design of stack-up |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \, e^{-E_a/RT} (1-\alpha)^n$ | $\alpha$ — degree of cure; $A$ — pre-exponential; $E_a$ — activation energy; $R$ — gas constant; $n$ — reaction order | s⁻¹ | Cure kinetics (Arrhenius) |
| 4 | $\eta(T) = \eta_\infty \, e^{E_\eta / RT}$ | $\eta$ — viscosity; $E_\eta$ — flow activation energy | Pa·s | Resin flow window |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | $Q$ — volumetric flow; $K$ — preform permeability; $\Delta P$ — pressure drop; $\mu$ — viscosity; $L$ — flow length | m³/s | RTM/VARTM fill time |
| 6 | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \, \phi \, L^2}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}$ | $\phi$ — preform porosity | s | Resin injection time |
| 7 | $F_{pull} = \mu \, P_{die} \, A_{contact} + \tau_{wall} \, A_{wall}$ | As above | N | Pultrusion die design |
| 8 | $E_\alpha = \left[\dfrac{\cos^4\alpha}{E_1} + \dfrac{\sin^4\alpha}{E_2} + \sin^2\alpha\cos^2\alpha\left(\dfrac{1}{G_{12}} - \dfrac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)\right]^{-1}$ | $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}$ — orthotropic constants | Pa | Filament winding stiffness |
| 9 | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{ct} - \rho_{meas}}{\rho_{ct}} \cdot 100$ | $\rho_{ct}$ — theoretical composite density; $\rho_{meas}$ — measured density | % | Quality control |
| 10 | $k_{eff} = k_m (1-V_f) + k_f V_f$ | $k$ — thermal conductivity; subscripts m, f | W/m·K | Rule of mixtures (thermal) |
| 11 | $\sigma_{ult} = \sigma_{f,u} \, V_f + \sigma_{m,u} \, V_m$ | $\sigma_{f,u}, \sigma_{m,u}$ — ultimate strengths | Pa | Rule of mixtures (tensile) |
| 12 | $P_{cure} \cdot V_{void} = n R T$ | Gas law for trapped voids | Pa·m³ | Void pressure prediction |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resin Injection Time in RTM (Darcy's Law Application)

**Engineering Context:** Determine how long resin takes to fill a rectangular mould of length $L$ under applied pressure $\Delta P$.

**Step 1 — State Darcy's Law** for one-dimensional resin flow through a porous preform:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = -\frac{K}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}
$$

> This is the linear momentum balance for fluid flow through a porous medium. $v$ is the superficial (Darcy) velocity, $K$ is the preform permeability tensor, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of the resin, and $dP/dx$ is the pressure gradient driving flow.

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions.** Inlet at $x=0$ is at pressure $P_{inj}$; outlet at $x=L$ is at vent pressure $P_{vent} \approx 0$ (atmospheric).

$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{P_{inj} - P_{vent}}{L} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

> This assumes linear pressure drop through the preform, valid for isothermal, low-Reynolds-number flow (Reynolds number ≪ 1).

**Step 3 — Compute superficial velocity.**

$$
v = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}
$$

> The velocity is uniform only when permeability is constant and flow is unidirectional — typical assumption in design-level RTM analysis.

**Step 4 — Relate velocity to macroscopic fill rate.** The macroscopic front position $x_f(t)$ moves at:

$$
\frac{dx_f}{dt} = \frac{v}{\phi}
$$

> $\phi$ is the preform porosity because the fluid occupies only the void space between fibers, not the bulk volume.

**Step 5 — Integrate to obtain fill time.**

$$
\int_0^{L} \phi \, dx_f = \int_0^{t_{fill}} v \, dt
$$

$$
\phi \, L = \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L} \cdot t_{fill}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \, \phi \, L^2}{2 \, K \, \Delta P} \,}
$$

> The factor of 2 emerges when flow is initiated from a line inlet (middle of mould). For inlet at one end, the relation becomes $t_{fill} = \mu \phi L^2 / (2 K \Delta P)$ for symmetric fill and $t_{fill} = \mu \phi L^2 / (K \Delta P)$ for one-sided fill.

**Engineering Insight:** The fill time scales **quadratically** with part length. Doubling the part length quadruples the fill time — a critical design constraint for large RTM parts.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cure Kinetics (Arrhenius Form)

**Engineering Context:** Predict the rate of thermoset cure as a function of temperature for process simulation.

**Step 1 — Define degree of cure $\alpha$.**

$$
\alpha(t) = \frac{Q(t)}{Q_{total}}
$$

> where $Q(t)$ is the cumulative heat released by the exothermic crosslinking reaction up to time $t$, and $Q_{total}$ is the total heat of reaction measured by DSC.

**Step 2 — State the rate law.**

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha)
$$

> The reaction rate is the product of a temperature-dependent rate constant $k(T)$ and a conversion-dependent function $f(\alpha)$ capturing the autocatalytic or $n$-th order behaviour of the resin.

**Step 3 — Apply Arrhenius temperature dependence.**

$$
k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

> $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (frequency of molecular collisions), $E_a$ is the activation energy (energy barrier for crosslink formation), $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$ is the universal gas constant.

**Step 4 — Choose reaction model.** For $n$-th order cure:

$$
f(\alpha) = (1 - \alpha)^n
$$

> For epoxy/amine systems, $n = 1$ to $2$ typically. Autocatalytic systems use $f(\alpha) = \alpha^m (1-\alpha)^n$ (Kamal model).

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT} \, (1-\alpha)^n \,}
$$

**Step 6 — Integration for isothermal cure** ($T = T_0$ constant):

$$
\int_0^{\alpha} \frac{d\alpha'}{(1-\alpha')^n} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT_0} \int_0^{t} dt'
$$

For $n \neq 1$:

$$
\frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^{1-n}}{1-n} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT_0} \cdot t
$$

> This result enables direct prediction of cure time at any operating temperature — critical for autoclave recipe design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pultrusion Pull Force (Force Balance)

**Engineering Context:** Estimate the force required to pull the cured profile out of the heated die.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the moving profile.** Steady-state condition:

$$
F_{pull} = F_{viscous} + F_{friction} + F_{gravitational}
$$

> The pulling force overcomes viscous drag of the resin on die walls, Coulomb friction at the die-profile interface, and gravity (often negligible for horizontal pultrusion).

**Step 2 — Viscous drag term** from thin-film lubrication theory:

$$
F_{viscous} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} = \mu_{resin} \cdot \frac{v_{pull}}{h_{film}} \cdot A_{wall}
$$

> where $\mu_{resin}$ is the resin viscosity, $v_{pull}$ is the line speed, $h_{film}$ is the thickness of the resin film between the profile skin and die wall.

**Step 3 — Friction term** from Coulomb's law:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot P_{die} \cdot A_{contact}
$$

> $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (typically 0.05–0.3 for resin-lubricated metal die), $P_{die}$ is the die back-pressure, $A_{contact}$ is the contact surface area.

**Step 4 — Combined expression:**

$$
\boxed{\, F_{pull} = \mu \, P_{die} \, A_{contact} + \mu_{resin} \cdot \frac{v_{pull}}{h_{film}} \cdot A_{wall} \,}
$$

> This formula guides die geometry and pull-speed selection. Excessive $F_{pull}$ causes fiber breakage; insufficient $F_{pull}$ indicates incomplete cure or die detachment.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Void Content by Density Method

**Engineering Context:** Determine the void volume fraction experimentally.

**Step 1 — Theoretical (rule-of-mixtures) density:**

$$
\rho_{ct} = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

> The composite density is the weighted sum of fiber density and matrix density, weighted by their respective volume fractions (assuming zero voids).

**Step 2 — Measured density from Archimedes' principle:**

$$
\rho_{meas} = \frac{m_{dry}}{m_{dry} - m_{submerged}}
$$

> where $m_{dry}$ is the dry mass and $m_{submerged}$ is the apparent mass when fully immersed in water (or another fluid of known density $\rho_{fluid}$).

**Step 3 — Void content formula:**

$$
V_v = \frac{\rho_{ct} - \rho_{meas}}{\rho_{ct}}
$$

> If $\rho_{meas} < \rho_{ct}$, voids are present because voids lower the actual density below the theoretical maximum. This is the standard ASTM D2734 test method.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — RTM Fill Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A rectangular RTM mould has dimensions $400 \, mm \times 400 \, mm \times 3 \, mm$. Preform permeability $K = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \, m^2$; resin viscosity $\mu = 0.3 \, Pa \cdot s$; porosity $\phi = 0.5$; injection pressure $\Delta P = 0.4 \, MPa$. Compute the fill time for one-sided injection.

**Solution:**

$$
L = 0.4 \, m
$$

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \, \phi \, L^2}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}
$$

Substitute:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{(0.3)(0.5)(0.4)^2}{2 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.4 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{(0.3)(0.5)(0.16)}{2 \cdot (0.4)}
$$

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{0.024}{0.8} = 0.03 \, s \quad (\text{Checking units})
$$

**Unit check:** $\dfrac{Pa \cdot s \cdot \text{dim} \cdot m^2}{m^2 \cdot Pa} = s$ ✓

Re-evaluate with correct unit handling:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{(0.3)(0.5)(0.16)}{2 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (4 \times 10^5)}
$$

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{0.024}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 30 \, s
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t_{fill} = 30 \, \text{seconds} \,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Acceptable for production. If the resin gel time is shorter than 30 s, fill is incomplete — gel time must be designed to exceed $t_{fill}$ by a safety factor of at least **1.5**.
