# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECHT742]**
*Compare hand lay-up and resin transfer moulding (RTM) with respect to fiber volume fraction, void content, and production volume.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | RTM |
|---|---|---|
| $V_f$ achievable | 0.20 – 0.35 | 0.45 – 0.55 |
| Typical void content | 3 – 5 % | < 1 % |
| Production volume | Low (1–50 parts/month) | Medium (100–10 000 parts/month) |
| Surface finish | One mould side only (gelcoat) | Both sides (matched mould) |
| Capital cost | Very low | Moderate |

RTM delivers superior quality because matched mould controls thickness and vacuum-assisted injection removes entrapped air; however, capital cost and tooling complexity are higher.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PECHT742]**
*State the significance of the viscosity-temperature profile in composite processing.*

**Model Answer:**
The viscosity-temperature profile dictates the **resin flow window** — the time interval during which $\eta$ is below a critical threshold (typically **100–500 mPa·s**), allowing adequate fiber wetting and air evacuation. As temperature rises, viscosity first decreases (enhanced molecular mobility) then sharply increases as the cure reaction crosses the gel point $\alpha_{gel}$. Process engineers design heating ramps to **stay within the flow window** long enough for full impregnation before gelation locks the structure. A mismatch causes dry spots (insufficient flow) or premature gelation (incomplete fill).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **pultrusion process** for continuous fiber-reinforced composites. Explain the role of the heated die, resin bath, and puller system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **pull force** required to overcome die-wall friction and viscous drag in pultrusion. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition:** Pultrusion is a continuous, steady-state process in which dry reinforcements (rovings, mats, veils) are pulled through a resin impregnation bath and then through a heated curing die to produce constant cross-section profiles.

2. **Process Steps (Schematic):**
   - Roving creels → Preforming guides → Resin bath → Preformer die → Heated curing die (typically 1–2 m long) → Puller mechanism (reciprocating caterpillar or hydraulic clamps) → Cut-off saw.
   - The die is heated in **three zones**: preheat, gel, and cure.

3. **Role of Each Component:**
   - **Resin bath:** Wets out fibers uniformly; controls resin content.
   - **Preformer:** Shapes the impregnated rovings into the desired cross-section before die entry.
   - **Heated die:** Provides activation energy for cure; constrains profile geometry.
   - **Puller:** Provides the motive force to advance the part at line speeds of **0.3–2 m/min**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Force balance on the cured profile being pulled from the die (steady state, horizontal configuration):

$$
F_{pull} = F_{viscous} + F_{friction}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state (constant line speed).
2. Constant die wall temperature.
3. Negligible gravitational contribution (horizontal die).
4. Uniform resin viscosity along the die length.
5. Fully developed laminar thin-film flow at the die wall.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Viscous drag from thin-film lubrication:

$$
F_{viscous} = \mu_{resin} \cdot \frac{v_{pull}}{h_{film}} \cdot A_{wall}
$$

Step 2 — Friction from Coulomb's law at the cured-profile die interface:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot P_{die} \cdot A_{contact}
$$

Step 3 — Combined expression:

$$
\boxed{F_{pull} = \mu \cdot P_{die} \cdot A_{contact} + \mu_{resin} \cdot \frac{v_{pull}}{h_{film}} \cdot A_{wall}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Typical $F_{pull}$ values for industrial pultrusion lines lie between **2 000–10 000 N** for small profiles; exceeding the **fiber tensile load** causes roving breakage inside the die — a critical process limit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **filament winding process** with reference to (i) winding patterns (hoop and helical), (ii) winding angle control, and (iii) the role of tow tension. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A glass-fiber/epoxy pressure vessel is filament-wound at $\pm 55°$ over an aluminium liner of internal diameter **300 mm**. The composite's orthotropic elastic constants are: $E_1 = 38 \, GPa$, $E_2 = 9 \, GPa$, $G_{12} = 3.5 \, GPa$, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$. Compute the **effective tensile modulus** along the winding direction. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Winding Patterns:**
   - **Hoop (90°):** Winding angle perpendicular to the mandrel axis; provides maximum circumferential (burst) strength.
   - **Helical:** Winding at a controlled angle $\pm \alpha$ to the axis; balances axial and hoop stress.

2. **Winding Angle Control:** Determined by the ratio of mandrel rotation speed to carriage feed rate:

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{v_{carriage}}{v_{mandrel} \cdot R_{mandrel}}
$$

3. **Role of Tow Tension:** Maintains fiber straightness, compacts the laminate, expels excess resin, and ensures uniform $V_f$. Excessive tension causes fiber breakage; insufficient tension creates voids and resin-rich zones.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Apply the off-axis modulus formula:

$$
E_\alpha = \left[\frac{\cos^4\alpha}{E_1} + \frac{\sin^4\alpha}{E_2} + \sin^2\alpha\cos^2\alpha\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)\right]^{-1}
$$

Given $\alpha = 55°$:
- $\cos 55° = 0.5736$, so $\cos^4 55° = 0.1083$
- $\sin 55° = 0.8192$, so $\sin^4 55° = 0.4503$
- $\sin^2 \cos^2 = 0.4702 \times 0.3290 = 0.1547$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{1}{E_\alpha} = \frac{0.1083}{38} + \frac{0.4503}{9} + 0.1547\left(\frac{1}{3.5} - \frac{2 \times 0.30}{38}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.00285 + 0.05004 + 0.1547(0.2857 - 0.01579)
$$

$$
= 0.00285 + 0.05004 + 0.1547 \times 0.2699
$$

$$
= 0.00285 + 0.05004 + 0.04175 = 0.09464 \, (GPa)^{-1}
$$

$$
E_\alpha = \frac{1}{0.09464} = 10.57 \, GPa
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{55°} \approx 10.57 \, GPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 55°, only ~28 % of the longitudinal modulus is retained, illustrating the strong anisotropy of unidirectional composites and the importance of winding-angle selection.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNINGS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Skipping assumptions** in derivation questions — examiners allocate **1–2 marks specifically for stating assumptions**. Always list them explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $V_f$, V_m, and $V_v$** — remember that they must sum to **1**, not individually equal 1.
> 3. **Mixing up SI units in Darcy's law** — $K$ is in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\Delta P$ in Pa, $L$ in m. Unit-check your answers.
> 4. **Wrong boundary conditions in RTM** — use $P_{vent} = 0$ (gauge) and $P_{inj}$ as gauge pressure applied.
> 5. **Omitting the factor of 2** in fill time — for one-sided injection, do NOT include the factor of 2; for center-line injection, you must.
> 6. **In filament winding modulus** — sign of $\nu_{12}$ is critical; using $\nu_{21}$ instead loses 1 mark.
> 7. **In pultrusion derivation** — forgetting the gravity term (acceptable if justified as horizontal die) but NOT mentioning it loses a mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember Before the Exam:**

- **Three families of processes:** Open Mould (Hand Lay-up, Spray-up), Closed Mould (RTM, SMC, Injection), Continuous (Pultrusion, Filament Winding).
- **Novel / Advanced:** Autoclave, OoA, AFP/ATL, Additive Manufacturing.
- **Master equation:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.
- **Darcy's law** drives RTM fill time: $t_{fill} = \mu \phi L^2 / (2 K \Delta P)$.
- **Cure kinetics:** Arrhenius, $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A e^{-E_a/RT} (1-\alpha)^n$.
- **Pultrusion pull force:** $F_{pull} = \mu P_{die} A_{contact} + \mu_{resin} v_{pull} A_{wall} / h_{film}$.
- **Filament winding modulus:** $E_\alpha$ formula with $\cos^4 + \sin^4$ terms.
- **Void content test:** ASTM D2734 density method using Archimedes' principle.
- **Quality targets:** $V_f = 0.50$–$0.65$; $V_v < 1\%$ aerospace, $< 5\%$ industrial.
- **Pressure classes:** Hand lay-up — atmospheric; RTM — 0.1–0.7 MPa; Autoclave — 0.3–1.4 MPa; SMC Compression — 5–20 MPa.
- **Temperature window:** Resin viscosity minimum $\eta \approx 100$–$500$ mPa·s.
- **Process selection criteria:** Production volume, part geometry, $V_f$ target, surface finish, capital cost.
- **AM of composites:** FFF (thermoplastic), SLA (photopolymer + nano-additives), Continuous Fiber Reinforced AM (Markforged-style).
- **Out-of-Autoclave (OoA):** Vacuum-only consolidation; requires low-void, breathable prepregs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Matrix Composites: Fabrication of Fibres, Plastic Fibre Forms, Pre-Pregs, Moulding Compounds and Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polymer Matrix Composites: Fabrication of Fibres, Plastic Fibre Forms, Pre-Pregs, Moulding Compounds and Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs) — Formal Definition

A **Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC)** is a heterogeneous material consisting of a high-strength, high-modulus **reinforcement phase** (typically fibres such as glass, carbon, or aramid) embedded in a **polymer matrix phase** (either a thermosetting resin like epoxy, polyester, vinylester, or a thermoplastic resin like PEEK, PPS). The matrix serves to bind the fibres together, transfer loads to the fibres, protect them from environmental degradation, and maintain the geometric shape of the component.

$$V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$$

where $V_f$, $V_m$, and $V_v$ are the **fibre volume fraction**, **matrix volume fraction**, and **void volume fraction** respectively. The sum of the volumetric constituents must always equal unity for a fully dense composite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within Module 2, the focus shifts from "why we use composites" to "how we actually make them." Mastery of fabrication routes is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) questions, where examiners frequently test process selection, sequence steps, and advantages/limitations of each manufacturing technique.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **reinforced concrete** — the steel rebars carry tensile loads while the surrounding concrete resists compression and holds everything together. A PMC works on the same principle, except the "concrete" is replaced by a lightweight polymer (like epoxy) and the "steel rebar" is replaced by ultra-strong fibres (like carbon or glass). The result is a material that is far lighter and stronger than either phase acting alone.

Another simple analogy: a **fibreglass boat hull** is essentially layers of glass-fibre cloth soaked in liquid polyester resin, hardened into a single stiff shell. The cloth gives strength; the resin keeps it stiff and water-resistant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for KTU Boards:** The **fibre volume fraction ($V_f$)** in commercial PMCs typically ranges from **0.30 to 0.60** (i.e., 30–60% by volume). Aerospace-grade carbon/epoxy prepregs aim for $V_f \geq 0.60$. Always quote a value with two significant figures in exams.

### 1.3 Sub-Classification of PMCs (Foundation for the Topic)

| Sub-class | Reinforcement Form | Typical Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Fibre-Reinforced PMC** | Continuous / discontinuous fibres | Hand lay-up, filament winding, pultrusion |
| **Particulate PMC** | Fillers, micro-spheres | Injection moulding, casting |
| **Laminate PMC** | Stacked fabric / prepreg plies | Autoclave, compression moulding |
| **Whisker / Nanocomposite** | Sub-micron fillers | Speciality processes |

The rest of this module deals primarily with the **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP)** sub-class, which is the most industrially relevant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Fibre Fabrication

The fabrication of reinforcing fibres is the upstream gateway for the entire PMC industry. The student must understand that fibres are not simply "found in nature" — they are engineered through carefully controlled processes.

**Step 1 — Selection of precursor chemistry.** Each fibre family has a distinct precursor. Glass fibres use molten silicate melts; carbon fibres use polyacrylonitrile (PAN) or pitch; aramid fibres use a liquid-crystal polymer solution.

**Step 2 — Conversion to fibre geometry.** The precursor is drawn through a spinneret (for polymers) or a bushing (for glass) to form fine filaments of diameter **5–25 µm**. Smaller diameter means higher flexibility and strength.

**Step 3 — Drawing and stretching.** The filaments are stretched at controlled temperature to align the molecular chains, increasing tensile strength dramatically.

**Step 4 — Surface treatment (sizing).** A chemical coating (silane for glass, epoxy-compatible for carbon) is applied so the fibre bonds chemically with the polymer matrix during composite fabrication. Without sizing, the fibre–matrix interface is weak.

**Step 5 — Collection into fibre forms.** Bare filaments are bundled into usable product forms such as rovings, tows, mats, and fabrics.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Fibre-Form

- **Why rovings?** Rovings (untwisted bundles of filaments) provide fast wet-out in resin baths, ideal for pultrusion and filament winding.
- **Why woven fabrics?** Woven rovings give balanced biaxial strength and drapeability over curved moulds.
- **Why chopped strand mats (CSM)?** CSM offers isotropic in-plane strength at low cost — perfect for hand lay-up of boat hulls.
- **Why unidirectional (UD) tapes?** UD tapes concentrate strength along one axis — used in aerospace primary structures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula you are likely to need in a KTU 14-mark problem on PMC fabrication. Memorize the units and the **boundaries** of each equation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule of mixtures (tensile modulus, longitudinal) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Predicts stiffness along fibre direction |
| 2 | Rule of mixtures (tensile modulus, transverse) | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Predicts stiffness across fibres |
| 3 | Rule of mixtures (strength, longitudinal) | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | First-pass strength estimate |
| 4 | Density of composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Weight estimation |
| 5 | Fibre volume fraction (from weights) | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | Quality-control check after fabrication |
| 6 | Void content | $V_v = 1 - \rho_{\text{actual}} / \rho_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Process-quality indicator ($V_v < 0.02$ is acceptable) |
| 7 | Specific strength | $\sigma / \rho$ | Used to justify fibre choice in aerospace |
| 8 | Pultrusion throughput | $L_{\text{out}} = v \cdot t$ | Line speed × time profile |
| 9 | Cure degree (DSC) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{released}}}{\Delta H_{\text{total}}}$ | Process-monitoring parameter |
| 10 | Mass of fibre needed | $M_f = \rho_f V_f A L$ | Pre-preparation calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Tip:** KTU answers accept either $V_f$ or $v_f$ for volume fraction, but always define the symbol the first time you use it to gain method marks. Use $W_f$ for weight (mass), $M_f$ for mass in SI — avoid mixing them.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Carbon-fibre/epoxy autoclave laminates reduce aircraft weight by **20–30%**, cutting fuel burn.
- **Automotive:** Glass-fibre/polyester SMC compression mouldings replace steel panels at half the density (**~1.9 g/cm³**).
- **Civil:** Glass-fibre/vinylester pultruded profiles are used in walkways, ladders, and chemical plant structures because they are corrosion-resistant.
- **Wind energy:** E-glass/epoxy blade roots and spars are filament-wound for high fatigue life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

### 3.1 Derivation — Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$) of a Unidirectional Composite

We begin with the classical **iso-strain** assumption: the fibre and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ when loaded along the fibre direction.

The load carried by the composite is the sum of loads carried by each phase (parallel-action, like a set of resistors in parallel).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= F_f + F_m \\
\sigma_c A_c &= \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Since strain is the same in both phases ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$) and stresses relate to strains via Hooke's law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$), we substitute.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c A_c &= E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m \\
\sigma_c &= E_f \varepsilon \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \varepsilon \frac{A_m}{A_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

The area ratios are equal to volume fractions for a unidirectional bar (cross-sections are identical at every station along the length). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c &= \varepsilon (E_f V_f + E_m V_m) \\
E_L &= \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **rule of mixtures for longitudinal modulus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic of the final step:** We divided stress by strain to obtain modulus. Because $E_L$ depends linearly on $V_f$, the fibre (stiffer) phase dominates — this is why a high $V_f$ is so valuable in aerospace laminates.

### 3.2 Derivation — Transverse Modulus ($E_T$) of a Unidirectional Composite

When the load is applied **perpendicular** to the fibres, the fibre and matrix experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ (iso-stress, like springs in series).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_c &= \varepsilon_f + \varepsilon_m \quad \text{(deformations add)} \\
\frac{\sigma}{E_T} &= \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\sigma$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transverse modulus $E_T$ is **dominated by the matrix** because of the inverse relationship. Even a small matrix volume fraction drops $E_T$ significantly — a fact KTU examiners often exploit in 14-mark problems.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.45$. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate (a) the longitudinal modulus $E_L$, (b) the transverse modulus $E_T$, and (c) the composite density $\rho_c$.

**Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.45 \Rightarrow V_m = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$.

**(a) Longitudinal modulus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
&= (72 \times 0.45) + (3.5 \times 0.55) \\
&= 32.4 + 1.925 \\
&= 34.325 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Transverse modulus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_T} &= \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \\
&= \frac{0.45}{72} + \frac{0.55}{3.5} \\
&= 0.00625 + 0.15714 \\
&= 0.16339 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.16339} \approx 6.12 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(c) Composite density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\
&= (2.55 \times 0.45) + (1.20 \times 0.55) \\
&= 1.1475 + 0.66 \\
&= 1.8075 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Glass/epoxy laminates typically have $\rho_c \approx 1.8$–$2.0$ g/cm³. Our answer **1.81 g/cm³** matches industry data, confirming the calculation is correct.

### 3.4 Process Selection Logic (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board often asks: "Select a process for the following component: small boat hull, aerospace wing skin, large pipe, car bumper." The matrix below gives the analytical reasoning.

| Component | Process Selected | Justification (Key Reasons) |
|---|---|---|
| Small boat hull | Hand lay-up | Low tooling cost, large area, low-volume production |
| Aerospace wing skin | Autoclave of prepreg | Very high $V_f$ (>60%), low void content (<1%) required |
| Large chemical pipe | Filament winding / pultrusion | Continuous fibre alignment along hoop/axial loads |
| Car bumper | SMC compression moulding | High production rate (cycle ~ 2 min), class-A finish |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow of PMC Manufacturing (Module-Level Map)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete value chain** of a polymer matrix composite — from raw precursor to finished part. Note the **alpha-rule** applied to every node identifier (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`) and the absence of any markdown formatting inside node labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PRECURSOR SELECTION"] --> nodeB["PAN OR PITCH OR SILICATE MELT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SPINNING OR DRAWING"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FIBRE SURFACE TREATMENT AND SIZING"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["FIBRE FORMS - ROVINGS MATS FABRICS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF1["DRY FIBRE ROUTES - HAND LAYUP SPRAYUP RTM"]
    nodeE --> nodeF2["PREPREG ROUTES - AUTOCLAVE COMPRESSION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF3["CONTINUOUS ROUTES - PULTRUSION FILAMENT WINDING"]
    nodeE --> nodeF4["MOULDING COMPOUND ROUTES - SMC BMC INJECTION"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG["FINISHED PMC COMPONENT"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeF4 --> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Pultrusion Process (Functional Architecture)

Pultrusion is one of the most tested continuous-fabrication processes in KTU exams. The diagram below shows the **functional blocks** in line order.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["ROVING CREELS - FIBRE SUPPLY"] --> node2["RESIN IMPREGNATION BATH"]
    node2 --> node3["PRE-FORMING AND GUIDE DIES"]
    node3 --> node4["HEATED DIE - CURE ZONE 120 TO 180 C"]
    node4 --> node5["PULLING MECHANISM - CATERPILLAR GRIP"]
    node5 --> node6["CUT-OFF SAW - LENGTH CONTROL"]
    node6 --> node7["PULTRUDED PROFILE STOCK"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Hand Lay-Up Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["MOULD PREPARATION - CLEAN RELEASE AGENT"] --> node2["GEL COAT APPLICATION IF REQUIRED"]
    node2 --> node3["FIRST RESIN LAYER BRUSH OR ROLLER"]
    node3 --> node4["FIRST FIBRE REINFORCEMENT PLACED"]
    node4 --> node5["DEBULKING WITH ROLLER - REMOVE AIR"]
    node5 --> node6["REPEAT LAYER STACKING AS PER LAMINATE SCHEDULE"]
    node6 --> node7["COVER AND CURE AT ROOM TEMPERATURE OR OVEN"]
    node7 --> node8["DEMOULDING AND TRIMMING"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Filament Winding Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FIBRE TOWS FROM CREELS"] --> nodeB["RESIN BATH OR HOT-MELT TANK"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PAY-OUT EYES AND GUIDES"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["WINDING HEAD - EYE AND CARRIAGE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ROTATING MANDREL WITH PROGRAMMED GEOMETRY"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CURE - ROOM TEMP OR OVEN"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["EXTRACT MANDREL AND MACHINE ENDS"]
```

### 4.5 Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DRY PREFORM PLACED IN MOULD"] --> nodeB["MOULD CLOSURE AND CLAMPING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["RESIN INJECTION UNDER PRESSURE 1 TO 5 BAR"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["RESIN FLOW THROUGH FIBRE PREFORM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["VENT AT MOULD EXIT CLOSES WHEN RESIN APPEARS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CURE STAGE AT ELEVATED TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DEMOULDING AND TRIM"]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Prepreg → Autoclave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["FIBRE TOW OR FABRIC"] --> node2["RESIN FILM OR HOT-MELT COATING UNIT"]
    node2 --> node3["PREPREG SHEET - STORED AT MINUS 18 C"]
    node3 --> node4["LAY-UP ON TOOL - PLY ORIENTATION CONTROL"]
    node4 --> node5["VACUUM BAG AND BREATHERS"]
    node5 --> node6["AUTOCLAVE - HEAT AND PRESSURE 6 TO 7 BAR"]
    node6 --> node7["CURED LAMINATE - DEMOULDED AND NDI INSPECTED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Reminder:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `node1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels use clean uppercase text only, no bold or italic markers inside the double-quoted strings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is a prepreg? State two advantages and one limitation of using prepregs in composite manufacturing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **prepreg** (pre-impregnated composite) is a reinforcement fabric or unidirectional tow that has been pre-impregnated with a partially cured (B-staged) thermoset resin system. The resin content, fibre alignment, and tack level are precisely controlled by the manufacturer.

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. High and consistent fibre volume fraction (typically $V_f = 0.55$ to 0.65) — better than hand lay-up.
2. Uniform resin distribution and low void content (<1%) when cured in autoclave.

**Limitation (1):**
Prepregs require **cold storage (–18 °C)** to prevent premature cure and have a limited **out-life / shelf life** of typically 6–12 months at room temperature. They are also more expensive than wet lay-up resins.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) and BMC (Bulk Moulding Compound) with respect to fibre length, form, and typical application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SMC | BMC |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre length | 25–50 mm (longer) | 6–12 mm (shorter) |
| Form | Sheet, 3–6 mm thick | Dough / putty-like bulk |
| Flow behaviour | High flow during moulding | Lower flow, easier to charge complex cavities |
| Typical application | Car body panels, bath tubs, large flat panels | Electrical insulators, small intricate parts |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In a 14-mark Part B question, the examiner's marking key allocates marks as follows — **Process description / sequence: 5–6 marks**, **Sketch / diagram: 3–4 marks**, **Advantages & limitations: 2–3 marks**, **Applications: 1–2 marks**. Skipping the sketch alone costs you **3–4 marks** even if your written description is perfect. Always include a labelled schematic.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] — 7 marks
*Describe the **pultrusion process** for manufacturing fibre-reinforced polymer composites with a neat sketch. List its advantages, limitations, and typical applications.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pultrusion is a continuous manufacturing process in which dry reinforcement fibres (rovings, mats, or fabrics) are pulled through a resin bath and then through a heated die where curing occurs, producing a constant-cross-section profile.

**Working sequence (3 marks):**
1. Rovings are drawn from creels and fed into a **resin impregnation bath** containing thermoset resin (polyester, vinylester, or epoxy) with hardener and fillers.
2. The wet fibres are guided through a **pre-forming die** that removes excess resin and shapes the bundle.
3. The bundle enters a **heated steel die** (120–180 °C) where the resin cures as the composite moves continuously.
4. A **caterpillar-type pulling mechanism** maintains a steady line speed (typically 0.5–2 m/min).
5. The cured profile exits the die and is cut to length by an **automatic cut-off saw**.

**Sketch (2 marks):** A labelled flow diagram showing Creels → Resin Bath → Pre-former → Heated Die → Puller → Cut-off Saw.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High fibre volume fraction (up to 65%), excellent strength along fibre direction.
- Continuous process — high production rate, low labour cost per metre.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Restricted to **constant cross-section** profiles only.
- High capital cost of die and puller system.

**Typical Applications:** Pultruded structural sections such as **I-beams, channels, ladder rails, walkways, and chemical plant gratings**.

---

**(b)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] — 7 marks
*Explain the **filament winding process** for polymer matrix composites. What are the different winding patterns used? Mention any three industrial applications.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Filament winding is a process in which continuous fibre tows pre-impregnated with resin are wound over a rotating mandrel in a controlled geometric pattern, then cured to produce hollow, axisymmetric composite parts.

**Sequence of steps (3 marks):**
1. Continuous fibre tows are drawn from a creel rack.
2. The tows pass through a **resin bath** (wet winding) or are pre-impregnated (dry winding).
3. The tows are guided by a **pay-out eye** mounted on a carriage that traverses along the rotating mandrel.
4. A computer-controlled winding programme lays the fibres at a precise **winding angle** and **band width**.
5. After winding, the part is cured at room temperature (with post-cure) or in an oven.
6. The mandrel is extracted, leaving a hollow composite part.

**Winding patterns (2 marks):**
- **Helical winding:** Fibres laid at a fixed angle (typically 30°–70° to the mandrel axis) — used for pressure vessels.
- **Polar/equatorial winding:** Fibres wound near the polar regions; suitable for end-closures.
- **Hoop winding:** Fibres wound nearly perpendicular to the axis (88°–90°), providing high hoop strength in pipes.

**Applications (1 mark):** Rocket motor casings, CNG cylinders, water and chemical storage tanks, drive shafts, golf club shafts.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 7 marks
*Describe the **hand lay-up technique** for fabricating polymer matrix composites. Compare it with the **spray-up technique** in terms of process, fibre form, fibre length, $V_f$ achieved, and production rate.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Hand Lay-Up Process (4 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hand lay-up is the oldest, simplest, and most widely used open-mould process in which reinforcement plies are placed manually into an open mould and impregnated with liquid resin by brushing or rolling.

**Sequence (3 marks):**
1. The mould surface is cleaned and coated with a **release agent** (wax or PVA) to enable easy demoulding.
2. A **gel coat** (pigmented resin) is applied if a smooth, finished outer surface is required.
3. The first layer of **liquid thermoset resin** (polyester or epoxy with hardener) is poured/brushed onto the mould.
4. A reinforcement layer (**chopped strand mat, woven roving, or fabric**) is laid and **rolled** with a metal roller to wet-out the fibre and remove entrapped air.
5. Steps 3–4 are repeated according to the **laminate schedule** until the desired thickness is reached.
6. The laminate is allowed to **cure at room temperature** (or with mild heating), then demoulded and trimmed.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre form | Mat / fabric | Chopped fibres + resin sprayed simultaneously |
| Fibre length | Long (continuous mat) or fabric | Short (10–40 mm chopped) |
| $V_f$ achieved | 0.25 – 0.40 | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| Production rate | Low (skilled labour intensive) | Higher (chopper gun speed) |
| Surface finish | Better (can use gel coat) | Rougher, less uniform |
| Typical product | Boat hulls, large tanks | Truck cabs, shower trays, simple enclosures |

---

**(b)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 7 marks
*Explain the **resin transfer moulding (RTM)** process with a neat diagram. What are its advantages over hand lay-up? List any three applications.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** RTM is a closed-mould process in which a dry fibre preform is placed inside a matched mould and liquid resin is injected under pressure to impregnate the reinforcement before curing.

**Working sequence (3 marks):**
1. A **dry fibre preform** (woven fabric, mat, or stitched stack) is placed in the lower half of a closed mould.
2. The mould is closed and clamped.
3. **Catalysed resin** is injected under pressure (1–5 bar) through a port until it appears at the vent.
4. Resin flows through the preform, displacing air which escapes through vents.
5. After resin fill, the mould is heated to cure the part.
6. The part is demoulded, trimmed, and inspected.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Labelled diagram showing upper and lower mould halves, preform, resin inlet, vent, clamping bolts.

**Advantages over hand lay-up (1.5 marks):**
- Higher and more consistent $V_f$ (up to 0.55).
- Lower void content (<1%) due to controlled resin flow.
- Better surface finish on **both sides** (closed mould).
- Reduced styrene emissions, healthier working environment.
- Higher repeatability and shorter cycle time for medium volumes.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Higher tooling cost than hand lay-up.
- Limited to part sizes that fit in the press/clamp.

**Applications (1.5 marks):** Automotive body panels (BMW 7-series roof), aerospace fairings, wind-turbine blade components, bath tubs, and small boat hulls.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Always state the resin system** (epoxy, polyester, vinylester) when describing a process — KTU examiners award half a mark for this.
- [ ] **Always include a labelled diagram** for any 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question — it carries 2–3 marks.
- [ ] **Memorize the rule of mixtures** in both longitudinal and transverse forms; many numericals are built on it.
- [ ] **Know the typical $V_f$ range** for each process: hand lay-up (0.25–0.40), RTM (0.45–0.55), autoclave prepreg (0.55–0.65), SMC (0.20–0.30).
- [ ] **Void content target** is **<2%** for structural PMCs and **<1%** for aerospace primary structures.
- [ ] **Prepregs** must be stored at **–18 °C** and have a **shelf life** of 6–12 months at room temperature.
- [ ] **Pultrusion** = constant cross-section; **filament winding** = hollow axisymmetric parts; **autoclave** = highest-performance aerospace laminates.
- [ ] **Sizing** is critical — it is the chemical coupling agent that bonds the fibre to the matrix; never omit it in process descriptions.
- [ ] **SMC** is for **flat or slightly curved** high-volume panels; **BMC** is for **intricate small** parts.
- [ ] **Hand lay-up** is still the **lowest-cost** process; **RTM** is the **best compromise** between cost and quality for medium volumes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning:** A common mistake is confusing **filament winding** with **pultrusion**. Filament winding wraps fibres around a *rotating mandrel* (hollow parts), while pultrusion *pulls straight fibres* through a *stationary die* (constant cross-section profiles). Losing this distinction costs you 2 marks per question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lay-Ups, Filament Winding, Pultrusion and Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lay-Ups, Filament Winding, Pultrusion and Recycling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lay-Up Processes
Lay-up techniques are the most fundamental and widely employed open-mold methods for manufacturing fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites. They involve the manual or semi-automated placement of reinforcing fibers and resin onto an open mold surface, followed by curing at ambient or elevated temperatures.

**Types of Lay-Up Processes:**
- **Hand Lay-Up (Contact Molding):** The oldest, simplest, and most labor-intensive method where reinforcement mats/fabrics are manually placed into a mold and impregnated with liquid resin using rollers or brushes.
- **Spray Lay-Up:** A semi-automated variant where chopped fibers and resin are simultaneously sprayed onto the mold surface using a spray gun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Lay-up processes are categorized as **open-mold processes** with low tooling cost, high flexibility, and suitability for low-to-medium volume production (typically 100–1000 parts/year).

### 1.2 Filament Winding
Filament winding is a closed-mold, automated process in which continuous rovings (fiber bundles) of reinforcement are impregnated with a resin bath and wound over a rotating mandrel in a predetermined geometric pattern under controlled tension. The angle, bandwidth, and sequence of winding are precisely programmed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 PECHT742):** *Filament winding is a process in which continuous reinforcing fibers pre-impregnated with a thermosetting resin are wound under controlled tension over a rotating mandrel whose surface contour corresponds to the inner geometry of the finished composite part.*

### 1.3 Pultrusion
Pultrusion is a continuous, automated, closed-mold manufacturing process for producing constant cross-section fiber-reinforced polymer profiles. The word "pultrusion" is derived from **"pull"** + **"extrusion"**, where dry reinforcements are pulled through a resin bath and then through a heated die where curing occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pultrusion produces profiles with a uniform cross-section continuously, like how pasta is extruded — but instead of pushing, the material is *pulled* through the die.**

### 1.4 Recycling of Composites
Composite recycling refers to the recovery and reprocessing of fiber-reinforced polymer waste (end-of-life parts, manufacturing scrap, cured laminates) to recover fibers, polymers, or energy while minimizing environmental impact.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a composite like a hard-boiled egg (fibers + cured resin). Recycling is like trying to separate the white (resin) from the yolk (fibers) after cooking — possible, but difficult because the original components have been chemically/physically fused.

**Physical/Process Constants to Remember:**
- Typical cure temperature for epoxy in filament winding: **80 °C – 180 °C**
- Typical pultrusion die temperature: **120 °C – 200 °C**
- Standard fiber tension in filament winding: **0.5 N to 50 N per tow**
- Hand lay-up production rate: **~0.5 to 2 kg/hr per worker**
- Pultrusion line speed: **0.5 to 4 m/min**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Composite Manufacturing (KTU 2024):** Lay-up (manual), Filament Winding (rotational automated), Pultrusion (continuous linear automated). Each addresses different geometries and production volumes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hand Lay-Up Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The hand lay-up process follows a strict sequence:

- **Step 1 — Mold Preparation:** The mold surface is cleaned and treated with a **mold release agent** (wax or PVA) to allow easy part demolding.
- **Step 2 — Gel Coat Application (Optional):** A pigmented resin layer (0.3–0.5 mm) is applied first to provide a smooth, aesthetically finished outer surface and UV protection.
- **Step 3 — Reinforcement Placement:** Fabrics, mats, or woven rovings are laid onto the mold manually. Multiple plies are stacked to achieve the desired thickness.
- **Step 4 — Resin Impregnation:** Liquid thermosetting resin (polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy) is poured or brushed onto the reinforcement.
- **Step 5 — Compaction / Roll-out:** Hand rollers (serrated or paddle) are used to remove entrapped air, consolidate the laminate, and ensure uniform resin distribution.
- **Step 6 — Curing:** The laminate is allowed to cure at room temperature (or with mild heating for post-cure). Cure time ranges from several hours to days.
- **Step 7 — Demolding and Trimming:** Once cured, the part is removed from the mold and edges are trimmed.

**Why and How:**
- **Why manual?** Lay-up is used for low-volume, large parts (boat hulls, wind turbine blades, chemical tanks).
- **How quality controlled?** Operator skill, resin-to-fiber ratio control, and roller technique govern the void content (target < 2%).

### 2.2 Spray Lay-Up Process

- A spray gun simultaneously chops continuous roving into short fibers (10–40 mm) and sprays them with catalyzed resin onto the mold.
- Operators manually roll the laminate to compact and remove air.
- **Disadvantage:** High styrene emission, variable fiber length distribution, and limited mechanical properties compared to hand lay-up with continuous fibers.

### 2.3 Filament Winding Process — Detailed Logic

- **Step 1 — Mandrel Preparation:** A mandrel matching the inner geometry of the part is mounted on the winding machine.
- **Step 2 — Fiber Delivery:** Continuous rovings from creels are drawn through a resin bath (wet winding) or used as prepreg (dry winding).
- **Step 3 — Winding Pattern:** The fiber delivery head traverses along the rotating mandrel at a programmed speed, defining the **winding angle (α)**.
- **Step 4 — Tension Control:** Each tow is maintained under controlled tension to ensure compaction, fiber alignment, and minimal slippage.
- **Step 5 — Curing:** After winding, the part is cured either at room temperature (with mandrel in place) or in an oven/autoclave.
- **Step 6 — Mandrel Extraction:** The mandrel is removed, leaving a hollow composite structure.

**Winding Pattern Geometries:**
- **Hoop (Circumferential) Winding:** α ≈ 90° (mostly circumferential, used for pressure vessels)
- **Helical Winding:** α between 15° and 85° (used for pipes, rocket motor cases)
- **Polar Winding:** α varies continuously (used for spherical pressure vessels)

### 2.4 Pultrusion Process — Sequential Logic

- **Step 1 — Fiber Roving Placement:** Continuous fiber rovings and mats are fed from creels into a guide system.
- **Step 2 — Resin Impregnation:** Fibers pass through a resin bath (or resin injection chamber) where they are thoroughly wet-out.
- **Step 3 — Pre-Forming:** Excess resin is removed and the bundle is shaped into the approximate die profile.
- **Step 4 — Heated Die Curing:** The wet-out bundle is pulled through a heated die (typically 1–1.5 m long) where it is compacted, shaped, and cured via exothermic resin reaction.
- **Step 5 — Pulling Mechanism:** A continuous caterpillar or reciprocating clamp puller maintains a constant line speed.
- **Step 6 — Cutting:** A flying cut-off saw cuts the cured profile to required lengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Pultrusion is Efficient:** It produces constant cross-section profiles continuously with minimal labor, high fiber volume fraction (V<sub>f</sub> up to 60–70%), and excellent alignment of fibers along the axis — making it the most cost-effective method for structural profiles.

### 2.5 Composite Recycling — Methodological Analysis

The three primary recycling routes for FRP composites are:

- **Mechanical Recycling:** Shredding/grinding composite scrap into filler-sized particles used as reinforcement in new composites or as fuel in cement kilns. Simple, low-cost, but downgrades fiber length.
- **Thermal Recycling (Pyrolysis & Fluidized Bed):**
  - **Pyrolysis:** Heating composites in an oxygen-free environment at 400 °C – 700 °C, decomposing the resin matrix and recovering clean fibers.
  - **Fluidized Bed Process:** Heated sand bed at 450 °C – 550 °C oxidizes the resin and liberates fibers.
- **Chemical Recycling (Solvolysis):** Using solvents (water, alcohols, acids) at elevated temperature and pressure to break down the resin and recover both fibers and monomeric chemicals.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** Recycling of wind turbine blades (e.g., the EU's EoL-Blade project), automotive shredder residue, and aerospace CFRP scrap is increasingly important for circular economy compliance.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber Volume Fraction (V<sub>f</sub>) | $V_f = \dfrac{V_{fiber}}{V_{composite}}$ | Ratio of fiber volume to total composite volume (dimensionless) |
| Resin-to-Fiber Weight Ratio | $R/F = \dfrac{W_{resin}}{W_{fiber}}$ | Typically 0.3 – 0.6 in hand lay-up |
| Theoretical Density of Composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| Winding Angle Relation | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\text{mandrel circumference}}{\text{pitch per revolution}}$ | Determines fiber orientation |
| Pultrusion Pulling Force Estimate | $F_{pull} = A_c \cdot \tau_{wall}$ | $A_c$ = cross-section area, $\tau_{wall}$ = wall shear stress in die (~0.05–0.2 MPa) |
| Cylinder Hoop Stress | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | For filament-wound pressure vessels, $P$ = internal pressure, $r$ = radius, $t$ = thickness |
| Cylinder Axial Stress | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2t}$ | For closed-end pressure vessels |
| Lame's Equation (Thick Cylinders) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \dfrac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r^2}$ | Used for thick-walled composite cylinders |
| Effective Composite Modulus (Rule of Mixtures, longitudinal) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | For unidirectional composites along fiber direction |
| Effective Composite Modulus (transverse) | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures for transverse direction |
| Recovery Yield (Recycling) | $\eta_R = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Mass-based recovery efficiency (%) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Filament Winding Winding Angle

The winding angle α is the angle that the fiber tow makes with the axis of the mandrel. It is determined by the ratio of axial travel of the fiber delivery carriage to the circumferential advance of the mandrel per revolution.

**Starting Geometry:**
Let:
- $D$ = mandrel diameter
- $v_c$ = carriage traverse speed (m/s) along the mandrel axis
- $N$ = mandrel rotational speed (rev/s)
- $P$ = pitch (axial distance covered in one full revolution) $= \dfrac{v_c}{N}$
- $C$ = mandrel circumference $= \pi D$

**Step 1: Arc length per revolution**
The fiber advances one full circumference per revolution:

$$L_{arc} = \pi D$$

**Step 2: Axial advance per revolution**

$$L_{axial} = P = \frac{v_c}{N}$$

**Step 3: Tangent of winding angle**
By geometry, the winding angle satisfies:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L_{arc}}{L_{axial}} = \frac{\pi D}{P} = \frac{\pi D \cdot N}{v_c}$$

$$\boxed{\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\pi D \cdot N}{v_c}}$$

**Step 4: Special cases**
- For **hoop winding** (α → 90°), the carriage advances very little per revolution: $v_c \to 0$, meaning $P \to 0$.
- For **low-angle helical winding** (α → small), $v_c$ is large relative to $\pi D N$.

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**
Given: $D = 0.4$ m, $N = 30$ rev/min $= 0.5$ rev/s, $v_c = 0.2$ m/s.

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 0.5}{0.2} = \frac{0.6283}{0.2} = 3.1416$$

$$\alpha = \arctan(3.1416) \approx 72.34°$$

This corresponds to a near-hoop winding.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density

The density of a fiber-reinforced composite, assuming perfect bonding and no voids, is the volume-weighted average of the constituent densities.

**Step 1: Define volume fractions**

$$V_f + V_m = 1 \quad \text{(assuming no void content)}$$

**Step 2: Express mass in terms of volume**

$$m_c = \rho_c V_c, \quad m_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad m_m = \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 3: Apply conservation of mass**

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 4: Normalize by $V_c$**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 5: Since $V_c = V_f + V_m$:**

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$$

**Numerical Example:**
Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $\rho_f$ (carbon) $= 1800$ kg/m³, $\rho_m$ (epoxy) $= 1200$ kg/m³.

$$\rho_c = 1800 \times 0.55 + 1200 \times 0.45$$

$$\rho_c = 990 + 540 = 1530 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Pultrusion Pulling Force Balance

In pultrusion, the cured profile is pulled by a constant force that must overcome viscous drag, friction at the die wall, and the internal pressure built up by resin exotherm and curing shrinkage.

**Step 1: Define control volume**
Consider a differential element of length $dz$ of the die.

**Step 2: Force balance on a cured profile segment**

$$F_{pull} = F_{viscous} + F_{friction} + F_{back\ pressure}$$

**Step 3: Wall shear stress contribution**
Assuming Couette-flow-like drag at the die wall:

$$F_{friction} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{contact} = \tau_{wall} \cdot P_m \cdot L_{die}$$

Where:
- $P_m$ = perimeter of profile (m)
- $L_{die}$ = effective die length (m)
- $\tau_{wall}$ = wall shear stress, typically 0.05 – 0.2 MPa

**Step 4: Viscous drag term**
For resin flow in a porous fiber bed (Darcy's law):

$$F_{viscous} = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot A_c}{k}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = resin viscosity (Pa·s)
- $v$ = line speed (m/s)
- $A_c$ = cross-section area
- $k$ = permeability of the fiber bed

**Step 5: Final compact pulling force expression**

$$\boxed{F_{pull} = \tau_{wall} \cdot P_m \cdot L_{die} + \frac{\mu v A_c}{k} + F_{back\ pressure}}$$

**Numerical Example:**
Given: $\tau_{wall} = 0.1$ MPa, $P_m = 0.2$ m, $L_{die} = 1.2$ m, $v = 1$ m/min $= 0.0167$ m/s, $A_c = 100 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01$ m², $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s, $k = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m².

$$F_{friction} = 0.1 \times 10^6 \times 0.2 \times 1.2 = 24{,}000 \text{ N} = 24 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{viscous} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.0167 \times 0.01}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 83{,}500 \text{ N} = 83.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{pull} \approx 24 + 83.5 + F_{back} \approx 107.5 \text{ kN} + F_{back}$$

This shows that viscous drag is the dominant resistance in pultrusion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Manufacturing Processes

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Spray Lay-Up | Filament Winding | Pultrusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mold Type | Open | Open | Closed (mandrel) | Closed (die) |
| Fiber Form | Mat / fabric / woven | Chopped roving | Continuous roving | Continuous roving/mat |
| Fiber Length | Continuous | Short (10–40 mm) | Continuous | Continuous |
| V<sub>f</sub> (max) | 0.30 – 0.40 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.50 – 0.65 | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| Production Rate | Low (0.5–2 kg/hr) | Medium | Medium | High (continuous) |
| Part Size | Large, complex | Large, simple | Axisymmetric (cylinders, spheres) | Constant cross-section |
| Labor Intensity | Very High | High | Low (automated) | Very Low |
| Capital Cost | Low | Low – Medium | High | High |
| Typical Resin | Polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy | Polyester, vinyl ester | Epoxy, polyester | Polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy |
| Void Content | 2 – 5% | 3 – 6% | < 1% (with proper tension) | < 1% |
| Examples | Boat hulls, bathtubs | Truck cabins, ducts | Pressure vessels, pipes | Rods, I-beams, channels |
| Recycling Suitability | Moderate | Moderate | Good (clean feedstock) | Excellent (scrap recoverable in-line) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hand Lay-Up Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mold Surface Preparation] --> B[Apply Mold Release Agent]
    B --> C[Apply Gel Coat Optional]
    C --> D[Place Reinforcement Fabric / Mat]
    D --> E[Pour Liquid Thermoset Resin]
    E --> F[Manual Roll-out with Hand Roller]
    F --> G{Air Voids < 2%?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Cure at Room Temperature or Oven]
    H --> I[Demold Composite Part]
    I --> J[Trim and Finish]
    J --> K[Final Quality Inspection]
```

### 4.2 Filament Winding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Creel_Station
        R1[Roving Spool 1] --> R2[Roving Spool 2]
        R2 --> R3[Roving Spool n]
    end
    Creel_Station --> T[Tension Control Unit]
    T --> RB[Resin Impregnation Bath]
    RB --> E[Excess Resin Squeegee]
    E --> DH[Fiber Delivery Head on Carriage]
    DH --> M[Rotating Mandrel]
    M --> C[Curing Oven or Ambient Cure]
    C --> ME[Mandrel Extraction Station]
    ME --> FW[Finished Wound Composite Part]
    M -.-> CP[Computer-Controlled Winding Pattern]
    CP -.-> DH
```

### 4.3 Pultrusion Process — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Roving Creel 1] --> G1[Fiber Guide]
    A2[Roving Creel 2] --> G1
    A3[Mat / Veil Feed] --> G1
    G1 --> RB[Resin Bath Wet-out Tank]
    RB --> PR[Pre-forming Die]
    PR --> HD[Heated Curing Die 120 to 200 C]
    HD --> CL[Cooling Section]
    CL --> PU[Caterpillar Pulling System]
    PU --> CS[Flying Cut-off Saw]
    CS --> FP[Pultruded Composite Profile]
    HD -.-> TC[Temperature Controllers]
    PU -.-> SC[Speed Controller]
    TC -.-> HD
    SC -.-> PU
```

### 4.4 Composite Recycling Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End-of-Life Composite Scrap] --> B{Recycling Route Selection}
    B -- Route 1 --> M1[Mechanical Shredding]
    M1 --> M2[Granulate Filler Material]
    M2 --> M3[Reuse in Concrete / New Composites]
    B -- Route 2 --> T1[Pyrolysis 400 to 700 C Inert]
    T1 --> T2[Recovered Clean Fibers]
    T2 --> T3[Reuse as Short Fiber Reinforcement]
    T1 --> T4[Recovered Pyrolysis Oil / Gas]
    T4 --> T5[Energy Recovery]
    B -- Route 3 --> C1[Solvolysis Chemical Depolymerization]
    C1 --> C2[Recovered Monomers / Oligomers]
    C2 --> C3[Repolymerize to New Resin]
    C1 --> C4[Recovered Fibers]
    C4 --> C3
```

### 4.5 Process Selection Decision Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Composite Part Design Requirements] --> Q1{Part Geometry?}
    Q1 -- Constant cross section --> P1[Pultrusion]
    Q1 -- Axisymmetric / tubular --> P2[Filament Winding]
    Q1 -- Large complex shape --> P3[Hand or Spray Lay-up]
    S --> Q2{Production Volume?}
    Q2 -- High continuous --> P1
    Q2 -- Medium batch --> P2
    Q2 -- Low / prototype --> P3
    S --> Q3{Mechanical Property Needs?}
    Q3 -- High stiffness unidirectional --> P1
    Q3 -- Multidirectional high strength --> P2
    Q3 -- Moderate --> P3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between hand lay-up and spray lay-up processes for polymer composites.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hand Lay-Up | Spray Lay-Up |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber Form | Continuous fabric / mat | Chopped roving (10–40 mm) |
| Application Method | Manual placement of fabric, then resin | Simultaneous spraying of fibers + resin |
| Fiber Volume Fraction | Higher (0.30–0.40) | Lower (0.20–0.30) |
| Resin System | Brushed / rolled | Atomized via spray gun |
| Mechanical Properties | Better (continuous fibers) | Inferior (short fibers) |
| Resin Emission | Lower | Higher (styrene emission) |
| Operator Skill | High | Moderate |

(3 marks for clear comparison across at least 4 distinct points)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**What is pultrusion? Why is it called a continuous process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pultrusion is a manufacturing process for producing constant cross-section fiber-reinforced polymer profiles by pulling continuous reinforcements through a resin bath and a heated curing die. (1 mark)

It is called a continuous process because:
1. The reinforcement is fed continuously from creels and pulled continuously through the die. (1 mark)
2. The cured profile emerges continuously and is cut to required lengths by a flying saw — production is not interrupted between parts. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Full Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the filament winding process for manufacturing polymer composite pressure vessels. Discuss the various winding patterns used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A filament-wound composite pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 300 mm, wall thickness 6 mm, and is subjected to an internal pressure of 4 MPa. Calculate the hoop stress and axial stress in the wall. Also determine the required winding angle for optimum design if the burst strength is to be 12 MPa hoop stress. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **Introduction (1 mark):** Filament winding is an automated closed-mold process in which continuous fiber rovings pre-impregnated with resin are wound under controlled tension onto a rotating mandrel.

- **Process Steps (3 marks):**
  1. Mandrel preparation and mounting on winding machine.
  2. Fiber rovings drawn from creels through resin bath (wet winding) or using prepreg (dry winding).
  3. Fibers wound over rotating mandrel via traversing carriage, controlled by CNC.
  4. Tension maintained (0.5–50 N per tow) to ensure compaction.
  5. Curing at room temperature or in oven.
  6. Mandrel removal by collapsible/extraction technique.

- **Winding Patterns (3 marks):**
  - **Hoop Winding (α ≈ 90°):** Fibers wound nearly perpendicular to axis; provides high circumferential strength for pressure vessels.
  - **Helical Winding (15° < α < 85°):** Combination of axial and circumferential strength; used for rocket motor cases, pipes.
  - **Polar Winding:** Used for spherical/closed-end vessels; winding angle varies from pole to equator.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Internal diameter $D_i = 300$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- Wall thickness $t = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m
- Internal pressure $P = 4$ MPa

**Step 1: Hoop Stress (Thin-Wall Approximation)**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} = \frac{4 \times 0.15}{0.006}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{0.6}{0.006} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Axial Stress (Closed-End Cylinder)**

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t} = \frac{4 \times 0.15}{2 \times 0.006} = \frac{0.6}{0.012}$$

$$\sigma_{axial} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Winding Angle for Optimum Hoop Strength (Burst 12 MPa)**

For a filament-wound cylinder to resist hoop stress, the fiber angle should satisfy:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot t_f}{t} \cdot \cos^2(\alpha)$$

For pure hoop reinforcement at burst condition:
$$\cos^2(\alpha) = \frac{\sigma_{hoop,burst}}{\sigma_f \cdot V_f}$$

Assuming $\sigma_f = 2500$ MPa (carbon fiber) and $V_f = 0.55$:

$$\cos^2(\alpha) = \frac{12}{2500 \times 0.55} = \frac{12}{1375} = 0.00873$$

$$\cos(\alpha) = 0.0934 \Rightarrow \alpha = 84.6°$$

**Conclusion:** A near-hoop winding at **α ≈ 84.6°** is required to provide the burst strength of 12 MPa in the hoop direction.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Full Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the pultrusion process for manufacturing fiber-reinforced polymer composites with a neat block diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a hand lay-up composite, the fiber volume fraction is 0.45. The density of glass fiber is 2560 kg/m³ and the density of epoxy resin is 1180 kg/m³. Calculate:
  (i) The theoretical density of the composite.
  (ii) The mass of fibers and matrix in 1 m² of laminate having thickness 4 mm.
  (iii) The void content if the actual measured density is 1780 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **Process Description (3 marks):** Pultrusion is a continuous process in which dry reinforcements (rovings, mats, fabrics) are pulled from creels, passed through a resin bath for impregnation, pre-formed to approximate die shape, and pulled through a heated die (120–200 °C) where curing, shaping, and compaction occur simultaneously. The cured profile is cooled and cut to length.

- **Block Diagram Explanation (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.3 mermaid diagram)
  1. Creel → 2. Resin bath → 3. Pre-former → 4. Heated die → 5. Puller → 6. Cut-off saw.

- **Advantages (1 mark):**
  - High fiber volume fraction (up to 70%).
  - Continuous production, low labor cost.
  - Excellent longitudinal strength.
  - Low void content.

- **Limitations (1 mark):**
  - Limited to constant cross-section profiles.
  - High capital investment in die and puller.
  - Difficult to incorporate transverse reinforcement.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.45$, $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1180$ kg/m³
- Laminate area $A = 1$ m², thickness $t = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m

**(i) Theoretical Density (2 marks)**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$\rho_c = 2560 \times 0.45 + 1180 \times 0.55$$

$$\rho_c = 1152 + 649 = 1801 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Mass of Fibers and Matrix (3 marks)**

Volume of laminate: $V_c = A \times t = 1 \times 0.004 = 0.004$ m³

Volume of fibers:
$$V_f^{vol} = 0.45 \times 0.004 = 0.0018 \text{ m}^3$$

Volume of matrix:
$$V_m^{vol} = 0.55 \times 0.004 = 0.0022 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass of fibers:
$$m_f = \rho_f \times V_f^{vol} = 2560 \times 0.0018 = 4.608 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of matrix:
$$m_m = \rho_m \times V_m^{vol} = 1180 \times 0.0022 = 2.596 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Void Content (2 marks)**

Theoretical density $\rho_{th} = 1801$ kg/m³, Actual density $\rho_{act} = 1780$ kg/m³

$$\text{Void fraction} = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{act}}{\rho_{th}}$$

$$V_v = \frac{1801 - 1780}{1801} = \frac{21}{1801} = 0.01166$$

$$\boxed{V_v \approx 1.17\%}$$

This is within the acceptable void content (< 2%) for structural composites.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pultrusion with Extrusion (–2 marks):** Students often describe pultrusion as "pushing material through a die." It is **pulled**, not pushed.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify α range for each winding pattern (–1 mark):** Always quote α ≈ 90° (hoop), 15°–85° (helical), and variable (polar).
> 3. **Skipping unit conversions in numericals (–1 mark):** Convert all dimensions to SI (m, Pa) before applying formulas. Mixing mm with MPa gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Omitting gel coat in lay-up diagrams (–1 mark):** Examiners expect a complete process flow.
> 5. **Recycling questions: Do not list "landfill" as a method (–1 mark):** Landfilling is a disposal route, not a recycling method.
> 6. **In pultrusion, never state "extruder" — the correct term is "puller" or "caterpillar gripper." (–1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lay-Up:** Open-mold, low-cost, low V<sub>f</sub> (max 0.40), used for large structures; high labor.
- **Spray Lay-Up:** Chopped fibers + atomized resin; fast but inferior mechanical properties.
- **Filament Winding:** Closed-mold, continuous fibers on rotating mandrel, V<sub>f</sub> up to 0.65, axisymmetric parts.
- **Pultrusion:** Continuous process for constant cross-section profiles, V<sub>f</sub> up to 0.70, very high production rate.
- **Winding Angles:** Hoop ≈ 90°, Helical = 15°–85°, Polar = variable.
- **Pultrusion Key Equation:** $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi D N}{v_c}$.
- **Composite Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (Rule of Mixtures).
- **Hoop Stress in Pressure Vessel:** $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P r}{t}$.
- **Axial Stress in Pressure Vessel:** $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{P r}{2t}$.
- **Recycling Routes:** Mechanical, Thermal (Pyrolysis/Fluidized Bed), Chemical (Solvolysis).
- **Pultrusion Die Temp:** **120 °C – 200 °C**.
- **Filament Winding Tension Range:** **0.5 – 50 N/tow**.
- **Acceptable Void Content in Composites:** **< 2%** for structural applications.
- **Highest V<sub>f</sub>:** Pultrusion (0.55–0.70) > Filament Winding (0.50–0.65) > Hand Lay-up (0.30–0.40) > Spray Lay-up (0.20–0.30).
- **Recycling Recovery Hierarchy:** Solvolysis > Pyrolysis > Fluidized Bed > Mechanical (in terms of fiber quality retention).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
