# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 pattern]`
**"Distinguish between stir casting and squeeze casting for MMCs. State two advantages and one limitation of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stir Casting | Squeeze Casting |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| Mechanism | Vortex mixing of particles into melt | Pressurized infiltration of preform |
| Pressure | Atmospheric (~0.1 MPa) | High (50–150 MPa) |
| V_f achievable | ≤ 30 % | Up to 45–50 % |
| Porosity | High (5–10 %) | Very low (<1 %) |
| Reinforcement | Particulates | Particles or short fibers |
| Cost | Lowest | Higher (special die) |

**Stir casting advantages:** (i) low cost; (ii) can use existing foundry equipment. **Limitation:** porosity & gas entrapment limit mechanical properties.

**Squeeze casting advantages:** (i) near-net-shape, high V_f; (ii) low porosity → superior strength. **Limitation:** higher tooling cost and limited to smaller parts.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of the diffusion bonding process for continuous-fiber MMCs."**

**Model Answer:**
Diffusion bonding is a solid-state joining process used to fabricate continuous-fiber MMCs. Clean matrix alloy foils and reinforcement fiber mats are stacked alternately (foil–fiber–foil lay-up), enclosed in a vacuum can, heated to **0.5–0.9 T_m** of the matrix, and pressed at **1–50 MPa** for a controlled time. Atomic diffusion at the cleaned interfaces produces a metallurgical bond without macroscopic deformation.

**Key parameters:** (i) bonding temperature, (ii) bonding pressure, (iii) bonding time, (iv) surface cleanliness, (v) vacuum/inert atmosphere. **Bond width** can be estimated using $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice (Q.A or Q.B) is given.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q.A(a)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Explain the squeeze casting process for MMCs with a suitable schematic. List the major process parameters and their effect on the properties of the MMC." (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Squeeze casting is a hybrid liquid-state process in which molten metal is poured into a preheated die containing a porous preform of reinforcement and forced into the preform under high pressure (50–150 MPa), with simultaneous feeding of solidification shrinkage.

**2. Schematic (1 mark):** Die (with preform) → molten metal poured → punch descends → pressure applied → metal infiltrates preform → solidification under pressure → punch retracts → part ejected.

**3. Process Steps (2 marks):**
- (i) Preform preparation (ceramic fibers/particles bound with a binder and sintered).
- (ii) Preform and die preheat to ~ 300–500 °C.
- (iii) Pour superheated melt (50–100 °C above liquidus).
- (iv) Apply pressure via punch and hold during solidification.
- (v) Eject near-net-shape part.

**4. Process parameters and their effects (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on MMC |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Applied pressure | 50–150 MPa | Higher P → less porosity, better infiltration, finer grains |
| Die temperature | 300–500 °C | Controls cooling rate; too low → thermal shock |
| Melt superheat | 50–100 °C | Higher → lower viscosity, better flow |
| Preform temp | 300–500 °C | Prevents premature solidification ("chill") |
| Infiltration time | 1–60 s | Must be < solidification time |

**5. Advantages (1 mark):** Low porosity; high V_f; refined microstructure; near-net shape.

**Q.A(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Discuss the in-situ technique of MMC fabrication. Explain the Lanxide (DIMOX) and XD processes with suitable chemical reactions." (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept of in-situ processing (1 mark):**
In-situ processing forms the reinforcement **within the matrix** through a chemical reaction during fabrication, rather than adding it externally. Advantages: (i) thermodynamically stable interface, (ii) clean, contaminant-free reinforcement–matrix interface, (iii) uniform sub-micron dispersion, (iv) lower cost.

**2. DIMOX / Lanxide Process (3 marks):**
- Molten Al (or Mg) is held in a furnace in an oxidizing atmosphere (air or O₂) at 900–1400 °C.
- The surface oxidizes to form a coherent Al₂O₃ layer:
  $$4\text{Al}_{(l)} + 3\text{O}_{2\,(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_{3\,(s)}$$
- The molten metal wicks through microchannels in the oxide by capillarity and continues to oxidize, so the ceramic layer grows outward.
- Reinforcement (SiC, Al₂O₃, TiB₂) placed in the molten metal becomes incorporated.
- Net result: a near-net-shape Al₂O₃-matrix composite grown from a parent metal bath.

**3. XD Process (2 marks):**
- A blend of matrix powder (e.g., Al) + elemental reactant powders (e.g., Ti + B or Ti + C) is heated above the matrix melting point.
- An **exothermic solid-state reaction** disperses fine ceramic whiskers/particles throughout the melt.
- Example reactions:
  $$\text{Ti} + 2\text{B} \rightarrow \text{TiB}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{Ti} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{TiC}$$
  $$\text{Al} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{AlN}$$
- The dispersion is thermodynamically stable, and reinforcement sizes are typically 0.1–5 µm.

**4. Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | DIMOX | XD |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Reaction type | Gas–liquid oxidation | Solid-state exothermic |
| Reinforcement formed | Al₂O₃, MgO | TiB₂, TiC, AlN |
| Typical V_f | Up to 80 % | 5–25 % |
| Particulate from reactant | — | Yes |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**Q.B(a)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**"Describe the liquid-infiltration method of MMC fabrication. Derive the Washburn equation and discuss the role of contact angle." (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Description (2 marks):**
Liquid infiltration is the process of forcing a molten metal into a porous ceramic preform. Variants are:
- **Pressureless infiltration** — capillary-driven; requires wetting.
- **Vacuum infiltration** — preform evacuated; melt drawn in by atmospheric pressure.
- **Pressure infiltration** — external gas/mechanical pressure; eliminates wettability constraint.

**2. Derivation of Washburn equation (3 marks):**
The driving pressure equals the capillary pressure:
$$P_c = \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$$
For laminar (Hagen–Poiseuille) flow in a cylindrical channel:
$$v = \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r_p^{\,2}}{8\eta}\frac{P_c}{L} = \frac{r_p\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta\,L}$$
Separating and integrating:
$$L\,dL = \frac{r_p\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta}\,dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{L^{2} = \frac{r_p\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}\,t}$$

**3. Role of contact angle (2 marks):**
- $\theta < 90°$ (wetting) ⇒ $\cos\theta > 0$ ⇒ spontaneous infiltration possible.
- $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting, e.g., Al/SiC with $\theta \approx 130°$) ⇒ $\cos\theta < 0$ ⇒ infiltration resisted; external pressure required.
- Surface modification of the reinforcement (e.g., coating SiC with a thin Ni or K₂ZrF₆ layer) reduces θ and enables pressureless infiltration.

**Q.B(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**"Explain the solid-state powder-metallurgy route for MMC fabrication. List its advantages and limitations compared to liquid-state routes." (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. P/M Process Steps (3 marks):**
- (i) **Powder blending** — matrix powder (e.g., Al, 10–100 µm) and reinforcement powder (SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C) are weighed, mixed in a V-blender or attritor.
- (ii) **Cold compaction** — the blend is cold-pressed (uniaxial or CIP at 200–400 MPa) into a green body.
- (iii) **Sintering / Hot pressing** — vacuum hot pressing (VHP) at 0.7–0.9 T_m and 10–50 MPa, or HIP (100–200 MPa inert gas) to densify.
- (iv) **Secondary forming** — extrusion, forging, or rolling for property enhancement.
- (v) **Machining** to final shape.

**2. Advantages over liquid-state routes (2 marks):**
- No melting → no chemical reaction between reinforcement and matrix → clean interface.
- Uniform reinforcement distribution, narrow particle-size tolerance.
- High V_f possible (up to 60–70 %).
- Lower processing temperatures → reduced thermal damage to fibers.

**3. Limitations (2 marks):**
- High cost of powders and HIP equipment.
- Limited to small-to-medium part sizes.
- Residual porosity (~1–3 %) degrades fatigue life.
- Health hazards from fine powders (Al, B₄C).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in MMC Processing Questions (Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "wetting" and "spreading":** A common error is to claim that $\theta < 90°$ always leads to complete infiltration. In reality, the **driving pressure** must also overcome **gravity** and **back-pressure from trapped gas** in the preform. The condition is $P_c \geq \rho g h + P_{\text{back}}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state the convention for contact angle:** When quoting Young's equation, students often forget to specify that θ is measured **through the liquid**. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the convention is not stated.
>
> 3. **Mixing up DIMOX and XD:** The DIMOX/Lanxide process is a **liquid–gas** oxidation process growing an oxide matrix; XD is a **solid-state** exothermic dispersion process forming ceramic particulates. Confusing the two is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in Part B.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in Washburn equation problems:** Always express length in **m**, time in **s**, viscosity in **Pa·s**, and surface tension in **N/m** before substituting. Examiners will not award full credit if units are inconsistent or missing.
>
> 5. **Diffusion bonding — missing the "T = 0.5–0.9 T_m" range:** Many students write "high temperature" without specifying the **homologous temperature window**. Examiners expect a quantitative range.
>
> 6. **Stir-casting V_f limits:** Do not claim stir casting can produce V_f > 30 %. Examiners will mark this as physically incorrect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — MMC Processing:**

- **MMC** = metal matrix (Al, Ti, Mg, Cu, Ni) + ceramic reinforcement (Al₂O₃, SiC, B₄C, TiC, TiB₂, C-fibers).
- **Three major process families:** Liquid-state, Solid-state, In-situ.
- **Stir casting:** Vortex method; particulate MMC; V_f ≤ 30 %; cheapest route; porosity is a problem.
- **Squeeze casting:** Pressure 50–150 MPa; near-net shape; V_f up to 45–50 %; very low porosity.
- **Compocasting:** Semi-solid (mushy) matrix; suppresses particle settling; thixocasting.
- **Pressureless infiltration:** Spontaneous capillary flow; requires $\theta < 90°$ (wetting).
- **Pressure / Vacuum infiltration:** External gas or vacuum aids infiltration; $\theta$ constraint relaxed.
- **Capillary pressure (cylindrical pore):** $P_c = -2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / r_p$.
- **Washburn:** $L^{2} = (r_p \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / 2\eta)\,t$ ⇒ $L \propto \sqrt{t}$.
- **Powder metallurgy:** Cold press → vacuum hot press / HIP → secondary forming; no melting; uniform; expensive.
- **Diffusion bonding:** Solid-state; 0.5–0.9 T_m, 1–50 MPa, time t; bond depth $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$.
- **Foil–Fiber–Foil (FFF) lay-up:** Standard for continuous-fiber MMCs (e.g., Ti/SiC).
- **DIMOX / Lanxide:** In-situ oxidation of molten metal (Al → Al₂O₃) with melt wicking; up to 80 % V_f.
- **XD process:** In-situ exothermic dispersion; e.g., Ti + 2B → TiB₂, Ti + C → TiC.
- **SHS:** Self-propagating high-temperature synthesis; very fast; porous product; needs post-densification.
- **Critical fiber radius for spontaneous infiltration:** $r_f^{\text{crit}} = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / (\rho_m g h)$.
- **Kozeny–Carman permeability:** $K = (1-V_f)^3 d_p^2 / (180 V_f^2)$ — used to design pressure-infiltration cycles.
- **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.
- **Contact angle convention:** θ measured **through the liquid**; $\theta < 90°$ = wetting.
- **Rule of mixtures (axial):** $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ — used in continuous-fiber MMC design.
- **Key engineering applications:** Brake rotors (Al–SiC), jet engine rings (Ti–SiC), connecting rods (Al–Al₂O₃), wear parts, heat sinks, missile structures.

**Memory Mnemonic:** **"L-S-I-D"** = **L**iquid / **S**olid / **I**n-situ / **D**eposition — the four MMC processing families.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibre Reinforced Thermoplastics (FRTP) Preparation: Brief Description of Coating Process, Melt Compounding Process and Dry Blending Process, Injection Moulding, Rotational Moulding and Cold Forming of Reinforced Thermoplastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fibre Reinforced Thermoplastics (FRTP) Preparation: Brief Description of Coating Process, Melt Compounding Process and Dry Blending Process, Injection Moulding, Rotational Moulding and Cold Forming of Reinforced Thermoplastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fibre Reinforced Thermoplastics (FRTP) — Formal Definition

**Fibre Reinforced Thermoplastics (FRTP)** are advanced composite materials produced by embedding high-performance reinforcing fibres (such as glass, carbon, or aramid fibres) into a thermoplastic polymer matrix (such as polypropylene, polyamide, polycarbonate, or PEEK). Unlike thermoset-based composites, the thermoplastic matrix in FRTP can be repeatedly softened upon heating and re-solidified upon cooling, which makes FRTP systems **recyclable, reprocessable, and suitable for high-volume manufacturing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECHT742 — Module 2):**
> The preparation of FRTP is a two-stage consideration:
> 1. **Pre-compounding / Preparation Stage** — preparing the fibre-matrix blend (Coating, Melt Compounding, Dry Blending).
> 2. **Conversion / Forming Stage** — shaping the compound into a final product (Injection Moulding, Rotational Moulding, Cold Forming).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Reinforced Concrete Sandwich, but Reusable"

Think of an FRTP as a **rebar-reinforced concrete slab that you can melt down and recast anytime**:

- The **thermoplastic matrix** is like the soft, meltable dough or butter in a cake — when heated, it flows; when cooled, it solidifies.
- The **reinforcing fibres** are like the steel rods embedded in the concrete — they carry the load and give strength.
- Unlike a thermoset composite (epoxy), where the matrix undergoes an irreversible chemical reaction (curing), the thermoplastic matrix in FRTP undergoes only a **physical phase change** (melting and solidifying). This is why you can shred an FRTP part, remelt it, and mould a new one — a property known as **thermoplastic recyclability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is preparation important?**
> The interface between the fibre surface and the polymer melt is the **single most critical factor** governing composite performance. If the fibres are not properly coated, compounded, or distributed, the load transfer from matrix to fibre fails and the composite behaves no better than the neat polymer.

## 1.3 Classification of FRTP Preparation Methods

| Category | Methods | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Preparation** | Coating Process | Chemical/physical treatment of fibres |
| **Compounding (with heat)** | Melt Compounding | Shear mixing of polymer melt + fibres |
| **Compounding (without heat)** | Dry Blending | Dry mixing of polymer + fibres at room temperature |
| **Primary Forming** | Injection Moulding | High-pressure injection of melt into mould |
| **Primary Forming** | Rotational Moulding | Centrifugal casting of powder + fibres in rotating mould |
| **Primary Forming** | Cold Forming | Solid-state stamping/pressing of reinforced sheets |

## 1.4 Typical Constituent Materials

| Reinforcement | Common Thermoplastic Matrices | Typical Fibre Length |
|---|---|---|
| **E-Glass** | PP, PA6, PA66, PBT | 0.1 – 25 mm |
| **Carbon Fibre** | PEEK, PPS, PA | 0.1 – 25 mm |
| **Aramid (Kevlar)** | PP, PA | 3 – 25 mm |
| **Natural Fibre (Jute/Flax)** | PP, PE | 1 – 10 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metric — Aspect Ratio (l/d):**
> The efficiency of fibre reinforcement depends on the **critical fibre length** $l_c$, given by:
> 
> $$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$
> 
> where $\sigma_f$ is the fibre tensile strength, $d$ is the fibre diameter, and $\tau_i$ is the interfacial shear strength. The actual fibre length $l$ must be **greater than $l_c$** for effective load transfer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Preparation Processes

### 2.1.1 Coating Process (Fibre Sizing / Surface Treatment)

**Concept:** The coating process (also called **sizing**) involves applying a thin polymeric or organo-functional film onto the fibre surface prior to compounding. This is the **first critical step** in FRTP preparation.

**Why is it done?**
- To protect fibres from **mechanical abrasion** during handling and processing.
- To improve **wettability** of the fibre by the polymer melt.
- To provide **chemical coupling** between fibre and matrix (e.g., via silane coupling agents).
- To reduce fibre-fibre **friction and agglomeration**.

**Types of Coating:**

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Starch / Oil-based Sizing** | Protective film for handling | E-glass roving |
| **Silane Coupling Agent** | Chemical bridge between fibre and matrix | Glass fibre + PP, PA |
| **Polymer Emulsion Coating** | Thermoplastic layer (e.g., polyurethane) | Carbon/Aramid fibres |
| **Plasma / Corona Treatment** | Surface activation | Carbon fibre, natural fibres |

**Typical procedure for glass fibre sizing:**
1. Glass fibres are drawn from the bushing at high speed.
2. They pass over a **sizing applicator roller** partially immersed in a coupling-agent solution.
3. The wet film is dried in an oven to remove solvent.
4. Coated fibres are wound onto a package for further use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silane Coupling Agent — Mechanism:**
> Silane has the structure $\text{R–Si(OR')}_3$ where R is an organo-functional group (compatible with polymer) and OR' is an alkoxy group (reacts with –OH groups on glass surface). This forms a chemical bridge: **Glass–O–Si–R ↔ Polymer matrix.**

---

### 2.1.2 Melt Compounding Process

**Concept:** This is the **most widely used industrial process** for preparing FRTP. The polymer is melted and the fibres are dispersed into the melt under high shear using an extruder (single-screw or twin-screw).

**Process Sequence:**
1. **Feeding** — Polymer pellets/granules and chopped fibres are metered into the extruder hopper (often via a side feeder for fibres to minimize breakage).
2. **Melting** — Polymer melts due to external heating and shear.
3. **Mixing / Dispersion** — Rotating screw elements (kneading, mixing blocks) distribute fibres within the melt and break up fibre bundles.
4. **Devolatilization** — Removal of moisture/volatiles via vacuum port.
5. **Extrusion** — Compound is extruded through a die as strands.
6. **Pelletizing** — Strands are cooled in a water bath and cut into pellets.

**Equipment Variants:**

| Type | Shear Level | Fibre Length Retention | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Screw Extruder** | Moderate | Better (less breakage) | Lower |
| **Co-rotating Twin-Screw** | High | Lower (more breakage) | Higher |
| **Counter-rotating Twin-Screw** | Very High | Poor | High |
| **Buss Ko-kneader** | Moderate | Good | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common pitfall is stating that twin-screw extruders always preserve fibre length. In reality, **high-shear kneading elements break fibres**, reducing the aspect ratio (l/d) and hence the composite's mechanical performance.

**Critical Process Parameters:**
- **Screw Speed (N):** Higher speed → more shear → more fibre breakage.
- **Temperature Profile:** Must exceed melting point $T_m$ but stay below polymer degradation.
- **Residence Time:** Affects thermal degradation and fibre attrition.
- **Fibre Feed Position:** Adding fibres downstream (after polymer melt) minimizes breakage.

---

### 2.1.3 Dry Blending Process

**Concept:** Dry blending is a **low-temperature, low-shear** process where polymer powder, pellets, or chips are physically mixed with short fibres in the solid state, without melting the polymer. The resulting dry blend is then fed to a downstream moulding process.

**Equipment Used:**
- **Tumble mixers** (double-cone, V-blender)
- **Ribbon blenders**
- **High-intensity paddle mixers**
- **Fluidized-bed mixers**

**Advantages:**
- Simple, low-cost, low energy.
- Avoids fibre breakage (since no shear).
- Suitable for heat-sensitive matrices (e.g., PVC).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Non-uniform fibre dispersion** is a major issue.
- Fibres tend to **agglomerate** due to electrostatic and entanglement effects.
- Air entrapment and dust generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Application:**
> Dry blending is widely used for **rotational moulding compounds**, where powders and short fibres are pre-mixed before being charged into the mould.

---

### 2.1.4 Injection Moulding of FRTP

**Concept:** Injection moulding is the **most important high-volume production process** for FRTP. A pre-compounded FRTP pellet is melted in a heated barrel, injected under high pressure into a closed mould, cooled, and ejected as a finished part.

**Process Cycle (Stages):**

1. **Feeding** — FRTP pellets fed via hopper.
2. **Plasticizing** — Screw rotates, melts pellets, pushes melt to the front.
3. **Injection (Filling)** — Screw acts as plunger, injects melt into mould cavity.
4. **Packing/Holding** — Additional melt is packed to compensate for shrinkage.
5. **Cooling** — Part solidifies in mould.
6. **Ejection** — Part is ejected; cycle repeats.

**Fibre Behaviour in Injection Moulding:**
- Fibres **orient** along the flow direction (fountain flow effect).
- This creates a **skin-core-skin morphology**:
  - **Skin layer:** Highly aligned fibres (high stiffness in flow direction).
  - **Core layer:** Randomly oriented fibres (more isotropic).
- At weld lines, fibres are **transverse** to flow → weak zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fibre Length Degradation Equation:**
> The final fibre length $L_f$ in an injection-moulded FRTP can be estimated as:
> 
> $$L_f = L_0 \cdot \exp(-K \cdot \gamma)$$
> 
> where $L_0$ is the initial fibre length, $\gamma$ is the cumulative shear strain, and $K$ is a degradation constant. Typical values give $L_f$ in the range of **0.2 – 0.5 mm** for short-fibre FRTP.

**Key Moulding Defects in FRTP:**

| Defect | Cause |
|---|---|
| Weld lines | Fibre transverse orientation at merge points |
| Fibre-matrix separation | Poor adhesion, inadequate packing pressure |
| Sink marks | Excessive fibre content increases viscosity |
| Warpage | Differential shrinkage due to fibre orientation |

---

### 2.1.5 Rotational Moulding of FRTP

**Concept:** Rotational moulding (rotomoulding) is a **low-pressure, low-shear** process used to produce **large, hollow, seamless parts** (e.g., tanks, containers, automotive ducting). The polymer and short fibres, in powder form, are charged into a closed mould which is then rotated biaxially (about two perpendicular axes) while being heated.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Charging** — Pre-measured powder polymer + short fibres are placed in the cold mould.
2. **Heating & Rotation** — Mould rotates (e.g., 1:4 speed ratio) inside an oven; powder tumbles, melts, and coats the inner mould wall.
3. **Cooling** — Mould is moved to a cooling chamber (still rotating); melt solidifies.
4. **Demoulding** — Mould is opened and the hollow part is removed.

**Why FRTP is Compatible with Rotomoulding:**
- **Low shear** preserves fibre length (better than injection moulding).
- Fibres must be **short** (typically < 1 mm) to flow freely with the powder.
- Common system: **LLDPE powder + short glass fibre (10–25 wt%)**.

**Limitations:**
- Long cycle time (15–40 min).
- Limited to relatively thin walls (3–15 mm).
- Fibre settling and non-uniform distribution are common issues.

---

### 2.1.6 Cold Forming of Reinforced Thermoplastics

**Concept:** Cold forming (also called **stamping, solid-phase forming, or cold pressing**) involves shaping a reinforced thermoplastic sheet at a temperature **below the melting point** of the polymer but **above its glass transition temperature (for amorphous polymers)** or just **below $T_m$** (for semi-crystalline polymers).

**Starting Materials:**
- **Glass Mat Thermoplastics (GMT)** — Continuous glass mat impregnated with thermoplastic (e.g., PP).
- **GMTex** — Directionally reinforced GMT using textile glass.
- **Organo-sheet / Thermoplastic Composite Laminates** — Woven fabric + thermoplastic film stacks.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-heating** — Sheet is heated (typically in an infrared oven) to **150 – 200 °C** for PP-based systems (below $T_m \approx 165$ °C but in the workable softening range).
2. **Transfer to Press** — Sheet is rapidly transferred to a matched-metal mould.
3. **Stamping** — Press closes in 1–5 seconds; high pressure (~1–10 MPa) forces the sheet into shape.
4. **Cooling under Pressure** — Part solidifies in 10–30 seconds.
5. **Ejection** — Trimmed and ejected.

**Advantages:**
- **Short cycle times** (10–60 seconds vs. minutes for injection moulding of long-fibre parts).
- **Retains long fibre length** (no high-shear melt processing).
- **Excellent mechanical performance** — closer to continuous-fibre composites.
- **High fibre volume fractions** (up to 40–45%).

**Applications:**
- Automotive front-end carriers, bumper beams, battery trays.
- Structural underbody shields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison — Cold Forming vs. Injection Moulding:**
> 
> | Parameter | Cold Forming | Injection Moulding |
> |---|---|---|
> | Fibre length retained | High (mm–cm range) | Low (< 1 mm) |
> | Cycle time | 10–60 s | 30–120 s |
> | Part complexity | Moderate | High |
> | Production volume | Medium–High | Very High |
> | Fibre volume fraction | Up to 45% | 15–30% |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fibre length for effective reinforcement | mm |
| 2 | $l/d$ (aspect ratio) | Must be $> l_c/d$ for efficient load transfer | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_f$ (fibre volume fraction) | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_f = L_0 \cdot \exp(-K\gamma)$ | Fibre length degradation in melt processing | mm |
| 5 | Rule of mixtures (tensile): $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Composite modulus estimation | GPa |
| 6 | $T_{process} > T_m$ (melt) or $> T_g$ (amorphous forming) | Processing temperature criteria | °C |
| 7 | $\eta_{FRTP} > \eta_{neat\ polymer}$ | Viscosity increases with fibre content | Pa·s |
| 8 | $P_{packing} \propto \dfrac{1}{V_f}$ | Higher $V_f$ requires higher injection pressure | MPa |
| 9 | Skin–core morphology thickness ratio | Depends on mould geometry and flow rate | µm–mm |
| 10 | Rotational speed ratio (minor:major axis) | Typically 1:4 for rotomoulding | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in the table above are **standard engineering units** as used in the KTU board examination. No proprietary or non-SI units are introduced.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix and Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Critical Fibre Length Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An FRTP composite is made from E-glass fibres in a polypropylene (PP) matrix. The fibre diameter is $d = 10\ \mu m$, the fibre tensile strength is $\sigma_f = 1800\ MPa$, and the interfacial shear strength (after silane coating) is $\tau_i = 12\ MPa$. Determine the critical fibre length and the minimum aspect ratio required.

**Step 1: Recall the critical fibre length formula.**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values into the formula. Note unit consistency.**

The diameter $d = 10\ \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ m$. The units of $\sigma_f$ and $\tau_i$ are both in MPa, so the ratio $\sigma_f / \tau_i$ is dimensionless.

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{1800\ \text{MPa} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}}{2 \times 12\ \text{MPa}} \\
&= \frac{1.8 \times 10^{3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{24} \\
&= \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{24} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \\
&= 0.75\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the critical aspect ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(l/d)_c &= \frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}}{10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}} \\
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

For effective reinforcement, the fibre length must be at least $l_c = 0.75\ mm$ and the aspect ratio must be at least **75**. This is why **chopped fibres of 3–6 mm length** (aspect ratio 300–600) are commonly used — they comfortably exceed the critical value.

> [!NOTE]
> If the fibres are too short ($l < l_c$), the matrix pulls out of the fibre and the composite fails prematurely.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fibre Volume Fraction Conversion

**Problem Statement:**
A glass-fibre/PP compound is prepared with a fibre weight fraction $W_f = 0.30$. Given $\rho_f = 2.55\ g/cm^3$ and $\rho_m = 0.91\ g/cm^3$, calculate the fibre volume fraction $V_f$.

**Step 1: Recall the conversion formula.**

$$
V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}
$$

where $W_m = 1 - W_f = 0.70$.

**Step 2: Compute $W_f/\rho_f$ and $W_m/\rho_m$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{W_f}{\rho_f} &= \frac{0.30}{2.55} = 0.1176\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g} \\
\frac{W_m}{\rho_m} &= \frac{0.70}{0.91} = 0.7692\ \text{cm}^3/\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the terms and compute $V_f$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \frac{0.1176}{0.1176 + 0.7692} \\
&= \frac{0.1176}{0.8868} \\
&= 0.1327 \approx 0.133
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A weight fraction of 30% glass in PP corresponds to only **~13.3% volume fraction**, because glass is much denser than PP. This is why high-performance FRTP grades specify compositions in **volume %**, not weight %.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Fibre Length Degradation in Extrusion

**Problem Statement:**
Initial fibre length $L_0 = 6\ mm$. The cumulative shear strain experienced in a co-rotating twin-screw extruder is $\gamma = 5.0$, and the degradation constant for glass-PP is $K = 0.45$. Calculate the final fibre length $L_f$.

**Step 1: Apply the exponential degradation model.**

$$L_f = L_0 \cdot \exp(-K\gamma)$$

**Step 2: Compute the exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K\gamma &= 0.45 \times 5.0 = 2.25 \\
\exp(-2.25) &= e^{-2.25} \approx 0.1054
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the final fibre length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_f &= 6\ \text{mm} \times 0.1054 \\
&\approx 0.63\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

Despite starting with 6 mm fibres, the final fibre length in the pellet is only **~0.63 mm** — a **90% reduction**. This is a major reason why injection-moulded FRTP has lower mechanical performance than compression-moulded GMT.

> [!WARNING]
> This is one of the most important practical insights in FRTP processing — students often overlook that **melt processing drastically reduces fibre length** and therefore effective reinforcement.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Modulus

**Problem Statement:**
For a glass-fibre/PP composite with $V_f = 0.20$, $E_f = 72\ GPa$ (E-glass), and $E_m = 1.5\ GPa$ (PP), estimate the composite longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Step 1: Apply the rule of mixtures.**

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Step 2: Compute $V_m$.**

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_c &= (72\ \text{GPa})(0.20) + (1.5\ \text{GPa})(0.80) \\
&= 14.4 + 1.2 \\
&= 15.6\ \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The composite is **~10× stiffer** than the neat PP matrix, even with only 20% fibre volume fraction — clearly demonstrating the reinforcing effect of short fibres.

---

## 3.5 Summary Table — When to Use Each Process

| Process | Best Suited For | Fibre Length Scale | Production Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coating | Surface preparation of fibres | Any | Industrial (pre-step) |
| Melt Compounding | Pellet/granule production | Short (0.2–1 mm after) | High |
| Dry Blending | Pre-mix for rotomoulding | Short (1–3 mm) | Medium |
| Injection Moulding | Complex, high-volume parts | Short (< 1 mm) | Very High |
| Rotational Moulding | Large hollow parts | Short (< 1 mm) | Medium |
| Cold Forming | Structural parts, long-fibre retention | Long (mm–cm) | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — FRTP Preparation and Forming Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fibre Reinforcement<br>E-glass, Carbon, Aramid] --> B[Coating Process<br>Sizing with Silane Coupling Agent]
    A2[Thermoplastic Resin<br>PP, PA, PEEK, PPS] --> C
    B --> C{Compounding Method}
    C --> D1[Melt Compounding<br>Twin-Screw Extruder]
    C --> D2[Dry Blending<br>Tumble or Ribbon Mixer]
    D1 --> E1[FRTP Pellets<br>with Short Fibres]
    D2 --> E2[Dry Blend<br>Powder + Short Fibres]
    E1 --> F1[Injection Moulding<br>High Pressure Melt Injection]
    E2 --> F2[Rotational Moulding<br>Biaxial Rotation in Oven]
    E1 --> F3[Cold Forming<br>Stamp GMT Sheets Below T sub m]
    F1 --> G1[Final FRTP Part<br>Complex Geometry]
    F2 --> G2[Final FRTP Part<br>Hollow Large Parts]
    F3 --> G3[Final FRTP Part<br>Structural Long Fibre Parts]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Melt Compounding Process Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Polymer Pellets<br>Hopper 1] --> M
    F1[Chopped Fibres<br>Side Feeder] --> M
    M --> Ex[Extruder Barrel<br>Heated Zones]
    Ex --> Kn[Kneading and<br>Mixing Elements]
    Kn --> DV[Devolatilization<br>Vacuum Port]
    DV --> Di[Extrusion Die]
    Di --> WB[Water Bath<br>Cooling]
    WB --> Pe[Pelletizer<br>Cutter]
    Pe --> FRTP[FRTP Compound<br>Pellets]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Injection Moulding Cycle Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Feeding<br>Hopper to Barrel] --> S2[Stage 2: Plasticizing<br>Screw Rotates Melts Pellets]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Injection<br>High Pressure Fill]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Packing<br>Compensate Shrinkage]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Cooling<br>Solidify in Mould]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Ejection<br>Open Mould and Eject]
    S6 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cold Forming of GMT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GMT[GMT or Organosheet<br>Stack of Mat plus Film] --> IR[Infrared Oven<br>Heat to 150 to 200 deg C]
    IR --> TR[Transfer Robot<br>Rapid Handling]
    TR --> PR[Hydraulic Press<br>1 to 10 MPa]
    PR --> CL[Cooling Under<br>Pressure in Mould]
    CL --> EJ[Eject Solid<br>FRTP Part]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Schematic — Fibre Length Scale vs. Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Process and Resulting Fibre Length"
    P1[Continuous Fibre<br>10 to 100 mm] --> P2
    P2[Cold Forming GMT<br>5 to 25 mm retained]
    P2 --> P3
    P3[Melt Compounding<br>0.5 to 2 mm]
    P3 --> P4
    P4[Injection Moulding<br>0.2 to 0.6 mm final]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams above map the sequential flow of material and process conditions. Each block represents a unit operation with its own critical parameters (temperature, pressure, shear). In KTU board answers, students are expected to **labelled-block diagrams with process conditions annotated** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> 
> **Q1. What is meant by the "coating process" in FRTP preparation? Mention the role of a silane coupling agent.**
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> The **coating process** (also called **sizing**) is the application of a thin polymeric or organo-functional film onto the surface of reinforcing fibres before they are compounded with the thermoplastic matrix. **(1 mark)**
> 
> The primary purposes are:
> 1. To **protect the fibre surface** from mechanical damage during handling and processing. **(0.5 marks)**
> 2. To improve **wettability** of the fibre by the molten polymer. **(0.5 marks)**
> 3. To provide **chemical coupling** between the inorganic fibre and the organic polymer matrix. **(0.5 marks)**
> 
> A **silane coupling agent** (general structure: $\text{R–Si(OR')}_3$) acts as a chemical bridge. The alkoxy (OR') group reacts with –OH groups on the surface of glass fibre to form a **covalent Si–O–Si bond**, while the organo-functional group R is compatible with the polymer matrix. This dramatically improves the **interfacial shear strength** $\tau_i$ and hence the composite's mechanical performance. **(0.5 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question 2]**
> 
> **Q2. Differentiate between melt compounding and dry blending as FRTP preparation methods.**
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> | Aspect | Melt Compounding | Dry Blending |
> |---|---|---|
> | **State of polymer** | Molten | Solid (powder/pellets) |
> | **Temperature** | Above melting point | Room temperature |
> | **Equipment** | Single/twin-screw extruder | Tumble or ribbon blender |
> | **Fibre breakage** | High (due to shear) | Negligible |
> | **Dispersion quality** | Excellent | Moderate (agglomeration possible) |
> | **Energy consumption** | High | Low |
> | **Typical output** | Pellets | Dry blend powder |
> 
> **(3 marks for a clear, tabular comparison covering at least four distinct points.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> 
> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **melt compounding process** for the preparation of fibre reinforced thermoplastics with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of screw configuration and process parameters on fibre length retention.
> 
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **injection moulding process** for FRTP with emphasis on the **skin-core morphology** developed during filling. How does fibre orientation affect the mechanical anisotropy of moulded parts?

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> 
> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **rotational moulding process** for FRTP composites. List the key process steps, advantages, limitations, and typical applications.
> 
> **(b) [7 marks]** What is **cold forming** of reinforced thermoplastics? Compare it with injection moulding in terms of fibre length retention, cycle time, achievable fibre volume fraction, and part complexity.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions — Question A

### Part (a) Solution — Melt Compounding Process

**1. Process Description (3 marks):**
Melt compounding is a high-shear, high-temperature process in which chopped fibres and thermoplastic polymer are simultaneously fed into an extruder, melted, mixed, and extruded into strands that are cooled and cut into pellets. The process is continuous and is the most widely used industrial method for preparing short-fibre FRTP compounds.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
Hopper (Polymer) ───┐
                   ├──→ [Extruder] ──→ [Kneading/Mixing] ──→ [Devolatilization] ──→ [Die] ──→ [Water Bath] ──→ [Pelletizer] ──→ FRTP Pellets
Side Feeder (Fibre)──┘
```

**3. Role of Screw Configuration (1 mark):**
- **Conveying elements** — transport polymer melt.
- **Kneading blocks** — provide high shear for fibre dispersion (but cause fibre breakage).
- **Reverse elements** — create pressure, residence time for mixing.
- Adding fibres **downstream** of the feed throat (after polymer is melted) reduces breakage.

**4. Process Parameters and Fibre Length (1 mark):**
- Higher **screw speed (N)** → more shear → more fibre breakage.
- **Higher temperature** lowers melt viscosity, reducing shear stress on fibres.
- A **degradation model** is given by $L_f = L_0 \cdot \exp(-K\gamma)$.

### Part (b) Solution — Injection Moulding & Skin-Core Morphology

**1. Process Description (2 marks):**
The FRTP pellets are melted in a heated barrel, injected under high pressure into a closed mould, packed, cooled, and ejected. Fibres align along the direction of melt flow.

**2. Skin-Core Morphology (3 marks):**
- **Skin layer:** High shear and rapid cooling near the cold mould wall align fibres strongly in the flow direction.
- **Core layer:** Lower shear in the centre allows fibres to remain randomly oriented.
- **Transitional layer:** Between skin and core.
- This creates a **sandwich-like laminate structure** within the moulded part.

**3. Mechanical Anisotropy (2 marks):**
- Modulus and strength are **highest in the flow direction** (skin layer, aligned fibres).
- **Weld lines and knit lines** are weak because fibres are transverse to flow.
- Designers must **position weld lines away from high-stress regions**.

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions — Question B

### Part (a) Solution — Rotational Moulding

**1. Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Charging** — Pre-measured polymer powder and short fibres loaded into a closed mould.
2. **Heating + Biaxial rotation** — Mould rotates on two perpendicular axes (e.g., 4:1 ratio) inside a heated oven. Powder melts and tumbles, coating the inner mould surface.
3. **Cooling** — Mould transferred to a cooling chamber while still rotating; melt solidifies uniformly.
4. **Demoulding** — Mould opened and hollow FRTP part removed.

**2. Advantages (2 marks):**
- Large, seamless, hollow parts.
- Low pressure → low mould cost.
- Low shear → better fibre length retention than injection moulding.
- Uniform wall thickness achievable.

**3. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Long cycle time (15–40 min).
- Limited to short fibres (< 1 mm) and thin walls.
- Fibre settling, non-uniform distribution in thick sections.

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
- Chemical storage tanks, water tanks, automotive fuel tanks, kayak hulls, ducting.

### Part (b) Solution — Cold Forming

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
Cold forming (solid-phase stamping) is a process in which a pre-heated reinforced thermoplastic sheet (e.g., GMT, GMTex, organo-sheet) is shaped in a matched-metal mould at a temperature **below the melting point** $T_m$ of the matrix but within its workable softening range (150–200 °C for PP). High pressure (1–10 MPa) forces the sheet into the mould shape.

**2. Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Cold Forming | Injection Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre length retained | Long (mm–cm) | Short (< 1 mm) |
| Cycle time | 10–60 s | 30–120 s |
| Fibre volume fraction $V_f$ | Up to 0.40–0.45 | 0.15–0.30 |
| Part complexity | Moderate | High (intricate) |
| Production volume | Medium–High | Very High |
| Matrix thermal exposure | Brief | Long (in barrel) |
| Equipment cost | Moderate | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing melt compounding with injection moulding** — they are different stages (preparation vs. forming).
> 2. **Forgetting that coating is a pre-compounding step** — students sometimes omit it entirely.
> 3. **Claiming that twin-screw always gives better mixing AND retains fibre length** — these are conflicting goals; the answer is a trade-off.
> 4. **Stating that rotational moulding can use long fibres** — in practice, only short fibres (< 1 mm) work due to powder flow requirements.
> 5. **Cold forming is not "room-temperature" forming** — the sheet is pre-heated; this is the most common exam error.
> 6. **Not annotating process conditions (T, P, time)** on diagrams — KTU examiners specifically award marks for labelled process parameters.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FRTP Preparation & Forming**

**Core Concepts:**
- FRTP = Thermoplastic matrix + reinforcing fibres; **recyclable, reprocessable**.
- Three preparation routes: **Coating → Compounding (Melt or Dry) → Forming (Injection / Rotational / Cold Forming)**.

**Coating Process:**
- Also called **sizing**.
- Applies a thin film (often with **silane coupling agent**) on fibre surface.
- Purpose: protection + wetting + chemical coupling.
- Silane structure: $\text{R–Si(OR')}_3$ → forms Glass–O–Si–R ↔ Polymer.

**Melt Compounding:**
- Most common industrial method.
- Uses **single/twin-screw extruder**.
- **High shear = good dispersion but fibre breakage**.
- Output: FRTP pellets.
- Degradation model: $L_f = L_0 \cdot \exp(-K\gamma)$.

**Dry Blending:**
- No heat, no melting.
- Simple mixing of polymer powder + short fibres.
- Used as **pre-mix for rotational moulding**.
- **Issue: poor fibre dispersion (agglomeration)**.

**Injection Moulding:**
- Highest volume production.
- **Skin-core-skin morphology** (fibre orientation varies through thickness).
- **Weld lines = weak zones**.
- Final fibre length: **0.2 – 0.5 mm** (highly degraded).

**Rotational Moulding:**
- Large hollow parts, low pressure, low shear.
- Biaxial rotation; powder + short fibres.
- Long cycle time; thin walls; limited fibre length.

**Cold Forming:**
- Shape **below $T_m$**, above $T_g$ (for amorphous) or in the workable range (semi-crystalline).
- Starting stock: **GMT, GMTex, organo-sheets**.
- **Retains long fibre length** → high performance.
- Applications: automotive structural parts (bumpers, battery trays, front-end carriers).

**Critical Metrics to Memorize:**

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Critical fibre length $l_c$ (glass/PP) | 0.5 – 1.0 mm |
| Critical aspect ratio $(l/d)_c$ | 50 – 100 |
| Fibre length after injection moulding | 0.2 – 0.5 mm |
| Fibre length after cold forming | 3 – 25 mm |
| GMT fibre volume fraction | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| Typical silane types | Amino-silane, epoxy-silane, vinyl-silane |
| Process temperature for PP cold forming | 150 – 200 °C |

**Common Exam Traps:**
- Coating ≠ Compounding ≠ Forming — they are **sequential distinct stages**.
- Twin-screw ≠ always best for fibre length.
- Rotational moulding ≠ suitable for long fibres.
- Cold forming ≠ forming at room temperature.
- A "good" composite has $l > l_c$ and a properly **coupled interface**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Criterion to be Adopted in the Selection of Matrix and Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Criterion to be Adopted in the Selection of Matrix and Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: BASIC CRITERION FOR SELECTION OF MATRIX AND REINFORCEMENT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *In composite material design, the selection of matrix and reinforcement is not arbitrary. It follows a systematic set of engineering criteria based on service requirements, mechanical loads, environmental conditions, manufacturing feasibility, and economic considerations.*

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Composite Selection Criteria

The **Basic Criterion for Selection of Matrix and Reinforcement** refers to the systematic engineering framework used to identify suitable constituent materials (matrix and reinforcement) for a composite system based on:

- **Mechanical requirements** (load-bearing capacity, stiffness, toughness)
- **Physical requirements** (density, thermal expansion)
- **Chemical requirements** (corrosion resistance, environmental stability)
- **Manufacturing requirements** (process compatibility, wettability)
- **Economic requirements** (cost, availability)
- **Service-life requirements** (fatigue, creep, durability)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Team"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Think of a Composite as a Construction Team:**
> 
> Imagine building a **reinforced concrete bridge**:
> - The **reinforcement (rebar)** acts like the **specialist worker** — provides the strength and handles the heavy lifting (tensile loads).
> - The **matrix (concrete)** acts like the **project manager** — holds everything together, distributes the load, protects the worker, and gives shape to the structure.
> 
> Now ask: *How do you select these?*
> - You pick the **worker** based on the **strength needed** (load), **flexibility needed** (ductility), and **work conditions** (corrosion, heat).
> - You pick the **manager** based on **how well they can bond with the worker** (interfacial adhesion), **how easily they can be shaped** (processability), and **how long they will last** (durability).
> 
> The **criterion** is essentially the "job description" you write before hiring either of them!

### 1.3 Why Selection Criteria Matter

A poorly chosen matrix-reinforcement pair results in:

- Premature failure under load
- Delamination at the interface
- Excessive weight
- Unaffordable manufacturing cost
- Reduced service life

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** The **matrix** is selected first to define the **operating environment**, and the **reinforcement** is selected second to **meet the structural demands**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Criteria for the MATRIX

The matrix in a composite serves **three primary functions**:

1. **Bind the reinforcement** together and maintain structural geometry.
2. **Transfer loads** to the reinforcement through shear at the interface.
3. **Protect the reinforcement** from environmental damage (moisture, chemicals, abrasion).

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Criteria for Matrix

| Criterion | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength** | Ability to withstand tensile stress without failure | Must be sufficient to transfer load to fibers |
| **Modulus (Stiffness)** | Resistance to elastic deformation | Determines load-sharing efficiency |
| **Fracture Toughness** | Resistance to crack propagation | Prevents catastrophic brittle failure |
| **Shear Strength** | Resistance to shear stresses | Critical for in-plane and interlaminar shear |
| **Elongation at Break** | Ductility measure | Must exceed fiber strain to avoid matrix cracking |

### 2.1.2 Physical & Thermal Criteria for Matrix

| Criterion | Description | Typical Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | Mass per unit volume | **Low** for aerospace applications |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | Dimensional change with temperature | Should be **close to fiber CTE** to minimize residual stresses |
| **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** | Temperature at which polymer becomes rubbery | Must be **above service temperature** |
| **Thermal Conductivity** | Heat transfer capability | **High** for thermal management composites |
| **Cure Temperature** | Temperature required for matrix solidification | Must **not damage the fiber** |

### 2.1.3 Chemical & Environmental Criteria for Matrix

- **Chemical Resistance** — Resistance to solvents, fuels, acids, alkalis
- **Moisture Absorption** — Should be **low** to prevent swelling and degradation
- **UV Resistance** — Important for outdoor applications
- **Oxidation Resistance** — Critical for high-temperature service

### 2.1.4 Processing & Economic Criteria for Matrix

- **Viscosity** — Should be **low** during processing to allow fiber impregnation
- **Pot Life** — Working time before curing begins
- **Cure Shrinkage** — Should be **minimal** to reduce residual stresses
- **Cost** — Should be **economically viable**
- **Availability** — Should be commercially accessible

---

## 2.2 Selection Criteria for the REINFORCEMENT

The reinforcement (typically fibers) carries the **majority of the load** in a composite. Selection depends on:

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Criteria for Reinforcement

| Criterion | Description | Typical Fiber Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength** | Maximum stress before failure | Carbon: **3–7 GPa**, Glass: **2–4.5 GPa** |
| **Young's Modulus ($E_f$)** | Resistance to elastic deformation | Carbon: **230–700 GPa**, Glass: **70–90 GPa** |
| **Strain to Failure** | Elongation before breaking | Should be **higher than matrix** |
| **Aspect Ratio ($l/d$)** | Length-to-diameter ratio | **Critical $> 1000$** for continuous fibers |
| **Density** | Mass per unit volume | Carbon: **1.7–2.2 g/cm³** |

### 2.2.2 Physical & Thermal Criteria for Reinforcement

- **Thermal Stability** — Must withstand processing temperature
- **CTE Compatibility** — Should match matrix to reduce thermal mismatch
- **Dimensional Stability** — Resistance to creep and relaxation
- **Thermal Conductivity** — Selected based on thermal dissipation need

### 2.2.3 Chemical Criteria for Reinforcement

- **Surface Chemistry** — Must enable good **fiber-matrix bonding**
- **Oxidation Resistance** — Important at elevated temperatures
- **Corrosion Resistance** — Especially for metal and natural fibers

### 2.2.4 Geometric Criteria for Reinforcement

| Fiber Type | Geometry | Selection Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Fibers** | Long, aligned | Maximum strength in one direction |
| **Short Fibers** | Discontinuous, random | Isotropic properties, lower cost |
| **Whiskers** | Single crystals, very fine | Used in metal/ceramic matrices |
| **Particulates** | Powder form | Used for property modification |

---

## 2.3 Key Governing Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations govern the **load transfer efficiency** between matrix and reinforcement. They are derived from the **shear-lag theory** and form the backbone of KTU numerical problems.

### 2.3.1 Critical Fiber Length

The **critical fiber length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length required for the fiber to carry its full tensile load before the matrix fails in shear:

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

Where:

- $\sigma_f$ = **Fiber tensile strength**
- $d$ = **Fiber diameter**
- $\tau_i$ = **Interfacial shear strength**

### 2.3.2 Rule of Mixtures for Strength (Longitudinal)

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

Where:

- $\sigma_c$ = Composite strength
- $V_f$ = **Volume fraction of fiber**
- $V_m$ = Volume fraction of matrix

### 2.3.3 Fiber Volume Fraction

$$
V_f = \frac{A_f}{A_c}
$$

### 2.3.4 Matrix Volume Fraction

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length | m or mm |
| $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Longitudinal strength (ROM) | Pa or MPa |
| $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (ROM) | Pa or GPa |
| $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse modulus (Inverse ROM) | Pa or GPa |
| $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction constraint | Dimensionless |
| $l/d$ | Fiber aspect ratio (must exceed **$l_c/d$**) | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection criteria are used in:

- **Aerospace** — Carbon/epoxy for wings and fuselage (high stiffness-to-weight)
- **Automotive** — Glass/polyester for body panels (low cost, easy molding)
- **Marine** — Glass/vinyl ester for boat hulls (moisture resistance)
- **Sports Equipment** — Carbon/epoxy for tennis rackets (high specific strength)
- **Civil Engineering** — Glass/polyester for bridge decks (corrosion resistance)
- **Electronics** — Carbon/copper for thermal management

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Critical Fiber Length** is the single most important selection parameter for reinforcement. KTU frequently tests this derivation for **7–14 mark** questions.

### Step 1: Define the Physical Setup

Consider a **single fiber** of diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix. The fiber is loaded in tension. Load is transferred from the matrix to the fiber through **shear stress ($\tau_i$)** at the cylindrical interface.

### Step 2: Apply Force Equilibrium

The tensile force on the fiber at its midpoint (where stress is maximum) equals the shear force transferred along half the fiber length:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** The left side is the tensile force in the fiber cross-section. The right side is the shear force acting on the cylindrical interface of length $l_c/2$ (load builds up from both ends to the center).

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation

Divide both sides by $\pi d$:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $l_c$

Multiply both sides by $2$:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{2} = \tau_i \cdot l_c
$$

Solve for $l_c$:

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - If $l < l_c$ → Fiber pulls out before reaching its full strength (**inefficient reinforcement**)
> - If $l \geq l_c$ → Fiber can develop its full tensile strength (**efficient reinforcement**)
> - Therefore, in design: **Choose fiber length $\geq l_c$**

### Step 5: Calculate the Critical Aspect Ratio

$$
\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Example Numerical Application:**

Given: $\sigma_f = 3$ GPa, $d = 10 \mu m$, $\tau_i = 50$ MPa:

$$
l_c = \frac{(3 \times 10^9) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \cdot (50 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{30}{100} \times 10^{-3} = 0.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.3 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the **minimum fiber length** must be **at least 0.3 mm**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Material Selection

> [!TIP]
> Use this **decision matrix** during exam answers to score full marks on selection-based questions.

| Step | Decision Point | Question to Answer | Selection Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Identify Service Conditions | What is the operating temperature, environment, and load? | Defines the **performance envelope** |
| **2** | Choose Matrix Type | Polymer / Metal / Ceramic? | Based on **temperature and load** |
| **3** | Match Matrix $T_g$ to Service $T$ | Is $T_g > T_{\text{service}}$? | If no → matrix fails |
| **4** | Choose Fiber Type | Carbon / Glass / Aramid / Boron? | Based on **strength and modulus** needed |
| **5** | Verify Critical Length | Is $l \geq l_c$? | Ensures **efficient load transfer** |
| **6** | Check CTE Compatibility | Is $\alpha_f \approx \alpha_m$? | Prevents **thermal residual stresses** |
| **7** | Evaluate Fiber-Matrix Bond | Is $\tau_i$ adequate? | Ensures **load transfer** |
| **8** | Assess Processability | Can matrix impregnate fibers? | Depends on **viscosity and wettability** |
| **9** | Optimize Volume Fraction | Is $V_f$ within $30\%$–$70\%$? | Balances **strength and processability** |
| **10** | Economic Feasibility | Is the system cost-effective? | Final filter for **commercial viability** |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:**
A glass fiber has tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2.5$ GPa and diameter $d = 15 \mu m$. The interfacial shear strength with the epoxy matrix is $\tau_i = 40$ MPa. Determine:

**(a)** The critical fiber length.
**(b)** Whether a fiber of length $2$ mm is sufficient.

### Solution:

**(a)** Using the critical fiber length formula:

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^9) \cdot (15 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \cdot (40 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{37.5}{80} \times 10^{-3} = 0.46875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
l_c \approx 0.469 \text{ mm}
$$

**(b)** Given fiber length $l = 2$ mm, compare:

$$
l = 2 \text{ mm} > l_c = 0.469 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Since $l > l_c$, the fiber is **long enough** to develop its full tensile strength. The reinforcement is **efficient** for this composite system.

---

```mermaid

    F --> G
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define critical fiber length. Why is it important in selecting reinforcement for a composite?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> Critical fiber length ($l_c$) is the minimum fiber length required for the fiber to develop its full tensile strength before the matrix fails in shear. It is given by:
> 
> $$l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$
> 
> **Importance (1.5 Marks):**
> - It determines whether the reinforcement will be **efficient** or **inefficient**.
> - If $l < l_c$, the fiber pulls out prematurely and does not reach its full strength.
> - If $l \geq l_c$, the fiber can bear maximum load and the composite achieves high strength.
> - Therefore, **fiber length must be chosen $\geq l_c$** during selection.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any SIX criteria for the selection of a matrix material in fiber-reinforced composites.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 each):

1. **Mechanical strength and stiffness** — to transfer loads to fibers.
2. **Fracture toughness** — to prevent brittle failure.
3. **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — must exceed service temperature.
4. **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** — should match the fiber.
5. **Chemical and moisture resistance** — for environmental durability.
6. **Low viscosity during processing** — for proper fiber impregnation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain in detail the basic criteria to be adopted in the selection of matrix material for a composite system.**

#### Model Answer:

**Introduction (1 Mark):**

The matrix in a composite binds the reinforcement, transfers loads to it through interfacial shear, and protects it from environmental damage. The selection of matrix depends on multiple engineering criteria.

**Mechanical Criteria (2 Marks):**

- Adequate **tensile, compressive, and shear strength** to transfer load.
- Sufficient **modulus** to maintain rigidity.
- High **fracture toughness** to avoid brittle failure.
- **Elongation at break** must exceed that of the fiber to prevent matrix cracking.

**Thermal Criteria (2 Marks):**

- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** must be higher than service temperature.
- **CTE** should be close to fiber CTE to minimize residual thermal stresses.
- Good **thermal stability** and **thermal conductivity** as required.

**Chemical and Environmental Criteria (1 Mark):**

- Resistance to moisture, chemicals, UV, and oxidation.
- Low moisture absorption to prevent swelling.

**Processing and Economic Criteria (1 Mark):**

- Low viscosity for fiber impregnation.
- Manageable cure temperature and time.
- Cost-effective and commercially available.

**Conclusion (0 Marks — included in total):**

The matrix must satisfy all these criteria simultaneously to ensure composite performance.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A carbon fiber of diameter $8 \mu m$ and tensile strength $4$ GPa is used with an epoxy matrix having interfacial shear strength of $60$ MPa. Determine: (i) the critical fiber length, and (ii) whether a fiber of $1.5$ mm length will be efficient.**

#### Model Answer:

**Given:**

- Fiber diameter, $d = 8 \mu m = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Fiber tensile strength, $\sigma_f = 4$ GPa $= 4 \times 10^9$ Pa
- Interfacial shear strength, $\tau_i = 60$ MPa $= 60 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Given fiber length, $l = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**(i) Critical Fiber Length (3 Marks):**

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{(4 \times 10^9) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \cdot (60 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{32}{120} \times 10^{-3} = 0.2667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{l_c \approx 0.267 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(ii) Comparison (4 Marks):**

Given $l = 1.5$ mm and $l_c = 0.267$ mm:

$$
l = 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{and} \quad l_c = 0.267 \text{ mm}
$$

Since:

$$
l > l_c \quad (1.5 \text{ mm} > 0.267 \text{ mm})
$$

> **Conclusion:** The fiber length is **sufficient** to develop full tensile strength. The reinforcement is **efficient**.

---

### QUESTION B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the basic criteria for the selection of reinforcement (fiber) in fiber-reinforced composites with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer:

**Introduction (1 Mark):**

The reinforcement carries the major portion of applied load in a composite. Selection of fiber depends on its mechanical, physical, chemical, geometric, and economic properties.

**Mechanical Criteria (2 Marks):**

- High **tensile strength** — e.g., Carbon fiber: $3$–$7$ GPa.
- High **Young's modulus** — e.g., Carbon fiber: $230$–$700$ GPa.
- Adequate **strain to failure** — must be higher than matrix.
- Sufficient **aspect ratio** — continuous fibers preferred for high strength.

**Physical and Thermal Criteria (2 Marks):**

- **Low density** — Carbon fiber: $1.7$–$2.2$ g/cm³ (lightweight).
- **CTE compatibility** — Should match matrix to prevent cracking.
- **Thermal stability** — Must survive matrix processing temperature.

**Geometric and Chemical Criteria (1 Mark):**

- **Surface chemistry** — Enables bonding with matrix; may need **sizing or coupling agents**.
- **Diameter** — Smaller diameter gives higher flexibility and surface area for bonding.

**Economic Criteria (1 Mark):**

- Cost per kg and availability in required form (roving, mat, fabric).

**Conclusion:**

Selection balances **performance requirements** with **cost and processability**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For a glass fiber reinforced composite, the following data is given: $\sigma_f = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa, $V_f = 0.45$, $V_m = 0.55$. Calculate the longitudinal strength of the composite using the rule of mixtures.**

#### Model Answer:

**Given:**

- $\sigma_f = 3.5$ GPa $= 3.5 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa $= 50 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $V_f = 0.45$
- $V_m = 0.55$

**Verification (1 Mark):**

$$
V_f + V_m = 0.45 + 0.55 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Applying Rule of Mixtures (4 Marks):**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

$$
\sigma_c = (3.5 \times 10^9)(0.45) + (50 \times 10^6)(0.55)
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 1.575 \times 10^9 + 27.5 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 1575 \times 10^6 + 27.5 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 1602.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 1.60 \text{ GPa} = 1602.5 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Interpretation (2 Marks):**

The composite strength is **dominated by the fiber** ($98\%$ contribution from fiber). This justifies the selection of high-strength fibers as reinforcement to maximize composite performance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing GPa and MPa without conversion. **Always convert all values to the same unit** before calculation.
> 2. **Forgetting the Volume Fraction Constraint** — $V_f + V_m = 1$ must be verified.
> 3. **Misinterpreting Critical Length Comparison** — Students often write "$l_c > l$" when they mean "$l < l_c$". Read carefully!
> 4. **Skipping the Conclusion** — KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if numerical answers end without a concluding statement.
> 5. **Listing Criteria Without Explaining Significance** — In 14-mark answers, merely listing criteria without **engineering reasoning** fetches only partial marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the Interface** — Students often discuss matrix and reinforcement separately but **forget to mention the fiber-matrix interface**, which is a critical selection criterion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### ✅ Matrix Selection — Key Points
- Matrix **binds, protects, and transfers load** to fibers.
- Must have $T_g > T_{\text{service}}$.
- CTE should match the fiber.
- Low viscosity for processing.
- Must be chemically compatible and resistant to environment.
- Cost-effective and available.

### ✅ Reinforcement Selection — Key Points
- Fiber must have **high tensile strength and modulus**.
- Fiber length must satisfy $l \geq l_c$.
- Critical fiber length formula: $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$.
- Aspect ratio must be high ($> 1000$) for continuous fibers.
- CTE should match the matrix.
- Surface must allow adequate bonding (use **sizing/coupling agents**).

### ✅ Interface Selection — Key Points
- Adequate **interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$)** for load transfer.
- **Too strong** → brittle failure.
- **Too weak** → fiber pull-out.
- **Optimum bonding** is required for tough composites.

### ✅ Numerical Must-Knows
- Critical fiber length formula.
- Rule of mixtures for strength and modulus.
- Volume fraction relationships.

### ✅ Common Material Pairs (KTU Favorite)
- **Carbon / Epoxy** — Aerospace, high performance
- **Glass / Polyester** — Marine, low cost
- **Kevlar / Epoxy** — Impact-resistant applications
- **SiC / Aluminum** — Metal matrix composites

### ✅ Frequently Asked Concepts
- Difference between **continuous vs discontinuous** fibers.
- **Aspect ratio** significance.
- **Why $T_g$ matters.**
- **Why CTE matching matters.**
- **Why fiber length $\geq l_c$ matters.**

---
