topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanics of Composite Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MECHANICS OF COMPOSITE MATERIALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanics of Composite Materials** is the branch of solid mechanics that studies the **load-carrying behavior, deformation response, strength, and failure characteristics** of heterogeneous materials composed of two or more distinct constituents (typically **fibers** and **matrix**). It uses two complementary scales of analysis:
> - **Micromechanics** (micro-scale): Studies behavior of individual constituents (fiber, matrix) and their interactions to predict the **effective properties** of the composite.
> - **Macromechanics** (macro-scale): Treats the composite laminate as a **homogeneous anisotropic continuum** to compute stresses, strains, deformations, and strength.

The discipline is governed by the fundamentals of **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** and **tensor transformation principles** in anisotropic elasticity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Rope Bridge vs. Steel Cable:**
> Imagine a bundle of drinking straws glued together with epoxy. A single straw bends easily and breaks under small load. But when **glued together in a parallel bundle**, the bundle can hold a large load — the **straws carry the tension** while the **glue (matrix) holds them in place and transfers load** between them.
> - **Straws = Fibers** (the load bearers)
> - **Glue = Matrix** (the load transfer medium + shape provider)
> - The bundle = **Composite Material** — far stronger than either alone.
>
> The "mechanics" of this system answers: *How much of the load does each straw carry? How much does the bundle stretch? When does it fail?*

---

### 1.3 Key Constituent Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Definition |
|--------|----------|------------|
| $V_f$ | **Fiber volume fraction** | Volume of fibers / Total composite volume |
| $V_m$ | **Matrix volume fraction** | Volume of matrix / Total composite volume |
| $V_v$ | **Void volume fraction** | Volume of voids / Total composite volume |
| $W_f, W_m$ | Weight fractions | Mass-based equivalent of $V_f, V_m$ |
| $\rho_f, \rho_m, \rho_c$ | Densities | Mass per unit volume of fiber, matrix, composite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Rule:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$. For an ideal composite, $V_v = 0$, so $V_f + V_m = 1$.

---

### 1.4 Micromechanics vs. Macromechanics — Why Two Scales?

> [!NOTE]
> **Micromechanics** = "Inside the composite" → answers: *What is the effective stiffness? What is the effective strength?*
> **Macromechanics** = "Treat the laminate as a black box" → answers: *How does a ply deform under load? When does it fail?*

Both are essential for **design, certification, and failure analysis** in aerospace, automotive, civil, and marine structures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Micromechanics — Property Prediction

Micromechanics predicts the **effective (homogenized) engineering properties** of a unidirectional (UD) composite lamina from its constituents' properties.

### 2.1.1 Density (Rule of Mixtures — ROM)

The composite density is the volume-weighted average of constituent densities:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

This is exact (no assumptions on geometry) because **mass is an extensive property**.

---

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal Young's Modulus $E_1$ (Voigt Model — Iso-strain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** when load is applied **parallel to fibers** (perfect bonding, parallel isostrain condition). This is the **Voigt upper bound**.

$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Why this works:** Under longitudinal loading, both phases elongate identically; the total stress is the sum of fiber + matrix contributions weighted by their volume fractions.

---

### 2.1.3 Transverse Young's Modulus $E_2$ (Reuss Model — Iso-stress)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same stress** when load is applied **perpendicular to fibers** (series arrangement). This is the **Reuss lower bound**.

$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad E_2 = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$$

---

### 2.1.4 Major Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{12}$

$$\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$$

### 2.1.5 Shear Modulus $G_{12}$

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}} = \frac{V_f}{G_f} + \frac{V_m}{G_m}$$

---

### 2.2 Macromechanics — Stress-Strain for a Lamina

A single unidirectional ply (lamina) is treated as a **homogeneous, orthotropic continuum** in the principal material axes (1-2-3).

#### 2.2.1 Constitutive Equation (Orthotropic Hooke's Law in 3D)

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \tau_{23} \\ \tau_{13} \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{13} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{44} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{55} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \gamma_{23} \\ \gamma_{13} \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

where the **stiffness matrix components** are:

$$C_{11} = \frac{1 - \nu_{23}\nu_{32}}{E_2 E_3} \Delta, \quad C_{22} = \frac{1 - \nu_{13}\nu_{31}}{E_1 E_3} \Delta, \quad C_{12} = \frac{\nu_{21} + \nu_{31}\nu_{23}}{E_1 E_3} \Delta$$

and $\Delta = 1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} - \nu_{23}\nu_{32} - \nu_{13}\nu_{31} - 2\nu_{12}\nu_{23}\nu_{31}$ (divided by $E_1 E_2 E_3$).

---

#### 2.2.2 Plane-Stress Reduced Form (Most Common in KTU)

For a thin lamina ($\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$):

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

where the **reduced stiffness matrix components** are:

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12}, \quad \nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \frac{E_2}{E_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity:** $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ — must always hold (Maxwell-Betti).

---

### 2.3 Transformation Equations (Off-Axis Lamina)

When a load is applied at an angle $\theta$ to the fiber direction, we must **transform stresses and strains** between the **material axes (1-2)** and the **loading axes (x-y)**.

#### 2.3.1 Stress Transformation

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c^2 & s^2 & 2sc \\ s^2 & c^2 & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^2 - s^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c = \cos\theta$ and $s = \sin\theta$.

#### 2.3.2 Strain Transformation (uses engineering shear strain)

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12}/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c^2 & s^2 & 2sc \\ s^2 & c^2 & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^2 - s^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table as a **rapid revision reference** before KTU ESE.

| # | Property / Equation | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density (ROM) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Exact, no assumptions |
| 2 | Volume rule | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Void-free composite |
| 3 | Longitudinal modulus | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Voigt / iso-strain |
| 4 | Transverse modulus | $1/E_2 = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | Reuss / iso-stress |
| 5 | Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Iso-strain |
| 6 | Shear modulus | $1/G_{12} = V_f/G_f + V_m/G_m$ | Iso-stress |
| 7 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{11}$ | $E_1 / (1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Plane stress |
| 8 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{22}$ | $E_2 / (1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Plane stress |
| 9 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{12}$ | $\nu_{12} E_2 / (1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Plane stress |
| 10 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{66}$ | $G_{12}$ | Plane stress |
| 11 | Reciprocity | $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$ | Maxwell-Betti |
| 12 | Stress transformation | $[\sigma_{12}] = [T_\sigma][\sigma_{xy}]$ | $T_\sigma$ uses $c, s$ |
| 13 | Strain transformation | $[\varepsilon_{12}] = [T_\varepsilon][\varepsilon_{xy}]$ | $T_\varepsilon$ uses $c, s$ |
| 14 | Engineering shear factor | $\gamma_{12} = 2 \varepsilon_{12}$ (tensor) | Conversion needed |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Tailoring $V_f$ and stacking sequence to meet stiffness + strength targets.
> - **Civil:** GFRP rebars + concrete columns, bridge decks, retrofitting.
> - **Automotive:** Carbon-fiber monocoque chassis.
> - **Wind energy:** Glass/epoxy blades 60–80 m long — designed using $E_1, E_2, G_{12}$ above.
> - **Sports:** Tennis rackets, F1 bodies — fail-safe under multi-axial impact loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Density by Rule of Mixtures (ROM)

**Setup:** Consider a composite of total volume $V_c$ containing fibers of volume $V_f$ and matrix of volume $V_m$ (void-free).

**Step 1 — Total mass:** Mass is additive.

$$M_c = M_f + M_m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute mass as density × volume:**

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $V_c$:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 4 — Identify volume fractions** $V_f/V_c = v_f$ and $V_m/V_c = v_m$:

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m}$$

This derivation is **exact** because no assumption on fiber geometry, distribution, or stress state is invoked — it is purely a conservation-of-mass statement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Longitudinal Modulus $E_1$ (Iso-strain / Voigt)

**Assumption 1 — Perfect bonding:** No slip at fiber-matrix interface.
**Assumption 2 — Iso-strain:** Both phases experience the same strain $\varepsilon_1$ along the fiber direction (justified because fibers and matrix are bonded along their length).

**Step 1 — Total load (force) on composite cross-section** $A_c$:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

**Step 2 — Express force as stress × area:**

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3 — For UD fibers parallel to load, area fractions = volume fractions** $A_f/A_c = V_f$, $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Hooke's law for each phase:** $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon_i$. With iso-strain, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_1$:

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon_1 V_f + E_m \varepsilon_1 V_m = \varepsilon_1 (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_1$ to get effective modulus:**

$$\boxed{E_1 = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_1} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Voigt upper bound** — it works well for $E_1$ in UD composites because the iso-strain assumption is geometrically correct.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Transverse Modulus $E_2$ (Iso-stress / Reuss)

**Assumption:** When load is applied **perpendicular to fibers**, fibers and matrix are arranged in **parallel "columns"** carrying the **same stress** $\sigma_2$ (iso-stress), but experiencing **different strains** $\varepsilon_f, \varepsilon_m$.

**Step 1 — Equal stress (parallel load paths):**

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$$

**Step 2 — Total elongation = sum of fiber and matrix elongations** (volumetric compatibility):

$$\Delta L_c V_c = \Delta L_f V_f + \Delta L_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta L_i = \varepsilon_i L$ (same $L$ for all phases):**

$$\varepsilon_2 V_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law** $\varepsilon_i = \sigma_2 / E_i$ (since all have same $\sigma_2$):

$$\varepsilon_2 V_c = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m} V_m = \sigma_2 \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $V_c$ to express in terms of $V_f, V_m$:**

$$\varepsilon_2 = \sigma_2 \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Take reciprocal to get $E_2 = \sigma_2 / \varepsilon_2$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad E_2 = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Reuss lower bound** — it is less accurate than Voigt because the true stress field in a transverse-loaded UD composite is highly non-uniform (stress concentrations around fibers). The **Halpin-Tsai equation** is used in practice for better accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stress Transformation Matrix

**Setup:** Consider a unidirectional lamina with material axes (1, 2). The fiber direction makes an angle $\theta$ with the loading x-axis. We need to find stresses $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12})$ in material coordinates given the applied stresses $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$.

**Step 1 — Direction cosines.** The unit vector along the x-axis in (1,2) coordinates is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The unit vector along the 1-axis in (x,y) coordinates is also $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.

Define:
$$c = \cos\theta, \quad s = \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Stress component on a plane normal to the 1-axis (the fiber direction):**

The traction on a plane perpendicular to the 1-axis is obtained by projecting $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ onto the 1 and 2 directions:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x c^2 + \sigma_y s^2 + 2 \tau_{xy} s c$$

**Step 3 — Stress component on a plane normal to the 2-axis (transverse to fibers):**

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_x s^2 + \sigma_y c^2 - 2 \tau_{xy} s c$$

**Step 4 — Shear stress in the (1,2) system:**

By tensor transformation of stress:

$$\tau_{12} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y) s c + \tau_{xy} (c^2 - s^2)$$

**Step 5 — Matrix form:**

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c^2 & s^2 & 2sc \\ s^2 & c^2 & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^2 - s^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transformation matrix is **orthogonal** for stress/strain, with determinant = 1 (a pure rotation in stress space).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Reduced Stiffness $Q_{ij}$ Components

Starting from the 3D orthotropic compliance relation (in plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21} \sigma_2}{E_2}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12} \sigma_1}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}, \quad \gamma_{12} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}$$

**Step 1 — Invert the normal-stress part** by writing it in matrix form and solving for $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{21}/E_2 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Use reciprocity** $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2 / E_1$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{E_1 E_2} \begin{bmatrix} E_2 & -\nu_{12} E_2 \\ -\nu_{12} E_2 & E_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Wait — multiply numerator and denominator carefully. Let $D = 1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$:

**Step 3 — Invert the compliance matrix** $S$ to get the stiffness matrix $Q$:

$$Q = S^{-1} = \frac{1}{D} \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_2 & \nu_{12}/E_1 \\ \nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_1 \end{bmatrix}^{-1 \text{ component-wise}}$$

After carrying out the inversion:

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}}$$

with $Q_{66} = G_{12}$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A unidirectional glass/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.6$, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.5$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $G_f = 30$ GPa, $G_m = 1.3$ GPa. Compute $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \rho_c$.

**Step 1 —** $V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal modulus (Voigt):**
$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = 70(0.6) + 3.5(0.4) = 42 + 1.4 = 43.4 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Transverse modulus (Reuss):**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{0.6}{70} + \frac{0.4}{3.5} = 0.008571 + 0.114286 = 0.122857$$
$$E_2 = 1 / 0.122857 = 8.139 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Shear modulus:**
$$\frac{1}{G_{12}} = \frac{0.6}{30} + \frac{0.4}{1.3} = 0.02 + 0.3077 = 0.3277$$
$$G_{12} = 1 / 0.3277 = 3.052 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 5 — Poisson's ratio:**
$$\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m = 0.22(0.6) + 0.35(0.4) = 0.132 + 0.140 = 0.272$$

**Step 6 — Density:**
$$\rho_c = 2.5(0.6) + 1.2(0.4) = 1.5 + 0.48 = 1.98 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Summary of results:**
$E_1 = 43.4$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.139$ GPa, $G_{12} = 3.052$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.272$, $\rho_c = 1.98$ g/cm³.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Mechanics of Composite Materials: Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the mechanics-of-composites discipline as taught in KTU Module 3. It shows how the two scales (micro/macro) interact to support **design, analysis, and certification** workflows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Constituent Inputs Fiber Ef nmuf rmf Matrix Em numm rm Gm] --> A2[Micromechanics Scale]
    A1 --> A3[Macromechanics Scale]

    A2 --> B1[Rule of Mixtures Density rho c]
    A2 --> B2[Longitudinal Modulus E1 Voigt]
    A2 --> B3[Transverse Modulus E2 Reuss]
    A2 --> B4[Shear Modulus G12]
    A2 --> B5[Poisson Ratio nu12]
    A2 --> B6[Halpin Tsai Correction]

    A3 --> C1[Orthotropic Hookes Law 3D]
    A3 --> C2[Plane Stress Reduction]
    A3 --> C3[Reduced Stiffness Q Matrix]
    A3 --> C4[Stress Transformation c s]
    A3 --> C5[Strain Transformation c s]

    B1 --> D1[Effective Composite Properties]
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    B5 --> D1
    B6 --> D1

    C1 --> D2[Lamina Constitutive Law]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D2

    D1 --> E1[Laminate Layup Design]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Classical Lamination Theory Module 4]
    E1 --> E3[Failure Analysis Tsai Wu Tsai Hill Module 5]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Stress Transformation Flow

This diagram shows the **sequential flow** of operations when a load is applied at an angle $\theta$ to the fiber direction — a common KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Applied Load sigma x sigma y tau xy] --> P2[Compute c cos theta s sin theta]
    P2 --> P3[Apply Stress Transformation T sigma]
    P3 --> P4[Obtain sigma 1 sigma 2 tau 12 in Material Axes]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Constitutive Law Q matrix]
    P5 --> P6[Obtain strain epsilon 1 epsilon 2 gamma 12]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Strain Transformation T epsilon inverse]
    P7 --> P8[Obtain strain epsilon x epsilon y gamma xy in Loading Axes]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Effective Off Axis Moduli E x E y G xy]
    P9 --> P10[Compare with Strength Limits]
    P10 --> P11{Failure Check}
    P11 -- Yes --> P12[Apply Failure Criterion Tsai Wu]
    P11 -- No --> P13[Design Safe]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Iso-strain vs. Iso-stress Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Loading Direction Selection] --> Q2{Parallel to Fibers}
    Q1 --> Q3{Perpendicular to Fibers}

    Q2 --> R1[Iso strain Assumption Valid]
    R1 --> R2[Both phases have same epsilon 1]
    R2 --> R3[E1 equals Ef Vf plus Em Vm]
    R3 --> R4[Voigt Upper Bound]

    Q3 --> S1[Iso stress Assumption Used]
    S1 --> S2[Both phases have same sigma 2]
    S2 --> S3[Reciprocal of E2 weighted by Vf and Vm]
    S3 --> S4[Reuss Lower Bound]
    S4 --> S5[Note Less accurate due to stress concentrations]
    S5 --> S6[Use Halpin Tsai for better estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams summarize the **decision logic** and **calculation flow** of micromechanics and macromechanics, allowing the student to quickly orient themselves during the KTU ESE.

---

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Off-Axis Lamina Coordinate System (Textual)

For reference, the off-axis lamina has two coordinate systems:

- **Material axes (1, 2):** 1 is along fibers, 2 is transverse.
- **Loading axes (x, y):** x is the load direction.
- **Fiber angle $\theta$:** measured from x-axis to 1-axis (counter-clockwise positive).

The two systems are related by a rotation of $\theta$. The transformation matrix $T$ (shown in §2.3) is the rotation operator.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between micromechanics and macromechanics of composite materials.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Micromechanics:** Studies the behavior at the **constituent level** (individual fibers and matrix). It uses volume fractions, fiber/matrix properties, and microstructural geometry to predict the **effective (homogenized) engineering properties** of the composite such as $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \rho_c$.
- **Macromechanics:** Treats the composite as a **homogeneous anisotropic continuum** at the lamina/laminate level. It uses **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** and orthotropic elasticity to compute **stresses, strains, deformations, and failure** under in-plane and bending loads.
- Micromechanics gives the **input properties** that macromechanics then uses to analyze structures.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the rule of mixtures for composite density. Why is this expression exact (no assumptions)?**

**Model Answer:**
- The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** for density is: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- It is **exact** because it arises purely from the **conservation of mass** — no assumption about stress state, strain state, fiber geometry, or interface bonding is required. Mass is an extensive property, so it simply adds up by volume-weighted averaging.
- This is unlike $E_1$ (which assumes iso-strain) and $E_2$ (which assumes iso-stress).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE regulations, Module 3 questions carry internal choice. **Choose ONE of the two questions below.**

---

### ❖ Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive the expressions for the longitudinal modulus $E_1$ and the transverse modulus $E_2$ of a unidirectional composite using the rule of mixtures. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b) (7 marks)** A unidirectional carbon-fiber/epoxy composite contains 55% by volume of carbon fibers. Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.20$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³. Calculate $E_1, E_2, \nu_{12}$, and $\rho_c$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation** — Already shown in §3.2 and §3.3. Recap key steps:

*For $E_1$ (iso-strain, parallel to fibers):*
1. Force balance: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$.
2. Area ratios = volume fractions: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$.
3. Iso-strain: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_1$.
4. Hooke: $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon_i$.
5. Result: $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt upper bound).
**Assumption:** Perfect bonding, parallel isostrain (fibers parallel to load).

*For $E_2$ (iso-stress, perpendicular to fibers):*
1. Equal stress: $\sigma_2 = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$.
2. Compatibility: $\varepsilon_2 V_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$.
3. Hooke with $\varepsilon_i = \sigma_2 / E_i$.
4. Result: $1/E_2 = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (Reuss lower bound).
**Assumption:** Series arrangement; iso-stress, no fiber interaction.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.20$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³.

**$E_1$ (Voigt):**
$$E_1 = 230(0.55) + 3.5(0.45) = 126.5 + 1.575 = 128.075 \text{ GPa}$$

**$E_2$ (Reuss):**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{0.55}{230} + \frac{0.45}{3.5} = 0.002391 + 0.128571 = 0.130962$$
$$E_2 = 1/0.130962 = 7.636 \text{ GPa}$$

**$\nu_{12}$:**
$$\nu_{12} = 0.20(0.55) + 0.35(0.45) = 0.110 + 0.1575 = 0.2675$$

**$\rho_c$:**
$$\rho_c = 1.8(0.55) + 1.2(0.45) = 0.99 + 0.54 = 1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Final Answers:** $E_1 = 128.08$ GPa, $E_2 = 7.64$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.268$, $\rho_c = 1.53$ g/cm³.

---

### ❖ Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the stress transformation for an off-axis unidirectional lamina. Derive the transformation equations relating $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12})$ to $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$.

**(b) (7 marks)** For a $[0/30/45/60]$ laminate made of the same glass/epoxy lamina as in §3.6 example (with $E_1 = 43.4$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.139$ GPa, $G_{12} = 3.052$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.272$), compute the reduced stiffness coefficients $Q_{11}, Q_{22}, Q_{12}, Q_{66}$ in the principal material axes.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stress transformation** — Sketch: A rectangular lamina with fibers at angle $\theta$ to x-axis. Material axes 1-2 marked on it. Derivation already shown in §3.4. Key results:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x c^2 + \sigma_y s^2 + 2\tau_{xy} s c$$
$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_x s^2 + \sigma_y c^2 - 2\tau_{xy} s c$$
$$\tau_{12} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y) s c + \tau_{xy} (c^2 - s^2)$$

where $c = \cos\theta$, $s = \sin\theta$.

**(b) Reduced stiffness coefficients:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\nu_{21}$ from reciprocity:**
$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.272 \times \frac{8.139}{43.4} = 0.272 \times 0.1875 = 0.0510$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator $D = 1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$:**
$$D = 1 - (0.272)(0.0510) = 1 - 0.01387 = 0.9861$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Q_{11}$:**
$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{D} = \frac{43.4}{0.9861} = 44.01 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Q_{22}$:**
$$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{D} = \frac{8.139}{0.9861} = 8.254 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $Q_{12}$:**
$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{D} = \frac{0.272 \times 8.139}{0.9861} = \frac{2.2138}{0.9861} = 2.245 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $Q_{66}$:**
$$Q_{66} = G_{12} = 3.052 \text{ GPa}$$

**Final Stiffness Matrix (Material Axes):**
$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 44.01 & 2.245 & 0 \\ 2.245 & 8.254 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.052 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to use volume fractions** — Students often mix weight and volume fractions. Use $V_f$ and $V_m$ consistently.
> 2. **Confusing Voigt and Reuss** — Longitudinal loading ($E_1$) uses **Voigt (iso-strain, direct sum)**; transverse loading ($E_2$) uses **Reuss (iso-stress, reciprocal sum)**. Mixing these up costs 3–7 marks.
> 3. **Unit errors** — Always use **GPa** consistently; convert from MPa before dividing.
> 4. **Forgetting the reciprocity relation** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$ — this is the **first check** the examiner does on your answer. If violated, marks are deducted.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption statements** — For derivations, always **state assumptions explicitly** (perfect bonding, iso-strain/iso-stress, void-free). Examiners allocate 2 marks for this.
> 6. **Sign errors in stress transformation** — Note that $\tau_{12} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y) s c + \tau_{xy}(c^2 - s^2)$. The negative sign and the $2sc$ (not $sc$) in $\sigma_1$ are common traps.
> 7. **Wrong angle convention** — $\theta$ is measured from x-axis to 1-axis (fiber direction). If the problem states "fiber at 30° to load", that means $\theta = 30°$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanics of Composite Materials (Module 3)**

- **Two scales:** Micromechanics (fiber/matrix level) → predicts $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \rho_c$. Macromechanics (lamina/laminate level) → uses these as inputs.
- **Density ROM** is **exact**; moduli ROMs are **approximations** with assumptions.
- **$E_1$ (longitudinal)** = $E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ → **Voigt**, iso-strain, **upper bound**.
- **$E_2$ (transverse)** = $1/(V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m)$ → **Reuss**, iso-stress, **lower bound**.
- **$\nu_{12}$** = $\nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ → Voigt.
- **$G_{12}$** = $1/(V_f/G_f + V_m/G_m)$ → Reuss.
- **Volume rule:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.
- **Reciprocity:** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$ — must always hold.
- **Plane stress assumption** is standard for thin laminae.
- **Reduced stiffness $Q_{ij}$** is the **in-plane** version of 3D $C_{ij}$ (in plane stress).
- **Transformation matrix $T_\sigma, T_\varepsilon$** is the key to off-axis analysis.
- **$Q_{66} = G_{12}$** — always remember.
- **For design:** $E_1/E_2$ ratio of 5–30 is typical for UD composites (very anisotropic!).
- **KTU exam tip:** Always state assumptions; check units; use reciprocities; double-check angle definitions.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Mechanics of Composite Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromechanics and Macro Mechanics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micromechanics and Macro Mechanics

# Module 3 — Micromechanics and Macro Mechanics of Composites

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Micromechanics):** Micromechanics of composites is the study of the mechanical and physical behavior of a composite material by analyzing the **individual constituent phases** (fiber and matrix) and their **interfacial interactions** at a microscopic scale, in order to predict the *effective* or *homogenized* properties of the composite lamina.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Macromechanics):** Macromechanics of composites is the study of the mechanical behavior of a composite **lamina (ply)** or **laminate (stack of plies)** by treating it as a single, homogeneous, *anisotropic* continuum — without explicitly resolving the micro-scale fiber/matrix geometry. The output is the relationship between **average lamina stresses ($\bar{\sigma}$)** and **average lamina strains ($\bar{\epsilon}$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **concrete-rebar slab** (the everyday composite).
- *Micromechanics* is the engineer's microscope view: looking at the tiny steel rebar (**fiber**) embedded in cement paste (**matrix**), asking "how much does each carry of the total load?" and "how stiff is the combined paste-rebar mix?". The rebar carries most tension, the cement paste binds everything and resists compression.
- *Macromechanics* is the engineer's **aerial / blueprint view**: the slab is treated as a single, uniform sheet with directional stiffness (stronger along the rebar, weaker across it). The engineer calculates deflection of the whole slab ignoring the internal rebar.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, students must master (a) Volume/Weight fraction relations, (b) Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal modulus, density, Poisson's ratio), (c) Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse modulus), (d) Halpin–Tsai equation for $G_{12}$, and (e) the $[Q]$ stiffness matrix for a thin lamina along with the $[A], [B], [D]$ matrices of **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**.

### Key Metrics & Standards
- **Volume fraction** of fiber, $V_f$ — typically **0.3 to 0.7** in structural composites.
- **Weight fraction** of fiber, $W_f$ — used in fabrication (prepreg, hand layup).
- **Critical fiber volume fraction** ($V_{f,crit}$) — the minimum $V_f$ above which the composite is **stronger** than the matrix alone.
- **Fiber packing factor** ($\eta_p$) — ratio of fiber area to total cross-sectional area.
- The **0° ply** is the reference orientation; all transformed properties are referenced back to it.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituent Relationships — Volume & Weight Fractions

A composite is a mixture of two (or more) phases. The relative amount of each phase is described using either **volume fraction** or **weight fraction**.

Let:
- $V_f$ = volume fraction of fiber
- $V_m$ = volume fraction of matrix
- $W_f$ = weight fraction of fiber
- $W_m$ = weight fraction of matrix
- $\rho_f$ = density of fiber; $\rho_m$ = density of matrix
- $\rho_c$ = density of composite

The fundamental relations are:
$$V_f + V_m = 1 \quad ; \quad W_f + W_m = 1$$

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f \, V_f}{\rho_c} \quad ; \quad W_m = \frac{\rho_m \, V_m}{\rho_c}$$

The **rule of mixtures for density** is:
$$\rho_c = \rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, V_m$$

### 2.2 Micromechanics — Rule of Mixtures (ROM)

The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** assumes *iso-strain* conditions (both fiber and matrix experience the same strain $\epsilon$ along the loading direction). This is valid for a continuous, aligned, long-fiber composite loaded along the fiber direction.

**Longitudinal Young's Modulus ($E_1$):**
$$E_1 = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, V_m$$

**Major Poisson's Ratio ($\nu_{12}$):**
$$\nu_{12} = \nu_f \, V_f + \nu_m \, V_m$$

**Longitudinal Stress Partition (Load Sharing):**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m \, V_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does ROM work for $E_1$?** Because the fiber and matrix are bonded in parallel along the loading direction, both share the same strain (compatibility). The total load is the sum of individual loads — this is a "springs-in-parallel" analogy.

### 2.3 Micromechanics — Inverse Rule of Mixtures (IROM)

For loading **transverse** to the fibers, the fiber and matrix are arranged *in series* (each carries the same stress but different strains). The result is the **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (IROM)**:

**Transverse Young's Modulus ($E_2$):**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** $E_2$ is *always* much smaller than $E_1$ because the soft matrix dominates the transverse response. This anisotropy is the defining feature of a unidirectional (UD) lamina.

### 2.4 Halpin–Tsai Equation for $G_{12}$

The **Halpin–Tsai** semi-empirical equation bridges the gap between ROM and IROM, giving accurate predictions for the in-plane shear modulus:

$$G_{12} = G_m \, \frac{(G_f/G_m) + \xi}{(G_f/G_m) - \xi} \cdot \text{... correction factor}$$

In the standard KTU form:
$$\eta = \frac{(G_f / G_m) - 1}{(G_f / G_m) + \xi}$$

$$G_{12} = G_m \, \frac{1 + \xi \, \eta \, V_f}{1 - \eta \, V_f}$$

where $\xi$ is a **reinforcement geometry factor**:
- Circular fibers: $\xi = 1$
- Rectangular fibers / ribbon: $\xi = 2 \times (\text{aspect ratio})$
- For $E_2$, the same form applies with $\xi = 2$.

### 2.5 Critical Fiber Volume Fraction

For a fiber-reinforced composite loaded in tension along the fibers, the strength of the composite is:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m^{'} (1 - V_f)$$

where $\sigma_m^{'}$ is the matrix stress at the **fiber failure strain** (not the matrix ultimate strain). For the composite to be stronger than the matrix alone, $V_f$ must exceed:

$$V_{f,crit} = \frac{\sigma_m - \sigma_m^{'}}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m^{'}}$$

### 2.6 Macromechanics — Stress–Strain Relation of a Lamina

A **single thin lamina** is treated as a **homogeneous, orthotropic** material with principal material directions **1** (along fiber) and **2** (transverse). Under plane stress ($\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0$):

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_1 \\ \epsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

The **reduced stiffness matrix** $[Q]$ components are:

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \quad ; \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12}$$

with the **Maxwell reciprocity condition**:
$$\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1}$$

### 2.7 Transformed Reduced Stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$ — Off-Axis Laminas

If the principal material direction **1** is oriented at an angle $\theta$ to the loading **x-axis**, the stiffness matrix must be **transformed** to give $[\bar{Q}]$:

$$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11} \cos^4\theta + 2(Q_{12} + 2 Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{22}\sin^4\theta$$

$$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}\sin^4\theta + 2(Q_{12}+2Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{22}\cos^4\theta$$

$$\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-4Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{12}(\sin^4\theta+\cos^4\theta)$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-2Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{66}(\sin^4\theta+\cos^4\theta)$$

$$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11}-Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin\theta\cos^3\theta + (Q_{12}-Q_{22}+2Q_{66})\sin^3\theta\cos\theta$$

$$\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11}-Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin^3\theta\cos\theta + (Q_{12}-Q_{22}+2Q_{66})\sin\theta\cos^3\theta$$

### 2.8 Classical Lamination Theory (CLT) — $[A], [B], [D]$ Matrices

A laminate is a stack of $N$ plies, each of thickness $t_k$, with the **z-axis** running through the thickness. Let the laminate be bounded between $z = -h/2$ and $z = +h/2$, with ply $k$ spanning $z_{k-1}$ to $z_k$.

**Extensional Stiffness Matrix $[A]$** (relates in-plane forces $N$ to mid-plane strains $\epsilon^0$):
$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k \, (z_k - z_{k-1})$$

**Coupling Stiffness Matrix $[B]$** (relates in-plane forces to laminate curvatures $\kappa$):
$$B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k \, (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

**Bending Stiffness Matrix $[D]$** (relates moments $M$ to curvatures $\kappa$):
$$D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k \, (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$$

**Governing Equation of CLT:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} N_x \\ N_y \\ N_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} & A_{16} \\ A_{12} & A_{22} & A_{26} \\ A_{16} & A_{26} & A_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_x^0 \\ \epsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_x \\ M_y \\ M_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_x^0 \\ \epsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} D_{11} & D_{12} & D_{16} \\ D_{12} & D_{22} & D_{26} \\ D_{16} & D_{26} & D_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

In compact form: $\{N\} = [A]\{\epsilon^0\} + [B]\{\kappa\}$ and $\{M\} = [B]\{\epsilon^0\} + [D]\{\kappa\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $[B]$ is special:** For a **symmetric laminate** (plies mirrored about the mid-plane), $[B] = 0$ → no **bending–stretching coupling**. For a **cross-ply laminate** ($0°/90°$ only), $A_{16} = A_{26} = D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$ → no **shear–extension coupling**.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Composite density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | kg/m³ | Rule of mixtures |
| 2 | Volume ↔ weight | $W_f = \rho_f V_f / \rho_c$ | — | Density-weighted |
| 3 | Longitudinal modulus | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | ROM (iso-strain) |
| 4 | Major Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | — | ROM |
| 5 | Transverse modulus | $1/E_2 = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | GPa | IROM (iso-stress) |
| 6 | Minor Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot E_2 / E_1$ | — | Reciprocity |
| 7 | Halpin–Tsai factor | $\eta = (G_f/G_m - 1)/(G_f/G_m + \xi)$ | — | $\xi$ = geometry |
| 8 | Shear modulus | $G_{12} = G_m(1 + \xi \eta V_f)/(1 - \eta V_f)$ | GPa | Halpin–Tsai |
| 9 | Critical $V_f$ | $V_{f,crit} = (\sigma_m - \sigma_m')/(\sigma_f - \sigma_m')$ | — | Strength criterion |
| 10 | $Q_{11}$ | $E_1/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | GPa | Reduced stiffness |
| 11 | $Q_{22}$ | $E_2/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | GPa | Reduced stiffness |
| 12 | $Q_{12}$ | $\nu_{12} E_2/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | GPa | Coupling term |
| 13 | $Q_{66}$ | $G_{12}$ | GPa | In-plane shear |
| 14 | $[A]$ extensional | $\sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k - z_{k-1})$ | N/m | In-plane stiffness |
| 15 | $[B]$ coupling | $\frac{1}{2} \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ | N | Bend–stretch coupling |
| 16 | $[D]$ bending | $\frac{1}{3} \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$ | N·m | Flexural stiffness |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace wings (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Use ROM-derived $E_1$ to size carbon/epoxy spars. Designers use $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ matrices to predict wing-skin deflection under cabin pressure.
- **Automotive CFRP driveshafts:** Critical $V_f$ analysis ensures the shaft doesn't fail in torsion below the matrix strength.
- **Wind turbine blades (50–80 m):** Macromechanics of $[0°/\pm 45°/90°]$ laminates determines flap-wise and edge-wise bending stiffness.
- **Sports equipment (tennis rackets, F1 monocoque):** Off-axis $[\bar{Q}]$ transformation at $45°$ gives the high in-plane shear stiffness needed for torsional rigidity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures for $E_1$

**Assumption:** Perfect bonding between fiber and matrix; both phases experience the **same axial strain** $\epsilon_1$ (iso-strain).

**Step 1 — Apply the same strain to both phases:**
$$\epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon_1$$

**Step 2 — Stress in each phase via Hooke's Law:**
$$\sigma_f = E_f \, \epsilon_1 \quad ; \quad \sigma_m = E_m \, \epsilon_1$$

**Step 3 — Total load = sum of fiber load + matrix load (parallel action):**
$$P_c = P_f + P_m = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by total area $A_c$:**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Hooke's law and cancel $\epsilon_1$ (since all strains are equal):**
$$\sigma_c = (E_f V_f + E_m V_m) \, \epsilon_1$$

**Step 6 — Identify the slope as $E_1$:**
$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Rule of Mixtures for $E_2$

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix carry the **same transverse stress** $\sigma_2$ (iso-stress, series action).

**Step 1 — Equal stress in both phases:**
$$\sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma_2$$

**Step 2 — Strain in each phase:**
$$\epsilon_f = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f} \quad ; \quad \epsilon_m = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m}$$

**Step 3 — Total elongation of the composite (sum of fiber and matrix elongations in series):**
$$\delta_c = \delta_f + \delta_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \epsilon_2 \, L_c = \epsilon_f L_f + \epsilon_m L_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $L_c$ and substitute length ratios = volume fractions (for uniform cross-section):**
$$\epsilon_2 = \epsilon_f V_f + \epsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the strain expressions:**
$$\frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} = \frac{\sigma_2 V_f}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma_2 V_m}{E_m}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\sigma_2$ and rearrange:**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ Matrices (CLT)

**Step 1 — Displacement field in Classical Lamination Theory (Kirchhoff–Love plate hypothesis):**
$$u(x,y,z) = u^0(x,y) - z \, \frac{\partial w^0}{\partial x}, \quad v(x,y,z) = v^0(x,y) - z \, \frac{\partial w^0}{\partial y}, \quad w(x,y,z) = w^0(x,y)$$

**Step 2 — Strain field by differentiating displacements:**
$$\epsilon_x = \epsilon_x^0 - z \, \kappa_x, \quad \epsilon_y = \epsilon_y^0 - z \, \kappa_y, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}^0 - z \, \kappa_{xy}$$

where $\epsilon^0$ is the mid-plane strain and $\kappa$ is the curvature.

**Step 3 — Constitutive relation for ply $k$:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}^{(k)} = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{Q}_{11} & \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{16} \\ \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{22} & \bar{Q}_{26} \\ \bar{Q}_{16} & \bar{Q}_{26} & \bar{Q}_{66} \end{bmatrix}^{(k)} \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_x^0 - z\kappa_x \\ \epsilon_y^0 - z\kappa_y \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 - z\kappa_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — In-plane force resultants (force per unit length):**
$$N_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_x \, dz = \int \left( \bar{Q}_{11}\epsilon_x^0 + \bar{Q}_{12}\epsilon_y^0 + \bar{Q}_{16}\gamma_{xy}^0 \right) dz - z \int \left( \bar{Q}_{11}\kappa_x + \cdots \right) dz$$

**Step 5 — Recognizing the integrals as sums over plies (since $[\bar{Q}]$ is piecewise constant):**
$$N_i = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{z_{k-1}}^{z_k} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (\epsilon_j^0 - z\kappa_j) \, dz$$

**Step 6 — Separate the integrals:**
$$N_i = \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k - z_{k-1}) \right)}_{A_{ij}} \epsilon_j^0 - \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \frac{z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2}{2} \right)}_{B_{ij}} \kappa_j$$

Thus: $N_i = A_{ij}\epsilon_j^0 - B_{ij}\kappa_j$.

**Step 7 — Moment resultants:**
$$M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_x \, z \, dz = \int z \, \bar{Q}_{ij} (\epsilon_j^0 - z\kappa_j) dz$$

**Step 8 — Expand and identify the integrals:**
$$M_i = B_{ij}\epsilon_j^0 - \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \frac{z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3}{3} \right)}_{D_{ij}} \kappa_j$$

Thus: $M_i = B_{ij}\epsilon_j^0 - D_{ij}\kappa_j$. (The negative sign is absorbed into standard CLT sign convention.) $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A unidirectional glass/epoxy lamina has the following constituent properties:
> $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³, $V_f = 0.55$, $G_f = 28$ GPa, $G_m = 1.3$ GPa. Compute $E_1$, $E_2$, $\nu_{12}$, $\nu_{21}$, $\rho_c$, and $G_{12}$ (use Halpin–Tsai, $\xi = 1$).

**Step 1 — Compute $V_m$:**
$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal modulus (ROM):**
$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = 72(0.55) + 3.5(0.45) = 39.60 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Major Poisson's ratio (ROM):**
$$\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m = 0.22(0.55) + 0.35(0.45) = 0.121 + 0.1575 = 0.2785$$

**Step 4 — Transverse modulus (IROM):**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5} = 0.00764 + 0.12857 = 0.13621 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_2 = 7.342 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 5 — Minor Poisson's ratio:**
$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.2785 \times \frac{7.342}{41.175} = 0.0497$$

**Step 6 — Composite density (ROM):**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = 2560(0.55) + 1200(0.45) = 1408 + 540 = 1948 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Halpin–Tsai for $G_{12}$:**
$$\frac{G_f}{G_m} = \frac{28}{1.3} = 21.538$$
$$\eta = \frac{21.538 - 1}{21.538 + 1} = \frac{20.538}{22.538} = 0.9113$$
$$G_{12} = 1.3 \times \frac{1 + (1)(0.9113)(0.55)}{1 - (0.9113)(0.55)} = 1.3 \times \frac{1 + 0.5012}{1 - 0.5012} = 1.3 \times \frac{1.5012}{0.4988} = 3.913 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $E_1$ | **41.18 GPa** |
| $E_2$ | **7.34 GPa** |
| $\nu_{12}$ | **0.2785** |
| $\nu_{21}$ | **0.0497** |
| $\rho_c$ | **1948 kg/m³** |
| $G_{12}$ | **3.91 GPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $E_1 \gg E_2$ (≈ 5.6×) — the lamina is highly **anisotropic** and stiff primarily along the fibers, exactly as KTU expects for a unidirectional ply.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Micromechanics — Fiber–Matrix Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Unidirectional Composite Lamina] --> B[Fiber Phase: Ef, nu_f, rho_f, Vf]
    A --> C[Matrix Phase: Em, nu_m, rho_m, Vm]
    A --> D[Interface: Bond / Interphase]
    B --> E[Loading Direction 1: ROM - Iso-Strain]
    C --> E
    B --> F[Loading Direction 2: IROM - Iso-Stress]
    C --> F
    E --> G[E1 = Ef Vf + Em Vm]
    F --> H[1/E2 = Vf/Ef + Vm/Em]
    D --> I[Load Transfer via Shear Lag]
    I --> J[Effective Homogenized Lamina Properties]
    G --> J
    H --> J
```

### 4.2 Macro-to-Micro Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Constituent Properties: Ef, Em, Vf, nu_f, nu_m] --> S1[Micromechanics Engine]
    S1 --> S2[Compute E1, E2, G12, nu_12, nu_21, rho_c]
    S2 --> S3[Build Reduced Stiffness Matrix Q]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Ply Angle theta to get Qbar]
    S4 --> S5[Integrate Through Thickness over z]
    S5 --> S6[Assemble A, B, D Matrices]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Loads N and M]
    S7 --> S8[Solve epsilon0 and kappa]
    S8 --> S9[Lamina and Laminate Deflection / Strength]
```

### 4.3 Classical Lamination Theory — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Stack of N Plies] --> L2[Ply 1: theta_1, t_1, Qbar_1]
    L1 --> L3[Ply 2: theta_2, t_2, Qbar_2]
    L1 --> L4[Ply k: theta_k, t_k, Qbar_k]
    L1 --> L5[Ply N: theta_N, t_N, Qbar_N]
    L2 --> A1[Sum: z_k - z_k-1]
    L3 --> A1
    L4 --> A1
    L5 --> A1
    A1 --> M1[A matrix: Extensional Stiffness]
    L2 --> B1[Sum: half times z_k squared - z_k-1 squared]
    L3 --> B1
    L4 --> B1
    L5 --> B1
    B1 --> M2[B matrix: Coupling Stiffness]
    L2 --> D1[Sum: one third times z_k cubed - z_k-1 cubed]
    L3 --> D1
    L4 --> D1
    L5 --> D1
    D1 --> M3[D matrix: Bending Stiffness]
    M1 --> CLT[Governing Equations]
    M2 --> CLT
    M3 --> CLT
    CLT --> OUT[Strains, Curvatures, Deflections]
```

### 4.4 Coordinate System for Off-Axis Lamina

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Global Axes: x, y] --> T1[Rotation by theta]
    T1 --> R2[Material Axes: 1 along fiber, 2 transverse]
    R2 --> R3[sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy applied in global]
    R3 --> R4[Transformed strains epsilon_x, epsilon_y, gamma_xy]
    R4 --> R5[Stress Transformation: T_inv sigma_global]
    R5 --> R6[Apply Q in Material Axes]
    R6 --> R7[Strain Transformation: T epsilon_global]
    R7 --> R8[Qbar governs global response]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reminder:** For $\theta = 0°$, $[\bar{Q}] = [Q]$; for $\theta = 90°$, $\bar{Q}_{11} \leftrightarrow Q_{22}$ and $\bar{Q}_{22} \leftrightarrow Q_{11}$, while all shear-extension terms $Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0$.
