## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the rule of mixtures and the inverse rule of mixtures. State the conditions under which each is valid.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** gives the *longitudinal modulus* $E_1$ of a unidirectional composite assuming **iso-strain** (both fiber and matrix experience the same strain). It is valid when load is applied **parallel to continuous, aligned fibers** with **perfect fiber–matrix bonding**.

$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

The **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (IROM)** gives the *transverse modulus* $E_2$ assuming **iso-stress** (fiber and matrix carry equal stress in series). It is valid for loading **perpendicular** to the fibers.

$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

*(3 marks: definition 1m, formula 1m, conditions 1m)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices in Classical Lamination Theory?**

**Model Answer:**
In Classical Lamination Theory (CLT), a laminate is described by three $3 \times 3$ matrices that relate resultant forces and moments to mid-plane strains and curvatures:
- $[A]$ — **Extensional stiffness matrix** — relates in-plane force resultants $\{N\}$ to mid-plane strains $\{\epsilon^0\}$; units N/m.
- $[B]$ — **Coupling stiffness matrix** — couples in-plane forces to curvatures and moments to mid-plane strains. For a **symmetric laminate** $[B] = 0$, eliminating bend–stretch coupling.
- $[D]$ — **Bending stiffness matrix** — relates moment resultants $\{M\}$ to laminate curvatures $\{\kappa\}$; units N·m.

These matrices enable prediction of laminate deformation under combined mechanical and thermal loads and are the foundation of composite structure design.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** expression for the longitudinal Young's modulus $E_1$ of a unidirectional composite lamina. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass fiber–epoxy lamina has the following properties:
$E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.20$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $G_f = 28$ GPa, $G_m = 1.2$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³, $V_f = 0.50$.

Compute: (i) $E_1$, $ii) $\nu_{12}$, (iii) $E_2$, (iv) $\nu_{21}$, (v) $\rho_c$, (vi) $G_{12}$ using the Halpin–Tsai equation with $\xi = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed.
2. Perfect bonding at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip).
3. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same axial strain** (iso-strain / parallel loading).
4. The composite is initially stress-free.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

*Step 1:* Apply load along the fiber direction (axis-1). Compatibility gives:
$$\epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon_1$$

*Step 2:* By Hooke's law:
$$\sigma_f = E_f \epsilon_1, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \epsilon_1$$

*Step 3:* Total load equilibrium (parallel arrangement):
$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

*Step 4:* Dividing by composite cross-sectional area $A_c = A_f + A_m$:
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

*Step 5:* Substituting Hooke's law and cancelling $\epsilon_1$:
$$\sigma_c = (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)\epsilon_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $V_f = 0.50$, $V_m = 0.50$.

**(i) Longitudinal modulus — ROM:**
$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = 70(0.50) + 3.5(0.50) = 35.0 + 1.75 = \mathbf{36.75 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(ii) Major Poisson's ratio — ROM:**
$$\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m = 0.20(0.50) + 0.35(0.50) = 0.10 + 0.175 = \mathbf{0.275}$$

**(iii) Transverse modulus — IROM:**
$$\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.50}{70} + \frac{0.50}{3.5} = 0.00714 + 0.14286 = 0.15000 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_2 = \mathbf{6.667 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iv) Minor Poisson's ratio:**
$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.275 \times \frac{6.667}{36.75} = 0.275 \times 0.1814 = \mathbf{0.0499}$$

**(v) Composite density — ROM:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = 2500(0.50) + 1200(0.50) = 1250 + 600 = \mathbf{1850 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**(vi) Halpin–Tsai shear modulus:**
$$\frac{G_f}{G_m} = \frac{28}{1.2} = 23.333$$
$$\eta = \frac{23.333 - 1}{23.333 + 1} = \frac{22.333}{24.333} = 0.9178$$
$$G_{12} = 1.2 \times \frac{1 + (1)(0.9178)(0.50)}{1 - (0.9178)(0.50)} = 1.2 \times \frac{1.4589}{0.5411} = 1.2 \times 2.6961 = \mathbf{3.235 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $E_1$ | 36.75 GPa |
| $\nu_{12}$ | 0.275 |
| $E_2$ | 6.667 GPa |
| $\nu_{21}$ | 0.0499 |
| $\rho_c$ | 1850 kg/m³ |
| $G_{12}$ | 3.235 GPa |

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With neat derivations, obtain the expressions for **$[A]$**, **$[B]$**, and **$[D]$** stiffness matrices of Classical Lamination Theory starting from the Kirchhoff–Love plate hypothesis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-ply cross-ply laminate is stacked as $[0°/90°/0°]$ with each ply of thickness $0.2$ mm. The material is T300/5208 carbon/epoxy with the following properties:
$E_1 = 132$ GPa, $E_2 = 10.8$ GPa, $G_{12} = 5.65$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.24$.

Compute the $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices. Show that the laminate is **symmetric and specially orthotropic**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) CLT Derivations (7 marks):**

**Assumption (Kirchhoff–Love plate, 1 mark):** Straight transverse normals remain straight and normal to the mid-plane after deformation; no transverse shear strain; no change in thickness.

**Step 1 — Displacement field (1 mark):**
$$u(x,y,z) = u^0 - z \frac{\partial w^0}{\partial x}, \quad v(x,y,z) = v^0 - z \frac{\partial w^0}{\partial y}, \quad w = w^0$$

**Step 2 — Strain field (1 mark):**
$$\epsilon_x = \epsilon_x^0 - z\kappa_x, \quad \epsilon_y = \epsilon_y^0 - z\kappa_y, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}^0 - z\kappa_{xy}$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive law for ply $k$ (1 mark):** $\{\sigma\} = [\bar{Q}]_k \{\epsilon\}$

**Step 4 — Force/moment resultants and identification of $A, B, D$ (3 marks):**
$$N_i = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_i \, dz = A_{ij} \epsilon_j^0 - B_{ij}\kappa_j$$
$$M_i = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_i \, z \, dz = B_{ij} \epsilon_j^0 - D_{ij}\kappa_j$$

with the explicit expressions:
$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k - z_{k-1})$$
$$B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$
$$D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $Q$ components:**
$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot E_2/E_1 = 0.24 \times 10.8/132 = 0.01964$$
$$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.24 \times 0.01964 = 1 - 0.004713 = 0.99529$$
$$Q_{11} = \frac{132}{0.99529} = 132.62 \text{ GPa}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{10.8}{0.99529} = 10.851 \text{ GPa}$$
$$Q_{12} = \frac{0.24 \times 10.8}{0.99529} = 2.604 \text{ GPa}, \quad Q_{66} = 5.65 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Ply orientations and Q-bar values:**

For $0°$ plies: $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11} = 132.62$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{22} = 10.851$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12} = 2.604$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66} = 5.65$, and $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$.

For $90°$ ply: $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{22} = 10.851$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11} = 132.62$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12} = 2.604$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66} = 5.65$, and $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Layup geometry:**
- Total thickness $h = 3 \times 0.2 = 0.6$ mm.
- Mid-plane at $z = 0$.
- Ply boundaries: $z_0 = -0.3$, $z_1 = -0.1$, $z_2 = 0.1$, $z_3 = 0.3$ mm.
- Ply 1 ($0°$): $-0.3$ to $-0.1$; Ply 2 ($90°$): $-0.1$ to $+0.1$; Ply 3 ($0°$): $+0.1$ to $+0.3$.

**Step 4 — Compute $[A]$ (units N/m = GPa·mm):**

Each ply has $t = 0.2$ mm. So $A_{ij} = 0.2(\bar{Q}_{ij}^{0°} + \bar{Q}_{ij}^{90°} + \bar{Q}_{ij}^{0°}) = 0.2(2\bar{Q}_{ij}^{0°} + \bar{Q}_{ij}^{90°})$.

$$A_{11} = 0.2(2 \times 132.62 + 10.851) = 0.2 \times 276.09 = 55.22 \text{ GPa·mm} = 55.22 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$A_{22} = 0.2(2 \times 10.851 + 132.62) = 0.2 \times 154.32 = 30.86 \text{ GPa·mm}$$

$$A_{12} = 0.2(3 \times 2.604) = 1.562 \text{ GPa·mm}$$

$$A_{66} = 0.2(3 \times 5.65) = 3.390 \text{ GPa·mm}$$

$$A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compute $[B]$:** By symmetry of the $[0°/90°/0°]$ layup about $z = 0$ (mirror of outer plies), the contributions cancel:
$$B_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{(for all } i,j\text{)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [B] = [0]$$

**Step 6 — Compute $[D]$:** $D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3}\sum \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$.

For $0°$ plies:
- Ply 1: $z_1^3 - z_0^3 = (-0.1)^3 - (-0.3)^3 = -0.001 + 0.027 = 0.026$ mm³
- Ply 3: $z_3^3 - z_2^3 = (0.3)^3 - (0.1)^3 = 0.027 - 0.001 = 0.026$ mm³

For $90°$ ply:
- Ply 2: $z_2^3 - z_1^3 = (0.1)^3 - (-0.1)^3 = 0.001 + 0.001 = 0.002$ mm³

So total contribution = $\frac{1}{3}[2 \times 0.026 \cdot \bar{Q}_{ij}^{0°} + 0.002 \cdot \bar{Q}_{ij}^{90°}]$

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3}[0.052 \times 132.62 + 0.002 \times 10.851] = \frac{1}{3}[6.896 + 0.0217] = 2.306 \text{ GPa·mm}^3$$

$$D_{22} = \frac{1}{3}[0.052 \times 10.851 + 0.002 \times 132.62] = \frac{1}{3}[0.564 + 0.265] = 0.276 \text{ GPa·mm}^3$$

$$D_{12} = \frac{1}{3}[0.054 \times 2.604] = 0.0469 \text{ GPa·mm}^3$$

$$D_{66} = \frac{1}{3}[0.054 \times 5.65] = 0.1017 \text{ GPa·mm}^3$$

$$D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Verify symmetric and specially orthotropic:**

$$[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 55.22 & 1.562 & 0 \\ 1.562 & 30.86 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.390 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$[B] = [0]$$

$$[D] = \begin{bmatrix} 2.306 & 0.0469 & 0 \\ 0.0469 & 0.276 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1017 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa·mm}^3$$

Since $[B] = 0$ and $A_{16} = A_{26} = D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$, the laminate is **symmetric** and **specially orthotropic (cross-ply)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing ROM and IROM:** Students often apply the rule of mixtures to transverse modulus $E_2$, producing absurdly high values. Remember: ROM = iso-strain (parallel) = $E_1$; IROM = iso-stress (series) = $E_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the off-diagonal product $\nu_{12}\nu_{21}$** when computing $Q_{11}$ or $Q_{22}$. The denominator $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} \approx 0.995$, not 1.
> 3. **Sign convention in $[B]$:** Some textbooks write $[B]$ with a negative sign; KTU follows the convention $N = A\epsilon^0 - B\kappa$, but the $[B]$ *components* are reported as **positive** for standard symmetric layups.
> 4. **Ply angle $\theta$ for $90°$:** $\sin^2\theta = 1$, $\cos^2\theta = 0$, but $\sin\theta\cos\theta = 0$ → so $Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0$. Don't plug $90°$ into $\sin^4\theta$ and forget the $0 \cdot 1 = 0$ cross terms.
> 5. **Unit conversion:** $[A]$ in GPa·mm = $10^6$ N/m; $[D]$ in GPa·mm³ = $10^3$ N·m. Mixing mm and m without scaling is the #1 source of mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions:** Even when the question doesn't explicitly ask, KTU board examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for stating assumptions in derivations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Micromechanics** = fiber + matrix interaction; **Macromechanics** = ply/laminate as a homogeneous anisotropic continuum.
- **ROM applies to $E_1$, $\nu_{12}$, $\rho_c$, $W_f$ ↔ $V_f$** (iso-strain, parallel).
- **IROM applies to $E_2$** (iso-stress, series).
- **Halpin–Tsai** is the *workhorse* for $G_{12}$ and $E_2$ (more accurate than IROM).
- **Maxwell reciprocity:** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$ — always enforce this to avoid inconsistency.
- **$[Q]$** is for a single ply in material coordinates (1–2).
- **$[\bar{Q}]$** is for the same ply rotated by $\theta$ in global coordinates (x–y).
- **$[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$** are obtained by integrating $[\bar{Q}]$ over the laminate thickness.
- **Symmetric laminate → $[B] = 0$** (no bend–stretch coupling).
- **Cross-ply laminate ($0°/90°$ only) → $A_{16} = A_{26} = D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$** (no shear–extension or shear–bending coupling).
- **Quasi-isotropic laminate** $[0°/\pm 60°]$ or $[0°/\pm 45°/90°]$ gives $A_{11} = A_{22}$, $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ — *in-plane* isotropic.
- **Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis** = the foundation of CLT (no transverse shear, no thickness stretching).
- The $z^3$ in $[D]$ makes **outer plies dominate bending stiffness** — place strong fibers on the outside for flexural performance.
- Volume/weight fractions must always satisfy the **closure relation** ($V_f + V_m = 1$, $W_f + W_m = 1$).
- Always **check units**: GPa ↔ MPa ↔ N/m² ↔ N/mm² — a factor of $10^3$ or $10^6$ error is the single most common numerical mistake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Load Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Load Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 3 | Mechanism of Load Transfer in Composites

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Transfer Mechanism** is the process by which an externally applied mechanical load is transmitted from the relatively weak and compliant **matrix** phase to the high-strength, high-stiffness **reinforcement (fiber)** phase through the **fiber-matrix interface** via interfacial shear stresses.

In any fiber-reinforced composite, the **matrix never carries the peak load directly**. Instead, the matrix acts as a *medium* that distributes the applied stress uniformly to every embedded fiber. The efficiency of this transfer governs the ultimate tensile strength, fracture toughness, and load-bearing capability of the composite.

### Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Interfacial Shear Stress ($\tau_i$)** | The shear stress developed at the fiber-matrix interface, responsible for transmitting the load into the fiber. |
| **Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)** | The minimum fiber length required to build up sufficient stress in the fiber to reach its ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{fu}$) before the interface fails. |
| **Aspect Ratio ($l/d$)** | The ratio of fiber length to fiber diameter; a key geometric parameter controlling load transfer efficiency. |
| **Fiber Efficiency Factor ($\eta_l$)** | The ratio of the average fiber stress ($\bar{\sigma}_f$) to the ultimate fiber stress ($\sigma_{fu}$). |
| **Fiber Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** | The volume of fibers divided by the total composite volume; determines how much load the fibers can collectively bear. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** According to the KTU 2024 PECHT742 syllabus (Module 3), students must clearly understand the **Cox Shear-Lag Model**, the **Kelly-Tyson Theory**, the derivation of **critical fiber length**, and the **fiber efficiency factor** — these are guaranteed high-yield topics for both Continuous Assessment Tests (CAT) and the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: The Tug-of-War Team

Imagine **eight people playing tug-of-war**, but **only the strongest person (the fiber)** is actually tied to the rope. The other seven teammates (the matrix) are simply *holding onto* the strongest person from behind and pushing him forward. As they push, the force is transmitted through their **grip (interface)** to the strong person, who then transmits it to the rope.

- If the **grip is weak** → the strong person slips, the rope never gets the full force → **composite fails prematurely**.
- If the **grip is strong** → the strong person pulls with full strength → **composite achieves peak performance**.

This "grip" is exactly what **interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$)** represents in composites. Even the strongest carbon fiber is useless without adequate load transfer.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Link:** In a **carbon-fiber bicycle frame**, every fiber is held by an epoxy matrix. The frame only feels "light and strong" because load is transferred from the polymer matrix into the carbon fibers via shear at the interface. Poor bonding → catastrophic delamination (e.g., the famous cycling crash frames).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Load Transfer

When a unidirectional composite is loaded in tension along the fiber direction, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Load is applied to the matrix** (because the matrix is the continuous phase that surrounds all fibers).
2. The matrix, being more compliant than the fiber, **deforms first**.
3. Because the fiber and matrix are bonded, the matrix attempts to slip past the fiber.
4. This slip is resisted by the **interfacial bond**, generating a **shear stress $\tau_i$** along the cylindrical fiber surface.
5. The cumulative effect of this shear stress **builds up an axial tensile stress $\sigma_f$** in the fiber, from zero at the fiber ends to a maximum somewhere along the fiber length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The fiber cannot "feel" the load at its tips — it only "feels" load through the shear stress acting on its cylindrical surface. This is the essence of the **shear-lag phenomenon**.

## 2.2 Cox Shear-Lag Theory (1952)

H.L. Cox (1952) proposed the first rigorous mathematical model for load transfer, treating the fiber as a rigid cylinder and the matrix as a deformable continuum. The fundamental assumptions are:

- The fiber-matrix bond is **perfect** (no debonding).
- The matrix carries load only through shear.
- The fiber stress builds up **parabolically** along its length.
- Both ends of the fiber carry zero stress (no end-loading).

The **Cox distribution** for fiber tensile stress is:

$$\sigma_f(x) = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_c \cdot \left[1 - \cosh\left(\beta \cdot \frac{x - l/2}{r}\right) \cdot \text{sech}\left(\beta \cdot \frac{l}{2r}\right)\right]$$

where the **shear-lag parameter** $\beta$ is defined as:

$$\beta = \sqrt{\frac{2 G_m}{E_f \cdot \ln(R/r)}}$$

with $G_m$ = matrix shear modulus, $E_f$ = fiber Young's modulus, $r$ = fiber radius, $R$ = mean inter-fiber spacing, and $l$ = fiber length.

## 2.3 Kelly-Tyson Theory (1965) — The KTU-Standard Model

Kelly and Tyson's model is the **most widely used** in KTU examinations because of its simplicity and engineering utility. It considers an **elementary force balance** on a fiber segment.

### Force Balance on a Fiber Segment of Length $x$ from One End:

If the interfacial shear stress is $\tau_i$ and the fiber diameter is $d$, then the shear force acting on the fiber surface over length $x$ must equal the axial tensile force built up in the fiber.

$$\text{Shear force on cylindrical surface} = \pi \cdot d \cdot x \cdot \tau_i$$

$$\text{Axial tensile force in fiber} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \sigma_f(x)$$

Equating these two forces:

$$\pi \cdot d \cdot x \cdot \tau_i = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \sigma_f(x)$$

Solving for the fiber stress at distance $x$ from the end:

$$\boxed{\sigma_f(x) = \frac{4 \tau_i x}{d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This linear relationship is the cornerstone of short-fiber composite mechanics.** The fiber stress grows **linearly** from zero at the fiber tip, with slope $4\tau_i/d$.

### 2.3.1 Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

Setting $x = l/2$ (midpoint) and requiring the fiber to reach its ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$\sigma_{fu} = \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

Solving for $l_c$:

$$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

The **critical aspect ratio** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}}$$

### 2.3.2 Fiber Efficiency Factor ($\eta_l$)

The fiber efficiency is the ratio of the average fiber stress along its length to the ultimate fiber stress.

**Case 1: Short fibers, $l \leq l_c$** (stress never reaches $\sigma_{fu}$)

The fiber stress profile is triangular from both ends, peaking at the center:

$$\sigma_f(x)_{max} = \frac{2 \tau_i l}{d} < \sigma_{fu}$$

Average fiber stress:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{l} \int_0^l \sigma_f(x)\, dx = \frac{\tau_i l}{d}$$

Fiber efficiency:

$$\eta_l = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_{fu}} = \frac{\tau_i l}{d \cdot \sigma_{fu}} = \frac{l}{2 l_c}$$

**Case 2: Long fibers, $l > l_c$** (stress plateaus at $\sigma_{fu}$ in the central region)

Two end regions of length $l_c/2$ each have a triangular stress profile; the central region $(l - l_c)$ carries the full $\sigma_{fu}$.

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For $l \geq 10 \, l_c$, the fiber efficiency $\eta_l \geq 0.95$, meaning the fiber is loaded to within 95% of its ultimate strength — this is the design criterion for high-performance composites.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Load Transfer Concept |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Carbon/epoxy wing skins — fibers must be long enough to develop full strength under aerodynamic loads. |
| **Automotive** | Glass-fiber/polyester body panels — short fibers require $l \geq 10 l_c$ to avoid pull-out failures. |
| **Civil Engineering** | Steel-reinforced concrete — rebar must have adequate embedded length (analogous to $l_c$) to develop full yield strength. |
| **Sports Equipment** | Carbon-fiber tennis rackets — interface treatment maximizes $\tau_i$, enabling thinner, lighter designs. |
| **Biomedical** | Hydroxyapatite/PLGA bone scaffolds — fiber-matrix bonding determines load-bearing capacity of implants. |

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_f(x) = \dfrac{4 \tau_i x}{d}$ | Fiber stress at distance $x$ from end | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length | m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{l_c}{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical aspect ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_l = \dfrac{l}{2 l_c}$ | Fiber efficiency for $l \leq l_c$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2 l}$ | Fiber efficiency for $l > l_c$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{\tau_i l}{d}$ | Average fiber stress ($l \leq l_c$) | Pa |
| 7 | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu}\left(1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}\right)$ | Average fiber stress ($l > l_c$) | Pa |
| 8 | $\tau_i = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 l}$ | Required interfacial shear stress | Pa |
| 9 | $\sigma_{cu} = \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$ | Composite strength (modified rule of mixtures) | Pa |
| 10 | $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G_m}{E_f \cdot \ln(R/r)}}$ | Cox shear-lag parameter | dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Critical Fiber Length (Kelly-Tyson Model)

**Starting Point:** A short cylindrical fiber of diameter $d$ and length $l$ is embedded in a matrix and loaded in tension along its axis. We wish to find the **minimum length** $l_c$ at which the fiber can be stressed to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ before interfacial failure.

### Step 1: Set up the Free Body Diagram

Consider a small segment of the fiber of length $dx$ located at distance $x$ from the fiber tip. The forces acting on this segment are:

- A tensile force on the right face: $F_R = \sigma_f(x+dx) \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$
- A tensile force on the left face: $F_L = \sigma_f(x) \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$
- A shear force on the cylindrical surface (acting tangentially to transfer load into the fiber): $F_S = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx$

### Step 2: Apply Force Equilibrium

The net tensile force in the fiber must equal the shear force transferred from the matrix:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_R - F_L &= F_S \\
\sigma_f(x+dx) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} - \sigma_f(x) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} &= \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Convert to a Differential Equation

Factoring out $\pi d^2 / 4$ and using the definition of the derivative:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \left[\sigma_f(x+dx) - \sigma_f(x)\right] &= \pi d \cdot \tau_i \cdot dx \\
\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot d\sigma_f &= \pi d \cdot \tau_i \cdot dx \\
d\sigma_f &= \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} dx
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Integrate from the Fiber End to the Midpoint

Boundary condition: at $x = 0$, $\sigma_f = 0$ (the fiber tip cannot support axial load). At the midpoint $x = l/2$, the fiber stress reaches its maximum value $\sigma_{f,max}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\sigma_{f,max}} d\sigma_f &= \int_0^{l/2} \frac{4 \tau_i}{d}\, dx \\
\sigma_{f,max} &= \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} \cdot \frac{l}{2} \\
\sigma_{f,max} &= \frac{2 \tau_i l}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** The factor of 2 in the denominator arises because load is built up from **both ends** of the fiber simultaneously. The total "load-collecting length" of the fiber is therefore effectively $l/2$ from each end.

### Step 5: Apply the Critical Condition

For the fiber to develop its **full ultimate strength** $\sigma_{fu}$, we set $\sigma_{f,max} = \sigma_{fu}$ and $l = l_c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{fu} &= \frac{2 \tau_i l_c}{d} \\
l_c \cdot \sigma_{fu} &= \frac{2 \tau_i l_c^2}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let's solve directly:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{fu} &= \frac{2 \tau_i l_c}{d} \\
\Rightarrow l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ Final Result:

$$\boxed{\,l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}\,}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The critical fiber length is **directly proportional** to the fiber's ultimate strength and diameter, and **inversely proportional** to the interfacial shear strength. Stronger fibers or weaker interfaces → longer critical length.

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Fiber Efficiency Factor (Long-Fiber Case, $l > l_c$)

**Starting Point:** For fibers longer than the critical length, the central region of length $(l - l_c)$ carries the maximum stress $\sigma_{fu}$, while two end-regions of length $l_c/2$ each have triangular stress profiles.

### Step 1: Decompose the Fiber into Three Regions

- **Left end region** ($0 \leq x \leq l_c/2$): Stress rises linearly from 0 to $\sigma_{fu}$
- **Central region** ($l_c/2 \leq x \leq l - l_c/2$): Stress constant at $\sigma_{fu}$
- **Right end region** ($l - l_c/2 \leq x \leq l$): Stress falls linearly from $\sigma_{fu}$ to 0

### Step 2: Compute the Average Fiber Stress

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \frac{1}{l} \left[ \int_0^{l_c/2} \frac{2 \sigma_{fu} x}{l_c/2} \, dx + \int_{l_c/2}^{l - l_c/2} \sigma_{fu} \, dx + \int_{l - l_c/2}^{l} \frac{2 \sigma_{fu} (l - x)}{l_c/2} \, dx \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Each Integral

**Integral 1** (left triangular region):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \int_0^{l_c/2} \frac{2 \sigma_{fu} x}{l_c/2}\, dx = \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l_c} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}\Big|_0^{l_c/2} \\
&= \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l_c} \cdot \frac{(l_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l_c} \cdot \frac{l_c^2}{8} = \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Integral 2** (constant central region):

$$I_2 = \sigma_{fu} \cdot (l - l_c)$$

**Integral 3** (right triangular region — by symmetry, identical to $I_1$):

$$I_3 = \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}$$

### Step 4: Sum the Integrals

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \frac{1}{l}\left[\frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2} + \sigma_{fu}(l - l_c) + \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}\right] \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{l}\left[l_c + l - l_c\right] \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{l}\left[l - l_c + l_c\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Reorganizing:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{l} \left[ l - \frac{l_c}{2} \cdot 2 + l_c \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Let me redo this more carefully by combining the triangular areas:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 + I_3 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2} = \sigma_{fu} l_c \\
\text{Total numerator} &= \sigma_{fu} l_c + \sigma_{fu}(l - l_c) = \sigma_{fu} l_c + \sigma_{fu} l - \sigma_{fu} l_c = \sigma_{fu} l
\end{aligned}$$

So $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} l}{l} = \sigma_{fu}$? That can't be right for $l$ only marginally greater than $l_c$. Let me recheck.

Actually, the issue is that **each triangular region has a peak of $\sigma_{fu}$ and base of $l_c/2$**, so the area of one triangle is $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{l_c}{2} \cdot \sigma_{fu} = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{4}$. The **two triangles combined** have area $\dfrac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}$.

Re-evaluating:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \int_0^{l_c/2} \sigma_f(x)\, dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{l_c}{2} \cdot \sigma_{fu} = \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{4} \\
I_2 &= \sigma_{fu} \cdot (l - l_c) \\
I_3 &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total area} &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{4} + \sigma_{fu}(l - l_c) + \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{4} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2} + \sigma_{fu} l - \sigma_{fu} l_c \\
&= \sigma_{fu} l - \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{l}\left[\sigma_{fu} l - \frac{\sigma_{fu} l_c}{2}\right] = \sigma_{fu}\left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right)$$

### ✅ Final Result:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_l = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_{fu}} = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\,}$$

> **Verification:** If $l = l_c$, then $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$ (50% efficiency), consistent with a single triangle. If $l \to \infty$, then $\eta_l \to 1$ (100% efficiency). ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has fibers of diameter $d = 10\,\mu m$ and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu} = 3.5\,GPa$. The fiber-matrix interfacial shear strength is $\tau_i = 50\,MPa$. Calculate:
1. The critical fiber length $l_c$
2. The fiber efficiency factor if the actual fiber length is $l = 2\,mm$
3. The average fiber stress in this case

**Solution:**

### Part 1: Critical Fiber Length

$$\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} \\
&= \frac{(3.5 \times 10^9\,Pa) \times (10 \times 10^{-6}\,m)}{2 \times (50 \times 10^6\,Pa)} \\
&= \frac{3.5 \times 10^4}{1.0 \times 10^8} \\
&= 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\,m = 0.35\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

### Part 2: Fiber Efficiency Factor

Since $l = 2\,mm > l_c = 0.35\,mm$, we use the long-fiber formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_l &= 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.35}{2 \times 2} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.35}{4} \\
&= 1 - 0.0875 \\
&= 0.9125 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 91.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Part 3: Average Fiber Stress

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} \\
&= 0.9125 \times 3.5\,GPa \\
&= 3.194\,GPa
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** Using the design rule of thumb $l \geq 10 l_c = 3.5\,mm$ would give $\eta_l \geq 0.95$. Our $l = 2\,mm$ gives $\eta_l \approx 0.91$, so the design is *good but not optimal*. This is a typical KTU-style calculation worth 7 marks.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Load Transfer Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of how an applied external load is transferred through the matrix, into the interface, and finally into the fiber:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Tensile Load Applied] --> B[Load Enters Matrix Phase]
    B --> C[Matrix Deforms Elastically]
    C --> D[Matrix Attempts to Slip Past Fiber]
    D --> E{Is Interfacial Bond Intact?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Shear Stress tau_i Generated on Fiber Surface]
    E -- No --> G[Debonding - Fiber Pulls Out]
    F --> H[Axial Tensile Stress sigma_f Builds Up in Fiber]
    H --> I[Fiber Reaches sigma_fu at Midpoint]
    I --> J[Composite Achieves Full Strength]
    G --> K[Composite Fails Prematurely]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> **Color Code:** 🔵 Blue = matrix/load input stages | 🔴 Red = interfacial mechanics | 🟢 Green = successful outcome | ⚪ Grey = failure mode.

## 4.2 Fiber Stress Distribution — Engineering Schematic

The diagram below shows the **distribution of tensile stress along a short fiber** at three different scenarios, which is the central picture every KTU examiner expects students to draw:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fiber Tip x=0 sigma=0] --> B[Linear Build-Up Region]
    B --> C{Is l Greater than l_c?}
    C -- No l leq l_c --> D[Peak at Midpoint sigma_max less than sigma_fu]
    C -- Yes l greater than l_c --> E[Constant Plateau at sigma_fu]
    E --> F[Linear Drop Region]
    F --> G[Fiber Tip x=l sigma=0]
    style A fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Short vs. Long Fiber Stress Profiles

| Geometry | Stress Profile Shape | Peak Stress | Fiber Efficiency |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **$l \ll l_c$** | Small triangle | $\ll \sigma_{fu}$ | $\eta_l \to 0$ |
| **$l = l_c$** | Single triangle across full length | $\sigma_{fu}$ at midpoint only | $\eta_l = 0.5$ |
| **$l = 2 l_c$** | Triangle + plateau + triangle | $\sigma_{fu}$ over half length | $\eta_l = 0.75$ |
| **$l = 10 l_c$** | Plateau dominates, triangles negligible | $\sigma_{fu}$ over most length | $\eta_l = 0.95$ |
| **$l \to \infty$** | Pure plateau | $\sigma_{fu}$ everywhere | $\eta_l \to 1.0$ |

## 4.4 Force Balance Schematic (Elementary Cell)

The elementary force balance used in the Kelly-Tyson derivation can be visualized as:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Force in Fiber: pi*d^2/4 * sigma_f x] --> B[Fiber Cylindrical Segment of Length dx]
    B --> C[Shear Stress tau_i on Cylindrical Surface]
    C --> D[Shear Force: pi*d*dx*tau_i]
    D --> E[Equilibrium: Axial Force Difference equals Shear Force]
    E --> F[Result: sigma_f x equals 4*tau_i*x / d]
    style A fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#00b050,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "critical fiber length" in a fiber-reinforced composite. State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Critical fiber length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length of a fiber required to develop its ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{fu}$) when embedded in a matrix, assuming the load is transferred solely through interfacial shear.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Fibers shorter than $l_c$ will **pull out** of the matrix before they can fracture, resulting in suboptimal composite strength.
> - Fibers longer than $l_c$ (typically $l \geq 10 l_c$) will **fracture** rather than pull out, ensuring maximum load-bearing efficiency.
> - It governs the design choice between **short-fiber** and **continuous-fiber** composites, with major implications for manufacturing cost and performance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between the Cox shear-lag model and the Kelly-Tyson model for load transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cox Shear-Lag Model (1952) | Kelly-Tyson Model (1965) |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Stress distribution** | Parabolic | Linear |
| **Mathematical complexity** | Higher (uses hyperbolic functions) | Simple (linear force balance) |
| **Key parameter** | $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2G_m}{E_f \ln(R/r)}}$ | $\tau_i$ (interfacial shear stress) |
| **Assumption about interface** | Perfectly bonded, no end loading | Perfectly bonded, shear constant at $\tau_i$ |
| **Practical use** | Detailed stress analysis | Engineering design calculations |

> **Conclusion:** The Cox model is more rigorous but mathematically heavier; the Kelly-Tyson model is the **KTU-preferred** approach for derivations and numerical problems due to its clarity and direct engineering utility.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **A glass-fiber reinforced nylon composite has the following properties: fiber diameter $d = 15\,\mu m$, fiber ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu} = 4.0\,GPa$, interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 60\,MPa$, and actual fiber length $l = 1.5\,mm$.**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical fiber length** in a fiber-reinforced composite. Calculate $l_c$ for the given data. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **fiber efficiency factor** and the **average fiber stress** in the composite. Comment on whether the fiber length is adequate for full strength development. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q-A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation and Critical Length

**Derivation:**

Consider a short fiber of diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix. The load is transferred from the matrix to the fiber through interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$.

Taking an element of the fiber of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the fiber tip and applying **force equilibrium**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Shear force on fiber surface} &= \text{Axial force difference in fiber} \\
\pi d \cdot \tau_i \cdot dx &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot d\sigma_f
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$d\sigma_f = \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $\sigma_f = 0$) to $x = l/2$ (where $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,max}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\sigma_{f,max}} d\sigma_f &= \int_0^{l/2} \frac{4 \tau_i}{d}\, dx \\
\sigma_{f,max} &= \frac{2 \tau_i l}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

For the fiber to develop its full ultimate strength, set $\sigma_{f,max} = \sigma_{fu}$ and $l = l_c$:

$$\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2 \tau_i l_c}{d} \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{(4.0 \times 10^9\,Pa)(15 \times 10^{-6}\,m)}{2 \times (60 \times 10^6\,Pa)} \\
&= \frac{6.0 \times 10^4}{1.2 \times 10^8} \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\,m = 0.5\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — Efficiency and Average Stress

Since $l = 1.5\,mm > l_c = 0.5\,mm$, the fiber is in the **long-fiber regime**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_l &= 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{0.5}{2 \times 1.5} = 1 - \frac{0.5}{3.0} = 1 - 0.1667 = 0.8333
\end{aligned}$$

$$\eta_l = 83.33\%$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} = 0.8333 \times 4.0\,GPa = 3.333\,GPa
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Adequacy:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **optimum strength development**, the design rule is $l \geq 10 l_c = 5.0\,mm$. Our fiber has $l = 1.5\,mm$, which is only **3 times** $l_c$, giving $\eta_l = 83.3\%$. The design is **acceptable but suboptimal**; the fibers will develop ~83% of their ultimate strength. For aerospace-grade composites, longer fibers or improved interfacial bonding (higher $\tau_i$) would be required.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **(a)** With the help of neat **stress distribution diagrams**, explain the mechanism of load transfer from the matrix to the fiber in a short-fiber composite. Discuss the cases of (i) $l \leq l_c$ and (ii) $l > l_c$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fiber/epoxy composite contains fibers of diameter $d = 7\,\mu m$, length $l = 3\,mm$, and fiber ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu} = 4.5\,GPa$. If the interfacial shear strength is $\tau_i = 75\,MPa$, determine: (i) the critical fiber length, (ii) the fiber efficiency factor, and (iii) the average stress in the fiber. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q-B:

#### Part (a) — Mechanism Explanation

**Mechanism of Load Transfer:**

In a fiber-reinforced composite, the **matrix is the continuous phase** and the **fibers are the discontinuous reinforcement**. When a tensile load is applied:

1. The load is first picked up by the **matrix** because it surrounds the fibers.
2. The matrix, being more compliant, **deforms more** than the fibers.
3. The differential deformation generates a **shear stress $\tau_i$** at the fiber-matrix interface.
4. This shear stress, acting on the fiber's **cylindrical surface**, builds up an **axial tensile stress $\sigma_f$** in the fiber.
5. At the **fiber tips**, $\sigma_f = 0$ (no cross-section to support axial load).
6. $\sigma_f$ increases with distance from the tip, reaching a maximum at the midpoint.

**Stress Profile Diagrams (Textual Representation):**

> **Case (i): $l \leq l_c$** — *Single triangular profile*
>
> ```
> σ_f
>  ↑        /\
>  |       /  \
>  |      /    \
>  |     /      \
>  |____/________\______
>  0   l/2        l   x
> ```
>
> - Fiber stress peaks at the **center** with maximum value $\sigma_{f,max} = \dfrac{2\tau_i l}{d} < \sigma_{fu}$.
> - The fiber **never reaches** its ultimate strength.
> - Failure occurs by **fiber pull-out**.

> **Case (ii): $l > l_c$** — *Trapezoidal profile*
>
> ```
> σ_f
>  ↑      ____________
>  |     /            \
>  |    /              \
>  |   /                \
>  |__/__________________\____
>  0  l_c/2     l-l_c    l   x
> ```
>
> - Fiber stress rises linearly over $l_c/2$ from each tip, reaching $\sigma_{fu}$.
> - Stress remains at $\sigma_{fu}$ in the central region of length $(l - l_c)$.
> - Failure occurs by **fiber fracture** at $\sigma_{fu}$.
> - The fiber efficiency $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Critical Fiber Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(4.5 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (75 \times 10^6)} \\
&= \frac{3.15 \times 10^4}{1.5 \times 10^8} \\
&= 2.1 \times 10^{-4}\,m = 0.21\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Fiber Efficiency Factor:**

Since $l = 3\,mm \gg l_c = 0.21\,mm$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_l &= 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{0.21}{2 \times 3} = 1 - \frac{0.21}{6} = 1 - 0.035 = 0.965
\end{aligned}$$

$$\eta_l = 96.5\%$$

**(iii) Average Fiber Stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}_f &= \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} = 0.965 \times 4.5\,GPa = 4.343\,GPa
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Since $l = 3\,mm \approx 14.3 \, l_c$, the fiber is **highly efficient** ($\eta_l > 95\%$), confirming excellent load transfer and near-full utilization of fiber strength.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls
>
> Students frequently **lose marks** on the following errors. Read carefully to avoid them:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The most common mistake is writing $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{\tau_i}$ instead of $\dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 \tau_i}$. Remember: load is built up from **both ends**, so the effective load-collecting length is $l/2$ from each end. **(−2 marks typical penalty)**
>
> 2. **Confusing the short and long fiber formulas:** Students sometimes use $\eta_l = \dfrac{l}{2 l_c}$ when $l > l_c$, which is **incorrect**. Always check the case first: if $l \leq l_c$ → triangular profile → use $\eta_l = l/(2 l_c)$; if $l > l_c$ → trapezoidal profile → use $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu m$ with $mm$ or $GPa$ with $MPa$ without conversion is a guaranteed deduction. Always convert to **SI base units** ($m$, $Pa$) before substituting. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Missing the derivation step:** For 7-mark questions, the **derivation is mandatory**. Writing only the final formula $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / 2 \tau_i$ without showing the force balance and integration will fetch only 2-3 marks at most.
>
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams:** In 14-mark questions, the examiner expects at least **one labeled stress-distribution diagram** showing the triangular vs. trapezoidal profile. Skipping the diagram costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 6. **Incorrect identification of long vs. short fiber:** Always compute $l_c$ first, compare it to the given $l$, and **explicitly state** which regime applies before plugging into a formula.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Load transfer** in composites occurs via **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ acting on the fiber's cylindrical surface.
- The **fiber stress grows linearly** from zero at the tip: $\sigma_f(x) = \dfrac{4 \tau_i x}{d}$.
- **Critical fiber length** $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 \tau_i}$ is the minimum length for the fiber to develop its ultimate strength.
- **Short fiber regime ($l \leq l_c$):** $\eta_l = \dfrac{l}{2 l_c}$, failure by **pull-out**.
- **Long fiber regime ($l > l_c$):** $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$, failure by **fracture**.
- **Design rule:** $l \geq 10 \, l_c$ → $\eta_l \geq 0.95$ → near-full strength utilization.
- **Cox model** gives a parabolic stress distribution; **Kelly-Tyson** gives a linear distribution and is the KTU standard.
- The **fiber efficiency factor** is a measure of how well the load is transferred — higher means better.
- **Stronger interfaces** (higher $\tau_i$) reduce $l_c$, allowing shorter fibers to be used.
- **Smaller fiber diameters** reduce $l_c$ for the same strength, but very thin fibers are difficult to handle and align.
- The **rule of mixtures** for composite strength is modified as $\sigma_{cu} = \eta_l \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ to account for incomplete fiber loading.
- For KTU ESE, **always show the force balance derivation** and **draw the stress profile diagram** — both are mandatory for full marks.
- **Cox shear-lag parameter:** $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G_m}{E_f \ln(R/r)}}$ — appears in advanced analytical questions.
- **Industrial design choice:** Continuous fibers (l → ∞) are used in aerospace; short fibers (l ≈ 10 l_c) are used in automotive and consumer goods for cost-effective mass production.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum and Critical Fibre Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a fibre-reinforced composite, the strength and load-bearing capability depend strongly on **how much fibre** is present relative to the matrix. Two threshold fibre volume fractions govern the design of any structural composite laminate:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_{f,crit}$):**
> The *minimum volume fraction of fibre* required for the composite to possess a strength **at least equal to** that of the unreinforced matrix. Below $V_{f,crit}$, the composite is **weaker** than the matrix itself, and the addition of fibre is counter-productive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_{f,min}$):**
> The *lower theoretical bound* of fibre content below which the **fibre cannot carry more load than the displaced matrix** in that volume. It represents the absolute floor of fibre volume fraction needed for any reinforcement effect to be observed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terms $V_{f,crit}$ and $V_{f,min}$ are sometimes used interchangeably in simplified KTU 2024 treatment, but the strict mechanical interpretation is:
> - $V_{f,min}$ → the fibre shares load equally with the displaced matrix
> - $V_{f,crit}$ → the composite strength matches the matrix strength

The composite fails by the **mode** (fibre or matrix) that reaches its ultimate strain first, and the corresponding volume fraction is the **transition point** of failure mode.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rebar in Concrete" Picture

Imagine a brittle chalk stick (matrix) being reinforced with steel wires (fibre). If you embed **just one** thin wire in the chalk, the chalk will still snap at almost the same low force as before — the wire is too few to share the load meaningfully. As you keep adding more wires, at some critical number of wires, the chalk+wire bundle becomes *stronger* than the bare chalk. That threshold number of wires (per unit cross-section) is exactly the **critical fibre content $V_{f,crit}$**.

Drop the wire count below this threshold, and the bundle becomes **weaker** than the bare chalk — because the wires introduce stress concentrations and tiny flaws at the interface, even though they should theoretically be strong. The matrix never gets a chance to develop its full strength because the brittle fibres snap first.

The minimum fibre content is the **bare minimum** beyond which the fibres at least carry as much load as the matrix volume they have replaced — beyond this, every additional fibre you put in **actively reinforces** the composite.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (Bold Constants Used in Formulas)

- $V_f$ — **Fibre volume fraction** (dimensionless, $0 \le V_f \le 1$)
- $V_m = 1 - V_f$ — **Matrix volume fraction**
- $\sigma_{fu}$ — **Ultimate tensile strength of the fibre (MPa)**
- $\sigma_{mu}$ — **Ultimate tensile strength of the matrix (MPa)**
- $E_f$ — **Young's modulus of fibre (GPa)**
- $E_m$ — **Young's modulus of matrix (GPa)**
- $\varepsilon_{fu}$ — **Failure strain of fibre**
- $\varepsilon_{mu}$ — **Failure strain of matrix**
- $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ — **Fibre stress at matrix failure strain** $= E_f \varepsilon_{mu}$
- $\sigma_m^{\prime}$ — **Matrix stress at fibre failure strain** $= E_m \varepsilon_{fu}$

## 1.4 Why $V_{f,crit}$ and $V_{f,min}$ Are Critical for KTU

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, this topic directly maps to:
- **CO3** (Module 3): *Apply micromechanics principles to determine the strength and stiffness bounds of fibre-reinforced composites.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyse (Level 3 & 4)
- **Mark Weightage:** 3 to 14 marks in End Semester Examinations

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Failure Mode First, Strength Formula Next

The fibre and matrix have different failure strains. Whichever component reaches its failure strain first dictates the **mode of failure** of the composite. This is the master key to all strength equations:

### Case A: Brittle Fibre + Ductile Matrix (Most Common)
$$\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$$

The brittle fibre snaps first. The composite fails at $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{fu}$. At this instant, the matrix has only reached a stress $\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu}$, far below its ultimate $\sigma_{mu}$. The composite strength is:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} \, V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} \, (1 - V_f)$$

### Case B: Ductile Fibre + Brittle Matrix
$$\varepsilon_{mu} < \varepsilon_{fu}$$

The matrix cracks first. The composite fails at $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{mu}$. At this instant, the ductile fibre has only reached $\sigma_f^{\prime} = E_f \, \varepsilon_{mu}$, below its ultimate. The composite strength is:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, V_f + \sigma_{mu} \, (1 - V_f)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for Strength** — derived from the same isostrain assumption as the modulus rule, but applied at the *failure* strain rather than the elastic region.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-up

1. **Isostrain Assumption** (valid for continuous, aligned fibres in longitudinal loading): both fibre and matrix experience the *same* strain at any load.
2. **Stress partition:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$.
3. **Identify the failure-triggering component** by comparing $\varepsilon_{fu}$ and $\varepsilon_{mu}$.
4. **Compute the matrix (or fibre) stress at the failure strain** using Hooke's law in the elastic range.
5. **Apply the ROM at the failure strain** to obtain $\sigma_{cu}$ as a linear function of $V_f$.
6. **Set the boundary condition** $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu}$ to find $V_{f,crit}$.
7. **Set the equality of fibre load and displaced matrix load** to find $V_{f,min}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Matrix stress at fibre failure | $\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu}$ | Elastic regime, **MPa** |
| 2 | Fibre stress at matrix failure | $\sigma_f^{\prime} = E_f \, \varepsilon_{mu}$ | Elastic regime, **MPa** |
| 3 | Composite strength (fibre-fails-first) | $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f)$ | $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$ |
| 4 | Composite strength (matrix-fails-first) | $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f^{\prime} V_f + \sigma_{mu} (1 - V_f)$ | $\varepsilon_{mu} < \varepsilon_{fu}$ |
| 5 | **Critical fibre volume fraction** (Case A) | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}$ | Brittle fibre, **dimensionless** |
| 6 | **Critical fibre volume fraction** (Case B) | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_f^{\prime} - \sigma_{mu}}$ | Brittle matrix, **dimensionless** |
| 7 | **Minimum fibre volume fraction** | $V_{f,min} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu} + \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}$ | Conservative lower bound |
| 8 | Lower-bound (equal load) | $V_{f,min} = \dfrac{\sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} + \sigma_m^{\prime}}$ | Fibres carry exactly the displaced matrix load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All $\sigma$ values must be in the **same unit system** (MPa or GPa) and all $E$ values in **the corresponding unit** to avoid dimensional inconsistency in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace structural panels**: Carbon-fibre/epoxy laminates are designed with $V_f \approx 0.55$–$0.65$, well above $V_{f,crit} \approx 0.10$–$0.20$ for typical carbon/epoxy systems.
- **Wind turbine blades**: Glass-fibre/polyester with $V_f \approx 0.40$ to ensure composite strength is twice the matrix strength.
- **Automotive brake pads**: Short-fibre composites use sub-critical $V_f$ because stiffness and wear resistance — not ultimate strength — are the design drivers.
- **Civil FRP rebars**: Must be sized so that $V_{f,crit}$ is well exceeded under the expected service stress to prevent brittle catastrophic failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Critical Fibre Volume Fraction (Case A: $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$)

### Step 1 — Start with the Rule of Mixtures (Strength form)
At any common strain $\varepsilon$ within the elastic range, the stresses in fibre and matrix are $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon$ and $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$. The composite stress under isostrain is:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$$

This is the standard **rule of mixtures** derived from force equilibrium on a representative volume element with parallel, continuous, aligned fibres.

### Step 2 — Identify the failure-triggering component
For brittle fibre / ductile matrix:

$$\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$$

So the **fibre reaches its ultimate strain first**, and the composite fails at $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{fu}$.

### Step 3 — Determine matrix stress at composite failure
At $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{fu}$, the matrix is still in its elastic range (or at least has not yet reached its ultimate strain). By Hooke's law:

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu}$$

This is the stress the matrix is *carrying* at the instant the fibre snaps. It is generally much less than $\sigma_{mu}$.

### Step 4 — Composite strength at failure
Substituting $\sigma_f = \sigma_{fu}$ and $\sigma_m = \sigma_m^{\prime}$ at $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{fu}$:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the critical condition
At the critical fibre volume fraction, the composite is **just as strong as the matrix alone**:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,crit} + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_{f,crit}) = \sigma_{mu}$$

### Step 6 — Algebraic expansion

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,crit} + \sigma_m^{\prime} - \sigma_m^{\prime} V_{f,crit} = \sigma_{mu}$$

Grouping the $V_{f,crit}$ terms on the left:

$$V_{f,crit} \left( \sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime} \right) = \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}$$

### Step 7 — Isolate $V_{f,crit}$

$$\boxed{\,V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** If $\sigma_{mu} \le \sigma_m^{\prime}$, then $V_{f,crit} \le 0$, meaning the matrix alone is already weaker than the matrix at the fibre failure strain. This is impossible physically — it would mean the matrix strain-hardens so steeply that it is stronger at $\varepsilon_{fu}$ than its own ultimate. In all realistic systems $\sigma_{mu} > \sigma_m^{\prime}$ and the result is positive.

## 3.2 Derivation of $V_{f,min}$ — The Equal-Load Lower Bound

The minimum fibre content is the volume fraction at which the **fibre carries exactly the same load as the volume of matrix it has displaced**, at the composite failure strain.

### Step 1 — Set the load balance
At $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{fu}$, the load in the fibre portion of unit cross-section is $\sigma_{fu} V_{f,min}$. The load the displaced matrix would have carried (had it been allowed to reach $\varepsilon_{mu}$) is $\sigma_{mu} V_{f,min}$.

For the fibres to be **carrying no less** than the displaced matrix would have:

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,min} = \sigma_{mu} V_{f,min}$$

This simplifies trivially to $\sigma_{fu} = \sigma_{mu}$ and is independent of $V_f$ — clearly not the intended condition.

### Step 2 — Correct formulation (using the *matrix stress at composite failure*)
A physically meaningful $V_{f,min}$ compares fibre load to the **actual matrix load that the displaced volume would have carried** at the composite failure strain $\varepsilon_{fu}$. The matrix stress at this strain is $\sigma_m^{\prime}$. Therefore:

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,min} = \sigma_m^{\prime} V_{f,min}$$

Again degenerate. So $V_{f,min}$ is actually defined by a *net positive reinforcement* condition: the **increment** in composite strength over the matrix must be attributable entirely to the fibre.

### Step 3 — Net-reinforcement condition
The composite strength exceeds the unreinforced matrix strength by:

$$\sigma_{cu} - \sigma_{mu} = \left( \sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime} \right) V_f - \left( \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime} \right)$$

Set this to zero to find the threshold:

$$\left( \sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime} \right) V_{f,min} = \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}$$

This recovers the same $V_{f,crit}$. Hence, in this formulation, $V_{f,min} \equiv V_{f,crit}$.

### Step 4 — Alternative definition (sometimes used in KTU texts)
A more relaxed definition of $V_{f,min}$ considers the condition that the fibre alone (in the absence of matrix contribution at that strain) carries the *entire* composite load:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_{f,min} \quad \text{at the fibre failure strain}$$

with the matrix assumed to have negligible contribution. Setting $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu}$:

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,min} = \sigma_{mu}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{f,min} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 question papers most commonly ask for the **stronger** definition: $V_{f,crit} = (\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}) / (\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime})$. The simpler $V_{f,min} = \sigma_{mu} / \sigma_{fu}$ appears as a sub-part when the matrix contribution is neglected (an idealising assumption).

## 3.3 Numerical Example (Worked Analytical Matrix)

**Given:**
- E-glass fibre: $E_f = 76$ GPa, $\sigma_{fu} = 2000$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.026$
- Epoxy matrix: $E_m = 3.1$ GPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 80$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$

**Check failure mode:** $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.026 < \varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$ → Case A (fibre fails first).

**Compute $\sigma_m^{\prime}$:**

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \varepsilon_{fu} = 3.1 \times 0.026 = 0.0806 \text{ GPa} = 80.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Compute $V_{f,crit}$:**

$$V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}} = \dfrac{80 - 80.6}{2000 - 80.6} = \dfrac{-0.6}{1919.4} \approx -0.000313$$

**Interpretation:** A **negative** $V_{f,crit}$ means the composite is **stronger than the matrix for every positive $V_f$**. The matrix contributes almost nothing to the failure load (its stress at $\varepsilon_{fu}$ is 80.6 MPa, essentially its own ultimate 80 MPa), but the fibre carries 2000 MPa over a much larger strain range. The result is that **any tiny fibre content** improves the strength.

This is typical of systems where the matrix is already at its ultimate at the fibre failure strain — i.e., the matrix and fibre are nearly strain-compatible.

**Now consider a carbon-fibre / epoxy system where this changes:**

- Carbon fibre: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\sigma_{fu} = 4000$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.017$
- Epoxy: $E_m = 3.1$ GPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 80$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$

**Check:** $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.017 < \varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$ → Case A.

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = 3.1 \times 0.017 = 0.0527 \text{ GPa} = 52.7 \text{ MPa}$$

$$V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{80 - 52.7}{4000 - 52.7} = \dfrac{27.3}{3947.3} \approx 0.00692$$

So for this carbon/epoxy system, $V_{f,crit} \approx 0.7\%$. Again, very small — meaning any reasonable carbon-fibre content easily exceeds it. **Practical design fibre contents are 50–60 %**, orders of magnitude above $V_{f,crit}$, ensuring a comfortable safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above shows why high-modulus, high-strength fibres like carbon and aramid produce composites that are *always* much stronger than their matrix for any meaningful $V_f$. The critical fibre content is a *guarantee of reinforcement*, not a *design point*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When $V_{f,crit}$ is Non-Trivial

$V_{f,crit}$ becomes large (and **practically limiting**) when:

| Condition | Effect on $V_{f,crit}$ |
|-----------|------------------------|
| $\sigma_{mu} \gg \sigma_m^{\prime}$ (matrix strain-hardens a lot) | Numerator increases → $V_{f,crit}$ **rises** |
| $\sigma_{fu}$ only slightly above $\sigma_m^{\prime}$ | Denominator shrinks → $V_{f,crit}$ **rises** |
| Fibres and matrix nearly strain-compatible | $V_{f,crit} \to 0$ |
| Fibres much weaker than matrix strength at $\varepsilon_{fu}$ | $V_{f,crit}$ can exceed 1 (impossible) |

The last case is unphysical and indicates the fibre-matrix combination cannot be reinforced.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Failure Mode and $V_{f,crit}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Material Properties] --> B[Compare failure strains]
    B --> C{Is epsilon_fu smaller than epsilon_mu?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Case A: Brittle Fibre / Ductile Matrix]
    C -->|No| E[Case B: Ductile Fibre / Brittle Matrix]
    D --> F[Compute sigma_m_prime = E_m times epsilon_fu]
    E --> G[Compute sigma_f_prime = E_f times epsilon_mu]
    F --> H[Apply ROM for Strength: sigma_cu = sigma_fu Vf + sigma_m_prime 1 minus Vf]
    G --> I[Apply ROM for Strength: sigma_cu = sigma_f_prime Vf + sigma_mu 1 minus Vf]
    H --> J[Set sigma_cu equal to sigma_mu]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Solve for V_f_crit]
    K --> L{V_f_crit is positive and less than 1?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Valid system: Use V_f greater than V_f_crit in design]
    L -->|No| N[System cannot be reinforced: Reconsider material choice]
    M --> O[End: Design V_f around 0.4 to 0.6 for safety]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Stress-Strain Architecture for Case A

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FibrePath[Fibre Behaviour]
        F1[Origin: 0,0] --> F2[Linear: slope E_f] --> F3[Peak: sigma_fu at epsilon_fu] --> F4[Sudden Drop: Brittle Failure]
    end
    subgraph MatrixPath[Matrix Behaviour]
        M1[Origin: 0,0] --> M2[Linear: slope E_m] --> M3[Continue: matrix still elastic] --> M4[Reaches: sigma_m_prime at epsilon_fu] --> M5[Continue to sigma_mu at epsilon_mu]
    end
    subgraph CompPath[Composite Behaviour]
        C1[Origin: 0,0] --> C2[Linear: slope E_c from ROM] --> C3[Peak: sigma_cu at epsilon_fu] --> C4[Matrix continues alone to sigma_mu]
    end
    F3 -.-> C3
    M4 -.-> C3
    M5 -.-> C4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In Case A, the fibre reaches its ultimate first at $\varepsilon_{fu}$. The composite peak is at this same strain, and its peak stress $\sigma_{cu}$ is the sum of $\sigma_{fu} V_f$ (fibre contribution) and $\sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f)$ (matrix contribution at that strain). The slope of the composite is the ROM-modulus $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$.

## 4.3 Geometric Picture of $V_{f,crit}$ — The Strength Crossing Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plot sigma_cu versus V_f] --> P2[At V_f = 0: sigma_cu = sigma_m_prime]
    P3[At V_f = 1: sigma_cu = sigma_fu] --> P1
    P1 --> P4[Draw horizontal line at sigma_cu = sigma_mu]
    P4 --> P5[Intersection point defines V_f_crit]
    P5 --> P6{Is V_f_crit less than 0.5?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Practical: any V_f above 0.5 is safe]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Impractical: need very high V_f; use aligned continuous fibres only]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why the Two Formulas Exist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Under Load] --> B[Isostrain: epsilon_f = epsilon_m = epsilon_c]
    B --> C[Fibre Stress: sigma_f = E_f epsilon_c]
    B --> D[Matrix Stress: sigma_m = E_m epsilon_c]
    C --> E[Rule of Mixtures: sigma_c = sigma_f V_f + sigma_m 1 minus V_f]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Which component fails first?}
    F -->|Fibre| G[Use sigma_cu = sigma_fu V_f + sigma_m_prime 1 minus V_f]
    F -->|Matrix| H[Use sigma_cu = sigma_f_prime V_f + sigma_mu 1 minus V_f]
    G --> I[Set sigma_cu = sigma_mu → solve V_f_crit]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Design V_f greater than V_f_crit with safety factor]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "critical fibre volume fraction" in a fibre-reinforced composite. Mention its significance.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **critical fibre volume fraction ($V_{f,crit}$)** is the minimum volume fraction of fibre at which the composite strength is **at least equal to** the strength of the unreinforced matrix. Below $V_{f,crit}$, the composite is **weaker** than the matrix.
>
> **Significance:** It provides a *design floor* — any practical composite must have $V_f > V_{f,crit}$ for the fibre reinforcement to be useful. It is the boundary between "fibre weakens the matrix" and "fibre strengthens the matrix".

---

### Q2. State the rule of mixtures used to compute the tensile strength of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite when the fibre fails first.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> When the fibre reaches its failure strain first ($\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$), the composite tensile strength is given by:
> $$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} \, V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} \, (1 - V_f)$$
> where $\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu}$ is the matrix stress at the fibre failure strain.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse $\sigma_{mu}$ (ultimate matrix strength) with $\sigma_m^{\prime}$ (matrix stress at the *fibre* failure strain). These are very different quantities. Using one in place of the other is the **most common** mistake and costs 3–4 marks instantly.
> - **Always** state the failure-mode assumption ($\varepsilon_{fu} \lessgtr \varepsilon_{mu}$) explicitly at the start of the solution.
> - **Units must be consistent** across all $\sigma$ and $E$ values. Convert GPa to MPa (or vice versa) before substitution.
> - Show the **isostrain assumption** as a justification for using the rule of mixtures — the examiner awards 1–2 marks for this foundation step alone.
> - Final answer for $V_{f,crit}$ must be a **dimensionless** number between 0 and 1 (or stated as a percentage).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A (a)** Derive an expression for the **critical fibre volume fraction** of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite, assuming the fibre fails before the matrix. State clearly the underlying assumption.  `(7 marks)`

**A (b)** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite has the following properties:
- Glass fibre: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_{fu} = 2400$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.033$
- Epoxy matrix: $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 90$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{mu} = 0.045$

Compute the critical fibre volume fraction and the composite strength at $V_f = 0.45$.  `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**A (a) — Derivation:**

(i) Under uniaxial loading along the fibre direction, the **isostrain assumption** (valid for parallel, continuous, well-bonded fibres) gives $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c$.

(ii) The stress in the composite by force equilibrium on a representative volume element:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

(iii) Failure-mode check: when $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$, the fibre reaches its ultimate first. The composite fails at $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{fu}$.

(iv) At this strain, the matrix stress (still elastic) is:

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

(v) Substituting into (i):

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

(vi) **Critical condition:** at $V_{f,crit}$, the composite strength equals the matrix ultimate:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

(vii) Equating (iii) and (iv) and solving for $V_f$:

$$\sigma_{fu} V_{f,crit} + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_{f,crit}) = \sigma_{mu}$$

$$V_{f,crit} (\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}) = \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}\,}$$

**A (b) — Numerical Computation:**

(i) Failure-mode check: $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.033 < \varepsilon_{mu} = 0.045$ → Case A (fibre fails first).

(ii) Matrix stress at fibre failure strain:

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu} = 3.5 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa} \times 0.033 = 115.5 \text{ MPa}$$

(iii) Critical fibre volume fraction:

$$V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{90 - 115.5}{2400 - 115.5} = \dfrac{-25.5}{2284.5} \approx -0.0112$$

(iv) **Interpretation:** $V_{f,crit} < 0$ indicates the composite is **stronger than the matrix for every positive $V_f$**. The matrix reaches its ultimate *before* the fibre reaches its failure strain (since $\sigma_m^{\prime} = 115.5$ MPa $> \sigma_{mu} = 90$ MPa). Hence, the matrix cracks first (technically Case B), but the fibre continues to carry load up to $\sigma_{fu}$. The result is a guaranteed reinforcement at any $V_f > 0$.

(v) Composite strength at $V_f = 0.45$ (using fibre-failure mode since $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$):

$$\sigma_{cu} = 2400 \times 0.45 + 115.5 \times 0.55 = 1080 + 63.5 = 1143.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> The composite strength is $\approx 1144$ MPa — about **12.7 times** the matrix strength.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B (a)** With a neat stress-strain sketch, explain the concept of **critical fibre volume fraction**. Differentiate between the cases when the fibre fails first and when the matrix fails first.  `(7 marks)`

**B (b)** A carbon-fibre / PEEK composite has:
- Carbon fibre: $E_f = 220$ GPa, $\sigma_{fu} = 3500$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.016$
- PEEK matrix: $E_m = 3.6$ GPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 100$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$

Determine the **minimum fibre volume fraction** at which the composite strength matches the matrix strength. Comment on the design implications.  `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**B (a) — Explanation:**

(i) The **critical fibre volume fraction** is the threshold volume fraction of fibre at which the strength of the composite equals the strength of the unreinforced matrix. Below $V_{f,crit}$, the composite is *weaker* than the matrix.

(ii) **Case A — Fibre fails first** ($\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$):

At composite failure ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{fu}$):

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f)$$

The composite strength is initially low at $V_f = 0$ (equals $\sigma_m^{\prime}$), rises linearly with $V_f$, and crosses $\sigma_{mu}$ at $V_{f,crit}$. For $V_f > V_{f,crit}$, $\sigma_{cu} > \sigma_{mu}$ and the composite is stronger than the matrix.

(iii) **Case B — Matrix fails first** ($\varepsilon_{mu} < \varepsilon_{fu}$):

At composite failure ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{mu}$):

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f^{\prime} V_f + \sigma_{mu} (1 - V_f)$$

The composite strength is initially $\sigma_{mu}$ at $V_f = 0$ and rises with $V_f$ (because $\sigma_f^{\prime} > \sigma_{mu}$ for typical stiff fibres).

(iv) **Stress-strain sketch description:**

- Draw strain $\varepsilon$ on the x-axis and stress $\sigma$ on the y-axis.
- Plot a steep line for the fibre (slope $E_f$), reaching $\sigma_{fu}$ at $\varepsilon_{fu}$ and then dropping vertically (brittle failure).
- Plot a less steep line for the matrix (slope $E_m$), reaching $\sigma_{mu}$ at $\varepsilon_{mu}$.
- Plot the composite line with slope $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$, terminating at whichever of $\varepsilon_{fu}$ or $\varepsilon_{mu}$ is smaller.

(v) The point where the composite strength line crosses the matrix strength line defines $V_{f,crit}$.

**B (b) — Numerical Solution:**

(i) Failure-mode check: $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.016 < \varepsilon_{mu} = 0.050$ → fibre fails first (Case A).

(ii) Matrix stress at $\varepsilon_{fu}$:

$$\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \, \varepsilon_{fu} = 3.6 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa} \times 0.016 = 57.6 \text{ MPa}$$

(iii) Critical fibre volume fraction:

$$V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}} = \dfrac{100 - 57.6}{3500 - 57.6} = \dfrac{42.4}{3442.4} \approx 0.0123$$

$$\boxed{V_{f,crit} \approx 0.0123 \text{ or } 1.23\%}$$

(iv) **Design implications:**

- The theoretical minimum fibre content is just **1.23%** — extremely low. This is the *bare minimum* for the composite to match the matrix strength.
- **Real design** uses $V_f$ between **0.40 and 0.60** (40–60%) — orders of magnitude above $V_{f,crit}$ — to provide:
  - Large safety margin against fibre misalignment and void defects.
  - Sufficient stiffness (modulus scales with $V_f$).
  - Consistent properties under off-axis loading.
  - Allowance for fibre-length efficiency factors in short-fibre systems.
- Practically, $V_{f,crit}$ is a **theoretical floor**, not a **design point**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Minimum and Critical Fibre Content**

- **Critical fibre volume fraction $V_{f,crit}$** = threshold fibre content below which the composite is *weaker* than the matrix alone.
- **Minimum fibre volume fraction $V_{f,min}$** = *theoretical lower bound* for any reinforcement effect; numerically identical to $V_{f,crit}$ in the standard KTU formulation.
- **Standard formula (fibre fails first):** $V_{f,crit} = (\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m^{\prime}) / (\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m^{\prime})$
- **Standard formula (matrix fails first):** $V_{f,crit} = (\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}) / (\sigma_f^{\prime} - \sigma_{mu})$
- **Always compute** $\sigma_m^{\prime} = E_m \varepsilon_{fu}$ (matrix stress at fibre failure) or $\sigma_f^{\prime} = E_f \varepsilon_{mu}$ (fibre stress at matrix failure) before applying the rule of mixtures.
- **Identify the failure mode** by comparing $\varepsilon_{fu}$ with $\varepsilon_{mu}$ — this is the **first step** in any KTU problem.
- **Negative $V_{f,crit}$** ⇒ the composite is automatically reinforced at any positive $V_f$ (the system is "easy" to reinforce).
- **$V_{f,crit} > 1$** ⇒ physically impossible; the fibre-matrix system cannot be reinforced.
- **Rule of mixtures for strength (isostrain):** $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} (1 - V_f)$ (fibre-fails-first) — derived from the same isostrain assumption as the modulus ROM.
- **Practical fibre content** in engineering composites (40–60% $V_f$) is much higher than $V_{f,crit}$ for safety, manufacturing tolerances, and to ensure adequate stiffness.
- **Units consistency** is non-negotiable: convert GPa to MPa (×1000) or vice versa.
- **Examiner's pet peeves:** mixing up $\sigma_{mu}$ vs $\sigma_m^{\prime}$, forgetting to state the isostrain assumption, omitting the failure-mode check, and using inconsistent units.
- **KTU Bloom's level** for this topic: *Apply* and *Analyse* (Level 3 & 4) — expect derivations, numerical computations, and case-by-case analysis.
- **Marks split (typical KTU pattern):** Part A = 3 marks (definition or short derivation); Part B = 14 marks split 7 + 7 (derivation in one part, numerical in the other).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Fibre Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Critical Fibre Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Critical Fibre Length ($L_c$)** is defined as the **minimum length of a fibre** that must be embedded in the matrix for the tensile stress developed in the fibre to reach its ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{fu}$) before any fibre fracture occurs. It is the threshold length below which the fibre will be pulled out of the matrix rather than fracture, because the load transferred to it is insufficient to break it.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{fu}$ = Ultimate tensile strength of the fibre
- $d$ = Diameter of the fibre
- $\tau_i$ = Interfacial (matrix-to-fibre) shear strength

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**: Critical fibre length is the **twice the length over which the fibre stress builds up from zero at the fibre ends to its maximum value at the centre**, when the interfacial shear stress is uniform and equal to $\tau_i$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to pull a **bamboo stick embedded in wet clay**:

- If the bamboo is **very short**, you simply pull it out whole — the clay does not grip tightly enough along the small contact area to break the bamboo.
- If the bamboo is **very long**, the accumulated gripping (shear) force along its length becomes so strong that the bamboo **snaps in the middle** before you can pull it out.

The exact length at which the bamboo just barely breaks (rather than slides out) is the **critical length**. Below this length, the fibre is **sub-critical** (pull-out dominates); above it, the fibre is **super-critical** (fibre fracture dominates). At exactly $L_c$, both pull-out and fracture occur simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in engineering**: If a designer uses fibres shorter than $L_c$, the composite is **under-utilising the fibre strength** and behaves more like the matrix alone. Hence, $L_c$ dictates the **minimum fibre length** that must be used for effective reinforcement.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Fibre Length | $L_c$ | mm or m | Minimum reinforcement length |
| Critical Aspect Ratio | $(L_c/d)$ | dimensionless | Material selection metric |
| Fibre Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{fu}$ | MPa or GPa | Fibre property |
| Fibre Diameter | $d$ | $\mu$m or mm | Geometric property |
