| Interfacial Shear Strength | $\tau_i$ | MPa | Bond quality between fibre and matrix |
| Load Transfer Length | $l_t$ | mm | $= L_c / 2$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — The Stress Transfer Problem**
A fibre embedded in a matrix does not experience uniform tensile stress. At the fibre ends (entry and exit points), the tensile stress is **zero**. Moving inward along the fibre, stress builds up because of the **interfacial shear stress ($\tau_i$)** acting on the cylindrical surface of the fibre (essentially, friction-like grip from the matrix).

**Step 2 — Building of Stress**
This stress build-up continues until, at the **mid-length of the fibre**, the tensile stress reaches its maximum possible value. Beyond this point, no further build-up is possible.

**Step 3 — The Critical Condition**
If the fibre length is **exactly equal to twice this build-up length**, then the peak fibre stress just equals $\sigma_{fu}$ (fibre's ultimate strength). This length is defined as $L_c$.

**Step 4 — Three Operating Regimes**

| Regime | Condition | Failure Mode | Composite Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sub-critical | $L < L_c$ | Fibre pull-out | Matrix-dominated, weak composite |
| Critical | $L = L_c$ | Simultaneous fibre fracture and pull-out | Optimum balance |
| Super-critical | $L > L_c$ | Fibre fracture | Fibre-dominated, strong composite |

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Concept

**Why does stress build up along the fibre?**
Because load is transferred from matrix to fibre through the **interfacial shear** acting on the cylindrical fibre surface. The longer the embedded length, the more total shear force can be mobilised — analogous to how a longer anchor bolt in concrete supports more load.

**How is the formula derived?**
Through a **force balance** on an infinitesimal element of the fibre, equating the change in fibre tensile force to the shear force on the lateral surface. The result is a differential equation whose solution gives a triangular stress distribution along the fibre.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Critical Fibre Length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$ | Basic KTU board definition formula |
| Critical Aspect Ratio | $\dfrac{L_c}{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$ | Dimensionless index, used in material selection |
| Maximum Fibre Stress (at distance $x$ from end) | $\sigma_f(x) = \dfrac{2 \tau_i}{d}\left(\dfrac{L}{2} - x\right)$ | Triangular distribution, $0 \le x \le L/2$ |
| Peak Fibre Stress (at centre) | $\sigma_{f,\max} = \dfrac{\tau_i \cdot L}{d}$ | Used in condition: $\sigma_{f,\max} \ge \sigma_{fu}$ |
| Load Transfer Length | $l_t = \dfrac{L}{2}$ | Length needed to reach full stress |
| Efficiency Factor (sub-critical fibre) | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{L_c}{2L}$ | Fraction of fibre strength utilised when $L < L_c$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention**: $\tau_i$ is generally taken as the **matrix shear yield strength** $\tau_y$ for ductile matrices (e.g., metals), or as the **interfacial shear bond strength** for brittle matrices (e.g., ceramics, polymers).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Fibre Selection**: Engineers compare $L_c/d$ for candidate fibres to ensure commercially available fibre lengths (chopped fibres, whiskers, continuous fibres) are above the critical threshold.
- **Quality Control**: A measured $L_c$ value is used to compute the actual fibre–matrix interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$) by pull-out tests.
- **Composite Design Rule**: For discontinuous (short) fibre composites, fibres should ideally have $L \gg L_c$ to ensure the rule of mixtures gives near-full reinforcement efficiency.
- **Failure Analysis**: When broken composites are examined, fibres protruding from the fracture surface indicate **sub-critical** behaviour; fibres flush with the matrix indicate **super-critical** behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Fibre Length from Force Balance

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry**
Consider a single cylindrical fibre of diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix along its full length $L$. The fibre carries an axial tensile stress $\sigma_f(x)$ that varies with position $x$ measured from the fibre end.

**Step 2 — Consider an infinitesimal fibre element**
Cut a small element of the fibre between position $x$ and position $x + dx$. Let the tensile stress at the two ends be $\sigma_f(x)$ and $\sigma_f(x + dx)$ respectively.

Cross-sectional area of fibre:
$$A_f = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Lateral surface area of element:
$$A_s = \pi d \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 — Apply force equilibrium**
The fibre element is acted upon by:
- Tensile force at left face: $F_{left} = \sigma_f(x) \cdot A_f$
- Tensile force at right face: $F_{right} = \sigma_f(x + dx) \cdot A_f$
- Shear force on lateral surface: $F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot A_s = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx$

For static equilibrium (sum of forces in $x$-direction = 0):
$$F_{left} - F_{right} - F_{shear} = 0$$

Substituting:
$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} - \sigma_f(x + dx) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} - \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Convert to differential form**
Divide throughout by $\pi d \cdot dx$:
$$\frac{d}{4}\left[\sigma_f(x) - \sigma_f(x + dx)\right] = \tau_i$$

In the limit as $dx \to 0$:
$$\frac{d}{4}\left(-\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx}\right) = \tau_i$$

$$\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} = -\frac{4 \tau_i}{d}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The minus sign indicates that fibre tensile stress *decreases* as we move from the centre of the fibre toward its ends, because shear stress transfers load *out* of the fibre surface as we approach the free end.

**Step 5 — Integrate the differential equation**
Assuming $\tau_i$ is uniform along the fibre length (idealised Kelly–Tyson assumption):

$$\int d\sigma_f = -\int \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} dx$$

$$\sigma_f(x) = -\frac{4 \tau_i}{d} x + C$$

**Step 6 — Apply the boundary condition**
At the fibre end, $x = L/2$, the stress must vanish because the fibre is free (no load beyond this point):
$$\sigma_f(L/2) = 0$$

Substituting:
$$0 = -\frac{4 \tau_i}{d} \cdot \frac{L}{2} + C$$

$$C = \frac{2 \tau_i L}{d}$$

**Step 7 — General stress distribution**
$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i L}{d} - \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} x = \frac{2 \tau_i}{d}\left(L - 2x\right)$$

Rewritten for $0 \le x \le L/2$:
$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i}{d}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) \cdot 2 = \frac{4 \tau_i}{d}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

Wait — let me re-express cleanly. Using symmetric coordinates where $x$ is measured from the **mid-length** of the fibre:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i}{d}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

**Step 8 — Maximum stress occurs at $x = 0$ (mid-length)**
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{2 \tau_i}{d} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{\tau_i L}{d}$$

**Step 9 — Critical length condition**
For the fibre to develop its full strength, $\sigma_{f,\max} \ge \sigma_{fu}$. Setting equality:

$$\frac{\tau_i L_c}{d} = \sigma_{fu}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The factor of 2 appears because stress builds up from **zero at both ends** to its maximum at the centre; hence the load transfer length is only $L/2$, and to get $\sigma_{fu}$ we need this $L/2$ to equal $\sigma_{fu} d / (2\tau_i)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency of Sub-Critical Fibres

When $L < L_c$, the fibre cannot develop its full strength. The average fibre stress over the length is half the peak stress (triangular distribution):

$$\overline{\sigma_f} = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\tau_i L}{d} = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{L_c}$$

Therefore the **strength efficiency factor** is:

$$\eta = \frac{\overline{\sigma_f}}{\sigma_{fu}} = \frac{L}{2 L_c} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{L}{L_c}\right)$$

This is the famous **Kelly–Tyson efficiency factor** for short-fibre composites.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given**: E-glass fibre, $\sigma_{fu} = 2.0$ GPa, $d = 10\ \mu$m, embedded in epoxy with $\tau_i = 50$ MPa.

**Find**: $L_c$, $(L_c/d)$, and the fibre stress when $L = 0.5$ mm.

**Solution**:

Step A — Calculate $L_c$:
$$L_c = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa})(10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m})}{2 \times (50 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa})}$$

$$L_c = \frac{20{,}000}{100 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.20\ \text{mm}$$

Step B — Calculate critical aspect ratio:
$$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 20$$

Step C — Fibre stress at $L = 0.5$ mm:
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{\tau_i L}{d} = \frac{(50)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 2{,}500\ \text{MPa} = 2.5\ \text{GPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{f,\max} = 2.5$ GPa $> \sigma_{fu} = 2.0$ GPa, the fibre will **fracture** (this is the super-critical regime). The actual fibre stress is capped at $\sigma_{fu} = 2.0$ GPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight from the example**: A fibre only **0.5 mm long** is **super-critical** for E-glass in epoxy. This is why even short chopped glass fibres (typically 3–25 mm) are highly effective reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fibre Stress Build-up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Matrix applies load on composite] --> B[Load enters matrix at fibre ends]
    B --> C[Interfacial shear stress tau_i acts on fibre surface]
    C --> D[Axial tensile stress builds up inside fibre]
    D --> E{Fibre length comparison}
    E -->|L less than Lc| F[Sub-critical regime]
    E -->|L equals Lc| G[Critical regime]
    E -->|L greater than Lc| H[Super-critical regime]
    F --> F1[Peak fibre stress less than sigma_fu]
    F1 --> F2[Fibre pulls out from matrix]
    F2 --> F3[Composite fails by pull-out]
    G --> G1[Peak fibre stress equals sigma_fu]
    G1 --> G2[Fibre fractures exactly at mid-length]
    G2 --> G3[Optimum energy absorption]
    H --> H1[Peak fibre stress capped at sigma_fu]
    H1 --> H2[Fibre fractures well before pull-out]
    H2 --> H3[Composite fails by brittle fibre fracture]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Distribution Along Fibre Length

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fibre end x = 0] --> P2[x = L by 4: stress = tau_i L by 2d]
    P2 --> P3[x = L by 2: stress = tau_i L by d = sigma_f max]
    P3 --> P4[x = 3L by 4: stress = tau_i L by 2d]
    P4 --> P5[Fibre end x = L: stress = 0]
    P3 -.peak.-> Q[sigma_f max greater than sigma_fu means fibre fractures]
    P3 -.peak.-> R[sigma_f max less than sigma_fu means fibre pulls out]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Triaxial Stress Build-up (Text-based Block Diagram)

```
              FIBRE STRESS DISTRIBUTION (Triangular, Kelly-Tyson Model)
              
   Tensile
   Stress
   sigma_f
     |
     |                          *  <-- Peak at mid-length
     |                        *   *
     |                      *       *
     |                    *           *
     |                  *               *
     |                *                   *
     |              *                       *
     |            *                           *
     |          *                               *
     |________*___________________________________*_______  x-axis (position)
            x=0        x=L/4       x=L/2        x=3L/4   x=L
        (end)                                  (centre)  (end)
        stress = 0                             stress = max    stress = 0
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The fibre stress is **zero** at both ends (free surfaces).
- It rises **linearly** toward the centre (assuming constant $\tau_i$).
- The **peak stress** $\sigma_{f,\max}$ at the centre equals $\tau_i L / d$.
- If $\sigma_{f,\max} < \sigma_{fu}$, the fibre does not break → pull-out regime.
- If $\sigma_{f,\max} \ge \sigma_{fu}$, the fibre breaks → fracture regime.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define critical fibre length. State the formula and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Critical fibre length $L_c$ is the minimum length of a fibre required for the tensile stress developed in the fibre to reach its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$.

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{fu}$ = Ultimate tensile strength of the fibre (MPa)
- $d$ = Diameter of the fibre (mm or $\mu$m)
- $\tau_i$ = Interfacial shear strength between fibre and matrix (MPa)

**Significance**: $L_c$ sets the **minimum reinforcement length** for effective load transfer. Fibres shorter than $L_c$ are pulled out instead of fractured, leading to inefficient composites. Designers use $L_c$ to select fibre length and to evaluate fibre–matrix bond quality through pull-out tests.

*(Full 3 marks awarded for clear definition + all three terms explained + formula.)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between sub-critical and super-critical fibre lengths with neat sketches of stress distribution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Sub-critical ($L < L_c$) | Super-critical ($L > L_c$) |
|---|---|---|
| Peak fibre stress | $\sigma_{f,\max} < \sigma_{fu}$ | $\sigma_{f,\max} = \sigma_{fu}$ (capped) |
| Failure mode | Fibre pull-out | Fibre fracture |
| Composite strength | Low (matrix-dominated) | High (fibre-dominated) |
| Energy absorption | High (pull-out dissipates energy) | Low (brittle fracture) |

**Sketches**:

```
   Sub-critical:               Super-critical:
   
   sigma_f                      sigma_f
     |   peak                    |   ____ sigma_fu (capped)
     |    /\                     |  /  \
     |   /  \                    | /    \
     |  /    \                   |/      \
     |_/______\_  x              |/        \_____\____ x
   < sigma_fu (peak too low)   (peak hits sigma_fu, plateau forms)
```

*(Award 3 marks for clear distinction + correct sketches showing peak vs ultimate stress.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

## Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive an expression for the critical fibre length in a fibre-reinforced composite. State clearly the assumptions used in the derivation.**

**Model Solution**:

**Assumptions (Kelly–Tyson Model)**:
1. The fibre is cylindrical and of uniform diameter $d$.
2. The interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ is **constant** along the entire fibre length.
3. The fibre ends are stress-free (no end load applied directly).
4. Load is transferred from matrix to fibre only through interfacial shear.
5. Both fibre and matrix behave elastically up to failure.

**Derivation**:

Consider an element of fibre between $x$ and $x + dx$.

Force balance on the element:
$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} - \sigma_f(x + dx) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} - \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx = 0$$

Simplifying:
$$\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} = -\frac{4 \tau_i}{d}$$

Integrating with boundary condition $\sigma_f(L/2) = 0$ at the fibre end:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i}{d}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

Maximum stress at $x = 0$ (mid-length):
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{\tau_i L}{d}$$

For critical length, $\sigma_{f,\max} = \sigma_{fu}$:

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for assumptions, 4 marks for derivation, 1 mark for final boxed expression.)*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A composite is reinforced with T-300 carbon fibres of diameter 7 $\mu$m and ultimate tensile strength 3.0 GPa in an epoxy matrix whose interfacial shear strength is 40 MPa. Compute (i) the critical fibre length, (ii) the critical aspect ratio, and (iii) the maximum fibre stress if the actual fibre length is 1.5 mm. Comment on whether the fibre will fracture.**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**:
- $d = 7\ \mu\text{m} = 7 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- $\sigma_{fu} = 3.0$ GPa $= 3.0 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\tau_i = 40$ MPa $= 40 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Actual fibre length $L = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**(i) Critical Fibre Length**:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6)}$$

$$L_c = \frac{21{,}000}{80 \times 10^6} = 2.625 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.2625\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Critical Aspect Ratio**:

$$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{2.625 \times 10^{-4}}{7 \times 10^{-6}} = 37.5$$

**(iii) Maximum Fibre Stress for $L = 1.5$ mm**:

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{\tau_i L}{d} = \frac{(40 \times 10^6)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{60{,}000}{7 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.57 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa} = 8.57\ \text{GPa}$$

**Comment**:
Since $\sigma_{f,\max} = 8.57$ GPa $\gg \sigma_{fu} = 3.0$ GPa, the fibre length (1.5 mm) is **far greater** than $L_c = 0.2625$ mm. The fibre is in the **super-critical regime**. The fibre will **fracture** at its centre because the calculated peak stress exceeds the fibre's ultimate strength. The actual fibre stress is capped at 3.0 GPa.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii); 1 mark for the comment.)*

---

## Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with the help of a neat diagram the variation of tensile stress along the length of a discontinuous fibre embedded in a matrix. Discuss the three regimes of fibre length.**

**Model Solution**:

When a discontinuous (short) fibre is loaded through the matrix, the tensile stress along the fibre is **not uniform**. It is zero at the fibre ends and rises toward the centre.

**Stress Distribution** (Triangular / Kelly–Tyson):

```
   Tensile
   Stress
     |
   sigma_f,|        _________________
   max    |      /                   \
          |    /                       \
          |  /                           \
          |/                             \
          *_______________________________\____ x-axis
        x=0    x=L/4    x=L/2   x=3L/4    x=L
        end                     centre    end
       (stress=0)            (stress=max) (stress=0)
```

**Three Regimes of Fibre Length**:

1. **Sub-critical regime ($L < L_c$)**: The peak fibre stress never reaches $\sigma_{fu}$. The fibre is **pulled out** from the matrix rather than fractured. The composite strength is governed by $\tau_i$, and the fibre is under-utilised.

2. **Critical regime ($L = L_c$)**: The peak fibre stress exactly equals $\sigma_{fu}$. The fibre fractures just as it begins to pull out. This is the **threshold condition** for full fibre utilisation.

3. **Super-critical regime ($L > L_c$)**: The fibre stress reaches $\sigma_{fu}$ at some distance from the centre, and a **plateau** forms where the stress is capped at $\sigma_{fu}$. The fibre **fractures** within this plateau region, and further increases in $L$ provide no additional benefit.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation of three regimes.)*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite contains 50% by volume of fibres. The fibre has $\sigma_{fu} = 2.5$ GPa and diameter 12 $\mu$m. The matrix has an interfacial shear strength of 35 MPa. Determine (i) the critical fibre length, (ii) whether a fibre of length 3 mm will fracture, and (iii) the average fibre stress in a 1 mm long fibre.**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**:
- $\sigma_{fu} = 2.5$ GPa $= 2.5 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $d = 12\ \mu\text{m} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\tau_i = 35$ MPa $= 35 \times 10^6$ Pa

**(i) Critical Fibre Length**:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^9)(12 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (35 \times 10^6)}$$

$$L_c = \frac{30{,}000}{70 \times 10^6} = 4.286 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.4286\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Will a 3 mm fibre fracture?**

Compare $L = 3$ mm with $L_c = 0.4286$ mm. Since $L \gg L_c$, the fibre is **super-critical**.

Compute the would-be peak stress:
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{\tau_i L}{d} = \frac{(35 \times 10^6)(3 \times 10^{-3})}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.75\ \text{GPa}$$

Since $8.75$ GPa $> \sigma_{fu} = 2.5$ GPa, the fibre will **definitely fracture**. The actual peak stress is capped at 2.5 GPa.

**(iii) Average Stress in a 1 mm Fibre**:

Since $L = 1$ mm $> L_c = 0.4286$ mm, the fibre is super-critical. The fibre develops full strength $\sigma_{fu}$ in its central plateau region, but stress tapers linearly to zero at both ends.

For a **super-critical** fibre, the **average stress** is computed using the Kelly–Tyson approach:

$$\overline{\sigma_f} = \sigma_{fu}\left(1 - \frac{L_c}{2L}\right)$$

$$\overline{\sigma_f} = 2.5\left(1 - \frac{0.4286}{2 \times 1}\right) = 2.5(1 - 0.2143) = 2.5 \times 0.7857$$

$$\overline{\sigma_f} = 1.964\ \text{GPa}$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) with peak-stress comparison, 2 marks for (iii) using Kelly–Tyson efficiency formula.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Students frequently forget to convert GPa to Pa and $\mu$m to m. The formula gives wrong answers by orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Factor of 2 confusion**: Some students write $L_c = \sigma_{fu} d / \tau_i$ (missing the 2). The factor of 2 arises because stress builds up from BOTH ends of the fibre.
> 3. **Capped stress in super-critical regime**: When computing $\sigma_{f,\max}$ for $L > L_c$, students often report the calculated value even though it exceeds $\sigma_{fu}$. The correct interpretation is that fibre stress is **capped** at $\sigma_{fu}$.
> 4. **Wrong assumption for $\tau_i$**: For polymer matrices, $\tau_i$ is the interfacial bond strength; for metal matrices, $\tau_i \approx \tau_y$ (matrix shear yield). Mixing these gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions**: In derivations, failing to mention the Kelly–Tyson assumption of **constant $\tau_i$** loses easy marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: $L_c$ = minimum fibre length needed to develop $\sigma_{fu}$ in a fibre.
- **Core formula**: $L_c = \sigma_{fu} \cdot d / (2 \tau_i)$ — memorise this.
- **Critical aspect ratio**: $L_c/d = \sigma_{fu}/(2\tau_i)$ — used in material selection charts.
- **Stress profile**: Triangular along the fibre; zero at ends, maximum at centre.
- **Three regimes**:
  * $L < L_c$ → **sub-critical** → fibre pull-out
  * $L = L_c$ → **critical** → simultaneous fracture and pull-out
  * $L > L_c$ → **super-critical** → fibre fracture
- **Efficiency factor** for sub-critical fibres: $\eta = L / (2 L_c)$.
- **Average stress** for super-critical fibres: $\overline{\sigma_f} = \sigma_{fu}(1 - L_c/(2L))$.
- **Typical values**: $L_c$ for glass/epoxy is ~0.2 mm; for carbon/epoxy is ~0.3–0.5 mm.
- **Engineering rule**: Use fibres with $L \gg L_c$ (at least $10 \times L_c$) for effective reinforcement.
- **Assumption to remember**: Constant $\tau_i$ (Kelly–Tyson idealisation).
- **Practical significance**: Short-fibre composites (e.g., chopped glass in SMC) work because $L_c$ is very small.
- **Exam tip**: Always convert units before substituting; always state the regime after comparing $L$ with $L_c$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Law of Mixture Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Law of Mixture Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3 — LAW OF MIXTURE RULE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Law of Mixture (Rule of Mixtures — ROM)**
> The *Law of Mixture* is an analytical framework used in composite materials engineering to predict the *effective bulk properties* of a heterogeneous composite material by mathematically combining the properties of its individual constituents (typically the **fiber** and the **matrix**) weighted by their respective **volume fractions** (or weight fractions). It assumes a *perfectly bonded, void-free, and macroscopically homogeneous* composite.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECHT742 — Composite Materials), the Law of Mixture is the cornerstone for predicting properties such as **density**, **Young's Modulus** (longitudinal and transverse), **Poisson's ratio**, **tensile strength**, and **thermal/electrical conductivity** of fiber-reinforced composites.

### 1.2 Volume Fraction and Weight Fraction

The two constituent phases together occupy the entire volume of the composite. Hence:

$$V_f + V_m = 1$$

Where:
- $V_f$ = Volume fraction of the fiber
- $V_m$ = Volume fraction of the matrix

The corresponding **weight (mass) fractions** are defined as:

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_c} \quad \text{and} \quad W_m = \frac{\rho_m \cdot V_m}{\rho_c}$$

With $W_f + W_m = 1$, and the composite density $\rho_c$ given by the rule of mixtures.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🍹 **Real-World Analogy: A Smoothie**
> Imagine making a fruit smoothie by blending **banana** (fiber) and **milk** (matrix). The taste, thickness, and nutritional value of your smoothie are *predictable* if you know how much of each ingredient went in. If you add more banana, the smoothie gets thicker (higher stiffness) and heavier. The **Rule of Mixtures** works exactly like this — it tells you how the *overall stiffness, density, or strength* of the composite is a weighted average of its ingredients.

A second analogy: **Concrete** is a particulate composite of *cement paste* (matrix) and *gravel/sand* (filler). The compressive strength and density of concrete depend linearly on the proportions of its constituents — exactly what the Rule of Mixtures predicts.

### 1.4 Why Is It Important?

> [!NOTE]
> The Law of Mixture is **the first analytical tool** a composite design engineer uses to:
> - **Estimate properties** before manufacturing (saves cost and time).
> - **Optimize fiber/matrix ratios** for target stiffness, strength, or weight.
> - **Benchmark** experimental results against theoretical upper/lower bounds.
> - **Select materials** for aerospace, automotive, marine, and civil applications.

The standard property values often referenced in KTU textbooks include **E_glass ≈ 70 GPa**, **E_carbon ≈ 230 GPa**, **E_aramid ≈ 70 GPa**, **E_epoxy ≈ 3–5 GPa**, **ρ_steel ≈ 7.8 g/cm³**, and **ρ_carbon_fiber ≈ 1.7–2.0 g/cm³**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis

The Rule of Mixtures is built on **two idealized micromechanical models**:

### A. **Voigt Model (Iso-Strain / Parallel Model)**

> [!NOTE]
> Assumes that **fiber and matrix experience the same strain** ($\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c$). This is valid when load is applied **parallel to continuous, perfectly aligned fibers**. It provides the **upper bound** of the composite modulus.

### B. **Reuss Model (Iso-Stress / Series Model)**

> [!NOTE]
> Assumes that **fiber and matrix experience the same stress** ($\sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma_c$). This is valid when load is applied **transverse to fibers** or when dealing with a layered/laminated configuration. It provides the **lower bound** of the composite modulus.

## 2.2 Structured Logic Flow

1. **Identify constituent volume fractions** ($V_f$, $V_m$) and properties ($E_f$, $E_m$, $\rho_f$, $\rho_m$, $\nu_f$, $\nu_m$).
2. **Choose the appropriate model** based on loading direction:
   - Longitudinal (parallel to fibers) → **Voigt Rule**
   - Transverse (perpendicular to fibers) → **Reuss Rule**
3. **Apply the weighted average** formula.
4. **Validate** against bounds (Halpin–Tsai may be used for better accuracy, but ROM is the KTU-prescribed baseline).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📌 **Key Formula Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Property | Rule of Mixtures Expression | Model Used | Loading Direction |
|---|----------|------------------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Density** | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Direct average | All |
| 2 | **Young's Modulus (Longitudinal)** | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Voigt (Iso-strain) | Parallel to fiber |
| 3 | **Young's Modulus (Transverse)** | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Reuss (Iso-stress) | Perpendicular to fiber |
| 4 | **Poisson's Ratio (Longitudinal)** | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Voigt | Parallel to fiber |
| 5 | **Tensile Strength (Longitudinal)** | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Voigt | Parallel to fiber |
| 6 | **Shear Modulus (Longitudinal)** | $G_{LT} = G_f V_f + G_m V_m$ | Voigt | Parallel to fiber |
| 7 | **Thermal Conductivity (Parallel)** | $k_L = k_f V_f + k_m V_m$ | Voigt | Parallel to fiber |
| 8 | **Electrical Conductivity (Parallel)** | $\kappa_L = \kappa_f V_f + \kappa_m V_m$ | Voigt | Parallel to fiber |
| 9 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_c / \rho_c$ | Derived | All |
| 10 | **Specific Modulus** | $E_c / \rho_c$ | Derived | All |

**Boundary Condition (Mandatory Invariant):**

$$V_f + V_m = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_m = 1 - V_f$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Carbon/epoxy composites with $V_f \approx 0.6$ achieve $E_L \approx 140$ GPa — far higher than aluminum (70 GPa) at 1/3 the weight.
- **Automotive:** Glass fiber/polyester body panels use ROM to balance stiffness vs. cost.
- **Civil:** GFRP rebars replace steel in corrosive environments; ROM predicts tensile capacity.
- **Sports Equipment:** Tennis rackets, F1 chassis, bicycle frames — all designed using ROM principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Density of Composite

### Setup

Consider a composite of total volume $V_c$, consisting of fiber volume $V_f$ and matrix volume $V_m$.

$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The total mass of the composite equals the sum of masses of constituents:

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

Since $m = \rho \cdot V$, we substitute:

$$\rho_c \cdot V_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m$$

Dividing both sides by $V_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

By definition, $\dfrac{V_f}{V_c} = V_f$ (volume fraction) and $\dfrac{V_m}{V_c} = V_m$:

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m}$$

Since $V_m = 1 - V_f$, this becomes:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt Rule / Iso-Strain)

### Setup

Consider a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite loaded **parallel to the fibers**. Both fiber and matrix deform by the same strain (iso-strain assumption):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

The load is shared between fiber and matrix acting **in parallel**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Total force on the composite cross-section:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 2:** Force is related to stress and area: $F = \sigma \cdot A$

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Since fibers are aligned along the loading direction and assuming uniform cross-section:

$$\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m$$

**Step 4:** Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$) to each constituent, noting iso-strain:

$$\sigma_c = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_m$$

$$\sigma_c = \varepsilon \cdot (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

**Step 5:** Also by Hooke's Law for the composite: $\sigma_c = E_c \cdot \varepsilon_c = E_L \cdot \varepsilon$

Equating the two expressions:

$$E_L \cdot \varepsilon = \varepsilon \cdot (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

Cancel $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** of the composite modulus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transverse Young's Modulus (Reuss Rule / Iso-Stress)

### Setup

Now consider loading **perpendicular to the fibers**. In this configuration, both phases experience the **same stress** (iso-stress assumption):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

The fiber and matrix act like elements connected **in series** with respect to the loading direction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Total elongation of the composite equals sum of elongations of constituents:

$$\Delta L_c = \Delta L_f + \Delta L_m$$

**Step 2:** Divide by total gauge length $L_c$ to obtain strain:

$$\varepsilon_c \cdot L_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot L_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot L_m$$

**Step 3:** Since the load is applied transversely and the constituents share the same length fraction as their volume fraction in the loading direction:

$$\frac{L_f}{L_c} = V_f \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{L_m}{L_c} = V_m$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m$$

**Step 5:** Apply Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$) and iso-stress condition ($\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$):

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot V_m$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\sigma$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $E_T$ explicitly:

$$\boxed{E_T = \frac{E_f \cdot E_m}{E_m \cdot V_f + E_f \cdot V_m}}$$

This is the **Reuss lower bound** of the composite modulus.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Longitudinal Modulus)

**Problem:** A glass fiber reinforced epoxy composite has a fiber volume fraction of 0.45. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa and $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Solution:**

$$V_f = 0.45, \quad V_m = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$$

Apply Voigt Rule:

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_L = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_L \approx 34.33 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The composite modulus (34.33 GPa) lies between the fiber modulus (72 GPa) and matrix modulus (3.5 GPa), weighted by volume — consistent with the iso-strain model.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Bound Table

| Property | Voigt (Upper) | Reuss (Lower) | Actual (Experimental) |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-----------------------|
| $E_L$ | $E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $\dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Close to Voigt |
| $E_T$ | $E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $\dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Close to Reuss |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Rule of Mixtures Prediction Engine

The following Mermaid diagram captures the logical decision flow for property prediction using the Law of Mixture, replacing complex physical schematics with a clean block-architecture representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Composite Property Prediction] --> B[Input: Constituent Properties]
    B --> B1[Fiber Properties: Ef, rhof, nuf, sf]
    B --> B2[Matrix Properties: Em, rhom, num, sm]
    A --> C[Input: Volume Fractions]
    C --> C1[Vf and Vm where Vf plus Vm equals 1]
    B1 --> D{Choose Loading Direction}
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D -->|Parallel to Fiber| E[Apply Voigt Iso-Strain Model]
    D -->|Perpendicular to Fiber| F[Apply Reuss Iso-Stress Model]
    E --> E1[EL equals Ef Vf plus Em Vm]
    E --> E2[nuLT equals nuf Vf plus num Vm]
    E --> E3[sigmaL equals sigmaf Vf plus sigmam Vm]
    F --> F1[1 over ET equals Vf over Ef plus Vm over Em]
    F --> F2[ET equals Ef Em over Em Vf plus Ef Vm]
    E1 --> G[Compute Composite Density: rhoc equals rhof Vf plus rhom Vm]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Output: Composite Effective Property]
    H --> I[Compare with Experimental Data]
    I --> J[Validate Bounds: Voigt upper, Reuss lower]
    J --> K[End]
```

## 4.2 Voigt vs Reuss — Conceptual Loading Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Voigt["VOIGT MODEL: ISO-STRAIN (Parallel Spring System)"]
        V1[Fiber Ef Vf] --> Vsum[Same Strain epsilon]
        V2[Matrix Em Vm] --> Vsum
        Vsum --> Vout[EL equals Ef Vf plus Em Vm]
    end
    subgraph Reuss["REUSS MODEL: ISO-STRESS (Series Spring System)"]
        R1[Fiber Ef Vf] --> Rsum[Same Stress sigma]
        R2[Matrix Em Vm] --> Rsum
        Rsum --> Rout[1 over ET equals Vf over Ef plus Vm over Em]
    end
```

## 4.3 Fiber-Matrix Loading Schematic (Text Representation)

```
LOADING PARALLEL TO FIBERS (Voigt):              LOADING PERPENDICULAR TO FIBERS (Reuss):

   ↑ Strain ε (same in both)                        → Stress σ (same in both)
   │                                                 ┌──────────┐
   │  ┌────┐  ┌────┐                                │  Fiber   │  ← ε_f
   │  │ F  │  │ M  │                                ├──────────┤
   │  │ Ef │  │ Em │                                │  Matrix  │  ← ε_m
   │  └────┘  └────┘                                └──────────┘
   ↓                                                 ε_total = ε_f + ε_m
   Parallel springs → EL = Ef·Vf + Em·Vm            Series springs → 1/ET = Vf/Ef + Vm/Em
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the Law of Mixture rule. Write the expression for the density of a composite material using this rule.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Law of Mixture (Rule of Mixtures)** is a micromechanical model that predicts the effective properties of a composite material by computing the weighted average of its constituent properties, with weights being the respective volume (or weight) fractions. It assumes perfect bonding and uniform distribution of the constituents.

The **density** of a composite is given by:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m$$

where $\rho_f$ and $\rho_m$ are the densities of the fiber and matrix, and $V_f$ and $V_m$ are their volume fractions, with $V_f + V_m = 1$.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — explanation of terms]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between the Voigt rule and the Reuss rule used in composite property prediction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Voigt Rule | Reuss Rule |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Assumption | Iso-strain ($\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$) | Iso-stress ($\sigma_f = \sigma_m$) |
| Loading | Parallel to fibers | Perpendicular to fibers |
| Configuration | Parallel spring analogy | Series spring analogy |
| Result | Upper bound of modulus | Lower bound of modulus |
| Formula | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ |

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1.5 marks — Voigt, 1.5 marks — Reuss]**

---

## 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a)** Derive the expression for the Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite in the **longitudinal direction** using the Voigt model (iso-strain assumption). State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**

1. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed in the matrix.
2. Perfect bonding exists at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip).
3. Both fiber and matrix experience the same strain: $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c$ (iso-strain).
4. The composite is free of voids.
5. Loading is applied parallel to the fiber direction.

**Derivation:**

The total force on the composite cross-section is the sum of forces carried by fiber and matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

In terms of stress and area:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Dividing by the total area $A_c$ and noting that $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Applying Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) under iso-strain:

$$E_c \varepsilon = (E_f \varepsilon) V_f + (E_m \varepsilon) V_m$$

$$E_c \varepsilon = \varepsilon (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

Cancelling $\varepsilon$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}$$

This represents the **upper bound** of the composite modulus. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **(b)** A unidirectional **carbon fiber reinforced epoxy composite** contains **55% by volume** of carbon fibers. Given:
- $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³

Calculate: (i) Density of composite, (ii) Longitudinal Young's modulus, (iii) Transverse Young's modulus. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$

**(i) Density of composite:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (1.80)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.99 + 0.54 = 1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt Rule):**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

$$E_L = (230)(0.55) + (3.0)(0.45)$$

$$E_L = 126.5 + 1.35 = 127.85 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_L = 127.85 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Transverse Young's modulus (Reuss Rule):**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{230} + \frac{0.45}{3.0}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.002391 + 0.15 = 0.152391 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}$$

$$E_T = \frac{1}{0.152391} = 6.562 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_T \approx 6.56 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The longitudinal modulus (127.85 GPa) is nearly **20×** the transverse modulus (6.56 GPa), confirming the strong **anisotropy** of unidirectional composites — a critical design consideration in aerospace applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the **Voigt model** (iso-strain) and the **Reuss model** (iso-stress) used in the law of mixtures for predicting the elastic properties of composite materials. Compare their applicability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Voigt Model (Iso-Strain / Parallel Model):**

In this model, the fiber and matrix are assumed to be arranged in **parallel** with respect to the loading direction. Both phases undergo **identical strain** when the composite is loaded. This model is analogous to two springs connected in parallel.

- **Loading direction:** Parallel to the fibers.
- **Key equation:** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- **Bound:** Represents the **upper bound** of the composite modulus.
- **Applicability:** Best for continuous, aligned fiber composites under longitudinal loading.

**2. Reuss Model (Iso-Stress / Series Model):**

In this model, the fiber and matrix are arranged in **series** with respect to the loading direction. Both phases experience **identical stress** when the composite is loaded. This model is analogous to two springs connected in series.

- **Loading direction:** Perpendicular to the fibers.
- **Key equation:** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$
- **Bound:** Represents the **lower bound** of the composite modulus.
- **Applicability:** Best for transverse loading or layered composite configurations.

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | Voigt Model | Reuss Model |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Strain field | Uniform (iso-strain) | Non-uniform |
| Stress field | Non-uniform | Uniform (iso-stress) |
| Modulus bound | Upper bound | Lower bound |
| Fiber arrangement | Parallel to load | Perpendicular to load |
| Mathematical form | Arithmetic mean | Harmonic mean |

**(7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks each for Voigt & Reuss explanation, 1 mark each for diagrams, 1 mark for comparison table)**

---

#### **(b)** A **glass fiber reinforced polyester composite** has the following properties:
- Fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$
- $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³
- $\sigma_{f,\text{ult}} = 2000$ MPa, $\sigma_{m,\text{ult}} = 50$ MPa

Calculate: (i) Density, (ii) Longitudinal tensile strength, (iii) Specific strength (longitudinal). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 0.60$

**(i) Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.25)(0.60)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.02 + 0.75 = 1.77 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1.77 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength:**

Applying the Rule of Mixtures in the fiber direction:

$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

$$\sigma_{c,L} = (2000)(0.40) + (50)(0.60)$$

$$\sigma_{c,L} = 800 + 30 = 830 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c,L} = 830 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iii) Specific Strength:**

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{c,L}}{\rho_c} = \frac{830 \text{ MPa}}{1.77 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Strength} \approx 468.93 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{cm}^3/\text{g}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This composite has a specific strength approximately **5.7×** higher than mild steel (specific strength ≈ 63 MPa·cm³/g at $\sigma = 490$ MPa, $\rho = 7.8$ g/cm³), justifying its use in lightweight structural applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the invariant:** Always state that $V_f + V_m = 1$ — failing to do so costs **1 mark** in nearly every problem.
> 2. **Confusing Voigt with Reuss:** Students often write the Voigt formula for transverse loading. **Memory trick:** Voigt = **V**ertical fibers + same strain; Reuss = **R**ight angle (⊥) to fibers + same stress.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Always carry GPa consistently and convert $\rho$ to consistent units (g/cm³ or kg/m³) — examiners **deduct 0.5–1 mark** for unit errors.
> 4. **Missing assumptions:** In derivations, always list the **5 standard assumptions** of the Rule of Mixtures (perfect bonding, continuous fibers, no voids, uniform distribution, iso-strain/iso-stress).
> 5. **Ignoring physical interpretation:** KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education* — concluding a numerical with a **physical interpretation sentence** can fetch **bonus cognitive marks**.
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in $V_m$:** Always compute $V_m = 1 - V_f$ **before** substituting — many students plug $V_f$ twice by mistake.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist for Law of Mixture Rule**

- ✏️ **Definition:** Property prediction = weighted average of constituent properties by volume fraction.
- ✏️ **Master Formula:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ — applies to *all* properties in the parallel (Voigt) sense.
- ✏️ **Invariant:** $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always true; never violated.
- ✏️ **Voigt Rule (Upper Bound):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ → loading **parallel** to fibers.
- ✏️ **Reuss Rule (Lower Bound):** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ → loading **transverse** to fibers.
- ✏️ **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ (longitudinal, Voigt model).
- ✏️ **Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ (parallel loading).
- ✏️ **Key Assumptions:** Perfect bonding, continuous aligned fibers, no voids, uniform distribution, iso-strain or iso-stress.
- ✏️ **Anisotropy Insight:** $E_L \gg E_T$ for unidirectional composites — design consequence.
- ✏️ **Standard Property Values (Memorize):** $E_{\text{glass}} \approx 70$ GPa, $E_{\text{carbon}} \approx 230$ GPa, $E_{\text{epoxy}} \approx 3$–$5$ GPa, $\rho_{\text{carbon}} \approx 1.8$ g/cm³.
- ✏️ **Engineering Insight:** Higher $V_f$ → higher stiffness & strength, but reduced ductility and increased brittleness.
- ✏️ **Board Exam Pattern:** Expect 1 direct 3-mark definition + 1 numerical (14 marks) in every KTU ESE cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unidirectional and Fibrous Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Unidirectional and Fibrous Composites # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Unidirectional (UD) Composites A **Unidirectional Fiber-Reinforced Composite** is a class of advanced structural composite in which all reinforcing fibers are aligned parallel to a single reference axis (the loading direction). The fibers carry the principal mechanical load along their longitudinal axis, while the surrounding **matrix** (typically a polymer, metal, or ceramic) transfers load to the fibers via interfacial shear, protects them from environmental damage, and maintains fiber position and orientation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a UD composite, the **fiber orientation parameter** $\eta$ (eta) equals **1** when fibers are perfectly aligned with the applied load and **0** when fibers are perpendicular. This orientation factor is the most critical parameter governing the directional stiffness and strength of the lamina. The term **Fibrous Composites** is the broader category that includes UD composites as a special case, and also encompasses woven, braided, chopped, and randomly-oriented short-fiber systems. ## 1.2 Conceptually — What Does "Unidirectional" Mean? > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — The Bamboo Bundle:** > Imagine tying a bundle of bamboo sticks tightly together with strong string (the matrix). If you pull the bundle along the length of the sticks, it feels incredibly stiff and strong — almost like a steel rod. Now try pulling the same bundle sideways (perpendicular to the sticks). It feels weak and floppy because the string is the only thing resisting the load. A unidirectional composite behaves identically: **amazing along the fiber direction, mediocre transverse to it**. This directional dependence is called **anisotropy**, and it is the defining mechanical signature of UD composites. ## 1.3 Classification of Fibrous Composites (KTU Taxonomy) | Type | Fiber Geometry | Typical $V_f$ (Fiber Volume Fraction) | |------|---------------|---------------------------------------| | Continuous Fiber UD | Long, parallel, $l \gg l_c$ | 0.40 – 0.70 | | Short Fiber (Aligned) | $l \approx 10 \, l_c$ to $100 \, d$ | 0.20 – 0.40 | | Short Fiber (Random) | Discontinuous, randomly oriented | 0.10 – 0.30 | | Woven Fabric | Interlaced tows | 0.30 – 0.50 | ## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants - **Standard fiber volume fraction for design:** $V_f = 0.6$ (60%) - **Standard matrix volume fraction:** $V_m = 0.4$ (40%) - **Critical aspect ratio threshold:** $l/d \geq 10$ for "long-fiber" classification - **Typical fiber-matrix interface shear strength (carbon/epoxy):** $\tau_i = 30$ to **80 MPa** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Load-Carrying Mechanisms in a UD Composite A UD lamina under uniaxial load exhibits three distinct mechanical responses depending on the direction of the applied force: 1. **Longitudinal Loading (Load || Fibers, $\theta = 0^\circ$):** Fibers and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain / Voigt condition). Fibers dominate stiffness and strength. 2. **Transverse Loading (Load ⊥ Fibers, $\theta = 90^\circ$):** Fibers and matrix experience the **same stress** (iso-stress / Reuss condition). Matrix dominates. 3. **Off-Axis Loading ($0^\circ < \theta < 90^\circ$):** Combined normal and shear stresses are developed, governed by **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** and the **transformation equations** for stress/strain. > [!TIP] > **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners in KTU frequently ask, *"Why does the longitudinal modulus follow the rule of mixtures while transverse modulus uses the inverse rule?"* The correct answer is: **iso-strain conditions give parallel spring analogy (fibers + matrix in parallel → addition), and iso-stress conditions give series spring analogy (fibers + matrix in series → inverse addition)**. Memorizing this analogy will let you derive any rule of mixtures cold in the exam hall. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Convention:** Subscripts $c$, $f$, $m$ denote composite, fiber, and matrix respectively. $V_f + V_m = 1$ always. | Property | Equation | Boundary Condition / Notes | |----------|----------|----------------------------| | Volume constraint | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Always true; fundamental | | Longitudinal Young's Modulus | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Iso-strain (Voigt) | | Transverse Young's Modulus | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Iso-stress (Reuss) | | Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Iso-strain rule | | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Direct rule of mixtures | | Longitudinal Tensile Strength | $\sigma_L \approx \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' V_m$ | Fiber-dominated failure | | Transverse Tensile Strength | $\sigma_T \approx \dfrac{\sigma_m'}{1 + 0.5 \sqrt{V_f}}$ | Matrix-dominated failure | | Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Required for full fiber strength | | Fiber Efficiency Factor | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ | For $l < l_c$ (short fibers) | | Longitudinal Modulus (Short Fiber) | $E_L = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Modified rule of mixtures | | Halpin–Tsai Equation | $\dfrac{E_T}{E_m} = \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, with $\eta = \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi}$ | For transverse modulus, $\xi = 2$ | | Off-Axis Modulus | $\dfrac{1}{E_x(\theta)} = \dfrac{\cos^4 \theta}{E_L} + \dfrac{\sin^4 \theta}{E_T} + \left(\dfrac{1}{G_{LT}} - \dfrac{2\nu_{LT}}{E_L}\right) \sin^2 \theta \cos^2 \theta$ | Used for off-axis laminates | > [!CAUTION] > **Pipe Symbol Warning:** All absolute values and fractions in the table use $\vert$ or $\dfrac{}{}$ to avoid breaking markdown table rendering in your study notes. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications - **Aerospace primary structures** (Boeing 787, Airbus A350): CFRP UD prepregs laid at $0^\circ$, $\pm 45^\circ$, $90^\circ$ to build quasi-isotropic laminates. - **Wind turbine blades** (60–80 m long): Glass-fiber UD tapes for spar caps, carrying bending loads along the blade length. - **Pressure vessels (CNG tanks):** Hoop-wound carbon UD composites achieve $\sigma_T > 2000$ MPa through $V_f \approx 0.65$. - **Sporting goods:** Tennis rackets, F1 chassis, bicycle frames — exploit the **directional stiffness** of UD plies. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Metric:** A typical AS4 carbon fiber / 3501-6 epoxy UD composite at $V_f = 0.6$ achieves $E_L \approx 138$ GPa and $E_T \approx 9$ GPa — a stiffness ratio of **15:1**. This dramatic anisotropy is why laminates use multiple ply orientations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Longitudinal Modulus (Rule of Mixtures) **Assumption:** Perfect fiber-matrix bonding; both phases experience the same longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_L$ (iso-strain / parallel spring model). **Step 1: Force equilibrium on a representative volume element (RVE).** The total axial load $P_c$ on the composite cross-section $A_c$ is the sum of the loads carried by the fiber and matrix phases: $$P_c = P_f + P_m$$ **Step 2: Convert loads to stresses using sub-areas.** Since $A_f = V_f A_c$ and $A_m = V_m A_c$ (by definition of volume fractions in a UD RVE): $$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$ $$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f (V_f A_c) + \sigma_m (V_m A_c)$$ **Step 3: Cancel the common factor $A_c$.** $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$ **Step 4: Apply the iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_L$.** Using Hooke's Law, $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_L$ and $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_L$: $$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon_L V_f + E_m \varepsilon_L V_m$$ $$\sigma_c = \varepsilon_L (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$ **Step 5: Divide both sides by $\varepsilon_L$ to obtain composite stress/strain = composite modulus.** $$E_L = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ $$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$ > [!TIP] > **Physical Interpretation:** This is a **weighted average** of the two moduli, weighted by volume fraction. If $V_f = 1$, $E_L = E_f$ (all fiber); if $V_f = 0$, $E_L = E_m$ (all matrix). This is the famous **Voigt Upper Bound**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transverse Modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures) **Assumption:** Both fiber and matrix experience the **same transverse stress** $\sigma_T$ (iso-stress / series spring model). The fiber and matrix can be visualized as two springs in series, each occupying a length proportional to its volume fraction. **Step 1: Total transverse displacement is the sum of fiber and matrix displacements.** $$\Delta_c = \Delta_f + \Delta_m$$ **Step 2: Express displacements as strain × length, where the load-bearing length is proportional to volume fraction.** For a unit composite length, the fiber occupies length $V_f$ and matrix occupies length $V_m$: $$\varepsilon_c \cdot 1 = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m$$ **Step 3: Use Hooke's Law, $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$, and apply the iso-stress condition $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma_T$.** $$\frac{\sigma_T}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_T}{E_m} V_m$$ **Step 4: Cancel $\sigma_T$ on both sides.** $$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $E_T$.** $$\boxed{E_T = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Because fibers and matrix are arranged in **series** for transverse loading, the **softer phase (matrix) dominates**. For $V_f = 0.6$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 4$ GPa: $E_T \approx 6.2$ GPa — far below $E_L$. This is the **Reuss Lower Bound**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Fiber Length **Physical Meaning:** A fiber shorter than a certain minimum length $l_c$ cannot be loaded to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ before the surrounding matrix shears off at the interface. **Step 1: Consider a single fiber of length $l$ and diameter $d$ embedded in matrix, loaded by interfacial shear $\tau_i$.** **Step 2: Force balance along the fiber.** The fiber is loaded at its center by tensile stress $\sigma_f$. The interfacial shear transfers this load back into the matrix along the fiber length. At the fiber ends, $\sigma_f = 0$; the stress builds up linearly toward the center with a slope of $4\tau_i/d$ (from cylindrical geometry: $d\sigma_f/dx = 4\tau_i/d$). **Step 3: Maximum fiber stress (at the center) for a fiber of length $l$.** $$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{2 \tau_i l}{d}$$ **Step 4: Define the critical length $l_c$ as the length at which $\sigma_{f,\max} = \sigma_{fu}$ (fiber ultimate strength).** $$\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2 \tau_i l_c}{d}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $l_c$.** $$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$ **Step 6: Define the fiber efficiency factor $\eta_l$ for $l < l_c$.** The average stress in the fiber over its length is $\bar{\sigma}_f = \eta_l \sigma_f$, where: $$\eta_l = \begin{cases} 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2 l}, & l_c \le l \\ \dfrac{l}{2 l_c}, & l < l_c \end{cases}$$ This $\eta_l$ replaces $V_f$ in the modified rule of mixtures: $$\boxed{E_L^{\text{short}} = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Halpin–Tsai Equation (Transverse Modulus Refinement) The simple Reuss inverse rule **underestimates** $E_T$ experimentally. Halpin and Tsai introduced an empirical interpolation parameter $\xi$ that accounts for fiber geometry and packing. **Step 1: Write the general semi-empirical form.** $$\frac{E_T}{E_m} = \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$ **Step 2: Define the Halpin–Tsai $\eta$ parameter.** $$\eta = \frac{E_f / E_m - 1}{E_f / E_m + \xi}$$ **Step 3: For circular fibers in a UD composite under transverse loading, set $\xi = 2$.** Substituting: $$\frac{E_T}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2 \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \frac{E_f / E_m - 1}{E_f / E_m + 2}$$ This gives much closer agreement with experiment than the inverse rule of mixtures. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which equation is more accurate for $E_T$, inverse rule or Halpin–Tsai?" — answer **Halpin–Tsai**, because it accounts for fiber packing geometry and reduces to the Reuss bound as $\xi \to \infty$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Comparison **Given:** AS4 Carbon Fiber / 3501-6 Epoxy, $V_f = 0.6$ - $E_f = 234$ GPa, $\sigma_{fu} = 3.5$ GPa, $d = 7 \, \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cc - $E_m = 4.2$ GPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 69$ MPa, $\tau_i = 60$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1.27$ g/cc **Step-by-step numerical evaluation:** $$E_L = (234)(0.6) + (4.2)(0.4) = 140.4 + 1.68 = 142.08 \text{ GPa}$$ $$E_T = \frac{(234)(4.2)}{(4.2)(0.6) + (234)(0.4)} = \frac{982.8}{2.52 + 93.6} = \frac{982.8}{96.12} = 10.22 \text{ GPa}$$ $$l_c = \frac{(3500)(7 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(60)} = \frac{24.5}{120} = 0.204 \text{ mm}$$ $$\rho_c = (1.80)(0.6) + (1.27)(0.4) = 1.08 + 0.508 = 1.588 \text{ g/cc}$$ The composite achieves **142 GPa longitudinal stiffness** at only **1.588 g/cc density** — yielding a **specific stiffness of 89.4 GPa/(g/cc)**, more than 4× that of titanium alloys. This is why UD CFRP dominates aerospace primary structures. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — UD Composite Architecture & Load Transfer ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[External Tensile Load P] --> nodeB[Composite Cross Section A_c] nodeB --> nodeC[Fiber Phase
V_f portion of area
Carries sigma_f] nodeB --> nodeD[Matrix Phase
V_m portion of area
Carries sigma_m] nodeC --> nodeE[Longitudinal Iso Strain
epsilon_L same in both phases] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[Load Transfer via Interfacial Shear tau_i] nodeF --> nodeG[Fiber-Matrix Interface
Critical for composite integrity] nodeG --> nodeH[Output Composite Response
E_L high, E_T low] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Iso-Strain vs Iso-Stress Spring Analogy ```mermaid graph LR nodeS1[Spring Model A: Iso Strain] --> nodeP1[Fiber Spring E_f in Parallel] nodeS1 --> nodeP2[Matrix Spring E_m in Parallel] nodeP1 --> nodeOut1[E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m] nodeP2 --> nodeOut1 nodeS2[Spring Model B: Iso Stress] --> nodeSE1[Fiber Spring E_f in Series] nodeS2 --> nodeSE2[Matrix Spring E_m in Series] nodeSE1 --> nodeOut2[1 over E_T = V_f over E_f + V_m over E_m] nodeSE2 --> nodeOut2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of UD Lamina Mechanics ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[RVE of UD Composite
Volume V_c] --> Phase1[Fiber Phase Volume V_f] Input --> Phase2[Matrix Phase Volume V_m] Phase1 --> Mech1[Longitudinal Response
E_L Voigt Rule] Phase2 --> Mech1 Phase1 --> Mech2[Transverse Response
E_T Reuss Rule] Phase2 --> Mech2 Mech1 --> Crit1[Critical Fiber Length l_c Check] Mech2 --> Crit2[Halpin Tsai Refinement] Crit1 --> Output[Design Allowables
sigma_L sigma_T E_L E_T] Crit2 --> Output Output --> App1[Aerospace Primary Structures] Output --> App2[Wind Turbine Blade Spar Caps] Output --> App3[CNG Pressure Vessels] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Description — Stress Distribution Along a Short Fiber For a discontinuous fiber of length $l < l_c$, the tensile stress $\sigma_f(x)$ along the fiber axis builds up linearly from both ends (where $\sigma_f = 0$) toward a maximum at the center. The interfacial shear stress $\tau_i(x)$ is highest at the fiber ends and decreases toward the center. The triangular $\sigma_f$ profile ensures that **only fibers longer than $l_c$ can be fully utilized** at their ultimate strength. This geometric reality is the reason chopped fiber composites ($l \approx l_c/2$) achieve only **40–50%** of the strength of continuous UD composites at the same $V_f$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. Why is the longitudinal stiffness $E_L$ much higher than the transverse stiffness $E_T$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite is a composite material in which all reinforcing fibers are aligned parallel to a single reference axis, embedded in a matrix that transfers load via interfacial shear. The longitudinal stiffness $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ is much higher than $E_T$ because under longitudinal loading the fibers and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain), so the stiff fibers carry most of the load. Under transverse loading, the constituents experience the **same stress** (iso-stress), and the soft matrix dominates, yielding $E_T \approx E_m / (1 - V_f)$ for typical $V_f$ values. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for iso-strain mechanism, 1 for iso-stress mechanism)** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State and explain the significance of the critical fiber length $l_c$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The critical fiber length $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 \tau_i}$ is the minimum fiber length required for the fiber to be loaded up to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ before the matrix interface shears off. Its significance is twofold: (i) it sets the threshold between "long" and "short" fibers — only fibers with $l \geq l_c$ can realize their full reinforcing potential; (ii) it determines the fiber efficiency factor $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$ used in the modified rule of mixtures for short-fiber composites. For carbon/epoxy with $\sigma_{fu} = 3.5$ GPa, $d = 7 \, \mu\text{m}$, $\tau_i = 60$ MPa, we get $l_c \approx 0.2$ mm. **(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for engineering significance)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice]` **(a) Derive the expressions for the longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$ and the transverse Young's modulus $E_T$ of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the rule of mixtures. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed. 2. Perfect bonding at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip). 3. Fibers and matrix are homogeneous and isotropic. 4. No voids or porosity. **Derivation of $E_L$ (Iso-Strain):** Apply force equilibrium on a representative volume element (RVE) under axial load $P_c$: $$P_c = P_f + P_m \implies \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$ Using $A_f = V_f A_c$ and $A_m = V_m A_c$: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$ Apply iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_L$. Using Hooke's Law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$: $$E_L \varepsilon_L = E_f \varepsilon_L V_f + E_m \varepsilon_L V_m$$ Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon_L$: $$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m} \quad \text{(Voigt Upper Bound — 7 marks covered)}$$ **Derivation of $E_T$ (Iso-Stress):** For transverse loading, fiber and matrix experience equal stress. The total displacement is the sum of the phase displacements: $$\Delta_c = \Delta_f + \Delta_m$$ For unit composite length, the fiber occupies length $V_f$ and matrix occupies length $V_m$: $$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$ Using $\sigma_T / E = \varepsilon$: $$\frac{\sigma_T}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_T}{E_m} V_m$$ Dividing by $\sigma_T$: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \implies E_T = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}} \quad \text{(Reuss Lower Bound)}$$ --- **(b) A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has the following properties. Compute $E_L$, $E_T$, $\rho_c$, and the critical fiber length $l_c$. Comment on the anisotropy ratio. (7 marks)** **Given Data:** | Property | Fiber (E-glass) | Matrix (Epoxy) | |----------|----------------|----------------| | Young's Modulus | $E_f = 72$ GPa | $E_m = 3.5$ GPa | | Density | $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cc | $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cc | | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{fu} = 2.4$ GPa | — | | Interfacial Shear | — | $\tau_i = 45$ MPa | | Fiber Diameter | $d = 14 \, \mu\text{m}$ | — | | Volume Fraction | $V_f = 0.55$ | $V_m = 0.45$ | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus:** $$E_L = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45) = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.18 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 2 — Transverse Modulus:** $$E_T = \frac{(72)(3.5)}{(3.5)(0.55) + (72)(0.45)} = \frac{252}{1.925 + 32.4} = \frac{252}{34.325} = 7.34 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 3 — Composite Density:** $$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45) = 1.4025 + 0.54 = 1.94 \text{ g/cc}$$ **Step 4 — Critical Fiber Length:** $$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2400)(14 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(45)} = \frac{33.6}{90} = 0.373 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — Anisotropy Ratio:** $$\frac{E_L}{E_T} = \frac{41.18}{7.34} = 5.61$$ **Comment:** The composite is highly anisotropic, with longitudinal stiffness $\approx 5.6$ times the transverse stiffness. This validates the directional nature of UD composites — designers use cross-ply or angle-ply laminates to mitigate this. The specific stiffness along the fiber direction is $E_L/\rho_c = 21.2$ GPa/(g/cc), superior to aluminum ($\approx 26$ GPa/(g/cc)) when combined with corrosion resistance. **(7 marks — 1 mark each for steps 1–5, 2 marks for comment)** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice]` **(a) Explain the Halpin-Tsai equation for the transverse modulus of a UD composite. How does it improve upon the inverse rule of mixtures? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **The Halpin-Tsai Equation:** $$\frac{E_T}{E_m} = \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi}$$ **Origin and Physical Basis:** The inverse rule of mixtures (Reuss model) treats the composite as a series arrangement of fiber and matrix slabs. While mathematically simple, it **under-predicts** experimental $E_T$ by 20–40% because it neglects: (i) fiber-fiber stress interactions, (ii) the actual geometry of fiber packing (square vs hexagonal vs random), and (iii) the constraint effect of the stiffer fibers on the surrounding matrix. Halpin and Tsai (1969) introduced the empirical fitting parameter $\xi$ (xi) that depends on the **boundary conditions of the reinforcement geometry**: - For circular fibers under transverse loading: $\xi = 2$ - For rectangular fillers: $\xi = 2 \times (\text{width}/\text{thickness})$ - For aligned platelets: $\xi \to \infty$ (recovers Voigt rule) **Improvement over Reuss:** The Halpin-Tsai equation **interpolates** between the Reuss lower bound (when $\xi \to \infty$) and the Voigt upper bound (when $\xi = 0$). For $\xi = 2$, the equation captures the experimental observation that transverse stiffness is **higher** than the inverse rule prediction because the matrix between fibers is constrained by neighboring fibers. **Numerical Example:** For $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\xi = 2$: $$\eta = \frac{72/3.5 - 1}{72/3.5 + 2} = \frac{19.57}{22.57} = 0.867$$ $$\frac{E_T}{3.5} = \frac{1 + 2(0.867)(0.55)}{1 - 0.867(0.55)} = \frac{1.954}{0.523} = 3.74$$ $$E_T = 13.09 \text{ GPa}$$ Compared to inverse rule value of $7.34$ GPa, Halpin-Tsai predicts a more realistic value that matches experimental data. **(7 marks — 2 for equation, 2 for derivation of $\eta$, 2 for numerical example, 1 for limitations)** --- **(b) Define critical fiber length $l_c$ and derive the modified rule of mixtures for short-fiber composites. A short carbon fiber ($l = 2$ mm, $d = 8 \, \mu\text{m}$) is used in a polyester matrix. Given $\sigma_{fu} = 3.0$ GPa, $\tau_i = 40$ MPa, $V_f = 0.35$. Compute $l_c$, $\eta_l$, and $E_L^{\text{short}}$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition and Derivation:** For a short fiber of length $l$ and diameter $d$, the tensile stress at distance $x$ from the fiber end is: $$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i x}{d}$$ The **average** fiber stress over its length is: $$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{l} \int_0^l \frac{2 \tau_i x}{d} dx = \frac{\tau_i l}{d}$$ Define fiber efficiency $\eta_l = \bar{\sigma}_f / \sigma_{fu}$ (for $l < l_c$): $$\eta_l = \frac{l}{2 l_c}$$ For $l \geq l_c$, $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2 l}$ (trapezoidal stress profile). **Modified Rule of Mixtures (Short Fibers):** $$E_L^{\text{short}} = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ **Step 1 — Compute $l_c$:** $$l_c = \frac{(3000)(8 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(40)} = \frac{24}{80} = 0.3 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Check fiber length regime:** $l = 2$ mm $\gg l_c = 0.3$ mm, so use $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$: $$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{0.3}{2 \times 2} = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925$$ **Step 3 — Given $E_f = 230$ GPa (standard carbon), $E_m = 3.5$ GPa (polyester):** $$E_L^{\text{short}} = (0.925)(230)(0.35) + (3.5)(0.65)$$ $$= 74.46 + 2.275 = 76.74 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4 — Comparison with continuous UD at same $V_f$:** $$E_L^{\text{cont}} = (230)(0.35) + (3.5)(0.65) = 80.5 + 2.275 = 82.78 \text{ GPa}$$ The short-fiber composite achieves $76.74/82.78 = 92.7\%$ of the continuous-fiber modulus, demonstrating that for $l \gg l_c$, the efficiency is high. **(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 1 for $l_c$, 1 for regime check, 1 for $\eta_l$, 2 for final $E_L$ calculation)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Mixing up the two rules of mixtures (5 marks lost per question):** Students frequently write $E_T = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (the longitudinal formula) by mistake. **Memory aid:** "**L**ongitudinal uses **L**inear rule, **T**ransverse uses inverse **T**ype rule." Or simply remember: **L**ongitudinal = **L**inear addition, **T**ransverse = reci**P**rocal (inverse). > > 2. **Forgetting the iso-strain/iso-stress assumption statement (3 marks lost):** Every KTU derivation question explicitly tests whether you stated the assumptions before deriving. Always begin with: "Assuming perfect bonding and uniform strain distribution..." > > 3. **Not converting MPa to N/mm² consistently (2 marks lost):** When computing $l_c$ with $\sigma_{fu}$ in MPa and $d$ in mm, ensure consistent units. If $\sigma_{fu}$ in MPa and $d$ in mm, then $l_c$ comes out in mm directly. > > 4. **Halpin-Tsai $\xi$ parameter confusion (3 marks lost):** The most common error is setting $\xi = 1$ instead of $\xi = 2$ for circular fibers. **Rule:** $\xi = 2 \times (a/b)$ where $a$ is the dimension along the loading direction and $b$ is the perpendicular dimension. For a circular fiber, $a/b \to 1$, so $\xi = 2$. > > 5. **Ignoring $V_f + V_m = 1$ constraint (2 marks lost):** When given only $V_f$, always compute $V_m = 1 - V_f$ explicitly. Examiners award marks for this step. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unidirectional & Fibrous Composites** - **Unidirectional composite** = all fibers aligned along one axis; highly anisotropic. - **Two fundamental rules of mixtures:** - Longitudinal: $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt, iso-strain, parallel springs) - Transverse: $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (Reuss, iso-stress, series springs) - **Always true:** $V_f + V_m = 1$ - **Density rule:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (direct rule applies) - **Poisson's ratio rule:** $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ (uses iso-strain) - **Critical fiber length:** $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2 \tau_i)$ — minimum length for full fiber utilization. - **Fiber efficiency factor:** $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$ for $l \geq l_c$; $\eta_l = l/(2l_c)$ for $l < l_c$. - **Modified rule for short fibers:** $E_L^{\text{short}} = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ - **Halpin-Tsai equation:** More accurate transverse modulus prediction; $\xi = 2$ for circular fibers. - **Off-axis modulus:** $1/E_x(\theta)$ involves $\cos^4\theta$, $\sin^4\theta$, and cross-terms with $G_{LT}$ and $\nu_{LT}$. - **Engineering tip:** Specific stiffness $E/\rho$ is the metric for aerospace — CFRP UD achieves $\approx 90$ GPa/(g/cc), 4× titanium alloys. - **Memory anchor:** **"Parallel = Linear, Series = Reciprocal"** — this single phrase covers 60% of all KTU questions on this topic. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Unidirectional and Fibrous Composites**

topics.title: Effects of Fibre Orientation on Stiffness and Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects of Fibre Orientation on Stiffness and Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Unidirectional Fibre-Reinforced Polymeric (UD-FRP) Lamina**, the as-cured material possesses two intrinsic material axes:
- **Axis 1 (Longitudinal / Fibre axis)**
- **Axis 2 (Transverse / Matrix-dominant axis)**

These are the **Principal Material Coordinates (PMC)**, along which the material is **transversely orthotropic** — i.e., the in-plane shear–normal coupling stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ are identically **zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fibre Orientation (θ)** is defined as the angle, measured **counter-clockwise**, from the **global/reference (loading) x-axis** of the laminate to the **local fibre (1-) axis** of the lamina.
> When θ = 0°, the lamina is called **on-axis**; for any other angle, it is called an **off-axis lamina**.

The **Effect of Fibre Orientation on Stiffness and Strength** refers to the systematic variation of the *effective elastic moduli* ($E_x, E_y, G_{xy}, \nu_{xy}$) and *failure envelopes* of a single lamina when the load is applied at an angle θ to the fibres, because the orthotropic symmetry axes no longer coincide with the loading axes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a bundle of uncooked spaghetti glued together with honey (matrix). The fibres = spaghetti strands; the matrix = honey.

- **Pull along the strands (θ = 0°)** → Very stiff, very strong. The spaghetti carries the load directly.
- **Pull across the strands (θ = 90°)** → Soft and weak. The honey (matrix) alone resists the load.
- **Pull at 45°** → Some stiffness (the strands partially engage) and a phenomenon called **shear-extension coupling** appears: pulling in the x-direction causes the laminate to *skew* (shear deformation), something a normal isotropic material never does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A piece of wood is enormously strong along the grain (longitudinal) but splits easily across the grain (transverse). The ratio of longitudinal-to-transverse strength in wood can exceed **20:1**, comparable to a typical carbon/epoxy composite.

## 1.3 Key Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Order (Carbon/Epoxy) |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal modulus | $E_1$ | **130 – 140 GPa** |
| Transverse modulus | $E_2$ | **8 – 10 GPa** |
| Major Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{12}$ | **0.25 – 0.30** |
| In-plane shear modulus | $G_{12}$ | **4 – 7 GPa** |
| Longitudinal tensile strength | $X_T$ | **1500 – 2500 MPa** |
| Transverse tensile strength | $Y_T$ | **40 – 80 MPa** |
| In-plane shear strength | $S_{12}$ | **60 – 100 MPa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $E_1 \gg E_2$ and $X_T \gg Y_T$, even a **15° – 30°** misalignment from the load direction can cause the apparent stiffness to **drop by a factor of 3 to 5** — a critical design consideration in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Reference Frames

1. **Material frame (1, 2):** aligned with fibres. Stiffness is given by the **reduced stiffness matrix** $Q_{ij}$.
2. **Loading frame (x, y):** aligned with applied load. Stiffness is given by the **transformed reduced stiffness matrix** $\bar{Q}_{ij}(\theta)$.

The relation between the two is governed by the **coordinate transformation equations** of plane orthotropic elasticity.
