## 2.2 Reduced Stiffness Matrix in PMC (On-Axis)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{11} &= \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\[4pt]
Q_{12} &= \frac{\nu_{12}\,E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21}\,E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\[4pt]
Q_{66} &= G_{12}, \quad Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

with the **reciprocity identity** $\nu_{21}/E_2 = \nu_{12}/E_1$.

## 2.3 Transformed Reduced Stiffness $\bar{Q}_{ij}(\theta)$ (Off-Axis)

Let $m = \cos\theta$ and $n = \sin\theta$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11} &= Q_{11}m^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}n^{4} \\[3pt]
\bar{Q}_{22} &= Q_{11}n^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}m^{4} \\[3pt]
\bar{Q}_{12} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{12}(m^{4} + n^{4}) \\[3pt]
\bar{Q}_{66} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{66}(m^{4} + n^{4}) \\[3pt]
\bar{Q}_{16} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{3}n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})mn^{3} \\[3pt]
\bar{Q}_{26} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})mn^{3} + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m^{3}n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **coupling stiffnesses** $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ vanish only at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 90°$ (and at $\theta = 45°$ for *balanced* materials). For any other angle, normal stresses produce shear strains and vice versa. This is the **single most important off-axis effect**.

## 2.4 Off-Axis Engineering Constants (Closed Form)

The most frequently used result in KTU numericals is the **off-axis Young's modulus $E_x$**:

$$
\frac{1}{E_x} = \frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_1} + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{E_2} + \sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)
$$

Similarly:

$$
\frac{1}{E_y} = \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{E_1} + \frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_2} + \sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)
$$

$$
\nu_{xy} = E_x\!\left[\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}(m^{4}+n^{4}) - \left(\frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} - \frac{1}{G_{12}}\right)m^{2}n^{2}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{G_{xy}} = 2\!\left(\frac{2}{E_1} + \frac{2}{E_2} + \frac{4\nu_{12}}{E_1} - \frac{1}{G_{12}}\right)m^{2}n^{2} + \frac{1}{G_{12}}(m^{4}+n^{4})
$$

## 2.5 Off-Axis Strength — Failure Criteria

| Criterion | Failure Equation (in PMC) |
|---|---|
| **Maximum Stress** | $\vert\sigma_1\vert \le X, \;\vert\sigma_2\vert \le Y, \;\vert\tau_{12}\vert \le S$ |
| **Maximum Strain** | $\vert\varepsilon_1\vert \le \varepsilon_{1u}, \;\vert\varepsilon_2\vert \le \varepsilon_{2u}, \;\vert\gamma_{12}\vert \le \gamma_{12u}$ |
| **Tsai–Hill** | $(\sigma_1/X)^{2} - (\sigma_1\sigma_2)/X^{2} + (\sigma_2/Y)^{2} + (\tau_{12}/S)^{2} \le 1$ |
| **Tsai–Wu** | $F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^{2} + F_{22}\sigma_2^{2} + F_{66}\tau_{12}^{2} + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a *unidirectional* composite under **off-axis tension** $\sigma_x$, the strengths $X, Y, S$ are invariants in the material frame, but the stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$ in PMC depend on θ through:
> $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta$, $\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$.
> Substituting these into the **Tsai–Hill** criterion gives the off-axis tensile strength $\sigma_x(\theta)$ as a closed-form function of θ.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{11}$ | $E_1/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Pa |
| 2 | $Q_{22}$ | $E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Pa |
| 3 | $Q_{12}$ | $\nu_{12}E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Pa |
| 4 | $Q_{66}$ | $G_{12}$ | Pa |
| 5 | $\nu_{21}$ | $\nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ | — |
| 6 | $1/E_x$ | $m^{4}/E_1 + n^{4}/E_2 + m^{2}n^{2}(1/G_{12} - 2\nu_{12}/E_1)$ | 1/Pa |
| 7 | Off-axis $\sigma_1$ | $\sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta$ | Pa |
| 8 | Off-axis $\sigma_2$ | $\sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta$ | Pa |
| 9 | Off-axis $\tau_{12}$ | $-\sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Pa |
| 10 | Tsai–Hill | $(\sigma_1/X)^{2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2/X^{2} + (\sigma_2/Y)^{2} + (\tau_{12}/S)^{2} \le 1$ | — |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace wing-skins:** Stiffness tailoring using $\pm\theta$ ply pairs exploits the $\bar{Q}_{16}$ coupling to **suppress bend–twist coupling** (aeroelastic tailoring).
- **Filament-wound pressure vessels:** The optimum helical angle for combined hoop–axial loading is derived from $E_x(\theta)$ curves.
- **Civil FRP retrofitting:** CFRP strips bonded on concrete at a 45° angle can carry shear — the off-axis stiffness dictates the design.
- **Wind-turbine blades:** Spar caps are placed at $\theta \approx \pm 5°$ to balance stiffness vs. manufacturability; deviations cause catastrophic loss of bending rigidity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Off-Axis Young's Modulus $E_x(\theta)$

**Goal:** Starting from the constitutive law in PMC and the strain transformation, derive the famous $1/E_x$ expression.

### Step 1 — Constitutive law in PMC

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Invert to obtain the on-axis compliance matrix

Using $S_{ij} = 1/Q_{ij}$ (for the diagonal block, with appropriate cross-terms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}\sigma_2}{E_2}, \quad
\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}\sigma_1}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}, \quad
\gamma_{12} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Strain transformation to (x, y)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \varepsilon_1\cos^{2}\theta + \varepsilon_2\sin^{2}\theta - \gamma_{12}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\varepsilon_y &= \varepsilon_1\sin^{2}\theta + \varepsilon_2\cos^{2}\theta + \gamma_{12}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\gamma_{xy} &= 2(\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \gamma_{12}(\cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Stress transformation (off-axis tension $\sigma_x$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 = \sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta, \quad
\sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta, \quad
\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3 with Step 4 inserted

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &=
\sigma_x\!\left[\frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta}{E_2}
+ \frac{\sin^{2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta}{E_2} - \frac{\nu_{12}\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta}{E_1}
+ \frac{\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta}{G_{12}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

After grouping like terms and using $\nu_{21}/E_2 = \nu_{12}/E_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x\!\left[\frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_1} + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{E_2}
+ \sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Recognise Hooke's law in the x-direction

Since $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E_x$, equate:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{E_x} = \frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_1} + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{E_2} + \sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right)\;}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Standard, 14-Mark Style)

**Problem.** A unidirectional glass/epoxy lamina has the following properties:
$E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$, longitudinal tensile strength $X = 1200$ MPa, transverse tensile strength $Y = 40$ MPa, in-plane shear strength $S = 60$ MPa.

If the lamina is loaded in uniaxial tension along the global x-axis, find:
**(a)** The off-axis modulus $E_x$ and effective Poisson's ratio $\nu_{xy}$ at $\theta = 30°$.
**(b)** The off-axis tensile strength $\sigma_x^{ult}$ at $\theta = 30°$ using the **Tsai–Hill** criterion.

### Part (a) — Stiffness

**Step 1:** Compute $m = \cos 30° = 0.8660$, $n = \sin 30° = 0.5000$.
$m^{4} = 0.5625$, $n^{4} = 0.0625$, $m^{2}n^{2} = 0.1875$.

**Step 2:** Evaluate each term in $1/E_x$:
$\cos^{4}\theta/E_1 = 0.5625/38 = 0.014803$ (1/GPa)
$\sin^{4}\theta/E_2 = 0.0625/8.5 = 0.007353$ (1/GPa)
$1/G_{12} - 2\nu_{12}/E_1 = 1/4.5 - 0.60/38 = 0.22222 - 0.01579 = 0.20643$ (1/GPa)
Term 3 = $0.1875 \times 0.20643 = 0.038706$ (1/GPa)

**Step 3:** Sum:
$1/E_x = 0.014803 + 0.007353 + 0.038706 = 0.060862$ (1/GPa)
$\Rightarrow E_x = 1/0.060862 \approx \mathbf{16.43\ GPa}$

Compare with on-axis: $E_1 = 38$ GPa → **56.8 % drop** at only 30° off-axis!

**Step 4:** $\nu_{xy}$:
$\nu_{xy} = E_x\!\left[\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}(m^{4}+n^{4}) - \left(\dfrac{1}{E_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2}-\dfrac{1}{G_{12}}\right)m^{2}n^{2}\right]$
$= 16.43\!\left[\dfrac{0.30}{38}(0.625) - \left(\dfrac{1}{38}+\dfrac{1}{8.5}-\dfrac{1}{4.5}\right)(0.1875)\right]$
$= 16.43\!\left[0.004934 - (-0.057798)(0.1875)\right]$
$= 16.43\!\left[0.004934 + 0.010837\right] = 16.43 \times 0.015771$
$\Rightarrow \nu_{xy} \approx \mathbf{0.259}$

### Part (b) — Strength via Tsai–Hill

**Step 1:** Express stresses in PMC under $\sigma_x$ at $\theta = 30°$:
$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x \cos^{2}30° = 0.75\,\sigma_x$
$\sigma_2 = \sigma_x \sin^{2}30° = 0.25\,\sigma_x$
$\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x \sin30°\cos30° = -0.4330\,\sigma_x$

**Step 2:** Plug into Tsai–Hill:
$(0.75\sigma_x/1200)^{2} - (0.75\sigma_x)(0.25\sigma_x)/1200^{2} + (0.25\sigma_x/40)^{2} + (0.4330\sigma_x/60)^{2} = 1$

Numerical coefficients:
$(0.75/1200)^{2}\sigma_x^{2} = 3.906\times10^{-7}\sigma_x^{2}$
$-(0.75 \times 0.25)/1200^{2} = -1.302\times10^{-7}\sigma_x^{2}$
$(0.25/40)^{2} = 3.906\times10^{-5}\sigma_x^{2}$
$(0.4330/60)^{2} = 5.206\times10^{-5}\sigma_x^{2}$

Sum of coefficients:
$0.3906 - 0.1302 + 39.06 + 52.06$ (all $\times 10^{-5}$)
$= 91.38\times10^{-5}\,\sigma_x^{2} = 9.138\times10^{-4}\,\sigma_x^{2}$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\sigma_x^{ult}$:
$\sigma_x^{ult} = \sqrt{1/9.138\times10^{-4}} = \sqrt{1094.3} \approx \mathbf{33.08\ MPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The off-axis strength at 30° is only **~33 MPa** versus **1200 MPa** on-axis — a 36-fold drop. **Matrix-dominated transverse and shear stresses control failure even at moderate fibre misalignment.**

## 3.3 Verification: $\bar{Q}_{16}$ vanishes at $\theta = 0°$ and 90°

**Step 1:** At $\theta = 0°$: $m = 1, n = 0$.
$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})(1)(0) + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})(0)(1) = 0$. ✓

**Step 2:** At $\theta = 90°$: $m = 0, n = 1$.
$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})(0)(1) + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})(1)(0) = 0$. ✓

$\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Off-Axis Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Properties in PMC: E1 E2 G12 nu12 X Y S] --> B[Step 1: Compute Reduced Stiffness Q Matrix]
    B --> C[Step 2: Choose Fibre Orientation theta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Transformation Equations]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Transformed Stiffness Qbar]
    E --> F[Step 5: Derive Off Axis Engineering Constants Ex Ey Gxy nuxy]
    F --> G[Step 6: Apply Stress Transformation to Loads]
    G --> H[Step 7: Select Failure Criterion]
    H --> I{Max Stress or Max Strain}
    H --> J[Tsai Hill]
    H --> K[Tsai Wu]
    I --> L[Compute Off Axis Strength sigma x ult]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Design Check and Margins of Safety]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Lamina with Off-Axis Angle and Sign Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Global Loading Axis x] -->|sigma x applied| B((Lamina Plane))
    B --> C[Local Fibre Axis 1]
    B --> D[Local Transverse Axis 2]
    C ---|rotation theta CCW| A
    D ---|theta plus 90| A
    B --> E[On axis: theta equals 0]
    B --> F[Off axis: 0 less than theta less than 90]
    B --> G[Cross ply: theta equals 90]
```

## 4.3 Functional Map: Coupling Effects versus theta

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[theta equals 0] --> P0[Pure Longitudinal: Qbar16 equals 0, Qbar26 equals 0]
    T45[theta equals 45] --> P45[Maximum Shear Extension Coupling: Qbar16 nonzero, Qbar26 nonzero]
    T90[theta equals 90] --> P90[Pure Transverse: Qbar16 equals 0, Qbar26 equals 0]
    P45 --> Out[Normal Load produces Shear Strain gamma xy]
    Out --> Out2[Triggers Matrix Dominated Failure]
```

## 4.4 Polar Chart Representation (Verbal Description)

A conceptual polar plot of $E_x(\theta)$ exhibits:
- A **lobe of high stiffness** elongated along the fibre direction (0°) — height = $E_1$.
- A **lobe of low stiffness** along the transverse direction (90°) — height = $E_2$.
- A **symmetric dumb-bell shape** about $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 90°$.
- A characteristic **drop near θ ≈ 45°** where the $1/G_{12}$ term dominates.

> [!NOTE]
> Because the same plot for **strength** $\sigma_x^{ult}(\theta)$ is even more dramatic — it has a sharp **cusp at $\theta = 0°$** and flattens to a low plateau near the transverse direction — KTU problems frequently ask: *"At what angle is the off-axis strength minimum?"* The answer is the **shear-controlled range $10° \le \theta \le 30°$** for typical carbon/epoxy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "off-axis lamina" and explain why the coupling stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ are non-zero for such a lamina.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **off-axis lamina** is a unidirectional fibre-reinforced lamina whose fibre direction (axis-1) is rotated by an angle θ ≠ 0° and θ ≠ 90° with respect to the global loading (x-) axis. (1 mark)
Because the loading axes (x, y) no longer coincide with the principal material axes (1, 2), the lamina loses its symmetric alignment of stiffness. (1 mark)
Consequently, a normal stress $\sigma_x$ produces not only extensional strains $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y$ but also a **shear strain** $\gamma_{xy}$, and a shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ produces normal strains. This is captured by the non-vanishing coupling stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{16}, \bar{Q}_{26} \neq 0$ in the transformed constitutive law, signifying **shear–extension coupling**. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State the Tsai–Hill failure criterion for a unidirectional composite lamina. Mention its physical basis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a UD lamina in principal material coordinates:
$$
(\sigma_1/X)^{2} - (\sigma_1\sigma_2)/X^{2} + (\sigma_2/Y)^{2} + (\tau_{12}/S)^{2} \le 1
$$
(2 marks)
where $X, Y, S$ are the longitudinal, transverse, and in-plane shear strengths.
It is a **phenomenological extension of the von Mises yield criterion** to anisotropic materials, treating failure as a single scalar "yield-like" event in stress space. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **Question Paper Pattern:** *"Answer any **one** full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks."*

---

### 🅰️ Question A — Stiffness Transformation (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A UD carbon/epoxy lamina has:
$E_1 = 140$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $G_{12} = 5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.
The lamina is oriented at $\theta = 45°$ with respect to the loading axis.

**(a)** Derive the **off-axis Young's modulus $E_x$** at $\theta = 45°$ and comment on the result.
**(b)** Compute all four components of the **transformed reduced stiffness matrix** $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ at $\theta = 45°$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) (7 marks):**

Step 1: Identify $m = n = 1/\sqrt{2}$ at $\theta = 45°$.
$\therefore m^{4} = n^{4} = 1/4$ and $m^{2}n^{2} = 1/4$.

Step 2: Substitute into the off-axis modulus formula:
$$
\frac{1}{E_x} = \frac{0.25}{140} + \frac{0.25}{10} + 0.25\!\left(\frac{1}{5} - \frac{2 \times 0.30}{140}\right)
$$
(in units of 1/GPa)
$$
= 0.001786 + 0.025000 + 0.25(0.200000 - 0.004286)
$$
$$
= 0.001786 + 0.025000 + 0.25(0.195714) = 0.001786 + 0.025000 + 0.048929 = 0.075714\ \text{GPa}^{-1}
$$
$$
E_x = 1/0.075714 \approx \mathbf{13.21\ GPa}
$$

**Comment:** The off-axis modulus at 45° is only **~9.4 %** of $E_1$ and is **dominated by the matrix shear modulus $G_{12}$** through the $(1/G_{12})$ term. This is why off-axis plies in a laminate contribute mainly to **shear rigidity** rather than to longitudinal stiffness. (1 mark for comment)

**(b) (7 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $Q_{ij}$:
$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}E_2/E_1 = 0.30 \times 10/140 = 0.02143$.
$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.30 \times 0.02143 = 1 - 0.00643 = 0.99357$.
$Q_{11} = 140/0.99357 = 140.91$ GPa.
$Q_{22} = 10/0.99357 = 10.06$ GPa.
$Q_{12} = 0.30 \times 10.06 = 3.019$ GPa.
$Q_{66} = 5$ GPa.

Step 2: At $\theta = 45°$ ($m = n = 1/\sqrt{2}$):
$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^{4} + 2(Q_{12}+2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}n^{4}$
$= 140.91(0.25) + 2(3.019 + 10)(0.25) + 10.06(0.25)$
$= 35.23 + 6.510 + 2.515 = \mathbf{44.25\ GPa}$

$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}n^{4} + 2(Q_{12}+2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}m^{4}$
$= 140.91(0.25) + 6.510 + 10.06(0.25) = \mathbf{44.25\ GPa}$ (by symmetry at 45°)

$\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-4Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{12}(m^{4}+n^{4})$
$= (140.91+10.06-20)(0.25) + 3.019(0.5)$
$= 130.97(0.25) + 1.510 = 32.74 + 1.510 = \mathbf{34.25\ GPa}$

$\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-2Q_{12}-2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{66}(m^{4}+n^{4})$
$= (140.91+10.06-6.04-10)(0.25) + 5(0.5)$
$= 134.93(0.25) + 2.5 = 33.73 + 2.5 = \mathbf{36.23\ GPa}$

For completeness:
$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11}-Q_{12}-2Q_{66})m^{3}n + (Q_{12}-Q_{22}+2Q_{66})mn^{3}$
$= (140.91-3.019-10)(0.125) + (3.019-10.06+10)(0.125)$
$= 127.89(0.125) + 2.959(0.125) = 15.99 + 0.370 = \mathbf{16.36\ GPa}$

$\bar{Q}_{26}$ = (by interchange) $\mathbf{16.36\ GPa}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (i) Do **not** forget to compute $\nu_{21}$ first — many students use $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}$, which is wrong.
> (ii) At $\theta = 45°$, $\bar{Q}_{11} = \bar{Q}_{22}$ and $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26}$ — this is a **special symmetry** valid only for *balanced* materials with $m = n$. **Do not** assume it at other angles.
> (iii) KTU expects the $Q_{ij}$ computation in a separate sub-step; skipping it costs 1 mark.

---

### 🅱️ Question B — Strength Transformation (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

A UD glass/epoxy lamina is loaded in uniaxial tension at an angle θ to the fibres. Material strengths: $X = 1200$ MPa, $Y = 40$ MPa, $S = 60$ MPa.

**(a)** Using the **Tsai–Hill criterion**, derive an expression for the off-axis strength $\sigma_x^{ult}(\theta)$ in terms of the given strengths.
**(b)** Compute $\sigma_x^{ult}$ at $\theta = 15°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 75°, 90°$ and plot/tabulate the off-axis strength variation. Identify the failure mode at each angle.

#### Model Solution

**(a) (7 marks):**

Step 1: Apply stress transformation for uniaxial $\sigma_x$:
$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta$, $\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$.

Step 2: Substitute into the Tsai–Hill criterion:
$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta}{X}\right)^{2} - \frac{\sigma_x^{2}\cos^{2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta}{X^{2}} + \left(\frac{\sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta}{Y}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta}{S}\right)^{2} = 1
$$

Step 3: Factor out $\sigma_x^{2}$:
$$
\sigma_x^{2}\!\left[\frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{X^{2}} - \frac{\cos^{2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta}{X^{2}} + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{Y^{2}} + \frac{\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta}{S^{2}}\right] = 1
$$

Step 4: Solve:
$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_x^{ult}(\theta) = \left[\frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{X^{2}} + \sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{S^{2}} - \frac{1}{X^{2}}\right) + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{Y^{2}}\right]^{-1/2}\;}
$$

(7 marks — full derivation as above)

**(b) (7 marks):**

Tabulating the coefficients inside the brackets, with $X = 1200, Y = 40, S = 60$ MPa:

| θ | $\cos^{4}\theta/X^{2}$ | $\sin^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\theta(1/S^{2}-1/X^{2})$ | $\sin^{4}\theta/Y^{2}$ | Sum | $\sigma_x^{ult}$ (MPa) | Dominant Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 6.944×10⁻⁷ | 0 | 0 | 6.944×10⁻⁷ | **1200.0** | Fibre tensile |
| 15° | 5.225×10⁻⁷ | 8.058×10⁻⁵ | 9.313×10⁻⁶ | 9.020×10⁻⁵ | **105.3** | Shear-controlled |
| 30° | 2.604×10⁻⁷ | 1.823×10⁻⁴ | 1.563×10⁻⁴ | 3.388×10⁻⁴ | **54.3** | Shear + transverse |
| 45° | 6.944×10⁻⁸ | 1.736×10⁻⁴ | 1.563×10⁻³ | 1.737×10⁻³ | **24.0** | Transverse tensile |
| 60° | 6.944×10⁻⁹ | 1.215×10⁻⁴ | 1.563×10⁻³ | 1.685×10⁻³ | **24.4** | Transverse tensile |
| 75° | 9.421×10⁻¹¹ | 3.940×10⁻⁵ | 6.156×10⁻⁴ | 6.550×10⁻⁴ | **39.1** | Transverse tensile |
| 90° | 0 | 0 | 1.563×10⁻³ | 1.563×10⁻³ | **25.3** * | Transverse tensile |

*At 90°, the Tsai–Hill gives $\sigma_x^{ult} = Y$ only if we interpret the off-axis as $\sigma_1$ aligning with the *original* 1-direction. In our formulation, at 90° $\sigma_2 = \sigma_x$ ⇒ strength = $Y = 40$ MPa. Recompute: actually at θ = 90°: $\cos^{4}=0$, $\sin^{4}=1$ → coefficient = $1/Y^{2} = 1/1600$ → $\sigma_x^{ult} = Y = 40$ MPa.

**Observations and failure mode identification:** (1 mark for the conceptual analysis)
- The strength drops **sharply by a factor of ~22** within just 15° of fibre misalignment.
- The **minimum strength (~24 MPa)** occurs near **θ ≈ 50°** and is **matrix/transverse dominated**.
- The **shear-controlled failure band** is roughly **15° ≤ θ ≤ 30°**.
- The **fibre-dominated region** is essentially limited to **θ ≤ 5°**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> (i) Many students write the Tsai–Hill criterion with the wrong sign of the **interaction term** $\sigma_1\sigma_2$. The correct sign is **negative** because $X_T \neq X_C$ in tension-only mode.
> (ii) Forgetting to convert **MPa units consistently** — use $10^{12}$ for $\text{MPa}^{2}$ to avoid scientific notation errors.
> (iii) A common error is to use $\tau_{12} = \sigma_x\sin\theta\cos\theta$ **without the negative sign**; the negative sign only affects the sign of shear and not the **square term**, so it does not change $\sigma_x^{ult}$ — but students should mention this for full marks.
> (iv) **Failure mode identification** must be backed by comparing the three stress ratios; merely stating "shear failure" without justification loses 2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Off-axis angle θ** is measured CCW from loading x-axis to fibre 1-axis.
- ✅ At θ = 0° and θ = 90°: $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$ (no coupling).
- ✅ At θ = 45° (and 135°): $\bar{Q}_{11} = \bar{Q}_{22}$ and $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26}$ (for *balanced* orthotropic laminae).
- ✅ The off-axis modulus $E_x$ is **always ≤ $E_1$** and **≥ $E_2$**; minimum typically occurs near 45°.
- ✅ The **off-axis strength** falls much more steeply than the modulus: even 5°–10° misalignment causes drastic strength loss.
- ✅ The **1/G₁₂ term** in $1/E_x$ is the largest contributor at intermediate angles; the matrix **shear modulus** dominates the off-axis stiffness.
- ✅ **Tsai–Hill** is the most common strength criterion in KTU problems; **Tsai–Wu** is more general but rarely used without interaction data.
- ✅ Always compute $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ **first** before $Q_{ij}$.
- ✅ Off-axis **shear-extension coupling** is the root cause of **bend–twist coupling** in unsymmetric laminates.
- ✅ **Units tip:** Express all moduli in **GPa** for cleaner arithmetic; convert to **Pa** only at the final substitution.
- ✅ Most-tested KTU numerical: $\sigma_x^{ult}(\theta)$ via **Tsai–Hill** with the closed-form derived in Question A(b).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bidirectional and Random Fibre Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bidirectional and Random Fibre Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bidirectional Fibre Composites — Formal Definition

A **Bidirectional Fibre Composite** is a class of fibre-reinforced composite in which the reinforcing fibres are oriented in **two principal directions** (typically designated as 0° and 90°) within a single lamina or laminate plane. This configuration is realised in two principal industrial forms:

1. **Cross-ply laminates** — produced by stacking unidirectional (UD) plies at orthogonal orientations, e.g., [0/90]s, [0/90/0/90].
2. **Woven fabric composites** — produced by interlacing warp (0°) and weft (90°) fibre tows into a textile preform that is then impregnated with a matrix (resin transfer moulding, prepreg lay-up, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bidirectional composites form the most widely used structural form of fibre-reinforced plastics (FRP) because they provide **near-isotropic in-plane stiffness** and balanced mechanical performance under multi-axial loads.

### 1.2 Random Fibre Composites — Formal Definition

A **Random Fibre Composite** is a composite in which the discontinuous fibres are distributed with **no preferred orientation** in the matrix. The fibres may be:
- **Short (chopped) fibres** randomly dispersed in a thermoset or thermoplastic matrix (SMC, BMC, injection-moulded thermoplastics).
- **Chopped Strand Mat (CSM)** — randomly oriented glass fibres held by a binder.

The mechanical behaviour is **quasi-isotropic in the plane of the laminate**, since the orientation averaging produces equal properties in all in-plane directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The degree of fibre orientation randomness is quantified by the **orientation factor (η₀)**, where η₀ = 1/5 for perfectly 3D-random, and η₀ = 1/3 for 2D (planar) random orientations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

- **Bidirectional Composite Analogy:** Think of a **bamboo mat**. The bamboo strips run in two perpendicular directions, woven together. No matter which way you push or pull on the mat, it resists equally well in both directions. This is exactly what a bidirectional composite gives you — **balanced strength in two directions**, like a chessboard pattern of reinforcement inside a plastic sheet.

- **Random Fibre Composite Analogy:** Imagine **steel wool (or coconut fibres) mixed into concrete**. The fibres point in every which way. The composite doesn't care which direction you pull it from — it has roughly the same stiffness and strength everywhere. It's like a **scrambled basket weave** rather than a neatly ordered one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Bidirectional = **two preferred directions** (anisotropic but balanced). Random = **no preferred direction** (quasi-isotropic).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Cross-Ply (Bidirectional) Laminates

Consider a symmetric cross-ply laminate [0/90]s made of unidirectional plies. The classical laminated plate theory (CLPT) provides the in-plane stiffness matrix as:

$$\begin{aligned}
[A] &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \cdot t_k
\end{aligned}$$

where $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$ are the **transformed reduced stiffnesses** of the k-th ply and $t_k$ is its thickness.

### 2.2 Stiffness Components for a [0/90]s Cross-Ply Laminate

For equal-thickness plies ($t_0 = t_{90}$), the effective in-plane Young's moduli are:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x &= E_{11} = \frac{1}{t} \left[ Q_{11} \cdot t_0 + Q_{22} \cdot t_{90} \right] = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_y &= E_{22} = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}{2} = E_x \quad \text{(for balanced cross-ply)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{xy} &= \frac{Q_{12} + Q_{66}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{xy} &= \frac{Q_{12} + \nu_{12} Q_{22}}{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}
\end{aligned}$$

The above confirms that a balanced [0/90]s laminate is **quasi-isotropic in-plane** with respect to stiffness.

## 2.3 Woven Fabric Composites

Woven fabrics are characterised by the **weave architecture**:
- **Plain weave** — maximum crimp, lowest drapability, high stability.
- **Twill weave** — moderate crimp, good drapability, used for curved surfaces.
- **Satin weave** — minimum crimp, best mechanical translation efficiency.

The **crimp** (undulation of tows) causes **knock-down factors** in stiffness compared to UD plies. Empirical knock-down factors typically used:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{crimp} \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.4 Random Fibre Composites — Halpin-Tsai Equation

The **Halpin-Tsai equation** is the workhorse formula for predicting the modulus of short-fibre random composites. For a random planar (2D) orientation of short fibres, the effective modulus is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{random} &= \frac{3}{8} E_L + \frac{5}{8} E_T
\end{aligned}$$

where $E_L$ and $E_T$ are the longitudinal and transverse moduli of a **unidirectional** short-fibre composite, computed via the Halpin-Tsai equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_L}{E_m} &= \frac{1 + 2(l/d)\eta_L V_f}{1 - \eta_L V_f}, \quad \eta_L = \frac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2(l/d)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_T}{E_m} &= \frac{1 + 2\eta_T V_f}{1 - \eta_T V_f}, \quad \eta_T = \frac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2}
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.5 Modified Rule of Mixtures (Random Fibres)

For random short fibre composites, a simpler **orientation-averaged rule of mixtures** is used:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{random} &= \eta_0 \cdot \eta_l \cdot E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $\eta_0$ = fibre orientation factor (1/5 for 3D random, 1/3 for 2D random)
- $\eta_l$ = fibre length efficiency factor (0 < ηl < 1)
- $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction
- $E_f, E_m$ = fibre and matrix Young's moduli

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A_{ij} = \sum \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} t_k$ | Extensional stiffness matrix (CLPT) | N/m |
| 2 | $E_x = (Q_{11} + Q_{22})/2$ | Cross-ply [0/90]s longitudinal modulus | Pa |
| 3 | $G_{xy} = (Q_{12} + Q_{66})/2$ | Cross-ply in-plane shear modulus | Pa |
| 4 | $E_{random} = \eta_0 \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m(1-V_f)$ | Modified rule of mixtures (random) | Pa |
| 5 | $E_{random} = (3/8)E_L + (5/8)E_T$ | 2D random composite modulus | Pa |
| 6 | $\eta_L = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2(l/d)}$ | Halpin-Tsai longitudinal factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_T = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2}$ | Halpin-Tsai transverse factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cu} = \eta_0 \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_{mu}(1-V_f)$ | Random composite ultimate strength | Pa |
| 9 | $E_x = E_y$ (balanced cross-ply) | Quasi-isotropic in-plane | Pa |
| 10 | $l/d$ | Fibre aspect ratio (length/diameter) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Cross-ply laminates are used in **wind turbine blades, aircraft wing skins, boat hulls, and pressure vessels**. Random fibre composites dominate **automotive panels (SMC/BMC), electronic enclosures, and consumer goods** because of low cost and formability.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** Cross-ply carbon/epoxy laminates for satellite panels (dimensional stability).
- **Automotive:** SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) — random chopped glass + polyester — for body panels.
- **Marine:** Woven glass/polyester for boat hulls (balanced impact + fatigue resistance).
- **Civil:** Glass random-fibre rebars as concrete reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Modulus of a [0/90]s Cross-Ply Laminate

**Starting Point:** Classical Laminated Plate Theory (CLPT) for a symmetric laminate.

### Step 1: Set up the A-matrix (extensional stiffness)

For a laminate with N plies, each of thickness $t_k$ and transformed reduced stiffness $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \cdot t_k
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Apply to a [0/90]s laminate

A [0/90]s laminate has 4 plies, two at 0° and two at 90°, with equal individual thickness $t_0$. The total laminate thickness is $t = 4t_0$.

For a 0° ply, $\bar{Q}^{(0)} = Q$, the on-axis reduced stiffness.

For a 90° ply, the transformed reduced stiffness has the components (using CLPT rotation rules):

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(90)} &= Q_{22} \\
\bar{Q}_{22}^{(90)} &= Q_{11} \\
\bar{Q}_{12}^{(90)} &= Q_{12} \\
\bar{Q}_{66}^{(90)} &= Q_{66}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute $A_{11}$ (which gives E_x)

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &= \bar{Q}_{11}^{(0)} t_0 + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(90)} t_{90} + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(90)} t_{90} + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(0)} t_0 \\
A_{11} &= Q_{11} t_0 + Q_{22} t_{90} + Q_{22} t_{90} + Q_{11} t_0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $t_0 = t_{90}$ (balanced):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{11} = 2 Q_{11} t_0 + 2 Q_{22} t_0 = 2 t_0 (Q_{11} + Q_{22})
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute effective $E_x$

The laminate modulus is $E_x = A_{11}/t$ where $t = 4t_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x = \frac{2 t_0 (Q_{11} + Q_{22})}{4 t_0} = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

By symmetry, $E_y = E_x$. **Hence a balanced cross-ply is in-plane quasi-isotropic.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: 2D Random Fibre Modulus (Halpin-Tsai Laminate Analogy)

### Step 1: Laminate analogy concept

A 2D random fibre composite is treated as a "laminate" of unidirectional short-fibre plies at all angles from 0° to 360° with equal weight. For each ply at angle θ, the longitudinal stiffness $Q_{11}(\theta)$ is averaged.

### Step 2: Orientation averaging

The average value of $\cos^4\theta$ over θ ∈ [0, 2π] is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle \cos^4\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \cos^4\theta \, d\theta = \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

The average of $\sin^4\theta$ is also $3/8$, and the average of $\cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle \cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Combine using laminate theory

For a UD short-fibre composite, the on-axis stiffnesses are $Q_{11} = E_L$, $Q_{22} = E_T$, $Q_{12} = \nu_{LT} E_T$, $Q_{66} = G_{LT}$.

The laminate-averaged modulus (analogy of [0/90] averaging, but extended to all angles) yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{random} = \frac{3}{8} E_L + \frac{5}{8} E_T
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **classical result** of Tsai-Pagano for 2D random short-fibre composites. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Orientation Factor (η₀) for Random Composites

### Step 1: Rule of mixtures for aligned fibre

For a unidirectional composite loaded along the fibre direction:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{UD} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Average over all fibre orientations

For a 3D random distribution, the effective contribution of the fibre modulus is reduced by a factor that depends on the average of $\cos^4\theta$ (Krenchel's factor). For 3D randomness:

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle \cos^4\theta \rangle_{3D} = \frac{1}{5}
\end{aligned}$$

For 2D randomness (in-plane):

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle \cos^4\theta \rangle_{2D} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Write the modified rule of mixtures

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{random} = \eta_0 \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\eta_0 = 1/5$ (3D random) or $\eta_0 = 1/3$ (2D random), and $\eta_l$ is the fibre length efficiency factor (Cox–Shear lag):

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_l = 1 - \frac{\tanh(\beta l/2)}{\beta l/2}, \quad \beta = \sqrt{\frac{2 G_m}{E_f r^2 \ln(R/r)}}
\end{aligned}$$

with $G_m$ = matrix shear modulus, $r$ = fibre radius, $R$ = interfibre spacing. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A glass/polyester cross-ply laminate [0/90]s has 4 plies, each 0.25 mm thick. Fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$. Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $\nu_m = 0.35$. Compute $E_x$ and $E_y$.

### Step 1: Compute $E_L$ and $E_T$ (UD properties)

$$\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f) = 72(0.45) + 3.5(0.55) \\
E_L &= 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_T &= \frac{E_m}{1 - \nu_m^2} \cdot \frac{1 + 2\eta_T V_f}{1 - \eta_T V_f} \\
\eta_T &= \frac{72/3.5 - 1}{72/3.5 + 2} = \frac{19.57}{22.57} = 0.867 \\
\frac{E_T}{E_m} &= \frac{1 + 2(0.867)(0.45)}{1 - 0.867(0.45)} = \frac{1.780}{0.610} = 2.918 \\
E_T &= 2.918 \times 3.5 = 10.21 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute Q-matrix components

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{11} &= \frac{E_L}{1 - \nu_{LT}\nu_{TL}}, \quad \nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m(1-V_f) = 0.22(0.45) + 0.35(0.55) = 0.2915 \\
\nu_{TL} &= \nu_{LT} \cdot \frac{E_T}{E_L} = 0.2915 \times \frac{10.21}{34.325} = 0.0867 \\
1 - \nu_{LT}\nu_{TL} &= 1 - (0.2915)(0.0867) = 0.9747 \\
Q_{11} &= \frac{34.325}{0.9747} = 35.22 \text{ GPa} \\
Q_{22} &= \frac{10.21}{0.9747} = 10.48 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute $E_x$ and $E_y$ of cross-ply

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x = E_y = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}{2} = \frac{35.22 + 10.48}{2} = 22.85 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The cross-ply laminate has $E_x = E_y = 22.85$ GPa, confirming in-plane quasi-isotropy.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a unidirectional, bidirectional, and random fibre composite with respect to fibre orientation and in-plane stiffness isotropy.**

**Model Answer:**
A **unidirectional (UD) composite** has all fibres aligned in a single direction, giving maximum stiffness along the fibre axis ($E_1$) but very low stiffness transverse ($E_2$) — strongly **anisotropic**. A **bidirectional composite** (e.g., [0/90]s cross-ply or woven fabric) has fibres in two perpendicular directions, producing **quasi-isotropic in-plane behaviour** where $E_x = E_y$ (for balanced lay-up). A **random fibre composite** has discontinuous fibres with no preferred orientation, also yielding **quasi-isotropic in-plane properties** but with lower absolute stiffness due to the orientation factor $\eta_0 < 1$. The progression is: UD (highly anisotropic) → Bidirectional (balanced, 2 principal directions) → Random (uniform, no preferred direction).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the fibre orientation factor and state its value for 2D and 3D random fibre distributions.**

**Model Answer:**
The **fibre orientation factor ($\eta_0$)** is a dimensionless constant that quantifies the reduction in the fibre contribution to composite stiffness when the fibres are randomly oriented rather than perfectly aligned. It is the average value of $\cos^4\theta$ over the orientation distribution. For a **2D (planar) random** distribution, $\eta_0 = 1/3$. For a **3D random** distribution, $\eta_0 = 1/5$. It appears directly in the modified rule of mixtures: $E_{random} = \eta_0 \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m(1-V_f)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the effective in-plane Young's modulus $E_x = E_y = (Q_{11} + Q_{22})/2$ for a balanced [0/90]s cross-ply laminate using classical laminated plate theory. State all assumptions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A balanced woven glass/epoxy fabric composite has equal warp and weft tows with $V_f = 0.40$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, and crimp knock-down factor $\eta_{crimp} = 0.90$. Compute (i) the longitudinal modulus of a single UD ply using the rule of mixtures, and (ii) the in-plane modulus of the woven laminate including the crimp factor.

### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1:** Classical Laminated Plate Theory (CLPT) gives the laminate extensional stiffness matrix:

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} t_k$$

**Step 2:** For a [0/90]s laminate (4 plies, equal thickness $t_0$), the on-axis reduced stiffnesses of a 0° ply are $Q_{ij}$, and a 90° ply has $\bar{Q}_{11}^{(90)} = Q_{22}$, $\bar{Q}_{22}^{(90)} = Q_{11}$, $\bar{Q}_{12}^{(90)} = Q_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66}^{(90)} = Q_{66}$.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$A_{11} = Q_{11} t_0 + Q_{22} t_0 + Q_{22} t_0 + Q_{11} t_0 = 2t_0(Q_{11} + Q_{22})$$

**Step 4:** Total thickness $t = 4t_0$. The laminate modulus:

$$E_x = \frac{A_{11}}{t} = \frac{2t_0(Q_{11} + Q_{22})}{4t_0} = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22}}{2}$$

By symmetry of [0/90]s, $E_y = E_x$. **Assumptions:** (i) Symmetric lay-up, (ii) equal ply thickness, (iii) plane stress, (iv) linear elastic behaviour, (v) perfect bonding, (vi) classical thin-plate kinematics.

### Model Solution to A(b)

**(i) Longitudinal modulus of single UD ply (rule of mixtures):**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m(1 - V_f) = 72(0.40) + 3.5(0.60) = 28.8 + 2.1 = 30.9 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) In-plane modulus of woven laminate (balanced warp/weft, ideal):**

$$E_{woven} = \frac{E_L + E_T}{2}$$

Using $E_T \approx E_m/(1-\nu_m^2) \cdot \text{correction} \approx 8.5$ GPa (computed from Halpin-Tsai for transverse):

$$E_{woven,ideal} = \frac{30.9 + 8.5}{2} = 19.7 \text{ GPa}$$

With crimp knock-down factor:

$$E_{woven} = \eta_{crimp} \cdot E_{woven,ideal} = 0.90 \times 19.7 = 17.73 \text{ GPa}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression $E_{random} = (3/8)E_L + (5/8)E_T$ for a 2D random short-fibre composite using the laminate analogy and orientation averaging.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 2D random glass/polyester composite has $V_f = 0.30$, $l/d = 100$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Compute the effective modulus using (i) the Halpin-Tsai approach, and (ii) the modified rule of mixtures with $\eta_0 = 1/3$ and $\eta_l = 0.92$.

### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1:** A 2D random composite is treated as a fictitious laminate of unidirectional plies at all angles θ ∈ [0, 2π], each with equal volume fraction.

**Step 2:** The on-axis stiffness $Q_{11}$ rotated to angle θ is (from transformation rules):

$$\bar{Q}_{11}(\theta) = Q_{11}\cos^4\theta + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta + Q_{22}\sin^4\theta$$

**Step 3:** Average over θ ∈ [0, 2π]:

$$\langle \bar{Q}_{11} \rangle = Q_{11}\langle\cos^4\theta\rangle + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\langle\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta\rangle + Q_{22}\langle\sin^4\theta\rangle$$

Using $\langle\cos^4\theta\rangle = \langle\sin^4\theta\rangle = 3/8$ and $\langle\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta\rangle = 1/8$:

$$\langle \bar{Q}_{11} \rangle = \frac{3}{8}Q_{11} + \frac{1}{4}(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66}) + \frac{3}{8}Q_{22}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the laminated effective modulus. For a UD composite, $E_L = Q_{11}$ (approximately, when $\nu_{LT}\nu_{TL} \ll 1$) and $E_T = Q_{22}$, while $G_{LT} = Q_{66}$ and $\nu_{LT} = Q_{12}/E_L$. Substituting and simplifying (Tsai-Pagano derivation):

$$E_{random} = \frac{3}{8}E_L + \frac{5}{8}E_T \quad \blacksquare$$

### Model Solution to B(b)

**(i) Halpin-Tsai approach:**

Compute $\eta_L$ and $\eta_T$:

$$\eta_L = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2(l/d)} = \frac{72/3.5 - 1}{72/3.5 + 2(100)} = \frac{19.571}{219.571} = 0.0891$$

$$\eta_T = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2} = \frac{19.571}{22.571} = 0.867$$

$$\frac{E_L}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2(100)(0.0891)(0.30)}{1 - 0.0891(0.30)} = \frac{1 + 5.347}{1 - 0.0267} = \frac{6.347}{0.9733} = 6.521$$

$$E_L = 6.521 \times 3.5 = 22.82 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\frac{E_T}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2(0.867)(0.30)}{1 - 0.867(0.30)} = \frac{1.520}{0.740} = 2.054$$

$$E_T = 2.054 \times 3.5 = 7.19 \text{ GPa}$$

$$E_{random} = \frac{3}{8}(22.82) + \frac{5}{8}(7.19) = 8.557 + 4.494 = 13.05 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Modified rule of mixtures:**

$$E_{random} = \eta_0 \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m(1 - V_f)$$

$$E_{random} = \frac{1}{3}(0.92)(72)(0.30) + 3.5(0.70) = 6.624 + 2.45 = 9.07 \text{ GPa}$$

**Comparison:** The Halpin-Tsai method gives $E \approx 13.05$ GPa (more accurate as it accounts for stress transfer), while the modified ROM gives $E \approx 9.07$ GPa. The difference arises because the simple ROM uses an approximate orientation factor without distinguishing between $E_L$ and $E_T$ contributions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the orientation factor** $\eta_0$ in the rule of mixtures for random composites — students often write $E = E_f V_f + E_m(1-V_f)$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing 2D and 3D random:** $\eta_0 = 1/3$ for 2D (planar) and $\eta_0 = 1/5$ for 3D. Wrong value = full marks lost.
> 3. **Wrong averaging in Halpin-Tsai:** $E_{random} = (3/8)E_L + (5/8)E_T$ is for **2D random**; for 3D, the coefficients change to $1/5$ and $4/5$ (and the analysis is on $E_{11}$ and $E_{22}$ averaged).
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (symmetric, balanced, thin laminate, perfect bonding) — KTU examiners explicitly look for these in 7-mark derivations.
> 5. **Crimp factor omission** for woven composites — examiners expect an explicit $\eta_{crimp}$ multiplier.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bidirectional composites** have fibres in **two perpendicular directions**, realised as cross-ply laminates or woven fabrics.
- A **balanced [0/90]s cross-ply** has $E_x = E_y = (Q_{11} + Q_{22})/2$ — **quasi-isotropic in-plane stiffness**.
- **Woven fabrics** exhibit **crimp** that reduces stiffness by a factor $\eta_{crimp} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$.
- **Random short-fibre composites** use the **Halpin-Tsai equation** or the **modified rule of mixtures** with $\eta_0$ orientation factor.
- **Orientation factor:** $\eta_0 = 1/3$ (2D random) and $\eta_0 = 1/5$ (3D random).
- **2D random modulus formula:** $E_{random} = (3/8)E_L + (5/8)E_T$ (Tsai-Pagano).
- **Modified ROM:** $E_{random} = \eta_0 \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m(1-V_f)$.
- **Industrial applications:** Cross-ply — aerospace, wind blades; Woven — marine, automotive; Random SMC/BMC — automotive panels, electronics.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $\langle\cos^4\theta\rangle_{2D} = 3/8$, $\langle\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta\rangle_{2D} = 1/8$, $\langle\cos^4\theta\rangle_{3D} = 1/5$.
- **Assumption checklist for KTU answers:** symmetric, balanced, linear-elastic, perfect bonding, plane stress, thin-plate kinematics.
- **Comparison mnemonic:** **"UD is strong-but-lopsided, BiDi is balanced-two-way, Random is uniform-everywhere."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Unit Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Unit Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Unit Cell** is the smallest repeating three-dimensional structural unit of a crystalline solid which, when translated repeatedly along the three principal crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal lattice without any gaps or overlaps. It is characterised by **six lattice parameters**: three edge lengths $a$, $b$, $c$ along the $x$-, $y$-, $z$-axes respectively, and three inter-axial angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ between these axes.

In the context of **composite materials**, the unit cell concept is fundamental because the macroscopic properties of fibre-reinforced or particulate-reinforced composites (such as stiffness, strength, thermal expansion, and density) are often predicted by modelling a representative repeating volume element (RVE) — a direct conceptual analogue of the crystallographic unit cell — and then applying periodic boundary conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT742 / Module 3):** The unit cell is the cornerstone concept required to understand crystallographic structure of reinforcing phases (e.g., ceramic whiskers, metal matrices), calculate theoretical density, predict slip systems, and justify anisotropic behaviour of crystalline reinforcements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tiling a large kitchen floor with identical decorative tiles. The **single tile** itself is your *unit cell*. By repeating that exact tile — without rotating, flipping, or cutting — across the entire floor, you regenerate the entire pattern. The unit cell is the "tile" of a crystal.

A second analogy more relevant to composites: Consider a honeycomb sandwich panel used in an aircraft wing. The **single hexagonal cell** of the honeycomb is the *unit cell* of the panel. By replicating it across the wing surface, the full panel is constructed. Engineers analyse **one unit cell** in finite element software, then *extrapolate* the response to the whole wing — saving enormous computational effort.

A third analogy: think of the unit cell as a **brick** in a wall. Every brick is identical; by stacking bricks, the entire wall is formed. The "brick" must, however, capture the symmetry and geometry of the wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two atoms sitting at corners of a cube are NOT considered equivalent to atoms at face centres.** This distinction is the foundation of the seven crystal systems and the 14 Bravais lattices.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The key quantitative descriptors that *define* any unit cell are tabulated below for quick recall:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|--------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Lattice constant | $a$ (cubic) | **metres (m)** or **Ångström (Å)** | Edge length of the cubic cell |
| Atomic radius | $R$ | **m** or **Å** | Radius of the hard-sphere atom |
| Atomic mass | $A$ | **kg·mol⁻¹** or **amu** | Molar mass of the element |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** | Atoms per mole |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg·m⁻³** | Mass per unit volume |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, remember that **1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m = 0.1 nm**. Always convert lattice parameters to SI units (metres) before substituting into density formulas to avoid losing marks.

## 1.4 Classification of Unit Cells — A First Glance

Unit cells fall into **two broad categories**:

1. **Primitive (P) unit cell** — atoms (or lattice points) reside *only* at the eight corners of the parallelepiped. Each corner atom is shared among **8** adjacent unit cells, so the net contribution per corner atom is $\frac{1}{8}$.
2. **Non-primitive unit cell** — additional atoms are positioned at interior or face locations, leading to **body-centred (I)**, **face-centred (F)**, or **base-centred (C)** variants.

A combination of the **seven crystal systems** with these centring types yields the famous **14 Bravais lattices**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Crystal Systems — Foundation Logic

Every crystal — and by extension, every crystalline reinforcement in a composite — belongs to one of **seven crystal systems**, distinguished solely by the values of $a, b, c$ and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$.

| # | Crystal System | Edge Constraints | Angle Constraints |
|---|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Cubic | $a = b = c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 2 | Tetragonal | $a = b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 3 | Orthorhombic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 4 | Hexagonal | $a = b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = 90°,\ \gamma = 120°$ |
| 5 | Rhombohedral (Trigonal) | $a = b = c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma \neq 90°$ |
| 6 | Monoclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \gamma = 90°,\ \beta \neq 90°$ |
| 7 | Triclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha \neq \beta \neq \gamma \neq 90°$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the table:** "$\neq$" means "not equal to"; the values listed are constraints that the parameters must satisfy for the crystal to belong to that system.

## 2.2 The Four Centring Types

| Centring Type | Symbol | Additional Lattice Points |
|---------------|--------|---------------------------|
| Primitive | P | 8 corners only |
| Body-centred | I | 8 corners + 1 body centre |
| Face-centred | F | 8 corners + 6 face centres |
| Base-centred | C | 8 corners + 2 opposite face centres (top & bottom) |

Combining 7 systems × 4 centring types (with restrictions) gives **14 Bravais lattices** — the foundational classification Auguste Bravais proposed in 1848.

## 2.3 Why Some Centre-Type Combinations are Forbidden

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Why is there no face-centred tetragonal lattice?"* The answer relies on symmetry:

- **Cubic + P, I, F** — all valid (3 lattices).
- **Tetragonal + P, I** only — an F-centred tetragonal can be re-described as a *body-centred tetragonal* with a smaller cell, so F is redundant.
- **Orthorhombic + P, I, F, C** — all four are distinct (4 lattices).
- **Hexagonal + P** only (1 lattice).
- **Rhombohedral + P** only (1 lattice).
- **Monoclinic + P, C** only (2 lattices).
- **Triclinic + P** only (1 lattice).

**Total = 3 + 2 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 14 Bravais lattices.** ✓

## 2.4 Number of Atoms per Unit Cell

The effective number of atoms belonging to *one* unit cell is:

$$n_{\text{eff}} = \underbrace{\frac{N_c}{8}}_{\text{corners}} + \underbrace{\frac{N_f}{2}}_{\text{faces}} + \underbrace{\frac{N_e}{4}}_{\text{edges}} + \underbrace{N_i}_{\text{interior}}$$

where:
- $N_c$ = number of atoms at corners (each shared by 8 cells → contributes $\frac{1}{8}$)
- $N_f$ = number of atoms at face centres (each shared by 2 cells → contributes $\frac{1}{2}$)
- $N_e$ = number of atoms at edges (each shared by 4 cells → contributes $\frac{1}{4}$)
- $N_i$ = number of atoms fully inside the cell (contributes 1)

**Application to common cells:**

| Cell Type | Corners | Faces | Body | Effective Atoms ($n$) |
|-----------|---------|-------|------|------------------------|
| Simple Cubic (SC) | 8 | 0 | 0 | $8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1$ |
| Body-Centred Cubic (BCC) | 8 | 0 | 1 | $1 + 1 = 2$ |
| Face-Centred Cubic (FCC) | 8 | 6 | 0 | $1 + 3 = 4$ |
| Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) | 12 (corners) | 2 (basal) | 3 (interior) | $12 \times \frac{1}{6} + 2 \times \frac{1}{2} + 3 = 6$ |

## 2.5 Coordination Number (CN)

The coordination number is the **number of nearest-neighbour atoms** equidistant from a given atom.

| Structure | CN | Geometry |
|-----------|-----|----------|
| Simple Cubic | 6 | Octahedral |
| BCC | 8 | Cubic |
| FCC | 12 | Cubo-octahedral |
| HCP | 12 | Cubo-octahedral |

## 2.6 Relationship Between Atomic Radius ($R$) and Lattice Parameter ($a$)

This is the **single most heavily-tested relation** in KTU Module 3:

$$a_{\text{SC}} = 2R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{a}{2}$$

$$\sqrt{3}\,a_{\text{BCC}} = 4R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$$

$$\sqrt{2}\,a_{\text{FCC}} = 4R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}$$

For **HCP**, the $c/a$ ideal ratio is $\sqrt{\tfrac{8}{3}} \approx 1.633$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of the numerical questions asked on Unit Cell in KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Cell |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Volume of cubic unit cell | $V = a^{3}$ | Cubic systems |
| 2 | Volume of HCP unit cell | $V = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^{2}\,c$ | HCP only |
| 3 | Atoms per cell | $n = n_c/8 + n_f/2 + n_e/4 + n_i$ | All |
| 4 | APF (Packing Fraction) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{V}$ | All |
| 5 | Theoretical density | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{N_A \cdot V}$ | All |
| 6 | SC packing efficiency | $\pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ (52.36 %) | SC |
| 7 | BCC packing efficiency | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8} \approx 0.6802$ (68.02 %) | BCC |
| 8 | FCC packing efficiency | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi}{6} \approx 0.7405$ (74.05 %) | FCC |
| 9 | HCP packing efficiency | Same as FCC = 0.7405 (74.05 %) | HCP ideal |
| 10 | Void fraction | $1 - \text{APF}$ | All |

> [!WARNING]
> Never write density in *g·cm⁻³* directly in $V = a^{3}$ calculations if $a$ is given in Å. Convert: $1\ \text{Å}^{3} = 10^{-24}\ \text{cm}^{3}$. Examiners penalise unit mismatches harshly.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Composite Engineering

Understanding unit cells is *not* purely academic for a composite-materials engineer:

- **Selection of reinforcing phases:** ceramic reinforcements such as **SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C** often possess HCP or FCC unit cells whose slip systems directly influence their *ductility/brittleness* and therefore their load-bearing behaviour in an MMC (metal-matrix composite).
- **Predicting thermal expansion mismatch:** the linear coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) is a tensor property tied to the crystal symmetry of each phase. In a unit cell of a composite RVE, the CTE anisotropy drives **residual thermal stresses** during cure/cooling.
- **Modelling fibre packing:** the arrangement of fibres in a unidirectional composite (square, hexagonal, or random) is *literally* a 2-D unit-cell problem — fibre volume fraction $V_f$ is bounded by the packing geometry of the chosen unit cell (max $V_f \approx 0.907$ for hexagonal packing of fibres).
- **Interfacial crystallography:** epitaxial matching between fibre and matrix (e.g., carbon fibre / aluminium) is described by the lattice misfit $\delta = \dfrac{\vert a_f - a_m \vert}{a_m}$, directly computed from unit-cell parameters.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between $R$ and $a$ for an FCC Unit Cell

**Problem context:** In FCC metals like Cu, Al, Ni, Au, atoms touch each other along the **face diagonal** of the cube.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the face diagonal.**
The face of an FCC cube is a square of side $a$. Its diagonal length is given by the Pythagorean theorem:

$$d_{\text{face}} = \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2}} = a\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2 — Counting atoms along the face diagonal.**
The face diagonal of an FCC cell passes through:
- One corner atom (radius $R$, contributing $R$),
- One face-centre atom (radius $R$, contributing $2R$ at its full diameter),
- The opposite corner atom (radius $R$, contributing $R$).

Hence the total length occupied by atoms along the diagonal is:

$$L_{\text{atoms}} = R + 2R + R = 4R$$

**Step 3 — Equating the two expressions.**
Since the atoms touch along this diagonal, geometry demands:

$$a\sqrt{2} = 4R$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $R$:**

$$R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}}$$

This is the exact algebraic relation; numerical values: if $a = 4.0\ \text{Å}$, then $R = 1.414\ \text{Å}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of an FCC Cell

**Step 1 — Identify the volume occupied by atoms.**
Number of atoms per FCC cell $n = 4$. Volume of one atom (hard sphere model):

$$V_{\text{atom}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$$

Total atomic volume in one cell:

$$V_{\text{occupied}} = n \cdot V_{\text{atom}} = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = \frac{16}{3}\pi R^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the total cell volume.**
For a cubic cell:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R$ in terms of $a$.**
From §3.1, $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}$, so:

$$R^{3} = \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}\right)^{3} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{64} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{32}$$

**Step 4 — Plug into occupied volume:**

$$V_{\text{occupied}} = \frac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{32} = \frac{16\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{96} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Compute APF:**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{occupied}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{6}}{a^{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi}{6}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{1.4142 \times 3.1416}{6} = \frac{4.4429}{6} \approx 0.7405$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi}{6} \approx 0.7405\ \text{or}\ 74.05\%}$$

**Physical meaning:** In an FCC arrangement, ~74 % of the total crystal volume is filled by atoms; the remaining ~26 % is *void space* (interstitial sites — important in composites for hosting solute atoms and forming precipitates).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — APF of a BCC Unit Cell

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
In BCC, the atoms touch along the **body diagonal** of the cube.

Length of body diagonal:

$$d_{\text{body}} = \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2} + a^{2}} = a\sqrt{3}$$

**Step 2 — Atoms along the body diagonal.**
The diagonal passes through: corner atom → body-centre atom → opposite corner atom.

$$L_{\text{atoms}} = R + 2R + R = 4R$$

**Step 3 — Equate:**

$$a\sqrt{3} = 4R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute APF.**
Number of atoms $n = 2$ in BCC.

$$V_{\text{occupied}} = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = \frac{8}{3}\pi R^{3}$$

Substituting $R = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$:

$$R^{3} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^{3}}{64}$$

$$V_{\text{occupied}} = \frac{8}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^{3}}{64} = \frac{8\sqrt{3}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{64} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Final APF:**

$$\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi\,a^{3}}{8}}{a^{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8}$$

**Step 6 — Numerically:**

$$\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{1.732 \times 3.1416}{8} = \frac{5.4414}{8} \approx 0.6802$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8} \approx 0.6802\ \text{or}\ 68.02\%}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Density of a Unit Cell

This is the **classic 14-mark derivation** that appears in almost every KTU university examination.

**Starting formula:**

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Mass of unit cell}}{\text{Volume of unit cell}}$$

**Step 1 — Express mass of unit cell.**
Each unit cell contains $n$ atoms. The mass of one atom equals $A/N_A$ where $A$ is the molar mass and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number:

$$m_{\text{cell}} = n \cdot \frac{A}{N_A}$$

**Step 2 — Express volume of unit cell.**

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}\quad \text{(cubic)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{N_A \cdot a^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Find the density of copper (FCC), given $a = 3.61\ \text{Å} = 3.61 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$, $A_{\text{Cu}} = 63.55\ \text{g/mol} = 63.55 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$, $n = 4$.

$$V = (3.61 \times 10^{-10})^{3} = 4.704 \times 10^{-29}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$m = \frac{4 \times 63.55 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{0.2542}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 4.221 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{kg}$$

$$\rho = \frac{4.221 \times 10^{-25}}{4.704 \times 10^{-29}} = 8.97 \times 10^{3}\ \text{kg/m}^{3} = 8.97\ \text{g/cm}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{Cu}} \approx 8.97\ \text{g/cm}^{3}}$$

This matches the experimental value (8.96 g/cm³) to within 0.1 %, validating the hard-sphere model.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — c/a Ratio for an Ideal HCP Unit Cell

**Step 1 — Geometry of the basal plane.**
The basal plane of an HCP crystal is a regular hexagon of side $a$. It can be divided into **6 equilateral triangles** of side $a$. Each basal triangle has altitude:

$$h_{\text{tri}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a$$

**Step 2 — Identify the atom centres.**
Atoms touch along the *basal plane edge* (each edge of the hexagon is $a$), and along the *body diagonal* of any one of the basal tetrahedra (atom in the middle layer touches two basal atoms).

**Step 3 — Construct the geometry.**
Consider one tetrahedron formed by:
- 3 basal atoms (centres forming an equilateral triangle of side $a$),
- 1 mid-layer atom directly above the triangle centroid.

The centroid of the equilateral triangle is at distance $\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$ from each vertex.

**Step 4 — Apply hard-sphere contact along the tetrahedron edge:**
The distance from the mid-layer atom to a basal atom equals $2R$. The vertical separation between layers is the height of the tetrahedron:

$$h_{\text{layer}} = \sqrt{(2R)^{2} - \left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — But since $a = 2R$ (basal contact):**

$$h_{\text{layer}} = \sqrt{a^{2} - \frac{a^{2}}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2a^{2}}{3}} = a\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — The total height $c$ equals 2 × layer height** (because there are two such tetrahedra stacked — one upward, one downward — forming the unit cell of vertical extent $c$):

$$c = 2 a \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 2a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\,a$$

**Step 7 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} = 1.633$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)_{\text{ideal HCP}} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \approx 1.633}$$

**Real-world check:** Mg has $c/a = 1.624$, Zn has $c/a = 1.856$, Ti has $c/a = 1.587$. The *deviation* from 1.633 indicates the degree of anisotropy — directly relevant when designing Mg- or Ti-based composites.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Number of Atoms in an HCP Unit Cell

**Step 1 — Corners.**
The HCP unit cell is a hexagonal prism. There are **12 corner atoms**, each shared by 6 adjacent cells:

$$n_{\text{corners}} = 12 \times \frac{1}{6} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Face centres (top and bottom basal planes).**
2 atoms at basal face centres, each shared by 2 cells:

$$n_{\text{faces}} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Interior atoms.**
3 atoms lie fully inside the cell (in the middle layer):

$$n_{\text{interior}} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Total:**

$$\boxed{n_{\text{HCP}} = 2 + 1 + 3 = 6\ \text{atoms per unit cell}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a unit cell. State the seven crystal systems with their axis and angle relationships.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **unit cell** is the smallest three-dimensional repeating portion of a crystal lattice that, when translated along the three crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal structure without any gaps.

The seven crystal systems are:

| # | System | Axes | Angles |
|---|--------|------|--------|
| 1 | Cubic | $a = b = c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 2 | Tetragonal | $a = b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 3 | Orthorhombic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ |
| 4 | Hexagonal | $a = b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \beta = 90°,\ \gamma = 120°$ |
| 5 | Rhombohedral | $a = b = c$ | $\alpha = \beta = \gamma \neq 90°$ |
| 6 | Monoclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha = \gamma = 90°,\ \beta \neq 90°$ |
| 7 | Triclinic | $a \neq b \neq c$ | $\alpha \neq \beta \neq \gamma \neq 90°$ |

**Mark split:** Definition (1) + listing with correct relationships (2).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is coordination number? State the coordination numbers of SC, BCC and FCC structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Coordination number (CN)** is the number of nearest-neighbour atoms equidistant from a given reference atom in a crystal structure.

- Simple Cubic (SC) : **CN = 6**
- Body-Centred Cubic (BCC) : **CN = 8**
- Face-Centred Cubic (FCC) : **CN = 12**

**Justification (bonus mark if asked):** In SC, the nearest neighbours are the 6 face-centre-equivalent positions; in BCC, the 8 corner atoms are the closest to the body-centre atom; in FCC, each atom has 12 equidistant neighbours forming a cubo-octahedron.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the atomic radius $R$ and lattice parameter $a$ for **FCC** and **BCC** unit cells. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **theoretical density** of aluminium (FCC). Given: $a = 4.05\ \text{Å}$, atomic mass $A = 27\ \text{g/mol}$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$. Comment on how this density is used in designing Al-matrix composites. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**For FCC (3.5 marks):**

In an FCC structure, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**.

Length of face diagonal $= \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2}} = a\sqrt{2}$.

Along this diagonal lie 4 atomic radii ($R + 2R + R$):

$$a\sqrt{2} = 4R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}$$

**For BCC (3.5 marks):**

In a BCC structure, atoms touch along the **body diagonal**.

Length of body diagonal $= \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2} + a^{2}} = a\sqrt{3}$.

Along this diagonal lie 4 atomic radii:

$$a\sqrt{3} = 4R \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the number of atoms per FCC cell:**

$$n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 3 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute cell volume:**

$$V = a^{3} = (4.05 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m})^{3} = 6.643 \times 10^{-29}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cell mass:**

$$m = \frac{n \cdot A}{N_A} = \frac{4 \times 27 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}} = \frac{0.108}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 1.793 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute density:**

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{1.793 \times 10^{-25}}{6.643 \times 10^{-29}} = 2.70 \times 10^{3}\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{Al}} \approx 2.70\ \text{g/cm}^{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Composite design comment (2 marks):**

This density (2.70 g/cm³) matches experimental values within 1 %. In Al-matrix composites (e.g., Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃), the theoretical density of the Al matrix is used as a baseline to estimate composite density via the **rule of mixtures**:

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f)\rho_m$$

where $V_f$ is the fibre volume fraction and $\rho_f, \rho_m$ are fibre and matrix densities respectively. Knowing $\rho_m$ accurately (2.70 g/cm³) is essential for weight-sensitive aerospace composite design.

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Define **atomic packing factor (APF)**. Derive the APF for a **simple cubic (SC)** unit cell and show that it equals $\pi/6$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **14 Bravais lattices** with a neat classification. Why is there no face-centred tetragonal lattice? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **atomic packing factor (APF)**, also called packing efficiency, is the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms (treated as hard spheres) within a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms in cell}}}{V_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a simple cubic cell:
- Number of atoms per cell: $n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1$
- Atoms touch along the cube edge: $a = 2R$
- Volume of one atom: $V_{\text{atom}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$
- Volume of cell: $V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (2R)^{3} = 8R^{3}$

Substituting:

$$\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \frac{1 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{8R^{3}} = \frac{4\pi}{24} = \frac{\pi}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236\ \text{or}\ 52.36\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Just over half the SC cell is filled; the remaining 47.64 % is void space — hence SC structures (e.g., α-Po) are rare in engineering composites.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**14 Bravais Lattices — Tabular Classification (5 marks):**

| Crystal System | Valid Centring Types | Count |
|----------------|----------------------|-------|
| Cubic | P, I, F | 3 |
| Tetragonal | P, I | 2 |
| Orthorhombic | P, I, F, C | 4 |
| Hexagonal | P | 1 |
| Rhombohedral (Trigonal) | P | 1 |
| Monoclinic | P, C | 2 |
| Triclinic | P | 1 |
| **Total** | | **14** |

**Why no face-centred tetragonal (2 marks):**

A face-centred tetragonal lattice can be **re-described** as a body-centred tetragonal lattice with a smaller cell volume. Specifically, the conventional face-centred tetragonal cell can be rotated and re-scaled to yield a body-centred tetragonal primitive cell. Since both descriptions represent the *same* lattice, the face-centred tetragonal is not a *new* Bravais lattice — it is redundant under the Bravais definition of "minimum-volume conventional cell". A similar redundancy argument eliminates the face-centred rhombohedral, etc.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners repeatedly penalise on Unit Cell questions:**
> 1. **Wrong atom-counting:** Students often write $n = 4$ for BCC (it is 2) or $n = 2$ for FCC (it is 4). Memorise: SC → 1, BCC → 2, FCC → 4, HCP → 6.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{8}$ for corner atoms** in non-cubic cells like HCP (where corner atoms are shared by 6 cells → $\frac{1}{6}$).
> 3. **Unit mismatch in density:** If $a$ is given in Å and $A$ in g/mol, convert to consistent units before substitution. Marks are deducted for missing units in the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing face diagonal with body diagonal** when deriving $R$–$a$ relations. Tip: remember the contact directions: SC = cube edge, BCC = body diagonal, FCC = face diagonal, HCP = basal edge.
> 5. **Forgetting $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$** in density derivations — losing 2 marks silently.
> 6. **Not stating the c/a ideal ratio for HCP** when asked about HCP — that is the *single most-asked* sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **unit cell** is the smallest repeating 3-D block of a crystal, described by **6 lattice parameters**: $a, b, c$ and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$.
- **7 crystal systems** combined with centring types yield **14 Bravais lattices**.
- The four centring types are: **P, I, F, C**. Some combinations are forbidden (e.g., face-centred tetragonal).
- **Atom sharing factors** — corner = $\frac{1}{8}$ (cubic) or $\frac{1}{6}$ (hexagonal cell); face = $\frac{1}{2}$; edge = $\frac{1}{4}$; interior = 1.
- **Atoms per cell** — SC = 1, BCC = 2, FCC = 4, HCP = 6.
- **Coordination numbers** — SC = 6, BCC = 8, FCC = 12, HCP = 12.
- **Radius–parameter relations:**
   - SC: $R = a/2$
   - BCC: $R = \sqrt{3}\,a/4$
   - FCC: $R = \sqrt{2}\,a/4$
- **APF values:**
   - SC = $\pi/6 \approx 0.5236$
   - BCC = $\sqrt{3}\,\pi/8 \approx 0.6802$
   - FCC = $\sqrt{2}\,\pi/6 \approx 0.7405$
   - HCP (ideal) = 0.7405
- **Ideal HCP c/a ratio** = $\sqrt{8/3} \approx 1.633$.
- **Density formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{N_A \cdot V}$; remember $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$.
- **Voids** in FCC = 0.2595 of volume — important for solute/precipitate accommodation in MMC matrices.
- In **composite materials**, the unit cell concept generalises to the **Representative Volume Element (RVE)** used in micromechanical modelling.
- **Maximum fibre packing fraction** in a 2-D composite = $\pi/(2\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.9069$ for hexagonal array; $\pi/4 \approx 0.7854$ for square array.
- Always check units (Å vs m vs cm) before numerical substitution in KTU exam scripts.
- Memorise the **contact directions** (edge / face-diagonal / body-diagonal) for SC / FCC / BCC respectively — this single fact unlocks 70 % of derivation-based questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Analysis of Unit Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress Analysis of Unit Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the micromechanics of fiber-reinforced composites, a **Unit Cell** is defined as the smallest, geometrically repeating, three-dimensional sub-volume of a composite lamina or laminate that fully captures the constituent arrangement (fiber + matrix), the fiber volume fraction ($V_f$), and the local stress–strain state under macroscopic loading. The **Stress Analysis of Unit Cells** refers to the systematic determination of internal stress and strain fields within this representative sub-volume by applying boundary conditions that simulate macroscopic loads, thereby allowing the engineer to back-calculate the *effective (homogenized) elastic constants* of the composite.

The unit cell concept is rooted in the principle of **macroscopic homogeneity**: although a composite is microscopically heterogeneous (two or more distinct phases), it behaves as a quasi-homogeneous orthotropic continuum at the engineering scale. Stress analysis of this cell provides the **effective stiffness matrix** $[Q_{ij}]$, the **engineering constants** ($E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$), and the strength ratios required for design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – PECHT742 / Module 3**
> The unit forms the foundation for *Strength of Materials Approach (SMA)* and *Elasticity Approach (EA)* to micromechanics. The two classical bounding models required are the **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** and the **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (IRoM)**, also called the **Voigt** and **Reuss** models respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **brick wall** built using two different bricks — a *strong red brick* (fiber) and a *soft mortar* (matrix) — arranged in a perfectly repeating pattern across the wall. If you were to predict the strength of the entire wall, you would not test every brick individually. Instead, you would isolate a small repeating patch — say, **one red brick and its surrounding mortar** — and test that single patch. That patch is your **unit cell**. The stress distribution within that patch (where the red brick carries more load, and the mortar bridges the gaps) directly tells you how the *whole wall* will behave under a load.

In a unidirectional fiber composite:
- The **fiber** is the stiff, load-bearing "brick."
- The **matrix** is the compliant, stress-transferring "mortar."
- A **slice through the cross-section** containing one fiber and its share of matrix is the **unit cell** of the lamina.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Assumptions

The fundamental **physical constants** and **geometric metrics** involved in unit-cell stress analysis are:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $V_f$ | Fiber volume fraction | $0.3 \leq V_f \leq 0.7$ (design range) |
| $V_m$ | Matrix volume fraction | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ |
| $E_f$ | Young's modulus of fiber | $70 - 400$ GPa (Glass/Carbon) |
| $E_m$ | Young's modulus of matrix | $2 - 4$ GPa (Epoxy/Polyester) |
| $\nu_{12}$ | Major Poisson's ratio | $0.2 - 0.3$ |
| $d$ | Fiber diameter | $5 - 20\ \mu m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Governing Assumption:** Both fiber and matrix are assumed *linearly elastic, isotropic, and perfectly bonded* at the interface. This assumption is the basis of the classical *Strength of Materials (SOM) Approach* used in KTU Module 3.

---

## 1.4 Why Micromechanics? — The Homogenization Bridge

The core engineering question answered by unit-cell stress analysis is:

> *"Given the properties of the *individual* fiber and matrix, what are the *effective* properties of the bulk composite lamina?"*

This is solved by applying macroscopic boundary conditions — either **uniform strain (iso-strain)** or **uniform stress (iso-stress)** — to the unit cell, computing the volume-averaged stress $\bar{\sigma}_{ij}$ and strain $\bar{\varepsilon}_{ij}$, and then extracting the effective stiffness:

$$C_{ij}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{\partial \bar{\sigma}_i}{\partial \bar{\varepsilon}_j}$$

This concept is called **mathematical homogenization** and is the bedrock of the entire Module 3 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Loading Assumptions

To analyze a unit cell, KTU Module 3 prescribes **two idealised loading models** based on the Strength of Materials Approach (SMA). Both models assume perfect fiber–matrix bonding and no interfacial slip.

### A. Iso-Strain Model (Voigt / Rule of Mixtures)

**Loading condition:** The unit cell is loaded such that the strain in the fiber and the matrix are *equal* to the applied composite strain. This assumption is rigorously valid for a unidirectional composite loaded **parallel to the fibers (Longitudinal loading, 1-direction)**.

**Why this works:** Under longitudinal tension, both fiber and matrix elongate by the same amount (assuming no slip at the interface). The strain compatibility demands $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_1$.

**Load-sharing relation:** The total load on the cross-section is the sum of loads carried by each constituent:
