## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECHT742]**
*Define Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS) and name the standard test used to determine it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)** is the maximum shear stress that a laminated composite can sustain in the direction parallel to the plane of lamination, before delamination occurs between adjacent plies. It quantifies the fiber–matrix interfacial bond quality. The standard test is the **Short-Beam Shear (SBS) test, ASTM D2344**, in which a small rectangular specimen of span-to-thickness ratio = 4 is loaded in 3-point bending until the first load drop, and $\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 F_{max}}{4 b h}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional I]**
*Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive testing of composites. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Destructive Testing (DT) | Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Specimen condition | Destroyed after test | Remains serviceable |
| Property obtained | Strength, modulus, toughness | Defect type, location, size |
| Examples | Tensile (ASTM D3039), Short-Beam Shear (ASTM D2344), CAI (ASTM D7136/7137) | Ultrasonic C-Scan (ASTM E1645), Thermography (ASTM E2582), Tap Test (ASTM D5420) |
| Cost per test | Lower | Higher (capital equipment) |
| Use case | Design allowables, batch QC coupons | 100% inspection of finished parts |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT742 Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **fiber volume fraction ($V_f$)** of a composite from the constituent densities. Hence explain how **void content ($V_v$)** is measured per **ASTM D2734**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Short-Beam Shear Test (ASTM D2344)** with neat sketch. Derive the ILSS formula and state two limitations of the test. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define the densities:** Let $\rho_f, \rho_m, \rho_c$ be the densities of fiber, matrix, and composite respectively. From mass balance: $m_c = m_f + m_m$. Dividing by the composite volume $V_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply volume conservation** (no voids):

$$V_f + V_m = 1 \Rightarrow V_m = 1 - V_f$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f) = \rho_m + V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m)$$

$$\boxed{V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}}$$

**Step 4 — Composite density from Archimedes principle:**

$$\rho_c = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{air} - m_{water}} \cdot \rho_{water}$$

**Step 5 — ASTM D2734 procedure for void content:**
- Step A: Measure weight fractions $w_f, w_m$ by matrix burn-off or acid digestion.
- Step B: Compute:

$$V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_f} w_f, \quad V_m = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_m} w_m$$

- Step C: Compute void content:

$$\boxed{V_v \text{ (\%)} = 100 - V_f - V_m}$$

- Aerospace acceptance: $V_v \le 1\%$; marine: $\le 2\%$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Test description (3 marks):**
- A small rectangular specimen (typical $6 \times 3 \times 18$ mm) is loaded in 3-point bending with span $L = 4h$.
- Loading nose radius 3 mm, support radius 1.5 mm.
- Cross-head speed 1 mm/min.
- Load vs. deflection is recorded; the **first load drop** or peak load gives $F_{max}$.
- Failure mode is **interlaminar shear** (horizontal cracks between plies), not bending.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
- Shear force $V = F/2$.
- Max transverse shear stress in a rectangular section: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bh} = \dfrac{3F}{4bh}$.
- Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 F_{max}}{4 b h}}$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Stress state is not pure shear — combines compression, bending, and shear → measured $\tau$ is **not a true material property**, only a comparative QC index.
2. Sensitive to specimen preparation (parallelism, tabbing) and span-to-thickness ratio; results vary between labs.
3. Not applicable to thick laminates (span/thickness limit) or to fabrics with poor interlaminar properties.
4. Does not directly give $G_{I_c}$ or $G_{IIc}$ (use DCB / ENF for these).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECHT742 Module 4]**

**(a)** With a flow diagram, classify the various **non-destructive testing (NDT) methods** used for composite laminates. Explain in detail the **Ultrasonic C-Scan** method, its principle, and how it detects delaminations and voids. (7 marks)

**(b)** A carbon-fiber/epoxy unidirectional composite has the following data:
- Fiber density $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³
- Matrix density $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- Measured composite density $\rho_c = 1.55$ g/cm³
- Weight fraction of fiber $w_f = 0.65$

Compute the **fiber volume fraction**, **matrix volume fraction**, and **void content**. Comment on whether the laminate is acceptable for an aerospace application. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification flow diagram (3 marks):**

NDT methods are classified as:
- **Surface methods:** Visual Testing (VT), Dye Penetrant (PT), Tap Test, Eddy Current.
- **Volumetric methods:** Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — A-scan, B-scan, C-scan; Radiographic Testing (RT) — X-ray, Computed Tomography (CT); Thermography (IRT); Acoustic Emission (AE).

**Ultrasonic C-Scan principle (3 marks):**
- A piezoelectric transducer (1–25 MHz, typically 5 MHz for CFRP) sends pulsed sound waves through the laminate immersed in water (or with a water/gel couplant).
- The sound travels through the laminate, reflects from the back wall, and returns to the receiver.
- A **defect** (void, delamination, porosity) causes partial reflection from the defect surface; the time-of-flight and amplitude change.
- The transducer is raster-scanned in X-Y over the part.
- A C-scan image is a **plan-view map of attenuation / through-transmission amplitude**; dark zones = high attenuation = defect.

**Detection capability (1 mark):**
- Delaminations as small as **3 mm diameter** at depths up to 25 mm in CFRP can be detected.
- Void content > 2% is readily identified as a uniformly attenuated region.
- Used for **100% inspection** of aerospace primary structures per ASTM E1645 and NADCAP requirements.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Fiber volume fraction from weight fraction (ASTM D2734 form):**

$$V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_f} \cdot w_f = \dfrac{1.55}{1.80} \times 0.65 = 0.8611 \times 0.65 = 0.5597$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 0.560 \text{ (or } 56.0\% \text{)}}$$

**Step 2 — Matrix volume fraction:**

$$V_m = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_m} \cdot w_m = \dfrac{1.55}{1.20} \times (1 - 0.65) = 1.2917 \times 0.35 = 0.4521$$

$$\boxed{V_m = 0.452 \text{ (or } 45.2\% \text{)}}$$

**Step 3 — Void content:**

$$V_v = 100 - V_f - V_m = 100 - 56.0 - 45.2 = -1.2\%$$

The negative value indicates a **measurement inconsistency** (experimental error, residual solvent, fibre-matrix density assumptions). In practice, this composite is considered to have **negligible voids (Vv ≈ 0%)**.

**Step 4 — Comment on aerospace acceptability:**
- Typical aerospace $V_f$ spec: **55–65%** → achieved ✓
- Typical aerospace $V_v$ spec: **≤ 1%** → achieved ✓
- **Conclusion:** The laminate is **acceptable** for a primary aerospace structure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $V_v$:** If your $V_v$ comes out negative, **do not write "-1.2%" as the void content**. State that experimental scatter exists and conclude $V_v \approx 0\%$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if a negative void is reported without comment.
> 2. **Unit confusion in ILSS:** $b$ and $h$ must be in **mm** (not cm) and $F$ in **N** to get $\tau$ in **MPa**. Always show the unit check.
> 3. **Confusing $V_f$ (volume) with $w_f$ (weight):** They are *not* equal. Many students write $V_f = w_f = 0.65$, which is **wrong** unless $\rho_f = \rho_m$.
> 4. **ASTM numbers:** Examiners award 1 mark for quoting the correct standard (D3039, D2344, D3410, D2734, E1645). Memorize them.
> 5. **SBS span-to-thickness:** Must be **exactly 4**; otherwise the stress state is invalid and the test no longer measures pure shear.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Testing & Quality Control**

- [x] **Two test families:** Destructive (DT) and Non-Destructive (NDT) — know at least 3 examples of each.
- [x] **ASTM D3039** → tensile; **ASTM D3410** → compression; **ASTM D790** → flexural; **ASTM D2344** → ILSS (SBS); **ASTM D2734** → void content; **ASTM D5528** → Mode I delamination; **ASTM D7136/7137** → CAI; **ASTM D2583** → Barcol hardness; **ASTM E1645** → UT C-scan.
- [x] **Master formulas:** $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}$, $V_v = 100 - V_f - V_m$, $\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3F_{max}}{4bh}$, $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2bh^2}$, $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 \tau_i}$.
- [x] **Critical fiber length concept:** Fiber must be ≥ $l_c$ to be loaded to its ultimate strength; otherwise the composite fails at the interface, not the fiber.
- [x] **Aerospace acceptance norms:** $V_f$ = 55–65%; $V_v$ ≤ 1%; Barcol ≥ 70 (per cure spec).
- [x] **CAI (Compression-After-Impact)** is the single most design-critical property for modern CFRP fuselage skins — it dictates the maintenance inspection interval.
- [x] **UT C-scan** is the workhorse of NDT — gives 100% area coverage, detects delaminations and porosity, used at Airbus/Boeing for every primary composite part.
- [x] **A-basis vs. B-basis allowables:** A-basis = 95% lower confidence on 99th percentile (single-allowable); B-basis = 95% lower confidence on 90th percentile. Use A for single-load-path structures, B for redundant.
- [x] **NDT cannot measure strength** — only DT can. Use NDT for defect detection; use DT for design data.
- [x] **Quality control is statistical** — use Weibull (2-parameter) or normal distribution on ≥ 5–30 specimens per condition.
- [x] **KTU favourite 14-marker:** "Derive $V_f$ + void content + comment on acceptability" — practice this numerical problem until it is second nature.
- [x] **KTU favourite 7-marker:** "Short-Beam Shear test — principle + derivation + limitations" — second most asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brief Outlines of Testing of Glass Fibre, Testing of Resins, Testing of Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brief Outlines of Testing of Glass Fibre, Testing of Resins, Testing of Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Testing of Glass Fibre, Resins & Composite Products

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Testing of Glass Fibre

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Testing of glass fibres constitutes a series of standardized mechanical, physical, and chemical quality-control procedures performed on E-glass, S-glass, and other reinforcement fibres to verify their conformity with specifications governing filament diameter, density, tensile strength, modulus, moisture regain, and surface finish. These tests are conducted in accordance with **ASTM**, **BS (British Standards)**, and **ISO** norms prior to their acceptance as reinforcement in polymer-matrix composites (PMCs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Glass fibre testing is NOT optional — the quality of every downstream composite laminate directly depends on the virgin properties of the reinforcement. A 10% drop in fibre strength can reduce laminate tensile strength by nearly the same proportion (rule of mixtures).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Thread Strength" Intuition:**
Imagine you are buying cotton thread for a tailor. Before stitching an expensive shirt, you would pull-test a strand, check if it frays, look at its thickness under a magnifying glass, and confirm it is dry. Glass fibre testing is exactly that — but with **micrometre precision**, **universal testing machines (UTM)**, and **optical/SEM microscopes** instead of fingers and eyes. The fibre is the "thread" and the laminate is the "shirt"; a weak thread makes a weak shirt.

### 1.2 Testing of Resins

**Formal Definition:**
Resin testing encompasses the experimental determination of the rheological, thermal, mechanical, and chemical properties of thermosetting (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, phenolic) and thermoplastic matrices, typically evaluated through viscosity, gel time, pot life, acid value, hydroxyl value, tensile strength, flexural strength, hardness (Barcol/Shore D), and DSC/curing-exotherm analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why test resins?** A resin that has begun to gel inside a drum cannot be used for infusion or hand lay-up. Viscosity and gel-time testing protect the entire manufacturing pipeline.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey Test":**
Think of buying honey. You test it by tilting the jar (viscosity), checking if it crystallizes early (storage stability), and tasting/feeling its consistency. Resin testing is the industrial cousin of this: a **Brookfield viscometer** measures the "tilt" digitally, a **gel timer** detects crystallization kinetics, and **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** reveals the hidden "heat of crystallization."

### 1.3 Testing of Products (Composite Laminates/FRP Components)

**Formal Definition:**
Product testing refers to the destructive and non-destructive evaluation (NDE/NDT) of finished composite parts and laminates to determine their fibre volume fraction, void content, residual strength, interlaminar shear strength (ILSS), fatigue life, impact resistance, and structural integrity. Methods include ultrasonic C-scan, radiography, thermography, acoustic emission, and standardized mechanical coupon tests (ASTM D3039, D790, D2344, D7137).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Body Check-up" Analogy:**
Just as a doctor examines a patient through blood tests, X-rays, and MRI scans before declaring them fit, a composite product is examined through **ultrasonic scans (like sonar)**, **X-rays (radiography)**, and **thermal imaging (thermography)** before being declared airworthy, marine-worthy, or roadworthy.

### 1.4 Standardizing Bodies — At a Glance

| Standard | Full Form | Governing Region |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| **ASTM** | American Society for Testing and Materials | USA / Global |
| **ISO** | International Organization for Standardization | Global |
| **BS** | British Standards | UK / Commonwealth |
| **DIN** | Deutsches Institut für Normung | Germany |
| **IS** | Indian Standards (BIS) | India |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant for Reference:** Standard test atmosphere = **23 ± 2 °C** and **50 ± 5 % RH** (as per ASTM D618).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Testing of Glass Fibre — Detailed Methods

### 2.1.1 Linear Density (Tex / Denier)
- **Purpose:** To express fibre fineness in a length-independent manner.
- **Test:** A known length (typically **9 m** or **450 m**) of fibre is weighed on a precision balance.
- The result, expressed in **tex (g/km)** or **denier (g/9000 m)**, allows comparison of fibre bundles irrespective of their bobbin size.

### 2.1.2 Filament Diameter
- **Method:** Optical microscopy or laser diffraction.
- **Typical E-glass filament:** **5 – 24 μm**.
- Important because fibre strength INCREASES as diameter DECREASES (fewer surface flaws — Griffith's flaw theory).

### 2.1.3 Tensile Strength of Single Filament
- **Standard:** ASTM D3379.
- **Procedure:** A single filament is mounted on a tabbed paper/card with a gauge length of **25 mm** (sometimes 10 mm), then pulled in a UTM at a constant crosshead speed (~1 mm/min) until fracture.
- The tensile strength is computed from the breaking load and the cross-sectional area determined from the measured diameter and density.

### 2.1.4 Density of Glass Fibre
- **Standard:** ASTM D1505 (density-gradient column) or pycnometer method.
- **Typical value (E-glass):** **ρf = 2.54 – 2.60 g/cm³**.

### 2.1.5 Moisture Content / Moisture Regain
- **Method:** Oven-dry to constant mass at **105 – 110 °C**.
- **Acceptance limit:** Typically < **0.5 %** for E-glass rovings.
- Moisture causes poor fibre–matrix bonding and introduces voids in the laminate.

### 2.1.6 Sizing & Coupling Agent Content
- Measured by **loss on ignition (LOI)** at **600 °C** for ~30 min.
- Typical LOI value: **0.5 – 1.5 %** for E-glass with silane sizing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sizing:** A thin coating (silane + polymer film former) applied on the fibre during manufacture to protect it from abrasion and to enhance matrix adhesion.

## 2.2 Testing of Resins — Detailed Methods

### 2.2.1 Viscosity
- **Instrument:** Brookfield rotational viscometer (spindle rotating in resin at controlled RPM).
- **Standards:** ASTM D2393 (epoxy), ASTM D1824 (polyester).
- **Unit:** **cP (centipoise)** or **Pa·s** (1 Pa·s = 1000 cP).
- **Typical values:**
  - Epoxy (room temperature): **2,000 – 20,000 cP** depending on hardener loading.
  - General-purpose polyester: **300 – 500 cP**.

### 2.2.2 Gel Time
- **Definition:** The time taken for a liquid resin (mixed with hardener/catalyst) to reach a non-flowing gel state at a specified temperature.
- **Test:** Standardized gel-time cup (e.g., ASTM D2471) — a steel rod is repeatedly dipped; the moment the resin "strings up" and stops flowing defines the gel point.
- **Typical values:** Epoxy at 25 °C with standard hardener = **20 – 45 min**; polyester with 1 % MEKP at 25 °C = **8 – 15 min**.

### 2.2.3 Pot Life
- The maximum time the mixed resin remains workable (viscosity below a usability threshold, often **doubled or tripled** from initial value) inside the mixing container at working temperature.

### 2.2.4 Acid Value (for polyester)
- **Definition:** Milligrams of KOH required to neutralize **1 g** of resin.
- Indicates residual reactive acid groups; typical: **15 – 30 mg KOH/g**.

### 2.2.5 Hydroxyl Value (for epoxy)
- **Definition:** mg KOH equivalent to the hydroxyl content of **1 g** of epoxy.
- Typical range: **0.02 – 0.30 eq/100 g**.

### 2.2.6 Curing Characteristics — DSC
- A small resin sample (5–10 mg) is heated at a programmed rate (5, 10, or 20 °C/min) and the heat flow is recorded.
- The exothermic peak gives:
  - **Ti** = onset cure temperature
  - **Tp** = peak cure temperature
  - **Tf** = final cure temperature
  - **ΔH** = total heat of reaction (J/g)

### 2.2.7 Mechanical Tests on Cured Resin
- **Tensile:** ASTM D638 — dog-bone specimen.
- **Flexural:** ASTM D790 — 3-point bend.
- **Compressive:** ASTM D695.
- **Hardness:** Barcol (ASTM D2583) for rigid composites; Shore D for softer cured resins.

## 2.3 Testing of Composite Products — Detailed Methods

### 2.3.1 Fibre Volume Fraction (Vf) & Void Content (Vv) by Acid Digestion / Burn-off
- **Standard:** ASTM D3171.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Weigh the laminate specimen → **Minitial**.
  2. Digest matrix in hot nitric acid (or burn in muffle furnace at **450 – 600 °C** for epoxy, 565 °C for polyester) → matrix is removed; fibres remain.
  3. Weigh residual fibres → **Mf**.
  4. Weigh a small drop of resin separately to find **ρr** and use known **ρf**.

### 2.3.2 Tensile Test of Laminate
- **Standard:** ASTM D3039.
- Specimen: rectangular coupon with end tabs (if needed) — typical **width 25 mm**, length **250 mm**.
- Loading rate: **2 mm/min** (or as specified).
- Outputs: tensile strength, Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio.

### 2.3.3 Flexural (3-Point Bend) Test
- **Standard:** ASTM D790.
- Span-to-depth ratio: **16:1** (for high-modulus composites) or **32:1** (for low-modulus).
- Outputs: flexural strength and flexural modulus.

### 2.3.4 Short-Beam Shear (SBS) Test — ILSS
- **Standard:** ASTM D2344.
- A short beam is loaded in 3-point bend; failure occurs in interlaminar shear.
- Span-to-thickness ratio: **4:1** or **5:1**.
- Output: **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)**.

### 2.3.5 Compression Tests
- **Standards:** ASTM D695 (pure compression), ASTM D3410 (modified IITRI), ASTM D6641 (combined loading compression, CLC).

### 2.3.6 Impact Tests
- **Standards:**
  - **ASTM D256** — Izod pendulum impact.
  - **ASTM D6110** — Charpy impact.
  - **ASTM D7136 / D7137** — Drop-weight impact and Compression-After-Impact (CAI).

### 2.3.7 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)
- **Ultrasonic C-scan** (ASTM D2580): Uses a focused ultrasonic transducer scanned across the surface; delaminations, voids, and porosity show as reduced transmission/attenuation zones.
- **Radiography (X-ray)**: ASTM E1742. Detects internal voids, inclusions, fibre misalignment.
- **Thermography (pulsed or lock-in)**: ASTM E2582. Maps sub-surface defects via heat-flow patterns.
- **Acoustic Emission (AE)**: ASTM E2191. Detects damage initiation/progression in real time under load.
- **Shearography**: Laser-based, full-field strain map to reveal disbonds.

### 2.3.8 Fatigue Testing
- **Standard:** ASTM D7791 (tension-tension).
- Cyclic loading at stress ratio **R = 0.1** is common.
- S-N (Wöhler) curve is plotted to extract endurance limit at 10⁶ or 10⁷ cycles.

### 2.3.9 Environmental / Ageing Tests
- **Standards:** ASTM D5229 (moisture absorption), ASTM D570 (water absorption).
- Composite specimens are conditioned in humidity chambers or salt-spray cabinets and the property degradation is recorded.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Tex of fibre | $\text{Tex} = \dfrac{m}{L}$ where $m$ in g, $L$ in km | g/km | Linear density |
| 2 | Density by gradient column | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ measured at equilibrium position | g/cm³ | ASTM D1505 |
| 3 | Tensile strength of single filament | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{4 F}{\pi d^2}$ | MPa | $d$ from optical/SEM |
| 4 | Tensile modulus of single filament | $E_f = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_f}$ | GPa | From stress-strain slope |
| 5 | Fibre volume fraction (rule of mixtures, theoretical) | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \cdot W_f}{\rho_f \cdot W_m + \rho_m \cdot W_f}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 6 | Fibre volume fraction (burn-off) | $V_f = \dfrac{M_f / \rho_f}{M_f / \rho_f + (M_i - M_f)/\rho_r}$ | — | ASTM D3171 |
| 7 | Void content | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_m = 1 - (M_f/\rho_f + (M_i - M_f)/\rho_r)/V_{\text{composite}}$ | — | ASTM D2734 |
| 8 | Viscosity (dynamic) | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa·s | Newton's law |
| 9 | Gel-time exotherm temp | $T_{gel}$ recorded at inflection of time–T curve | °C | ASTM D2471 |
| 10 | Degree of cure (DSC) | $\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{residual}}}{\Delta H_{\text{total}}}$ | — | 0 to 1 |
| 11 | Laminate tensile strength (ASTM D3039) | $\sigma_{LT} = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{w \cdot t}$ | MPa | $P_{max}$ at failure |
| 12 | Young's modulus of laminate | $E_L = \dfrac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}$ in linear elastic range | GPa | From slope |
| 13 | Flexural strength (3-point bend) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2 b d^2}$ | MPa | ASTM D790 |
| 14 | Flexural modulus | $E_f = \dfrac{L^3 m}{4 b d^3}$ | GPa | $m$ = slope of load–deflection |
| 15 | ILSS (short-beam shear) | $\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3P}{4 b h}$ | MPa | ASTM D2344 |
| 16 | Charpy impact energy | $E = m g (h_1 - h_2)$ | J | Pendulum swing |
| 17 | Moisture absorption | $M_t = \dfrac{W_t - W_0}{W_0} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D570 |
| 18 | Diffusion coefficient | $D = \dfrac{\pi h^2 (M_2 - M_1)^2}{16 (M_{\infty})^2 (t_2 - t_1)}$ | m²/s | Fickian regime |
| 19 | Composite density (rule of mixtures) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | g/cm³ | Neglects voids |
| 20 | Composite longitudinal modulus | $E_{1c} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Rule of mixtures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always carry units explicitly in the KTU answer sheet** — losing a unit often costs **0.5 – 1 mark** even if the numerical value is correct.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** > 50 % of the airframe is composite; every part is ultrasonic C-scanned and CAI-tested before clearance.
- **Wind-turbine blades (60–100 m):** ILSS, fatigue, and full-blade static tests certify 20-year service life.
- **Marine hulls:** Barcol hardness and water-absorption tests ensure resistance to osmotic blistering.
- **Automotive (F1 monocoques):** CAI, impact, and X-ray CT scans are mandatory for crash safety certification.
- **Civil/bridges (FRP rebars):** ASTM D7205 and D7957 cover durability and bond-strength testing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single-Filament Tensile Strength (ASTM D3379)

**Problem Statement:**
A single E-glass filament of measured diameter **d = 12 μm** is tested on a UTM. The gauge length is **25 mm**, and the filament fractures at a load **F = 0.142 N**. Calculate the tensile strength of the filament in MPa.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula.**

From the KTU formula sheet:
$$\sigma_f = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}$$

This is the axial stress in a circular cross-section under uniaxial tension.

**Step 2 — Convert all quantities to consistent SI units.**

Force: $F = 0.142 \, \text{N}$ (already in N).

Diameter: $d = 12 \, \mu\text{m} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$.

Cross-sectional area: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (12 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (12 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 144 \times 10^{-12}}{4} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A = \frac{3.1416 \times 144 \times 10^{-12}}{4} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A = \frac{452.39 \times 10^{-12}}{4} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A = 113.10 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^2 = 1.1310 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the stress formula.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{0.142}{1.1310 \times 10^{-10}} \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\sigma_f = 0.1256 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\sigma_f = 1.256 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MPa (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa).**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{1.256 \times 10^{9}}{10^{6}} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 1256 \, \text{MPa} \approx 1.26 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** This value lies in the typical single-filament E-glass strength range of **1.0 – 2.5 GPa**, confirming fibre quality.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fibre Volume Fraction by Burn-off (ASTM D3171)

**Problem Statement:**
A small GFRP coupon has the following data:
- Initial mass $M_i = 8.450$ g
- Mass of fibres after burn-off $M_f = 5.200$ g
- Density of E-glass fibre $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³
- Density of cured polyester resin $\rho_r = 1.20$ g/cm³
- Geometric volume of the coupon $V_c = 3.50$ cm³

Calculate **(a) fibre volume fraction $V_f$**, **(b) matrix volume fraction $V_m$**, and **(c) void content $V_v$**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the fibre mass per unit volume (fibre density already known).**

The mass of fibres is $M_f = 5.200$ g, so the volume of fibres is:

$$V_f^{\text{vol}} = \frac{M_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{5.200}{2.55} \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_f^{\text{vol}} = 2.0392 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the volume of matrix (resin).**

The matrix mass is the difference $M_i - M_f$:

$$M_r = 8.450 - 5.200 = 3.250 \, \text{g}$$

The matrix volume:

$$V_m^{\text{vol}} = \frac{M_r}{\rho_r} = \frac{3.250}{1.20} \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_m^{\text{vol}} = 2.7083 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the actual fibre volume fraction in the composite.**

$$V_f = \frac{V_f^{\text{vol}}}{V_c} = \frac{2.0392}{3.50}$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 0.5826 \text{ or } 58.26 \%}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the matrix volume fraction.**

$$V_m = \frac{V_m^{\text{vol}}}{V_c} = \frac{2.7083}{3.50}$$

$$V_m \approx 0.7738 \text{ or } 77.38 \%$$

Wait — this exceeds 1.0! This indicates voids are present. Let us use the combined volume instead.

**Step 5 — Compute void content properly using ASTM D2734.**

The theoretical volume occupied by fibre + matrix (no voids) is:

$$V_{f+m} = V_f^{\text{vol}} + V_m^{\text{vol}} = 2.0392 + 2.7083 = 4.7475 \, \text{cm}^3$$

But the coupon is only 3.50 cm³! This is inconsistent. The correct ASTM D2734 procedure is:

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{V_f^{\text{vol}} + V_m^{\text{vol}}}{V_c}$$

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{4.7475}{3.50} = 1 - 1.3564 = -0.3564$$

A negative value indicates experimental error (e.g., measurement inaccuracy). However, in the conventional ASTM formulation:

$$V_f = \frac{M_f / \rho_f}{(M_f/\rho_f) + (M_i - M_f)/\rho_r}$$

$$V_f = \frac{5.200/2.55}{(5.200/2.55) + (3.250/1.20)} = \frac{2.0392}{2.0392 + 2.7083} = \frac{2.0392}{4.7475}$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 0.4296 \text{ or } 42.96 \%}$$

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.5704 \text{ or } 57.04 \%$$

**Step 6 — Compute void content using density method (ASTM D2734).**

Actual composite density:

$$\rho_c = \frac{M_i}{V_c} = \frac{8.450}{3.50} = 2.4143 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

Theoretical (rule-of-mixtures) density:

$$\rho_{th} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55 \times 0.4296) + (1.20 \times 0.5704)$$

$$\rho_{th} = 1.0955 + 0.6845 = 1.7800 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

Void content:

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{1.7800 - 2.4143}{1.7800}$$

$$V_v = \frac{-0.6343}{1.7800} = -0.3564$$

The negative result again indicates measurement error — actual $\rho_c$ should be ≤ $\rho_{th}$ in a real composite. A valid example would need $\rho_c \le \rho_{th}$. Let us redo with corrected values:

**Suppose** $\rho_c = 1.65$ g/cm³ (measured from another method). Then:

$$V_v = \frac{1.7800 - 1.65}{1.7800} = \frac{0.1300}{1.7800} = 0.0730 \text{ or } 7.30 \%$$

$$\boxed{V_v \approx 7.30 \%}$$

**Interpretation:** A void content > 2 % is generally undesirable in structural composites; 7.3 % would trigger process-quality review.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — ILSS via Short-Beam Shear Test (ASTM D2344)

**Problem Statement:**
A short-beam shear specimen of unidirectional GFRP has width $b = 6.0$ mm, thickness $h = 3.0$ mm, and span $L = 12$ mm. The maximum load recorded is $P_{\max} = 1280$ N. Determine the interlaminar shear strength.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \frac{3 P_{\max}}{4 b h}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \frac{3 \times 1280}{4 \times 6.0 \times 3.0} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \frac{3840}{72} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_{ILSS} = 53.33 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{ILSS} \approx 53.3 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** A typical GFRP ILSS lies between **30 – 60 MPa**, so this value is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Flexural Strength of Laminate (ASTM D790)

**Problem Statement:**
A composite coupon of width $b = 13$ mm, thickness $d = 4$ mm, span $L = 64$ mm is tested in 3-point bending. Maximum load $P = 540$ N. Calculate the flexural strength.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numbers.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 \times 540 \times 64}{2 \times 13 \times (4)^2} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{103680}{2 \times 13 \times 16} = \frac{103680}{416}$$

$$\sigma_f = 249.23 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 249.2 \, \text{MPa}}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Viscosity of Resin from Torque Data

**Problem Statement:**
A Brookfield viscometer with spindle #2 rotating at 60 RPM shows a torque reading of 48 units (full scale = 100). The viscometer constant for this spindle/speed is $K = 1.0$ cP per unit. Find the dynamic viscosity.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the viscometer equation.**

$$\mu = K \times \text{Torque reading}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate.**

$$\mu = 1.0 \times 48 = 48 \, \text{cP}$$

Convert to Pa·s:

$$\mu = \frac{48}{1000} = 0.048 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.048 \, \text{Pa·s (or 48 cP)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

| Goal of Testing | Glass Fibre | Resin | Composite Product |
|-----------------|-------------|-------|-------------------|
| **Strength** | Single-filament tension (ASTM D3379) | Cast coupon tension (ASTM D638) | Laminate tension (ASTM D3039) |
| **Stiffness** | Tensile modulus (D3379) | Tensile modulus (D638) | Tensile modulus (D3039) / DMA |
| **Cure / Process** | Sizing LOI (E1358) | DSC, gel time, viscosity | Ultrasonic C-scan, Barcol hardness |
| **Quality of Interface** | Sizing chemistry (FTIR) | — | ILSS (ASTM D2344) |
| **Durability** | Moisture regain | Water absorption (D570) | Ageing (D5229), fatigue (D7791) |
| **Defect detection** | Visual + SEM | Visual | Radiography, thermography, C-scan |
| **Impact** | — | Charpy/Izod on cast resin | Drop-weight (D7136), CAI (D7137) |

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Filament Tensile Test Setup (ASTM D3379)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Glass Filament] --> B[Mounted on Tabbed Card with Adhesive]
    B --> C[Side View Mounting Jig in UTM]
    C --> D[Card Sides Cut Before Loading]
    D --> E[UTM Pulls at 1 mm per min]
    E --> F[Load Cell Records F vs Elongation]
    F --> G[Diameter Measured by Optical Microscope]
    G --> H[Compute sigma_f = 4F over pi d squared]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Resin Quality Control Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Lab Technician
    participant R as Resin Drum
    participant V as Brookfield Viscometer
    participant G as Gel Timer
    participant D as DSC Instrument
    participant Q as QC Report

    L->>R: Draw representative sample
    L->>V: Measure viscosity at 25 C
    V-->>L: Viscosity in cP
    L->>G: Record gel time with hardener
    G-->>L: Gel time in minutes
    L->>D: Heat sample 10 C per min
    D-->>L: Cure onset, peak, delta H
    L->>Q: Compile all data
    Q-->>L: Approve or Reject batch
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Ultrasonic C-Scan of Composite Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Panel Mounted on Scanner] --> B[Transmitter emits 5 MHz US pulse]
    B --> C[Pulse travels through laminate]
    C --> D{Defect Present?}
    D -- No --> E[Full signal received at receiver]
    D -- Yes --> F[Attenuation and reflection at defect]
    E --> G[Map of amplitude over XY plane]
    F --> G
    G --> H[C-scan image with color-coded attenuation]
    H --> I[Defect identified: void, delamination, porosity]
    I --> J[Accept or Reject panel]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Burn-off Procedure for Vf and Vv

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cut Composite Coupon of Known Volume Vc] --> B[Weigh M initial]
    B --> C[Place in Muffle Furnace at 575 C for 5 hours]
    C --> D[Polyester Matrix Burns Off Completely]
    D --> E[Weigh Residual Glass Fibres Mf]
    E --> F[Compute Vf = Mf slash rho f divided by Vc]
    E --> G[Compute Matrix Mass Mr = Mi minus Mf]
    G --> H[Compute Vm = Mr slash rho r divided by Vc]
    F --> I[Void content Vv = 1 minus Vf minus Vm]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compare with Spec; Typical Vv less than 2 percent for Structural]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any three tests commonly performed on glass fibres and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Filament diameter measurement** (ASTM D578): Done by optical microscopy to verify the diameter (typically 5 – 24 μm) since strength is inversely related to flaw density, which scales with diameter.
2. **Tensile strength of single filament** (ASTM D3379): A single filament is mounted on a tab and pulled in a UTM to compute σf = 4F/πd²; typical E-glass strength lies between 1.0 – 2.5 GPa.
3. **Moisture content** (oven-dry at 105 °C): Ensures fibres are dry (moisture < 0.5 %) to prevent voids and poor fibre–matrix bonding in the laminate.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define gel time and pot life of a thermosetting resin. Why are they important in composite manufacturing?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Gel time:** Time taken for the liquid resin (mixed with hardener/catalyst) to reach a non-flowing, gelled state at a specified test temperature (ASTM D2471).
- **Pot life:** Maximum duration for which the mixed resin remains workable (viscosity below usability threshold) inside the mixing container at working temperature.
- **Importance:** They dictate the available time for **impregnation, lay-up, and consolidation**; exceeding them causes voids, poor wet-out, and incomplete cure, leading to weak laminates.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with neat sketches the procedure for determining the **tensile strength of a single glass filament** as per **ASTM D3379**. Mention the formulae, the role of diameter measurement, and the importance of taking at least **30 specimens** for averaging.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A unidirectional GFRP laminate is tested in **3-point bending** as per **ASTM D790**. The specimen has span L = 80 mm, width b = 12 mm, thickness d = 4 mm. The maximum load sustained is P = 720 N. Calculate the **flexural strength** and the **flexural modulus** if the initial slope of the load–deflection curve is m = 320 N/mm.

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Mounting:** A single glass filament is carefully separated from the roving and glued across a rectangular card window (gauge length = 25 mm) using quick-setting adhesive.
2. **Diameter measurement:** The filament diameter is measured at five points along its length using an **optical microscope** (or SEM for high accuracy) and the mean value is used to compute the cross-sectional area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$.
3. **Mounting in UTM:** The card is clamped in the UTM jaws; the two side strips of the card are cut/burnt through so that only the filament carries the load.
4. **Loading:** The specimen is pulled at a constant crosshead speed of about **1 mm/min** until fracture; the load–elongation curve is recorded by the load cell and extensometer.
5. **Calculation:**
   - Stress at break: $\sigma_f = F_{max} / A = 4 F_{max} / \pi d^2$.
   - Strain at break: $\varepsilon_f = \Delta L / L_0$.
   - Modulus: $E_f = \sigma_f / \varepsilon_f$ (from the linear portion of the curve).
6. **Statistical reliability:** At least **30 specimens** are tested so that the mean strength and its **standard deviation / Weibull modulus** can be reported. Glass is brittle and exhibits large scatter (Weibull statistics), so a single reading is statistically meaningless.
7. **Report** includes: diameter, breaking load, gauge length, breaking stress, modulus, mean, standard deviation, and number of specimens.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute flexural strength.**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b d^2} = \frac{3 \times 720 \times 80}{2 \times 12 \times 4^2} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_f = \frac{172800}{2 \times 12 \times 16} = \frac{172800}{384}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = 450 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute flexural modulus.**

$$E_f = \frac{L^3 \, m}{4 b d^3} = \frac{(80)^3 \times 320}{4 \times 12 \times (4)^3} \, \text{MPa}$$

$$E_f = \frac{512000 \times 320}{4 \times 12 \times 64} = \frac{1.6384 \times 10^8}{3072}$$

$$E_f = 53333 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_f \approx 53.33 \, \text{GPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **burn-off method (ASTM D3171 / D2734)** for determining the **fibre volume fraction and void content** in a GFRP laminate. List the formulae and precautions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A small GFRP specimen weighs 6.50 g. After burn-off at 575 °C the residual fibre mass is 3.90 g. The fibre density is 2.55 g/cm³ and the resin density is 1.18 g/cm³. The geometric volume of the specimen is 2.60 cm³. Calculate **$V_f$**, **$V_m$**, and **$V_v$**. Comment on whether the void content is acceptable for a structural laminate.

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Cut a representative coupon** of known geometric volume $V_c$ (measured by water displacement or vernier caliper).
2. **Weigh the coupon** to obtain $M_i$.
3. **Burn in muffle furnace** at **565 – 600 °C** for 4 – 6 h. Thermoset matrix (polyester / epoxy) decomposes into volatile gases; only glass fibres remain.
4. **Cool in desiccator** and re-weigh to obtain $M_f$.
5. **Compute fibre volume fraction:**
   $$V_f = \frac{M_f / \rho_f}{V_c}$$
6. **Compute matrix volume fraction:**
   $$V_m = \frac{(M_i - M_f)/\rho_r}{V_c}$$
7. **Compute void content** (ASTM D2734):
   $$V_v = 1 - V_f - V_m$$
8. **Precautions:**
   - Use a representative, undamaged specimen.
   - Calibrate furnace temperature.
   - Ensure complete burn-off (no char residue).
   - Cool in desiccator to prevent moisture absorption.
   - For carbon fibres, use acid digestion (HNO₃) instead of burn-off.
   - For hygroscopic matrices, pre-dry specimens.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Fibre volume fraction (mass method).**

$$V_f^{\text{vol}} = \frac{M_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{3.90}{2.55} = 1.5294 \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_f = \frac{V_f^{\text{vol}}}{V_c} = \frac{1.5294}{2.60} = 0.5882$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 58.82 \%}$$

**Step 2 — Matrix volume fraction.**

$$M_r = M_i - M_f = 6.50 - 3.90 = 2.60 \, \text{g}$$

$$V_m^{\text{vol}} = \frac{2.60}{1.18} = 2.2034 \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_m = \frac{2.2034}{2.60} = 0.8475$$

$$\boxed{V_m = 84.75 \%}$$

(Note: $V_f + V_m = 1.4357 > 1$, indicating inconsistency in measurements; let us use ASTM D2734 density method instead.)

**Step 3 — Void content via density method.**

Actual composite density:

$$\rho_c = \frac{M_i}{V_c} = \frac{6.50}{2.60} = 2.50 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

Theoretical (rule-of-mixtures) density:

$$\rho_{th} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

(Using the mass-based $V_f$ and $V_m$ that sum to 1.0)

Re-normalise: $V_f = 0.5882 / 1.4357 = 0.4097$, $V_m = 0.8475 / 1.4357 = 0.5903$.

$$\rho_{th} = (2.55 \times 0.4097) + (1.18 \times 0.5903) = 1.0447 + 0.6966 = 1.7413 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

Void content:

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{1.7413 - 2.50}{1.7413} = \frac{-0.7587}{1.7413} = -0.4357$$

The negative result indicates experimental error; the actual $\rho_c$ should be ≤ $\rho_{th}$. This is a common classroom scenario where the student is expected to comment on the discrepancy.

**Realistic interpretation:** If we accept that the geometric volume is overestimated (e.g., the caliper measurement included some surface roughness or porosity), then in practice the **void content of a well-made GFRP should be < 2 %** for structural use. The student should write:

> "The data appears inconsistent (negative $V_v$) likely due to measurement error. In a well-controlled process, a structural GFRP should have $V_v \le 2\%$. If $V_v$ falls between 2–5 %, the laminate is suitable for non-structural use; values > 5 % are generally rejected."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant in ILSS formula** — writing $P/bh$ instead of $3P/4bh$ costs 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — using mm² instead of m² in single-filament stress calculation gives a 1000× error.
> 3. **Ignoring span-to-depth ratio** in flexural test — ASTM D790 requires L/d = 16:1 or 32:1; otherwise shear failure occurs and the test is invalid.
> 4. **Confusing gel time with pot life** — gel time is the time to reach a non-flowing point; pot life is the time the resin remains *workable* (often 2–3× the gel time).
> 5. **Forgetting to re-weigh after cooling in desiccator** during burn-off — moisture re-adsorption skews the fibre mass.
> 6. **Not mentioning standard reference numbers (ASTM D3039, D2344, etc.)** — KTU's marking scheme explicitly rewards the standard citation (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Glass fibre tests** (D3379, D1505, D578, D2584): focus on **diameter, density, single-filament tensile strength, moisture content, and sizing LOI**.
- **Resin tests** (D2393, D2471, D638, D790, DSC): revolve around **viscosity, gel time, pot life, acid/hydroxyl value, and cure exotherm**.
- **Product tests** are of two kinds:
  - **Destructive:** ASTM D3039 (tension), D790 (flexure), D2344 (ILSS), D256/D6110 (impact), D7136/D7137 (CAI), D7791 (fatigue).
  - **Non-Destructive:** Ultrasonic C-scan, X-ray radiography, thermography, acoustic emission, shearography.
- **Key formulae** (in order of frequency in KTU papers):
  - $\sigma_f = 4F/\pi d^2$ (single filament)
  - $\sigma_{LT} = P_{max}/(w \cdot t)$ (laminate tension)
  - $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$ (flexure)
  - $\tau_{ILSS} = 3P/(4bh)$ (short-beam shear)
  - $V_f = (M_f/\rho_f)/V_c$ (burn-off)
  - $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_m$ (void content)
- **Standard test atmosphere:** **23 °C, 50 % RH** (always quote in answers).
- **Void content guidelines:** $V_v < 1 \%$ excellent; 1 – 2 % good; 2 – 5 % acceptable for non-structural; > 5 % rejected.
- **Weibull statistics** are essential for brittle fibre data — always report mean ± standard deviation from ≥ 30 specimens.
- **Sizing chemistry** (silane coupling agents) is what bonds glass to resin — a missing or wrong sizing causes **ILSS drop by 30 – 50 %**.
- **C-scan** detects delamination and porosity; **radiography** detects inclusions and fibre waviness; **thermography** detects disbonds and water ingress.
- **Always cite the ASTM standard number** in KTU answers — it is a free mark and demonstrates professional competence.
- **Units are non-negotiable:** final answers must always carry units (MPa, GPa, %, g/cm³, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Criteria, Laminate Strength, Stress Concentrations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Criteria, Laminate Strength, Stress Concentrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Failure Criteria — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Criterion (Composite Materials):** A mathematically defined boundary in stress (or strain) space that separates the **safe region** (where the laminate will not fail) from the **failure region** (where at least one failure mechanism — fiber breakage, matrix cracking, delamination, or fiber–matrix debonding — initiates). Because composites are **anisotropic** and **inhomogeneous**, no single scalar equivalent stress (like von Mises in metals) suffices; instead, interaction-based polynomial criteria are employed.

The most common failure criteria used in KTU-scope composite analysis are:

| Criterion | Type | Order | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Stress** | Independent (no-interaction) | Linear | Compares each stress component to allowables |
| **Maximum Strain** | Independent (no-interaction) | Linear | Compares each strain component to ultimate strains |
| **Tsai–Hill** | Interactive (quadratic) | Quadratic | Extension of Hill's anisotropic yield to composites |
| **Tsai–Wu** | Interactive (quadratic, general) | Quadratic | Most general 2nd-order; includes coupling term $F_{12}$ |
| **Hashin** | Mode-based | Quadratic | Separates fiber & matrix failure modes |

## 1.2 Laminate Strength — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Laminate Strength:** The maximum in-plane load (expressed as force per unit width, e.g., N/m, or as an equivalent global stress) that a multi-ply laminate can sustain before a designated failure event. Three reference levels are universally used:
> - **First-Ply Failure (FPF)** — the load at which the *first* ply (usually the most off-axis or highest-stressed) satisfies a failure criterion.
> - **Last-Ply Failure (LPF)** — the load at which *all* plies have failed.
> - **Ultimate Laminate Strength** — experimentally measured peak load prior to catastrophic collapse.

## 1.3 Stress Concentration — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Concentration:** A localized amplification of stress near a geometric discontinuity (hole, notch, free edge, ply drop, inclusion, or bonded joint). The ratio of peak stress to nominal (far-field) stress is called the **Stress Concentration Factor (SCF)**, denoted $K_t$.

$$
K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nom}}}
$$

For an isotropic plate with a central circular hole under uniaxial tension, $K_t \approx 3.0$. In composites, $K_t$ depends strongly on **laminate stacking sequence, ply orientation, anisotropy ratio, and hole-to-width ratio**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bundled Sticks" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you have a **bundle of dry twigs tied with rubber bands** (the fibers) embedded inside a **wax block** (the matrix). Now imagine pulling, twisting, and bending this block:
>
> - **Failure Criterion** = the rulebook that tells you when a twig snaps, or when the wax cracks, or when a twig pulls out of the wax. Different rules (Max Stress vs Tsai-Wu) just give different "judgment calls."
> - **Laminate Strength** = the load at which the *weakest* layer in your sandwiched bundle (say, a 45° layer) fails first (FPF), but the bundle may still carry load through the other layers until everything collapses (LPF).
> - **Stress Concentration** = drilling a hole in a wooden plank. The wood fibers have to "go around" the hole, bunching up at the edges — those crowded fibers experience much higher stress than the rest of the board. In composites, because fibers are direction-locked, the "bunching" pattern is even more severe and orientation-dependent.

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in KTU & Engineering Practice

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this Module is high-yield for KTU Exams:**
> - Almost every KTU question paper from Composite Materials (PECHT742) carries **at least one 14-mark problem** asking you to apply a failure criterion to a laminate under combined loading.
> - Numerical problems on **Tsai–Wu / Tsai–Hill** and **First-Ply Failure** are predictable high-scoring items.
> - Stress concentration questions frequently test the **Whitney–Nuismer** or **point-stress / average-stress** approaches for notched laminates.

**Engineering utility:** Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350 — carbon/epoxy fuselage skins with cutouts), wind-turbine blades (with bolted joints and access holes), pressure vessels, and racing monocoques all rely on these exact analyses for **certification and sizing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maximum Stress Failure Criterion (Independent Mode)

### Theory
The simplest criterion — assumes each stress component acts **independently**. The laminate is safe as long as every non-zero stress in the **principal material coordinate system (1–2)** satisfies:

$$
-X_c \le \sigma_1 \le X_t
$$

$$
-Y_c \le \sigma_2 \le Y_t
$$

$$
\vert \tau_{12} \vert \le S
$$

where:
- $X_t, X_c$ = **longitudinal tensile / compressive strength**
- $Y_t, Y_c$ = **transverse tensile / compressive strength**
- $S$ = **in-plane shear strength**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "independent" matters:** This criterion ignores any **synergistic interaction** between stresses. If $\sigma_1$ alone is 50 % of $X_t$ and $\tau_{12}$ alone is 50 % of $S$, the criterion says SAFE — even though in reality the combined state may already be failing. This makes it **non-conservative under combined loading**.

---

## 2.2 Maximum Strain Failure Criterion

$$
-\varepsilon_{1c} \le \varepsilon_1 \le \varepsilon_{1t}
$$

$$
-\varepsilon_{2c} \le \varepsilon_2 \le \varepsilon_{2t}
$$

$$
\vert \gamma_{12} \vert \le \gamma_{12}^{\text{ult}}
$$

These strains must be evaluated in the **material coordinate system**, which requires transforming the global strain tensor via the **transformation matrix $[T]$**.

---

## 2.3 Tsai–Hill Criterion (Quadratic, Special Case of Tsai–Wu)

Derived from Hill's anisotropic yield function specialized to orthotropic composites (omitting $\sigma_1 \sigma_2$ interaction):

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \le 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing feature:** Uses only **strengths**, not separate interaction coefficients. Suitable when $\sigma_1 \sigma_2$ term is negligible. Often used in KTU textbook problems for uniaxial or biaxial stress states.

**Common variation (compressive strengths differ from tensile):**

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X_t} \text{ if } \sigma_1>0 \text{ else } \frac{\sigma_1}{X_c}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X_t X_c} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y_t}\text{ or }Y_c\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \le 1
$$

---

## 2.4 Tsai–Wu Criterion (Most General Quadratic)

The **gold-standard** KTU criterion. General tensor polynomial of degree 2 in stresses:

$$
F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12}\sigma_1 \sigma_2 \le 1
$$

The six coefficients are determined from **strength tests**:

$$
F_1 = \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c}
$$

$$
F_2 = \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c}
$$

$$
F_{11} = \frac{1}{X_t X_c}
$$

$$
F_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c}
$$

$$
F_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2}
$$

$$
F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}} \quad \text{(commonly assumed, "Tsai–Wu simplified form")}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $F_{12}$ term is what makes Tsai–Wu "interactive."** It captures coupling between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ that the Hill criterion cannot. Experimental determination of $F_{12}$ requires a **biaxial test**, which is why most textbooks use the conservative approximation $F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}}$.

---

## 2.5 Hashin Failure Criterion (Mode-Decomposed)

Hashin (1973, 1980) separates **fiber-dominated** and **matrix-dominated** failure:

**Fiber Tensile Failure ($\sigma_1 \ge 0$):**

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X_t}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \ge 1
$$

**Fiber Compressive Failure ($\sigma_1 < 0$):**

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X_c}\right)^2 \ge 1
$$

**Matrix Tensile Failure ($\sigma_2 \ge 0$):**

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y_t}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \ge 1
$$

**Matrix Compressive Failure ($\sigma_2 < 0$):**

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y_c}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S_c}\right)^2 \ge 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Used in:** Finite-element software (ABAQUS, ANSYS Composite PrepPost). Distinguishes failure *modes*, useful for **progressive failure analysis (PFA)**.

---

## 2.6 First-Ply Failure (FPF) — Laminate Strength Analysis

### Procedure
1. Apply global loads $\{N_x, N_y, N_{xy}\}$ to the laminate.
2. Solve **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** to obtain mid-plane strains $\{\varepsilon^0\}$ and curvatures $\{\kappa\}$.
3. Compute ply strains in global coordinates: $\{\varepsilon\}_{xy} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$.
4. **Transform** to material coordinates using $[T]$.
5. Compute ply stresses: $\{\sigma\}_{12} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}_{12}$.
6. Apply chosen failure criterion to each ply. The first ply to violate the criterion defines the **FPF load**.

The FPF load is found by **load-proportional scaling**:

$$
\text{Let } \lambda = \text{scale factor on applied loads.}
$$

$$
\text{At FPF: } \lambda = \lambda_{\text{FPF}}, \quad \text{when } \Phi(\lambda \sigma) = 1
$$

where $\Phi$ is the chosen criterion function.

---

## 2.7 Stress Concentration Around Holes in Composites

### Isotropic Reference (Kirsch, 1898)
For an infinite plate with central hole of radius $r$, loaded by remote uniaxial tension $\sigma_\infty$:

$$
\sigma_{yy}(x, 0) = \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{x^2}\right) + \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 + \frac{3a^4}{x^4}\right)
$$

At $x = a$ (hole edge): $\sigma_{yy} = 3 \sigma_\infty$, so $K_t = 3$.

### Anisotropic (Composite) Plates — Lekhnitskii Solution
For an **orthotropic infinite plate** (orifice in an infinite laminate, fibers along 1-direction):

$$
K_t^{\infty} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{E_y}{E_x}} - \nu_{yx}\frac{E_y}{E_x}
$$

(approximate form for $E_x / E_y$ ratio typical of unidirectional CFRP).

### Finite-Width Correction — Howland's Equation

$$
K_t = K_t^{\infty}\left[1 + \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{1 - \frac{2r}{W}}} - \frac{1.5(2r/W)}{(1-2r/W)^2}\right]
$$

where $W$ = plate width and $2r/W$ = hole-to-width ratio.

### Whitney–Nuismer Notch Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **finite-width composite laminates** with notches/holes, KTU-expected formulas:

**Point-Stress Criterion (PSC):**

$$
\frac{\sigma_\infty}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \frac{2r}{W} + k^2 \left(\frac{2r}{W}\right)^2}}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ = unnotched strength, $k$ = characteristic length parameter (ply-dependent).

**Average-Stress Criterion (ASC):**

$$
\frac{\sigma_\infty}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1 - \frac{2r}{W}}{\sqrt{1 - \frac{2r}{W} + k^2\left(\frac{2r}{W}\right)^2}}
$$

**Open-Hole Tensile Strength (OHTS) Semi-Empirical:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{OH}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{1 + 0.45 \left(\frac{2r}{W}\right) - 0.18 \left(\frac{2r}{W}\right)^2}}
$$

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Print this table as a single-page revision card before the exam. **Avoid `|` inside math** — write $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ only.

| # | Formula / Concept | Compact Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum Stress | $\vert \sigma_i \vert \le X_i$ for each component $i$ |
| 2 | Maximum Strain | $\vert \varepsilon_i \vert \le \varepsilon_i^{ult}$ |
| 3 | Tsai–Hill | $\left(\sigma_1/X\right)^2 - \left(\sigma_1 \sigma_2 / X^2\right) + \left(\sigma_2 / Y\right)^2 + \left(\tau_{12} / S\right)^2 \le 1$ |
| 4 | Tsai–Wu General | $F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1$ |
| 5 | Tsai–Wu $F_1, F_2$ | $F_1 = 1/X_t - 1/X_c$, $F_2 = 1/Y_t - 1/Y_c$ |
| 6 | Tsai–Wu $F_{11}, F_{22}$ | $F_{11} = 1/(X_t X_c)$, $F_{22} = 1/(Y_t Y_c)$ |
| 7 | Tsai–Wu $F_{66}, F_{12}$ | $F_{66} = 1/S^2$, $F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}}$ |
| 8 | Hashin Fiber Tension | $\left(\sigma_1/X_t\right)^2 + \left(\tau_{12}/S\right)^2 \ge 1$ |
| 9 | Hashin Matrix Tension | $\left(\sigma_2/Y_t\right)^2 + \left(\tau_{12}/S\right)^2 \ge 1$ |
| 10 | FPF scaling | $\lambda_{\text{FPF}} = 1 / \sqrt{\Phi(\sigma)}$ for quadratic criteria |
| 11 | SCF isotropic | $K_t = 3$ (small hole in infinite plate) |
| 12 | Lekhnitskii SCF | $K_t^{\infty} \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{E_y/E_x}$ |
| 13 | Whitney–Nuismer PSC | $\sigma_\infty / \sigma_0 = 1/\sqrt{1 - 2r/W + k^2(2r/W)^2}$ |
| 14 | Whitney–Nuismer ASC | $\sigma_\infty / \sigma_0 = (1 - 2r/W)/\sqrt{1 - 2r/W + k^2(2r/W)^2}$ |
| 15 | OHTS semi-empirical | $\sigma_{\text{OH}} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{1 + 0.45(2r/W) - 0.18(2r/W)^2}$ |

**Units used throughout:**
- Strengths $X, Y, S$ → **MPa** or **GPa**
- Moduli → **GPa**
- $k$ (characteristic length) → **mm** or **inches**
- $r, W$ (radius, width) → **mm** or **inches**

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Application | Criterion Used |
|---|---|---|
| Aircraft fuselage skins | Open-hole tensile panels (Boeing 787, A350) | Whitney–Nuismer / OHTS |
| Pressure vessels | Filament-wound CFRP | Tsai–Wu |
| Wind-turbine blades | Root joint + trailing-edge ply drops | Hashin (in FE) |
| F1 monocoque | Impact + buckling + combined | Tsai–Wu + Puck |
| Marine hulls | Sandwich panels with cut-outs | Max Stress + SCF |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tsai–Hill Criterion from Hill's Anisotropic Yield Function

### Starting Point — Hill's General Yield (1948)

For an **orthotropic** material with principal axes aligned with 1, 2, 3, Hill proposed:

$$
F(\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + G(\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 + H(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + 2 L \tau_{23}^2 + 2 M \tau_{31}^2 + 2 N \tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

where $F, G, H, L, M, N$ are **anisotropic strength coefficients**.

### Restriction to Plane Stress ($\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{31} = 0$)

Setting $\sigma_3 = 0$ and dropping the out-of-plane shear terms:

$$
F \sigma_2^2 + G \sigma_1^2 + H(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + 2 N \tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

Expanding $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2$:

$$
F \sigma_2^2 + G \sigma_1^2 + H(\sigma_1^2 - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2) + 2 N \tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

Grouping terms:

$$
(G + H)\sigma_1^2 + (F + H)\sigma_2^2 - 2H \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + 2 N \tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

### Identifying the Coefficients from Uniaxial Tests

**Test 1:** Uniaxial tension along 1 ($\sigma_1 = X$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\tau_{12} = 0$):

$$
(G + H) X^2 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G + H = \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

**Test 2:** Uniaxial tension along 2 ($\sigma_2 = Y$, $\sigma_1 = 0$, $\tau_{12} = 0$):

$$
(F + H) Y^2 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F + H = \frac{1}{Y^2}
$$

**Test 3:** Pure in-plane shear ($\tau_{12} = S$, others zero):

$$
2 N S^2 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = \frac{1}{2 S^2}
$$

### Combining to Eliminate $G, F$

The **Tsai–Hill form** assumes $H = 1/X^2$ (so that $G \to 0$ effectively; this matches the original Tsai interpretation that the fiber-direction strength dominates the longitudinal normal stress term):

From Test 1: $G = 1/X^2 - H = 0$ (if $H = 1/X^2$). From Test 2: $F = 1/Y^2 - H = 1/Y^2 - 1/X^2$.

Substituting back:

$$
(G + H)\sigma_1^2 + (F + H)\sigma_2^2 - 2H \sigma_1\sigma_2 + 2N\tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

becomes

$$
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{X^2} + \frac{1}{Y^2}\sigma_2^2 - \frac{2}{X^2}\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \frac{2}{2S^2}\tau_{12}^2 = 1
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{X^2} - \frac{\sigma_1\sigma_2}{X^2} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{Y^2} + \frac{\tau_{12}^2}{S^2} = 1
$$

**Final Tsai–Hill Criterion:**

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 = 1\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tsai–Wu Coefficients from Strength Data

### Starting Polynomial

$$
F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \le 1
$$

### Test 1: Longitudinal Tension ($\sigma_1 = X_t$, $\sigma_2 = \tau_{12} = 0$)

$$
F_1 X_t + F_{11} X_t^2 = 1 \quad \cdots (i)
$$

### Test 2: Longitudinal Compression ($\sigma_1 = -X_c$, $\sigma_2 = \tau_{12} = 0$)

$$
-F_1 X_c + F_{11} X_c^2 = 1 \quad \cdots (ii)
$$

### Solving (i) and (ii) for $F_1, F_{11}$

From (i): $F_{11} X_t^2 = 1 - F_1 X_t$
From (ii): $F_{11} X_c^2 = 1 + F_1 X_c$

Adding after multiplying by appropriate factors:

$$
F_{11}\left(\frac{1}{X_t^2} \text{ and } \frac{1}{X_c^2}\right) \text{ yields} \quad F_{11} = \frac{1}{X_t X_c}, \quad F_1 = \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c}
$$

### Test 3 & 4: Transverse Tension / Compression
By identical algebra:

$$
F_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c}, \quad F_2 = \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c}
$$

### Test 5: Pure Shear ($\tau_{12} = S$)

$$
F_{66} S^2 = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad F_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2}
$$

### The $F_{12}$ Coefficient
Biaxial test required; if unavailable, the **Tsai–Wu stability condition** restricts $F_{12}$:

$$
F_{11} F_{22} - F_{12}^2 \ge 0
$$

The commonly used **simplified form** sets $F_{12}$ to its maximum negative value (most conservative):

$$
F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Application of Tsai–Wu for First-Ply Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-style problem:** A unidirectional glass/epoxy ply is loaded such that the stresses in the material coordinates are:
> $\sigma_1 = 150$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{12} = 25$ MPa.
>
> **Material strengths:**
> $X_t = 1500$ MPa, $X_c = 800$ MPa, $Y_t = 40$ MPa, $Y_c = 140$ MPa, $S = 70$ MPa.
>
> **Test whether the ply fails** using (a) Maximum Stress, (b) Tsai–Hill, (c) Tsai–Wu.

### Step 1 — Maximum Stress Check

$$
\sigma_1 = 150 \le X_t = 1500 \;\; \text{OK}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 30 \le Y_t = 40 \;\; \text{OK}
$$

$$
\vert \tau_{12} \vert = 25 \le S = 70 \;\; \text{OK}
$$

**Verdict:** **No failure** under Maximum Stress criterion. The reserve factor is $\sim 1.33$ in transverse tension.

### Step 2 — Tsai–Hill Calculation

Using $X = X_t = 1500$ MPa, $Y = Y_t = 40$ MPa, $S = 70$ MPa:

$$
\Phi_{TH} = \left(\frac{150}{1500}\right)^2 - \frac{(150)(30)}{(1500)^2} + \left(\frac{30}{40}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{25}{70}\right)^2
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\left(\frac{150}{1500}\right)^2 = (0.10)^2 = 0.0100
$$

$$
\frac{(150)(30)}{(1500)^2} = \frac{4500}{2\,250\,000} = 0.0020
$$

$$
\left(\frac{30}{40}\right)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625
$$

$$
\left(\frac{25}{70}\right)^2 = (0.3571)^2 = 0.1276
$$

Sum:

$$
\Phi_{TH} = 0.0100 - 0.0020 + 0.5625 + 0.1276 = 0.6981
$$

Since $\Phi_{TH} = 0.6981 < 1$, the ply is **SAFE** under Tsai–Hill. Reserve factor:

$$
\text{RF}_{TH} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.6981}} \approx 1.197
$$

### Step 3 — Tsai–Wu Calculation

First compute the strength coefficients:

$$
F_1 = \frac{1}{1500} - \frac{1}{800} = 0.000667 - 0.001250 = -5.83 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
F_2 = \frac{1}{40} - \frac{1}{140} = 0.02500 - 0.00714 = 1.786 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
F_{11} = \frac{1}{1500 \times 800} = \frac{1}{1\,200\,000} = 8.333 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-2}
$$

$$
F_{22} = \frac{1}{40 \times 140} = \frac{1}{5600} = 1.786 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-2}
$$

$$
F_{66} = \frac{1}{70^2} = \frac{1}{4900} = 2.041 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-2}
$$

$$
F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(8.333 \times 10^{-7})(1.786 \times 10^{-4})} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1.488 \times 10^{-10}} = -\tfrac{1}{2}(1.220 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
F_{12} = -6.10 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{MPa}^{-2}
$$

Now evaluate the Tsai–Wu polynomial:

$$
\Phi_{TW} = F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12}\sigma_1 \sigma_2
$$

Term-by-term:

$$
F_1 \sigma_1 = (-5.83 \times 10^{-4})(150) = -8.75 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
F_2 \sigma_2 = (1.786 \times 10^{-2})(30) = 5.358 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
F_{11}\sigma_1^2 = (8.333 \times 10^{-7})(150)^2 = (8.333 \times 10^{-7})(22\,500) = 1.875 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
F_{22}\sigma_2^2 = (1.786 \times 10^{-4})(30)^2 = (1.786 \times 10^{-4})(900) = 1.607 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 = (2.041 \times 10^{-4})(25)^2 = (2.041 \times 10^{-4})(625) = 1.276 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = 2(-6.10 \times 10^{-6})(150)(30) = 2(-6.10 \times 10^{-6})(4500) = -5.49 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Summing:

$$
\Phi_{TW} = -0.0875 + 0.5358 + 0.01875 + 0.1607 + 0.1276 - 0.0549 = 0.7004
$$

Since $\Phi_{TW} \approx 0.7004 < 1$, the ply is **SAFE** under Tsai–Wu. Reserve factor:

$$
\text{RF}_{TW} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.7004}} \approx 1.195
$$

### Comparison Table

| Criterion | Failure Index $\Phi$ | Verdict | Reserve Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Stress | N/A (component-wise) | SAFE | 1.33 (transverse) |
| Tsai–Hill | 0.698 | SAFE | 1.197 |
| Tsai–Wu | 0.700 | SAFE | 1.195 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Tsai–Hill and Tsai–Wu agree closely here. Maximum Stress is the most lenient (non-conservative) — it does not see the combined-state effect. For laminate design, **Tsai–Wu is recommended** as it captures $\sigma_1\sigma_2$ interaction.

---

## 3.4 First-Ply Failure Scaling Procedure (Full Worked-Out)

**Problem:** A $[0/45/-45/90]_s$ carbon/epoxy laminate, total thickness $t = 2$ mm, is subjected to in-plane loads $N_x = 100$ N/mm (only). Find the FPF load using the Tsai–Wu criterion.

Assume each ply has thickness $0.25$ mm, $E_1 = 140$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $G_{12} = 5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$, and strengths: $X_t = 1500$, $X_c = 1200$, $Y_t = 50$, $Y_c = 200$, $S = 70$ (all in MPa).

### Step 1 — Apply Classical Lamination Theory

For the symmetric $[0/45/-45/90]_s$ laminate, the $[B]$ matrix is zero, so mid-plane strains are governed by:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} N_x \\ N_y \\ N_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = [A] \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Because $N_y = N_{xy} = 0$ and the laminate is balanced, $\varepsilon_y^0$ and $\gamma_{xy}^0$ are very small (non-zero due to coupling of the ±45° plies). For brevity, assume from CLT output:

$$
\varepsilon_x^0 = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \varepsilon_y^0 = -2.0 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \gamma_{xy}^0 = 0
$$

For symmetric laminate, $\kappa = 0$, so ply strains equal mid-plane strains.

### Step 2 — Compute Ply Stresses (Material Coordinates)

For the 0° ply: $\sigma_1 = E_1 \varepsilon_x^0 = 140\,000 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} = 84$ MPa; $\sigma_2 = \nu_{12} E_2 \varepsilon_x^0 = 0.3 \times 10\,000 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8$ MPa; $\tau_{12} = 0$.

### Step 3 — Compute Tsai–Wu Index for the 0° Ply

Using precomputed coefficients from §3.3 methodology:

$$
F_1 = 1/1500 - 1/1200 = -1.667 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_2 = 1/50 - 1/200 = 0.0150
$$

$$
F_{11} = 1/(1500 \times 1200) = 5.556 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
F_{22} = 1/(50 \times 200) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{66} = 1/70^2 = 2.041 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}} = -2.63 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Evaluate at 0° ply stresses:

$$
\Phi_{0} = (-1.667 \times 10^{-4})(84) + (0.0150)(1.8) + (5.556 \times 10^{-7})(84)^2 + (1.0 \times 10^{-4})(1.8)^2 + (2.041 \times 10^{-4})(0)^2 + 2(-2.63 \times 10^{-6})(84)(1.8)
$$

Computing each term:

- Linear $\sigma_1$: $-1.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 84 = -1.40 \times 10^{-2}$
- Linear $\sigma_2$: $0.0150 \times 1.8 = 2.70 \times 10^{-2}$
- Quadratic $\sigma_1^2$: $5.556 \times 10^{-7} \times 7056 = 3.92 \times 10^{-3}$
- Quadratic $\sigma_2^2$: $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.24 = 3.24 \times 10^{-4}$
- Quadratic $\tau_{12}^2$: $0$
- Interaction: $2 \times (-2.63 \times 10^{-6}) \times 151.2 = -7.96 \times 10^{-4}$

Sum:

$$
\Phi_0 = -0.0140 + 0.0270 + 0.00392 + 0.000324 + 0 - 0.000796 = 0.0165
$$

For the 0° ply: $\Phi_0 = 0.0165 \ll 1$. (Very safe — expected, as fibers aligned with load.)

### Step 4 — Repeat for 90° Ply (in Material Coordinates)

The 90° ply has fibers perpendicular to the load. After transformation:

$$
\sigma_1^{(90)} = 1.8 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2^{(90)} = 84 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{12}^{(90)} = 0
$$

Now apply Tsai–Wu:

- Linear $\sigma_1$: $(-1.667 \times 10^{-4})(1.8) = -3.0 \times 10^{-4}$
- Linear $\sigma_2$: $(0.0150)(84) = 1.260$
- Quadratic $\sigma_1^2$: $(5.556 \times 10^{-7})(3.24) = 1.80 \times 10^{-6}$
- Quadratic $\sigma_2^2$: $(1.0 \times 10^{-4})(7056) = 0.7056$
- Quadratic $\tau_{12}^2$: $0$
- Interaction: $2(-2.63 \times 10^{-6})(1.8)(84) = -7.95 \times 10^{-4}$

Sum:

$$
\Phi_{90} = -0.0003 + 1.260 + 0.0000018 + 0.7056 + 0 - 0.000795 = 1.965
$$

Since $\Phi_{90} \gg 1$, the 90° ply has **already failed** at the applied load of $N_x = 100$ N/mm.

### Step 5 — Determine the Critical Ply and Scale Factor

The 90° ply is critical. To find FPF, scale the loads by $\lambda$:

$$
\Phi_{90}(\lambda) = F_2 (\lambda \sigma_2^{(90)}) + F_{22}(\lambda \sigma_2^{(90)})^2 + \cdots = 1
$$

Since $\sigma_1^{(90)}$ and $\tau_{12}$ are small, ignore their contributions. Then:

$$
F_2 \lambda \sigma_2 + F_{22} \lambda^2 \sigma_2^2 \approx 1
$$

$$
(0.0150)(84)\lambda + (1.0 \times 10^{-4})(7056)\lambda^2 = 1
$$

$$
1.260 \lambda + 0.7056 \lambda^2 = 1
$$

Solve quadratic $0.7056 \lambda^2 + 1.260 \lambda - 1 = 0$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{-1.260 + \sqrt{1.260^2 + 4(0.7056)(1)}}{2(0.7056)} = \frac{-1.260 + \sqrt{1.5876 + 2.8224}}{1.4112}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{-1.260 + \sqrt{4.410}}{1.4112} = \frac{-1.260 + 2.100}{1.4112} = \frac{0.840}{1.4112} \approx 0.595
$$

### Step 6 — FPF Load

$$
N_x^{\text{FPF}} = \lambda_{\text{FPF}} \times N_x = 0.595 \times 100 = 59.5 \text{ N/mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Even though the 0° plies carry the load well, the transverse (90°) plies fail early because of their low $Y_t$. **Design implication:** reduce 90° content, or use a tougher matrix, or shift to a quasi-isotropic layup with thinner 90° plies.

---

## 3.5 Stress Concentration Derivation — Kirsch Solution (Isotropic Reference)

For an infinite isotropic plate under uniaxial tension $\sigma_\infty$ with a circular hole of radius $a$, the stresses in **polar coordinates** $(r, \theta)$ are:

$$
\sigma_{rr} = \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) + \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 - \frac{4a^2}{r^2} + \frac{3a^4}{r^4}\right)\cos 2\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta} = \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) - \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 + \frac{3a^4}{r^4}\right)\cos 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_{r\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}\left(1 + \frac{2a^2}{r^2} - \frac{3a^4}{r^4}\right)\sin 2\theta
$$

### Evaluating at $r = a$, $\theta = 90°$ (top of hole, perpendicular to load):

$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}(a, 90°) = \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}(1 + 1) - \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}(1 + 3)\cos 180°
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}(a, 90°) = \sigma_\infty - \frac{\sigma_\infty}{2}(4)(-1) = \sigma_\infty + 2 \sigma_\infty = 3 \sigma_\infty
$$

**Hence $K_t = 3$** for an infinite isotropic plate with a small circular hole — a result known as the **Kirsch solution**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Failure Criterion

| Scenario | Recommended Criterion | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Unidirectional ply under uniaxial tension/compression | Maximum Stress | Simplest, sufficient for principal-axis loading |
| Combined loading with non-zero $\tau_{12}$ | Tsai–Hill | Captures shear–normal interaction (no $F_{12}$ required) |
| General in-plane stress state | Tsai–Wu | Most general; includes $F_{12}$ coupling |
| Progressive failure / FE simulation | Hashin / Puck | Mode-decomposed, suitable for PFA |
| Open-hole strength prediction | Whitney–Nuismer PSC / ASC | Empirical + characteristic length |
| Quick screening during design iteration | Maximum Stress | Fast, conservative for some load cases |

---

## 3.7 Validity & Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> **Where each criterion breaks down:**
> - **Max Stress / Max Strain** — no interaction; unsafe for combined states.
> - **Tsai–Hill** — does not distinguish tension vs compression asymmetry.
> - **Tsai–Wu** — requires biaxial test for accurate $F_{12}$; otherwise the simplified form may under- or over-predict.
> - **Hashin** — still quadratic; doesn't capture kink-band or delamination onset.
> - **Whitney–Nuismer** — empirical; "characteristic length" $k$ must be calibrated per layup.

---

## 3.8 Summary of Physical Meaning of Each Term

| Term in Tsai–Wu | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $F_1 \sigma_1$ | Hydrostatic asymmetry between tension and compression along fibers |
| $F_2 \sigma_2$ | Hydrostatic asymmetry between tension and compression transverse to fibers |
| $F_{11} \sigma_1^2$ | Squared fiber-direction strength |
| $F_{22} \sigma_2^2$ | Squared transverse strength |
| $F_{66} \tau_{12}^2$ | Squared shear strength |
| $2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Coupling — interaction of normal stresses (Poisson-effect amplified) |

## 4.1 Failure Criterion Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Global Loads Nx Ny Nxy Applied to Laminate"] --> B["Apply Classical Lamination Theory"]
    B --> C["Compute Mid-plane Strains epsilon0 and Curvatures kappa"]
    C --> D["For Each Ply: Compute Global Strains"]
    D --> E["Transform Strains to Material Coords via T"]
    E --> F["Compute Material-Coord Stresses sigma1 sigma2 tau12"]
    F --> G{"Choose Failure Criterion"}
    G --> G1["Max Stress: Compare each component to allowable"]
    G --> G2["Max Strain: Compare each strain to ultimate"]
    G --> G3["Tsai Hill: Evaluate quadratic polynomial"]
    G --> G4["Tsai Wu: Evaluate general quadratic"]
    G --> G5["Hashin: Evaluate mode-decomposed indices"]
    G1 --> H{"Phi less than or equal 1 for ALL plies"}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I["SAFE — No Ply Failure"]
    H --> J["FAIL — Identify Critical Ply and Load Multiplier lambda FPF"]
    J --> K["Scale back lambda to get FPF Load"]
    K --> L["Output Nx FPF, Ny FPF, or Strain FPF"]
```

## 4.2 Laminate Hierarchy — From Micromechanics to FPF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Micromechanics: Fiber + Matrix Constituents"] --> B["Ply Engineering Constants E1 E2 G12 nu12"]
    B --> C["Ply Strengths Xt Xc Yt Yc S"]
    C --> D["Single-Ply Failure Criterion: Tsai Wu / Hashin"]
    D --> E["Stack Sequence: 0/45/-45/90 etc"]
    E --> F["Laminate A B D Matrices via CLT"]
    F --> G["Apply Loads to Get Ply Stresses"]
    G --> H["Ply-by-Ply Failure Index"]
    H --> I{"Any Phi exceeds 1"}
    I -- No --> J["Laminate Safe at Current Load"]
    I -- Yes --> K["First Ply Failure Occurred"]
    K --> L["Reduce Load Scale by Lambda FPF"]
    L --> M["Report FPF Strength"]
```

## 4.3 Stress Concentration Architecture Around a Circular Hole

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Far Field Stress sigma infinity Applied to Laminate Plate"] --> B["Laminate Contains Circular Hole of Radius a"]
    B --> C["Stress Field Disturbs Around Hole"]
    C --> D["Lekhnitskii Orthotropic Solution for Kt"]
    D --> E["Howland Correction for Finite Width W"]
    E --> F["Final Stress Distribution sigma theta theta at Hole Edge"]
    F --> G["Maximum sigma at theta = 90 degrees"]
    G --> H["Kt equals sigma max divided by sigma nominal"]
    H --> I["Compare with Allowable Strength"]
    I --> J{"sigma max less than allowable"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Notch is Safe"]
    J -- No --> L["Strength Reduction Required"]
    L --> M["Apply Whitney Nuismer PSC or ASC"]
    M --> N["Predict Notched Strength sigma OH"]
```

flowchart TD
    A["Compute Ply Stresses sigma1 sigma2 tau12"] --> B{"sigma1 greater than or equal 0"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Fiber Tensile Index FFt"]
    B -- No --> D["Fiber Compressive Index FFc"]
    A --> E{"sigma2 greater than or equal 0"}
