1. **Pulse-Echo (single-sided):** One transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver. Requires access from one side only.
2. **Through-Transmission (TTU):** Two transducers on opposite faces. Best sensitivity; doubles handling.

### 2.1.3 Attenuation in Composites

Ultrasonic intensity decays exponentially with distance:

$$ I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-2 \alpha x} $$

$$ \alpha = \alpha_a + \alpha_s $$

where $\alpha_a$ = absorption (viscous + thermal), $\alpha_s$ = scattering (from fibres, voids, porosity). In CFRP, $\alpha$ is **strongly frequency-dependent** and **anisotropic** — much higher across plies than along fibres.

## 2.2 Radiographic Testing (RT)

**Operating Principle:** Ionising electromagnetic radiation (X-rays, $\gamma$-rays) passes through the part and is recorded on a film or digital detector (DR/CR). Local attenuation by inclusions, moisture, or density variations produces a **shadowgraph**.

Beer–Lambert Law governs the intensity reaching the detector:

$$ I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} $$

where $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (m⁻¹), $x$ = path length (m).

For composites, **low-kV X-rays (10–60 kV)** are preferred because CFRP has low atomic number (C dominates), giving low contrast for delaminations *parallel* to the beam. Hence, delaminations are notoriously difficult with RT — they appear only when the beam is *angled* to the ply plane (oblique RT).

**Computed Tomography (CT):** Reconstructs a full 3-D volumetric map from many 2-D projections (Radon transform → filtered back-projection). Resolution down to < 5 µm in micro-CT. *Gold standard* but extremely expensive and slow.

## 2.3 Infrared Thermography (IRT)

**Operating Principle:** The specimen is heated (by flash lamp, halogen, or ultrasound) and the **time-evolution of surface temperature** is recorded by an IR camera. Subsurface defects disturb heat diffusion, creating **hot/cold spots** on the surface.

For a defect of depth $d$, the surface temperature response to a Dirac heat pulse (1-D semi-infinite model) is:

$$ T(0,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\rho c \sqrt{\pi \alpha t}} \cdot \left( 1 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^n e^{-n^2 d^2/\alpha t} \right) $$

**Best contrast** is achieved at the *characteristic time*:

$$ t_c = \dfrac{d^2}{\pi \, \alpha} $$

This is the famous **"time-of-flight of maximum contrast"** rule. *Larger / deeper* defects appear *later* in the thermogram.

- **Pulsed Thermography (PT):** Fast flash heating, time-domain analysis. Best for shallow defects.
- **Lock-in Thermography (LT):** Modulated heating, frequency-domain (phase) analysis. Better SNR, deeper penetration.
- **Ultrasonic / Vibrothermography:** Defect is excited by high-power UT or mechanical vibration; frictional heating at crack faces is detected by IR.

## 2.4 Acoustic Emission (AE)

**Operating Principle:** Sudden release of strain energy inside the composite (e.g., matrix crack initiation, fibre snap, delamination growth) generates **transient elastic stress waves** (20 kHz – 1 MHz). Piezoelectric sensors detect these *passively*.

Key AE parameters:
- **Amplitude (dB)** — energy of event.
- **Duration (µs)** — event length.
- **Counts (N)** — oscillation counts above threshold.
- **Rise time (µs)** — time to peak.
- **Frequency content (kHz)** — distinguishes damage modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AE is the *only* real-time NDI that **listens to damage as it happens** during loading. It is used in **proof tests** of pressure vessels, **in-flight SHM** of aircraft, and **fatigue testing** of coupons. Frequency-spectrum clustering (Felicity ratio, b-value) is used to discriminate matrix cracks vs. fibre breaks vs. delamination.

## 2.5 Shearography (Electronic Speckle Pattern Interferometry — ESPI)

**Operating Principle:** The part is **lightly stressed** (vacuum, thermal, or acoustic load). Surface displacement gradient (out-of-plane strain) is recorded interferometrically. A defect **locally reduces the strain field**, producing a "butterfly" fringe pattern in the difference image.

Shearography is **one-sided, full-field, fast, and insensitive to rigid-body motion** — making it ideal for large composite aerospace parts in the field. Detects delamination, disbond, and impact damage down to ~ 1 mm depth.

## 2.6 Tap Test & Visual Testing (VT)

The **oldest, cheapest, and most accessible** NDI:
- A coin or special hammer **taps** the surface; the operator listens to the tone.
- A **delaminated region** produces a dull "thud" vs. a sharp "ring" of sound laminate.
- Enhanced by **electronic tap-test devices** with microphones and FFT analysis.
- ASTM D2530 standardisation exists for composite sandwich panels.

VT is mandatory as the **first step** in any NDI campaign (ASTM E2845, NAS 410).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | MRayl |
| 2 | $R = \left( \frac{Z_2-Z_1}{Z_2+Z_1} \right)^2$ | Reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T = \frac{4Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1+Z_2)^2}$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2\alpha x}$ | Ultrasonic attenuation | W/m² |
| 5 | $d = \frac{c \Delta t}{2}$ | Defect depth (pulse-echo) | m |
| 6 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert X-ray law | W/m² |
| 7 | $t_c = \frac{d^2}{\pi \alpha}$ | Thermography optimum time | s |
| 8 | $f = \frac{c}{2d}$ | Resonant frequency of delamination | Hz |
| 9 | $V = \frac{E}{\rho}$ | Ultrasonic velocity (long.) relation | (m/s)² |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations let an NDI engineer choose the *right transducer frequency* (high freq → small defects but high attenuation), the *right time-gate* in a C-scan, the *right flash energy* in thermography, and the *right kV* in X-ray. They are also the basis of all NDI **acceptance criteria** in aerospace (e.g., MIL-HDBK-17, ASTM E2533, EN 3915).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defect Depth from Pulse-Echo Time-of-Flight

We are given a CFRP laminate ($\rho = 1600$ kg/m³, $c = 2900$ m/s) and a pulse-echo UT reading showing a reflection at $\Delta t = 1.5 \; \mu s$ after the front-surface echo.

**Step 1 — Recall the governing relationship.**
The ultrasonic wave travels from the transducer to the defect and back. Total path length = $2d$. Travel time at constant velocity $c$ is:

$$ 2 d = c \cdot \Delta t $$

**Step 2 — Solve for defect depth $d$.**

$$ d = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$ d = \frac{2900 \;\text{m/s} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}}{2} $$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$ d = \frac{4.35 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}}{2} = 2.175 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} $$

**Step 5 — Express the result in millimetres.**

$$ d = 2.175 \;\text{mm} \approx 2.2 \;\text{mm} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The defect (a likely delamination) lies **2.2 mm below the entry surface**. For a 16-ply CFRP laminate at 0.125 mm/ply, this is at the **interface of plies 17 and 18** (i.e., 8 plies deep). A C-scan gated at this depth would map the area of the defect.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient at a Delamination

A typical 8-ply quasi-isotropic CFRP laminate ($Z_{CFRP} = 4.64$ MRayl) has a delamination. The delamination is air-filled ($Z_{air} = 0.000411$ MRayl).

**Step 1 — Write the reflection coefficient expression.**

$$ R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2 $$

**Step 2 — Insert $Z_1 = Z_{CFRP} = 4.64 \times 10^6$ and $Z_2 = Z_{air} = 411$.**

$$ R = \left( \frac{411 - 4.64 \times 10^6}{411 + 4.64 \times 10^6} \right)^2 $$

**Step 3 — Approximate the denominator $\approx 4.64 \times 10^6$ since $4.64 \times 10^6 \gg 411$.**

$$ R \approx \left( \frac{-4.6396 \times 10^6}{4.6404 \times 10^6} \right)^2 \approx (-0.9998)^2 $$

**Step 4 — Final value.**

$$ R \approx 0.9996 \approx 99.96\% $$

**Step 5 — Implication.**
More than **99.9 % of the incident ultrasonic energy is reflected** by the air gap. This is why UT is *so* sensitive to delamination in composites — a 1 mm deep, 5 mm wide delamination is essentially a perfect acoustic mirror.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Inspection Time in Pulsed Thermography

A 3 mm deep delamination is suspected in a GFRP panel. The thermal diffusivity of GFRP is $\alpha = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s.

**Step 1 — Use the time-of-maximum-contrast rule.**

$$ t_c = \frac{d^2}{\pi \alpha} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $d = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m and $\alpha = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s.**

$$ t_c = \frac{(3 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi \times 1.8 \times 10^{-7}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$ (3 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$ \pi \times 1.8 \times 10^{-7} = 5.6549 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s} $$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$ t_c = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{5.6549 \times 10^{-7}} = 15.91 \;\text{s} $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The IR camera should be **sampled at ~ 0.1 s intervals for the first ~ 30 s** after the flash, and the **maximum thermal contrast** at the delamination site is expected around **15.9 s**. Earlier frames will show only surface features; later frames will show thermal smearing.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Selection of NDI Method

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU "Method-Selection Matrix"** is a *high-yield* tool. Memorise it for Part-A and ESE.

| Damage Type | Best NDI Method | Reason | Minimum Detectable Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delamination | UT C-scan / Shearography | Huge acoustic mismatch with air | 3–5 mm Ø |
| Porosity | UT attenuation / X-ray | Scattering / density variation | 1–2 % vol |
| Matrix cracks | AE / high-freq UT | Active crack emission / scattering | 0.5 mm |
| Fibre breakage | RT / micro-CT | Density contrast in fibre tow | 0.1 mm |
| Impact (BVID) | IRT / Shearography | Subsurface sub-mm delamination | 0.5 mm depth |
| Disbond (honeycomb) | UT through-transmission | Full attenuation across core | 10 mm |
| Moisture ingress | Microwave NDT / Neutron RT | Dielectric / H-sensitivity | bulk % |
| Ply waviness | Phased-array UT / CT | Geometric mapping | mm-scale |
| Surface cracks | VT / Dye-penetrant | Direct visual | 0.05 mm |

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Tree (Textual)

- **One-sided access + large area + quick** → Shearography or IRT.
- **Quantitative + small defects + cm depth** → UT C-scan.
- **Volume info + research/lab** → CT (micro-CT for sub-mm).
- **Real-time damage under load** → Acoustic Emission.
- **In-service / cure-process monitoring** → Embedded PZT / FBGs + AE.
- **No equipment / field screening** → Visual + Tap test.
- **Through honeycomb core** → UT through-transmission or X-ray.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an NDI Workflow for Composites

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Composite Component Entering NDI] --> B1{Initial Visual + Tap Test}
    B1 -- Suspect --> B2[Mark Region of Interest]
    B1 -- Pass --> B3[Move to In-Service Use]
    B2 --> C1{Defect Type Suspected}
    C1 -- Subsurface / Delamination --> C2[Ultrasonic C-Scan]
    C1 -- Shallow / Impact --> C3[Pulsed Thermography]
    C1 -- Active Loading --> C4[Acoustic Emission Monitoring]
    C1 -- Honeycomb Disbond --> C5[Through Transmission UT]
    C1 -- Volume / Research --> C6[X-Ray CT]
    C2 --> D1[Post-Process: B C Scan + AI Defect Classifier]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{Defect Exceeds Acceptance Criteria}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Repair or Reject]
    E1 -- No --> F2[Clear and Document]
    F2 --> B3
    F1 --> G1[Document Repair and Re-Inspect]
```

## 4.2 Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Test Setup — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pulse Generator 1 to 50 MHz] --> B1[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    B1 -- 1 Mechanical Wave --> C1[Composite Laminate]
    C1 -- 2 Reflected Echo --> B1
    B1 -- 3 Electrical Echo --> D1[Receiver Amplifier TGC]
    D1 --> E1[Time-Gated Analyser]
    E1 -- 4 Depth Signal --> F1[A Scan Display]
    E1 -- 5 XY Stage Encoders --> G1[C Scan Image]
    E1 -- 6 Cross Section --> H1[B Scan Image]
    F1 --> I1[Defect Depth d = c Delta t over 2]
    G1 --> J1[Defect Area and Location]
    H1 --> K1[Defect Through Thickness Profile]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Mapping of NDI Techniques vs. Damage Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start NDI Selection] --> B1{Defect Classification}
    B1 --> C1[Delamination and Disbond]
    B1 --> C2[Porosity and Voids]
    B1 --> C3[Matrix Cracks]
    B1 --> C4[Fibre Breakage]
    B1 --> C5[Impact Damage BVID]
    C1 --> D1[UT C Scan]
    C1 --> D2[Shearography]
    C2 --> D3[UT Attenuation]
    C2 --> D4[Radiography]
    C3 --> D5[Acoustic Emission]
    C3 --> D6[High Frequency UT]
    C4 --> D7[Micro CT]
    C4 --> D8[Radiography]
    C5 --> D9[Pulsed Thermography]
    C5 --> D10[Shearography]
    C5 --> D11[Phased Array UT]
```

## 4.4 Damage-Mode vs. NDI Technique — Cross-Mapping Table

| Damage Mode | Visual | Tap | UT C-Scan | Shearography | Thermography | Radiography | AE | CT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Delamination | Weak | Strong | Excellent | Excellent | Strong | Weak | Medium | Excellent |
| Porosity | None | Weak | Strong | Weak | Medium | Strong | Medium | Excellent |
| Matrix Cracks | Weak | Weak | Medium | Medium | Strong | Weak | Excellent | Strong |
| Fibre Breakage | None | None | Medium | None | Weak | Strong | Strong | Excellent |
| BVID | None | Medium | Strong | Excellent | Excellent | Weak | Strong | Excellent |
| Disbond | Weak | Strong | TTU Excellent | Excellent | Strong | Weak | Medium | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 3 marks]**
*Define acoustic impedance. Why is ultrasonic C-scan exceptionally sensitive to delamination in CFRP laminates?*

**Model Answer:**
Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of material density $\rho$ and longitudinal wave velocity $c$:

$$ Z = \rho \cdot c \quad (\text{Rayl}) $$

In CFRP, $Z_{CFRP} \approx 4.64$ MRayl, while air (inside a delamination) has $Z_{air} \approx 411$ Rayl. The reflection coefficient

$$ R = \left( \frac{Z_{CFRP} - Z_{air}}{Z_{CFRP} + Z_{air}} \right)^2 \approx 0.9996 $$

i.e., nearly 100 % of the ultrasonic energy is reflected. Hence even sub-5 mm delaminations act as acoustic mirrors and are easily detected by C-scan, making UT the workhorse of composite NDI.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 3 marks]**
*List four NDI techniques suitable for detecting impact damage (BVID) in composite laminates. Mention one limitation of X-radiography for detecting delamination.*

**Model Answer:**
Four NDI techniques for BVID: **(i)** Ultrasonic C-scan, **(ii)** Pulsed Infrared Thermography, **(iii)** Shearography, **(iv)** Phased-Array UT.
*Limitation of X-radiography:* Delaminations lie **parallel to the X-ray beam direction** (through-thickness), producing very little in-plane density contrast. Radiography is therefore *insensitive* to delaminations oriented parallel to the beam — oblique or CT radiography is required.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ▶ QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **pulsed thermography** for NDI of composites. Derive the expression for the *time of maximum contrast* and discuss how the choice of flash energy and frame rate affects detectability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **ultrasonic C-scan, shearography, and acoustic emission** for in-service inspection of a composite wing-skin. State the physical principle, sensitivity, and a typical application limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Pulsed Thermography

**Principle:** A short, high-energy flash (typically 2–10 kJ xenon, 1–10 ms) deposits heat on the front face of the composite. The IR camera records the surface temperature decay. A subsurface defect acts as a **thermal barrier**, retarding heat flow; this appears as a **hot spot** in early frames. As heat accumulates, the contrast evolves; the **time at which contrast is maximum** is determined by defect depth $d$ and thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ of the host composite.

**Derivation:** Consider 1-D heat diffusion in a semi-infinite solid with a planar delamination at depth $d$. The surface temperature response to a Dirac heat pulse $Q$ per unit area is:

$$ T(0,t) = \frac{Q}{\rho c_p \sqrt{\pi \alpha t}} \left[ 1 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^n \, e^{-n^2 d^2 / (\alpha t)} \right] $$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ and setting $\frac{dT}{dt} = 0$ gives the time of maximum contrast:

$$ t_c = \frac{d^2}{\pi \alpha} $$

**Effect of flash energy & frame rate:**
- Higher flash energy → larger temperature rise → higher SNR, but risk of surface ablation.
- Frame rate must be ≥ 60 Hz to capture the early-time transient for shallow defects (< 1 mm).
- For deep defects (> 5 mm), lower frame rate (1–5 Hz) over 30–60 s is preferred.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Comparison Table

| Feature | UT C-Scan | Shearography | Acoustic Emission |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Reflection of US waves at impedance discontinuities | Interferometric measurement of strain gradient under load | Detection of transient elastic waves from active damage |
| **Sensitivity** | Delamination ≥ 3 mm Ø; porosity ≥ 1 % | Delamination ≥ 5 mm; impact damage ≥ 1 J | Matrix cracks, fibre breaks in real time |
| **Access** | One-sided (pulse-echo) or two-sided (TTU) | One-sided | Sensor-mounted; not scanning |
| **Limitation** | Couplant required; slow on large parts | Sensitive to vibration, rigid body motion | No pre-existing damage; only active damage |
| **Application** | Aerospace panels, rotor blades | Field inspection of large airframes | Proof tests, fatigue, in-flight SHM |

---

### ▶ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **A-scan, B-scan, and C-scan** modes of ultrasonic testing. Derive the relationship between defect depth and round-trip time of flight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the principle, equipment, and limitations of **X-ray computed tomography (CT)** for NDI of fibre-reinforced composites. Compare it with conventional 2-D radiography. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — UT Scan Modes

**A-Scan (Amplitude Scan):**
The transducer emits a short pulse and the receiver records voltage vs. time at one point on the surface. The X-axis is time (or depth), the Y-axis is amplitude (dB). Defects produce echoes between the front-surface echo and the back-wall echo.

**B-Scan (Brightness Scan):**
The transducer scans along a line; each A-scan is displayed as a vertical column whose brightness corresponds to amplitude. The resulting 2-D image is a **cross-section** of the laminate, showing defect through-thickness position and lateral extent along the scan line.

**C-Scan (Constant-Depth Scan):**
The transducer is raster-scanned in X and Y over the part. A **time gate** selects reflections from a chosen depth. The peak amplitude (or time-of-flight) in the gate is colour-mapped to produce a **plan-view image** of defect distribution.

**Derivation of depth–time relation:**
Round-trip distance = $2d$. Wave travels at constant $c$:

$$ \Delta t = \frac{2d}{c} $$

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} $$

For CFRP ($c = 2900$ m/s), a 1.5 µs round-trip ⇒ $d = 2.175$ mm.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — CT vs. Radiography

**CT Principle:** The part is rotated (or the source–detector pair rotates) through 360°, recording hundreds of 2-D projections at different angles. A **Radon transform** is inverted using **filtered back-projection** to reconstruct a 3-D volumetric map of linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y,z)$. Each voxel has $\sim 5 \,\mu m$ resolution in micro-CT.

**Equipment:** Micro-focus X-ray tube (50–300 kV), high-resolution flat-panel detector, precision rotation stage, GPU reconstruction workstation, shielding cabinet.

**Limitations of CT:** Extremely expensive (> ₹ 5 Cr for industrial micro-CT), long scan times (minutes to hours), limited to small parts or coupons, ring artefacts, beam-hardening artefacts in thick CFRP, health-safety constraints for high-kV sources.

**Comparison with 2-D Radiography:**

| Aspect | 2-D Radiography | CT (3-D) |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single shadowgraph | 3-D volumetric map |
| Delamination | Poor (parallel to beam) | Excellent (any orientation) |
| Cost | Low | Very high |
| Throughput | High (seconds) | Low (minutes–hours) |
| Depth resolution | None (integrated) | ~ 5–50 µm |
| Industrial use | Production line, large parts | Lab, R&D, failure analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Skipping the derivation step** in pulse-echo depth: writing $d = c\Delta t/2$ without showing the *path = 2d* logic → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing C-scan with B-scan:** C-scan is *plan-view at constant depth*; B-scan is *cross-sectional*. Many students mix them up.
> 3. **Missing the Beer–Lambert law** in radiography questions — it is a *must-cite* equation.
> 4. **Forgetting the "1.5 μs" round-trip vs. one-way time** — always 2× for pulse-echo.
> 5. **Writing "AE is similar to UT"** — wrong. AE is *passive* (no transmitter); UT is *active* (transmitter + receiver). This is a 3-mark trap.
> 6. **Omitting units** in numerical answers: $d = 2.175$ *mm*, not just "2.175".
> 7. **No mention of acceptance criteria** (MIL-HDBK-17, ASTM E2533) — examiners award 1 mark for standards awareness.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 4 NDI**

- ✅ **NDI = NDT = NDE** — all refer to damage detection *without harm* to the part.
- ✅ **Five families:** Conventional (VT, Tap) | Sonic/UT | Radiation (RT, CT) | Thermal (IRT) | Optical (Shearography, DIC).
- ✅ **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ governs UT reflection; air–CFRP interface gives $R \approx 0.9996$ (huge sensitivity to delamination).
- ✅ **Pulse-echo depth:** $d = c \Delta t / 2$. **One-way:** $d = c \Delta t$.
- ✅ **UT modes:** A (1-D), B (2-D cross-section), C (2-D plan-view). **Phased array** = electronic steering.
- ✅ **UT attenuation:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2 \alpha x}$; $\alpha$ rises with frequency and porosity.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — governs RT.
- ✅ **Thermography optimum time:** $t_c = d^2 / (\pi \alpha)$ — *larger/deeper* defects appear *later*.
- ✅ **AE is passive** — listens to active damage (matrix crack, fibre break, delamination growth).
- ✅ **Shearography** = full-field, one-sided, vacuum/thermal stress; detects delamination, disbond, BVID.
- ✅ **CT is the gold standard** — 3-D volumetric, micro-CT down to 5 µm; expensive.
- ✅ **Damage catalogue:** Delamination, porosity, matrix crack, fibre break, fibre–matrix debond, BVID, disbond, inclusion, ply waviness.
- ✅ **Selection rule of thumb:**
  - Delamination → UT C-scan or Shearography.
  - Porosity → UT attenuation or RT.
  - Active damage → AE.
  - Field/full-area → Shearography or IRT.
  - Lab/research → CT.
- ✅ **Acceptance standards:** MIL-HDBK-17, ASTM E2533, ASTM E2845, EN 3915, NAS 410.
- ✅ **Couplant for UT:** water, gel, or dry-coupled elastomer (water-jet for production).
- ✅ **Resolution vs. depth trade-off:** higher frequency = better resolution but less penetration.
- ✅ **BVID = "Barely Visible Impact Damage"** — can reduce compression strength by 50 % with no external sign → NDI is *critical*.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — NON-DESTRUCTIVE INSPECTION OF COMPOSITES**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Types of Composite Repair and Their Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Types of Composite Repair and Their Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Repair** is a damage-mitigation technique in which a fiber-reinforced polymeric (FRP) patch, doubler, or insert is adhesively or mechanically bonded to a damaged parent laminate to restore the original load-carrying capacity, stiffness, and fatigue life of the structure without replacing the entire component.

In **PECHT742 Module 4**, the term is restricted to *in-situ* repair of laminated composites (CFRP/GFRP) using additional composite laminates, adhesives, and/or fasteners. The repair philosophy rests on **load re-distribution** — the patch shares the service load with the damaged parent material through an interlaminar shear transfer mechanism across the bonded interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology You Must Use in Exams**
> - **Adherend** – the original (parent) composite part being repaired.
> - **Adhesive** – the polymeric film (e.g., epoxy FM 300, AF 163) that bonds the patch to the adherend.
> - **Doubler / Patch** – the additional composite laminate added over the damaged zone.
> - **Scarf Ratio** – the slope of the tapered cut, expressed as 1:N (e.g., 1:10, 1:20, 1:50).
> - **Restored Strength Efficiency (η)** – ratio of repaired strength to undamaged (pristine) strength, usually expressed as a percentage.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Composite Repair like a Plaster on a Broken Bone.**
> When you fracture a bone, a doctor applies a *fibreglass cast* (composite patch) over the injury. The cast does not become part of the bone, but it *shares the load* while the bone heals. A *stepped-lap* cast (overlapping strips) is stronger than a flat plaster because it gradually transfers stress — no sudden stress peak tears the cast off. In the same way, composite patches restore strength by **bridging** the damaged zone and **shedding** the service load around the crack.

**Engineering Snapshot — Where You Will See This in Industry:**
- **Aerospace**: Boeing 737/A320 composite wing-skin repairs using **scarf patches**.
- **Marine**: GRP boat hull delamination repaired with **external wet-layup patches**.
- **Civil**: Carbon-FRP wraps retrofitting damaged bridge columns.
- **Automotive & Wind**: Carbon-fiber spar caps repaired with **flush-stepped doublers**.

The standard reference benchmark used by KTU examiners is the **ASTM D 5573 / D 5801** standard for *Class A and Class B* aerospace composite repair, and the **CMH-17 (Composite Materials Handbook)** design philosophy — both of which mandate the use of **micromechanics-validated** repair schemes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Composite Repair — Load Transfer Mechanism

Every repair geometry exists to solve **one** physical problem: how to move the load out of the *damaged* zone and back into the *healthy* parent material without creating a new stress concentration at the edge of the patch. The mechanism is the **shear-lag effect** — the adhesive layer carries **interlaminar shear stress (τ)** that gradually transfers in-plane normal load (σ) from the adherend to the patch.

The five design parameters that govern this transfer are:

1. **Adherend stiffness** ($E_1 t_1$)
2. **Patch stiffness** ($E_2 t_2$)
3. **Adhesive shear stiffness** ($G_a / t_a$)
4. **Bonded overlap length** ($L$)
5. **Taper / step geometry** (controlling peel stress $\sigma_{peel}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** The most important dimensionless group is the **Goland–Reissner parameter** $\lambda$. It decides whether your repair is *over-designed* (wasted material) or *under-designed* (premature failure).

### 2.2 Classification of Composite Repairs (the Master Tree)

| **Category** | **Sub-Type** | **KTU Standard Reference** |
|---|---|---|
| **External Patch (Adhesive)** | Single-side external patch | Class A, CMH-17 Vol. 3 |
| **External Patch (Adhesive)** | Double-side (symmetric) external patch | Class A, CMH-17 Vol. 3 |
| **Flush / Contour Repair** | Scarf-bonded patch | Class B, ASTM D 5573 |
| **Flush / Contour Repair** | Stepped-lap patch | Class B, CMH-17 Vol. 3 |
| **Hybrid Repair** | Bolted + Bonded combination | SAE AS7250 |
| **Process Variants** | Wet layup / Prepreg / VARTM infusion | — |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols are defined in-text. Do NOT reproduce the vertical-pipe `$\vert$` symbol literally inside a table — use `$\mid$` or `$\vert$` to keep LaTeX safe.**

| **Equation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Use in Exam** |
|---|---|---|
| Shear-Lag Parameter (Volkersen) | $\lambda^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{G_{a}}{t_{a}}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_{1}t_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{E_{2}t_{2}}\right)$ | Characteristic stiffness of bonded joint |
| Bonded Overlap Shear Stress | $\tau(x) \;=\; \dfrac{P\,\lambda}{2\,b}\!\left[\dfrac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}-\dfrac{(1-\psi)}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}\right]$ | Peak shear at joint ends |
| Scarf Repair Axial Stress | $\sigma_{x}(x) \;=\; \dfrac{P}{t_{1}\,b}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1+\dfrac{E_{1}\,t_{1}}{E_{2}\,t_{2}(x)}}$ | Stress along tapered repair |
| Scarf Angle (Slope) | $\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{t_{1}}{L_{s}}$ | Geometric parameter |
| Repair Efficiency | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{repaired}}}{\sigma_{\text{pristine}}}\times 100\,\%$ | Strength restoration metric |
| Peeling Stress (Goland–Reissner) | $\sigma_{peel,\max} \;=\; k\!\left(\dfrac{P}{b\,t_{1}}\right)$ | Avoid bond-line failure |
| Stiffness Mismatch Factor | $\psi \;=\; \dfrac{E_{2}t_{2}-E_{1}t_{1}}{E_{2}t_{2}+E_{1}t_{1}}$ | Controls stress peak location |

**Where P** is the in-plane load, **b** is the joint width, **L** is the overlap length, **G_a / t_a** is the shear stiffness of the adhesive, and **E₁t₁, E₂t₂** are the tensile stiffnesses of adherend and patch respectively.

### 2.4 Why Each Repair Type is Different — The "How" Reasoning

- **External Patch** is *fast, cheap, no material removal* — but it adds aerodynamic drag and a peel-stress singularity at the patch edge.
- **Scarf Repair** *removes* the damaged material and *tapers* the cut → no abrupt stiffness change → minimal stress concentration. Trade-off: it requires precision machining (1:50 to 1:80 taper) and is *time-intensive*.
- **Stepped-Lap Repair** is a *discretized approximation* of a scarf, easier to fabricate in shop-floor conditions, gives ~80-90% efficiency of a true scarf.
- **Bolted + Bonded Hybrid** combines the *load sharing* of adhesive with the *fail-safe* nature of mechanical fasteners — vital for thick-section aerospace laminates.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The scarf ratio determines the *restorable compressive strength* of a fighter-aircraft composite skin. A 1:20 scarf can typically restore **>90%** of pristine strength, whereas a 1:10 scarf only achieves **70-75%**. This is why the U.S. Air Force TO 1-1-690 manual mandates **1:30 or steeper** for primary structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volkersen Shear-Lag Equation for a Single-Lap Bonded Composite Repair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** Every KTU Module-4 question on bonded-repair load transfer can be solved *only* if you can derive or apply the Volkersen equation. The examiner's marking scheme assigns **at least 5 marks** for this derivation.

**Statement of the Problem:**
Consider a *double-lap* bonded joint (analogous to a *double-sided patch repair* on a composite panel). Let subscripts 1, 2 refer to the adherends (the parent laminate and the patch) and 'a' to the adhesive. We assume:
- Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli assumption).
- The adhesive carries only **shear** stress.
- Loads are in-plane and quasi-static.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium of the upper adherend (patch, region 2)**

A free-body cut at position $x$ of the upper adherend of width $b$ gives:

$$
P_{2}(x) \;=\; b\,t_{2}\,\sigma_{2}(x)
$$

Differentiating and using the fact that the *adhesive shear* must equal the rate of change of axial load:

$$
\frac{dP_{2}}{dx} \;=\; b\,\tau(x)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_{2}}{dx} \;=\; \frac{\tau(x)}{t_{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility / Constitutive relation**

The displacement of the patch is:

$$
u_{2}(x) \;=\; \varepsilon_{2}(x)\,x \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{2}(x)}{E_{2}}\,x
$$

Similarly, the displacement of the adherend (region 1) is:

$$
u_{1}(x) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{1}(x)}{E_{1}}\,x
$$

The adhesive shear strain is the *difference* in displacements divided by adhesive thickness $t_a$:

$$
\gamma_{a} \;=\; \frac{u_{2}-u_{1}}{t_{a}}
$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's law for the adhesive**

$$
\tau(x) \;=\; G_{a}\,\gamma_{a} \;=\; \frac{G_{a}}{t_{a}}\,(u_{2}-u_{1})
$$

Substituting the displacement expressions:

$$
\tau(x) \;=\; \frac{G_{a}}{t_{a}}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_{2}}{E_{2}}-\frac{\sigma_{1}}{E_{1}}\right)x
$$

(Linear in $x$ for constant stresses — a key approximation.)

**Step 4 — Global equilibrium**

Total load is conserved:

$$
P \;=\; b\,t_{1}\,\sigma_{1}(x) \;+\; b\,t_{2}\,\sigma_{2}(x)
$$

Solving for $\sigma_1$:

$$
\sigma_{1}(x) \;=\; \frac{P}{b\,t_{1}} \;-\; \frac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}\,\sigma_{2}(x)
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate and eliminate $\sigma_1$**

Differentiating the equilibrium and Step-3 equation, then combining:

$$
\frac{d^{2}\sigma_{2}}{dx^{2}} \;-\; \lambda^{2}\,\sigma_{2} \;=\; 0
$$

where the *Volkersen shear-lag parameter* is:

$$
\lambda^{2} \;=\; \frac{G_{a}}{t_{a}}\!\left(\frac{1}{E_{1}\,t_{1}}+\frac{1}{E_{2}\,t_{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the second-order ODE**

The general solution is:

$$
\sigma_{2}(x) \;=\; A\,\cosh(\lambda x) \;+\; B\,\sinh(\lambda x)
$$

Applying symmetry / boundary conditions for a balanced double-lap joint of half-length $L/2$:

$$
\sigma_{2}(\pm L/2) \;=\; \frac{P}{2\,b\,t_{2}}
$$

we obtain:

$$
\sigma_{2}(x) \;=\; \frac{P}{2\,b\,t_{2}}\!\left[\frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}\right] \;+\; \frac{\psi\,P}{2\,b\,t_{2}}\!\left[\frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}\right]
$$

where the **stiffness mismatch factor** is:

$$
\psi \;=\; \frac{E_{2}t_{2}-E_{1}t_{1}}{E_{2}t_{2}+E_{1}t_{1}}
$$

**Step 7 — Final shear-stress distribution in the adhesive**

Differentiating the expression for $\sigma_2$ and using $d\sigma_2/dx = \tau/t_2$:

$$
\tau(x) \;=\; \frac{P\,\lambda}{2\,b}\!\left[\frac{\sinh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L/2)} \;+\; \psi\cdot\frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Result:** Shear stress is **NOT uniform** along the bond line. It peaks at the joint ends ($x = \pm L/2$) and is *minimum* at the centre. This is why adhesive joints always fail *from the ends* inward — the Volkersen theory mathematically predicts this experimental observation.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Use This in the 14-Mark Answer)

| **Property** | **External Patch** | **Scarf Joint** | **Stepped-Lap** | **Bolted + Bonded** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aerodynamic Profile | Poor (raised) | **Excellent (flush)** | Good (near-flush) | Poor (raised) |
| Material Removal | **None** | Yes (tapered) | Yes (stepped) | Minimal |
| Peel Stress Concentration | **High** | Low | Moderate | Low (bolts share) |
| Typical Efficiency $\eta$ | 50-70% | **85-95%** | 75-88% | 80-90% |
| Fabrication Skill Required | Low | **High (precision)** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Inspection Difficulty | Low | **High** | Moderate | Low (X-ray of bolts) |
| Repair Time (hrs) | 2-4 | **8-24** | 4-8 | 4-6 |
| Cost (relative) | Low | **High** | Medium | Medium |
| Best Suited For | Secondary structure, field repair | **Primary structure** | Medium-critical parts | Thick laminates, fail-safe design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree of Composite Repairs (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Composite Repair Methods"]
    rootB["Bonded Repairs"]
    rootC["Mechanical-Bonded Hybrid"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC

    rootB --> extA["External Patch Repair"]
    rootB --> scarfA["Scarf Joint Repair"]
    rootB --> stepA["Stepped Lap Repair"]
    rootB --> flushA["Flush Contour Repair"]

    extA --> extSingle["Single Sided Patch"]
    extA --> extDouble["Double Sided Patch"]

    scarfA --> scarfUni["Unidirectional Scarf"]
    scarfA --> scarfDouble["Double Scarf Symmetric"]

    stepA --> stepUni["Single Sided Stepped Lap"]
    stepA --> stepDouble["Double Sided Stepped Lap"]

    rootC --> boltA["Bolted Doubler"]
    rootC --> hybridB["Bolted Plus Bonded Doubler"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Composite Repair Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    detectA["Damage Detection NDT Scan"]
    assessA["Damage Severity Assessment"]
    designA["Repair Geometry Selection"]
    fabA["Patch Fabrication Wet Layup or Prepreg"]
    prepA["Surface Preparation Scarf or Step Machining"]
    bondA["Adhesive Application Cure Cycle"]
    inspectA["Post Repair NDT Validation"]
    certA["Structural Re certification"]

    detectA --> assessA
    assessA --> designA
    designA --> fabA
    fabA --> prepA
    prepA --> bondA
    bondA --> inspectA
    inspectA --> certA
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Scarf-Bonded Repair (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    parentL["Parent Laminate Adherend 1"]
    taperZone["Tapered Scarf Zone 1 colon 20"]
    patchA["Composite Patch Adherend 2"]
    adhesiveL["Adhesive Film 0 dot 1 mm"]
    loadIn["Applied Load P"]
    loadOut["Load Transferred via Shear Lag"]

    parentL --> taperZone
    taperZone --> adhesiveL
    adhesiveL --> patchA
    loadIn --> parentL
    patchA --> loadOut
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional View of a Stepped-Lap Repair (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layerA["Step 1 Removed Material"]
    layerB["Step 2 Removed Material"]
    layerC["Step 3 Removed Material"]
    patchStep["Composite Patch With Matching Steps"]
    adhA["Adhesive Layer Between Each Step"]

    layerA --> adhA
    layerB --> adhA
    layerC --> adhA
    adhA --> patchStep
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mermaid instead of hand sketches?**
> Mermaid renders natively on GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, and the KTU e-Learning portal. The blocks above clearly show the *flow of load* and the *layered architecture* of each repair — a schematic representation that examiners reward with full marks when a hand-drawn sketch is not feasible in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between an *external patch repair* and a *scarf joint repair* in composite laminates. Mention one specific advantage of each.**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **External Patch** | **Scarf Joint** |
|---|---|---|
| Surface profile | Raised (non-flush) | Flush / contoured |
| Stress at edge | High peel peak | Low (tapered) |
| Aerodynamic penalty | Yes | **None** |
| Material removal | **Not required** | Required (taper cut) |
| Best for | Temporary / secondary | **Primary structure** |

- *Advantage of external patch:* Quick, no material removal, suitable for field repairs (1 mark).
- *Advantage of scarf joint:* Eliminates peel-stress singularity; restores >90% of pristine strength (1 mark).
- *Comparison of edge stress:* Explanation of geometric discontinuity (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q2. State any three benefits of using composite repair over conventional metallic repair.**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High specific strength and stiffness** — composite patches add load capacity with negligible weight penalty (1 mark).
2. **Corrosion resistance** — CFRP/GFRP patches do not corrode, unlike aluminium or steel doublers (1 mark).
3. **Tailorable anisotropy** — fibre orientation can be matched to the principal stress direction, giving optimum load transfer (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "composite is light" or "composite is strong" without specifying *over what* or *with which metallic baseline*. Always quantify: "specific strength (3–5× that of aluminium alloy 2024-T3)" and cite the relevant standard (ASTM D3039, CMH-17).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU End-Semester Examination – Module 4 Choice Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat schematic diagrams, explain the **single-sided external patch repair** and **double-sided scarf-bonded repair** procedures for a damaged composite laminate. Compare their *load transfer efficiency* and *stress concentration behaviour*.

**Model Solution Outline (Examiner's Key):**

1. **Single-sided external patch (3 marks)**
   - Describe surface preparation: sandblast + solvent clean (0.5 mark).
   - Apply adhesive film (e.g., FM 300) on parent laminate (0.5 mark).
   - Place pre-cured or co-cured patch, vacuum-bag, cure at 120°C / 0.3 MPa (0.5 mark).
   - Schematic: raised patch with **peel-stress singularity at patch termination** (0.5 mark).
   - Comment on efficiency $\eta \approx$ 50–70% (1 mark).

2. **Double-sided scarf-bonded repair (3 marks)**
   - Machining of tapered cut at 1:20 to 1:30 (0.5 mark).
   - Apply prepreg patch on *both* sides for symmetry (0.5 mark).
   - Vacuum-bag and autoclave cure (0.5 mark).
   - Schematic: smooth taper, **no stress concentration** (0.5 mark).
   - Comment on efficiency $\eta \approx$ 85–95% (1 mark).

3. **Comparative statement (1 mark)** — scarf joint is preferred for *primary structure*; external patch is *field-expedient*.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Volkersen shear-lag theory**, derive the shear-stress distribution $\tau(x)$ in the adhesive layer of a single-lap bonded composite repair. State the **two key assumptions** of the theory.

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. Free-body diagram of upper adherend — equilibrium equation (1 mark).
2. Compatibility condition: adhesive shear strain $\gamma_a = (u_2 - u_1)/t_a$ (1 mark).
3. Constitutive relation: $\tau = G_a \gamma_a$ (0.5 mark).
4. Derivation of second-order ODE and identification of $\lambda^2$ (1.5 marks).
5. General solution $\sigma_2 = A\cosh(\lambda x) + B\sinh(\lambda x)$ (0.5 mark).
6. Apply BCs and obtain final $\tau(x)$ expression (1.5 marks).
7. State the two key assumptions: (i) adhesive carries **only** shear, (ii) adherends are elastic with no bending (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Volkersen Derivations:**
> 1. Forgetting to specify that $\tau(x)$ is **maximum at the joint ends** — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. Writing $\lambda^{2} = G_a/t_a$ instead of the *full* expression with both adherend stiffnesses — examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. Not stating the **two assumptions** in part (b) — common 1-mark deduction.
> 4. Skipping the *step showing* the ODE derivation (going straight to the answer) — KTU penalises this heavily.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with diagrams the **stepped-lap bonded repair** and **bolted-plus-bonded hybrid repair** techniques. Under what conditions is each preferred?

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Stepped-lap repair (3.5 marks)**
   - Schematic of stepped cut (3–5 steps typical) — neat diagram (1 mark).
   - Each step carries shear — discretised Volkersen (1 mark).
   - Fabrication is simpler than scarf — shop-floor feasible (0.5 mark).
   - $\eta \approx$ 75–88% (1 mark).

2. **Bolted + Bonded hybrid repair (3.5 marks)**
   - Pre-drill parent and patch, install hi-lok / hi-tigue fasteners (0.5 mark).
   - Apply adhesive between patch and parent (0.5 mark).
   - Bolts provide **fail-safe** redundancy (1 mark).
   - Schematic (1 mark).
   - Preferred for *thick* laminates (t > 3 mm) and *certified primary structure* where NDT of bond is uncertain (0.5 mark).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of composite repair in **aerospace** applications. State the relevant **certification standards**.

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Benefits (4 marks) — at least 5 needed for full credit:**
   - Specific strength 4–5× aluminium (1 mark).
   - Fatigue life extension (1 mark).
   - Corrosion resistance (0.5 mark).
   - Conformal / flush aerodynamic surface (0.5 mark).
   - Reduced aircraft on-ground (AOG) time — saving lakhs per day (0.5 mark).
   - Tailorable fibre orientation (0.5 mark).

2. **Limitations (2 marks):**
   - Sensitivity to *moisture ingress* at bond line (0.5 mark).
   - Difficulty of *in-situ NDT* of adhesive (0.5 mark).
   - High *skill* requirement for scarf machining (0.5 mark).
   - Thermal mismatch (CTE) at elevated temperature (0.5 mark).

3. **Certification Standards (1 mark):**
   - **CMH-17** (Composite Materials Handbook, Vol. 3).
   - **ASTM D 5573 / D 5801** (Aerospace repair classification).
   - **SAE AS7250** (Bonded repair of composite structures).
   - **FAR 25.571** (Damage-tolerance evaluation).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Year B.Tech — 60-Second Recall Map**

- **Definition:** Composite repair = adhesively/bolted addition of FRP laminate to restore strength/stiffness.
- **Four canonical types:** External patch, Scarf, Stepped-lap, Bolted + Bonded hybrid.
- **Best efficiency:** Scarf joint ($\eta$ = 85–95%) — but hardest to fabricate.
- **Worst efficiency:** Single-side external patch ($\eta$ = 50–70%) — but fastest.
- **Key dimensionless group:** Volkersen parameter $\lambda^{2} = \dfrac{G_{a}}{t_{a}}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_{1}t_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{E_{2}t_{2}}\right)$.
- **Peak shear location:** Always at the *joint ends* (x = ±L/2), never at the centre.
- **Peel stress:** Highest in external patch, lowest in scarf — Volkersen theory does NOT predict peel; **Goland–Reissner** extension does.
- **Design rule of thumb (Air Force TO 1-1-690):** Scarf ratio ≥ 1:30 for primary aircraft structure.
- **Five major benefits:** High specific strength, corrosion resistance, fatigue improvement, reduced AOG time, tailorable anisotropy.
- **Three major limitations:** Moisture sensitivity, NDT difficulty of bond line, thermal mismatch.
- **Standards to quote in answers:** CMH-17, ASTM D 5573, SAE AS7250, FAR 25.571.
- **Equation to memorise cold:** $\tau(x) = \dfrac{P\,\lambda}{2\,b}\!\left[\dfrac{\sinh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L/2)} + \psi\cdot\dfrac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}\right]$.
- **One-liner for viva:** *"The lower the Volkersen λ, the longer the joint you need; the higher the stiffness mismatch ψ, the higher the end-peel stress."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Composite Materials: Synthesis, Properties and Applications of Green Composites, Nanocomposites and Hybrid Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT742 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Composite Materials: Synthesis, Properties and Applications of Green Composites, Nanocomposites and Hybrid Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Advanced Composite Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Advanced composite materials* are engineered multi-phase systems in which at least one constituent (the **reinforcement** or **filler**) is engineered at the **micro/nano scale**, or is derived from **renewable/biodegradable** sources, or combines **two or more distinct reinforcement types** within a single matrix, to deliver property profiles unattainable by conventional composites.

In the context of **PECHT742 (Module 4)**, advanced composites are classified into three principal families:

| # | Class | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Green Composites** | Both reinforcement and matrix are bio-derived / biodegradable |
| 2 | **Nanocomposites** | At least one reinforcement phase has **≤ 100 nm** in at least one dimension |
| 3 | **Hybrid Composites** | Two or more **different** reinforcements embedded in a single matrix |

---

## 1.2 Green Composites — Intuitive Overview

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Green Composite (KTU Definition):** A composite material in which **either the reinforcement (natural fibre) or the matrix (biopolymer resin) or both** are sourced from renewable, bio-based, and/or biodegradable feedstocks, and which exhibits reduced carbon footprint, end-of-life biodegradability, and sustainable life-cycle performance.

Common constituents:

- **Natural fibres (reinforcement):** jute, flax, hemp, sisal, banana, kenaf, coir, bamboo, ramie, abaca.
- **Biopolymer matrices:** poly(lactic acid) PLA, poly(hydroxybutyrate) PHB, starch, soy protein, chitosan, natural rubber, lignin-phenolic, cellulose acetate.

### 1.2.2 Real-World Analogy 🍃

> **"Think of a leaf sandwich."**
> A leaf has **cellulose fibres** (the green reinforcement) held together by **pectin & lignin** (the bio-matrix). It is **light, strong along the veins, biodegradable, and made entirely by sunlight.** A green composite is the *engineered cousin* of that leaf — natural fibres such as jute or flax laid in a controlled orientation inside a biodegradable resin such as PLA. Drop it in soil; in months it disappears. No microplastics, no petroleum.

### 1.2.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **Fibre volume fraction $V_f$**: typically **0.20 – 0.40**
- **Density** $\rho_c$: typically **1.1 – 1.4 g/cm³**
- **Tensile strength**: **30 – 90 MPa**
- **Young's modulus**: **2 – 8 GPa**
- **Specific modulus** $E/\rho$: comparable to glass-fibre composites
- **Biodegradation time (soil burial, ASTM D5988)**: **60 – 180 days** for fully green composites.

---

## 1.3 Nanocomposites — Intuitive Overview

### 1.3.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nanocomposite (KTU Definition):** A composite in which the reinforcing filler has **at least one dimension < 100 nm**, leading to an exceptionally high **interfacial area-to-volume ratio** ($\geq 500 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ for exfoliated clays) and quantum/microstructural effects that produce property enhancements far beyond rule-of-mixtures predictions.

The **three geometric families** of nano-fillers are:

| Geometry | Dimension < 100 nm | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **0-D** (isometric nanoparticles) | All three | Silica $\text{SiO}_2$, titania $\text{TiO}_2$, nano-alumina |
| **1-D** (nanofibres/tubes) | Two | Carbon nanotubes (CNT), carbon nanofibres, halloysite nanotubes, cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) |
| **2-D** (platelets) | One | Exfoliated organoclays (montmorillonite MMT), graphene, graphene oxide (GO), layered double hydroxides (LDH), MXenes |

### 1.3.2 Real-World Analogy 🧬

> **"Imagine reinforcing a wall of bricks with single-atom-thick graphene sheets."**
> A normal composite is like a wall with stones the size of footballs (micro-fibre glass). A nanocomposite is the *same wall* but with **2D atomically-thin sheets (graphene)** slipped between every brick. Because each sheet has the **strength of steel at 1/6th the density** and covers enormous area, even **3 wt%** graphene can boost strength and barrier properties by **tens of percent** — the nano-scale surfaces **talk to the entire matrix**.

### 1.3.3 Defining Parameter — Interfacial Area per Unit Volume

For a platelet of thickness $t$ and aspect ratio $\alpha = L/t$, the **specific surface area** is:

$$A_{\text{sp}} = \frac{2}{\rho_f \, t} \quad \left[ \text{m}^2/\text{g} \right]$$

- For exfoliated **MMT clay** ($t \approx 1 \,\text{nm}$, $\rho_f \approx 2.6 \,\text{g/cm}^3$): $A_{\text{sp}} \approx \mathbf{770 \, m^2/g}$.
- For **graphene** ($t \approx 0.34 \,\text{nm}$): $A_{\text{sp}} \approx \mathbf{2600 \, m^2/g}$.

---

## 1.4 Hybrid Composites — Intuitive Overview

### 1.4.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Composite (KTU Definition):** A composite in which **two or more distinct reinforcement types** (differing in chemistry, geometry, length-scale, or origin) are intentionally combined in a **single matrix** to obtain a **synergistic balance of properties** that neither individual reinforcement could provide alone.

Hybridisation categories:

1. **Fibre–Fibre hybrid:** e.g., glass + carbon, flax + carbon, Kevlar + glass.
2. **Fibre–Particle hybrid:** e.g., glass-fibre + nano-$\text{SiO}_2$, carbon-fibre + CNT.
3. **Nano–Nano hybrid:** e.g., graphene + CNT, MMT + CNC.
4. **Natural–Synthetic hybrid:** e.g., jute + glass, hemp + carbon.

### 1.4.2 Real-World Analogy 🏗️

> **"A hybrid composite is like a reinforced-concrete beam that ALSO has steel cables pre-tensioned inside."**
> The concrete (matrix) handles **compression**; the steel rebars (micro-fibre) handle **tension**; the pre-stressed cables (carbon or aramid fibre) handle **fatigue and impact**. Each material plays a **specific role**, and together they outperform any one of them alone. The negative 'hybrid effect' or positive 'synergistic effect' emerges from how these reinforcements share the load and arrest cracks.

### 1.4.3 Hybrid Effect — A Quantitative Marker

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **hybrid effect** is defined as the **positive or negative deviation** of an actual hybrid composite property from the **rule-of-mixtures (ROM) prediction**.
> 
> $$\Delta_{\text{hybrid}}(\%) = \frac{P_{\text{exp}} - P_{\text{ROM}}}{P_{\text{ROM}}} \times 100$$
> 
> A **positive** $\Delta_{\text{hybrid}}$ indicates synergistic load-sharing; a **negative** value indicates poor interfacial coupling or stress concentration.

---

## 1.5 Syllabus-Highlight Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Must-Know Topics for ESE:**
> 1. Classification of natural fibres, chemical composition, and properties.
> 2. Biopolymer resins — PLA, PHB, epoxy (bio-based).
> 3. Synthesis routes: hand lay-up, compression moulding, injection moulding, extrusion, sol-gel, in-situ polymerisation, melt intercalation, *in-situ* polymerisation intercalation, 3R (resin-transfer) variants.
> 4. Nanoclay modification — organophilisation, cation-exchange capacity (CEC), d-spacing.
> 5. Exfoliation, intercalation, and immiscible microcomposite morphologies.
> 6. Hybrid effect — rule of mixtures, modified ROM, and laminate theory.
> 7. Applications: automotive interiors, wind-turbine blades, packaging, biomedical, aerospace secondary structures, EMI shielding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Green Composites — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Natural Fibre Architecture & Chemistry

Every plant fibre is a **natural composite** itself — cellulose **microfibrils** (the reinforcement) embedded in a hemicellulose-lignin matrix.

| Fibre | Cellulose (%) | Hemicellulose (%) | Lignin (%) | Pectin (%) | Density (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jute | 61 – 71 | 14 – 20 | 12 – 13 | 0.2 | **1.3 – 1.46** |
| Flax | 71 | 18.6 – 20.6 | 2.2 | 2.3 | **1.5** |
| Hemp | 70 – 74 | 17.9 – 22.4 | 3.7 – 5.7 | 0.9 | **1.48** |
| Sisal | 65 – 67 | 10 – 14 | 9.9 – 14 | 10 | **1.45** |
| Coir | 36 – 43 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 41 – 45 | 3 – 4 | **1.15 – 1.25** |
| Banana | 63 – 67 | 10 – 19 | 5 – 10 | 3 – 5 | **1.35** |
| Kenaf | 45 – 57 | 21.5 | 8 – 13 | 3 – 5 | **1.4** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cellulose** carries the load (the reinforcement analogue). Higher cellulose content ⇒ higher tensile strength. **Lignin** is amorphous, hydrophobic, UV-resistant — it gives the fibre its rigidity and weather tolerance. **Hemicellulose** is hydrophilic and is responsible for **moisture absorption**.

### 2.1.2 The Hydrophilicity Problem — Why Surface Treatment is Mandatory

Natural fibres are inherently **hydrophilic** ($\theta < 90°$) due to -OH groups on cellulose. Most polymer matrices are **hydrophobic**. This polarity mismatch ⇒ **poor interfacial adhesion** ⇒ inferior composite strength.

**Common surface treatments (Bulleted Logic Steps):**

1. **Alkali treatment (mercerisation)** — NaOH 2–8 wt%, 1–2 h, RT or 60 °C.
   - *How:* Removes hemicellulose, lignin, pectin, wax; exposes -OH; increases surface roughness; converts cellulose I → cellulose II (more crystalline).
   - *Effect:* $\uparrow$ tensile strength, $\uparrow$ fibre-matrix bonding, $\downarrow$ moisture uptake.
2. **Silane coupling** — e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES).
   - *How:* Hydrolyses to silanol, reacts with fibre -OH; organo-tail entangles with matrix.
   - *Effect:* Forms chemical bridge; $\uparrow$ interfacial shear strength (IFSS) by 30–60%.
3. **Acetylation** — acetic anhydride / pyridine.
   - *How:* Replaces -OH with -COCH₃; reduces polarity.
   - *Effect:* $\downarrow$ hygroscopicity, $\uparrow$ dimensional stability.
4. **Permanganate, peroxide, maleic-anhydride grafting (MAPP for PP).**
5. **Plasma treatment (O₂, Ar, NH₃)** — clean, eco-friendly, sub-minute exposure.

### 2.1.3 Synthesis of Green Composites

| Method | Principle | Typical $V_f$ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand lay-up** | Manual impregnation, room-temp cure | 0.20 – 0.30 | Low-cost panels, lab coupons |
| **Compression moulding** | Prepreg stacked, hot-pressed 120–180 °C, 5–25 MPa | 0.30 – 0.45 | Automotive door-trim panels |
| **Injection moulding** | Pellets (compounded) injected | 0.15 – 0.30 | Consumer goods |
| **Extrusion + calendering** | Film-stacking, continuous | 0.25 – 0.40 | Sheets |
| **Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM)** | Closed mould, resin injected through fibre preform | 0.40 – 0.55 | Higher-performance structural |
| **Filament winding** | Fibre roving wetted & wound on mandrel | 0.50 – 0.65 | Pipes, tubes |

---

## 2.2 Nanocomposites — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Why Nano? The Three Mechanisms

1. **Ultra-high interfacial area** — At $A_{\text{sp}} \approx 770 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}$ (MMT), even **3 wt%** clay provides an interfacial area equivalent to covering the entire football field inside a gram of polymer.
2. **Confined-polymer / interphase effects** — Polymer chains within 1–50 nm of a filler adopt conformations, $T_g$, and crystallinity distinct from the bulk.
3. **Geometric/quantum effects** — At sub-100 nm, the filler can be smaller than the polymer radius of gyration, so it acts as a *nanoscale* stress-transfer and barrier element rather than a *defect*.

### 2.2.2 Polymer-Clay Nanocomposite Morphologies

The XRD pattern (Bragg's law) reveals three morphologies:

$$\lambda = 2 d_{001} \sin\theta$$

- **Immисible (microcomposite)** — Clay tactoids dispersed, $d_{001}$ unchanged ⇒ two-phase with no nano-effect.
- **Intercalated** — Polymer chains insert into the gallery, $d_{001}$ **increases** by 1–4 nm (one or two peaks, ordered).
- **Exfoliated (delaminated)** — Individual 1-nm silicate layers dispersed randomly in matrix; **no peak** in XRD; TEM shows single layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exfoliation = best properties**, but is thermodynamically challenging. It requires organophilisation of clay to make the gallery organophilic.

### 2.2.3 Organophilisation — Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC)

Natural **Na⁺-MMT** has hydrophilic galleries. To intercalate hydrophobic polymers, we exchange Na⁺ with **onium ions** (alkylammonium):

$$\text{Na}^+\text{-MMT} + [\text{R}_4\text{N}^+]\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow [\text{R}_4\text{N}^+]\text{-MMT} + \text{NaCl}$$

- Typical CEC of MMT: **80 – 120 meq/100 g**.
- Common surfactants: **dimethyl dehydrogenated tallow quaternary ammonium (DMDHT)**; trimethyl stearyl ammonium.

### 2.2.4 Synthesis Routes for Nanocomposites

| Route | Principle | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **In-situ polymerisation** | Monomer diffuses into gallery, then polymerises | Nylon-6/clay (Toyota, 1990s) |
| **Melt intercalation** | Polymer melt blended at $T > T_m$ | Thermoplastics + organoclay |
| **Solution intercalation** | Polymer dissolved, mixed with clay, cast | Water-soluble polymers |
| **Sol-gel** | Hydrolysis + condensation of metal alkoxides | $\text{SiO}_2$, $\text{TiO}_2$ / polymer hybrids |
| **Electrospinning + CNT dispersion** | High-shear dispersion, then electrospun | Fibre mats for filtration |
| **3-roll milling / ultrasonication** | Dispersion of CNT/graphene | Thermoset/thermoplastic masterbatches |

### 2.2.5 CNT — Key Theoretical Parameters

- **Chirality** ($n,m$) determines metallic or semiconducting behaviour.
- **Aspect ratio** $L/D$ typically **1000 – 10 000** ⇒ exceptional stress transfer.
- **Young's modulus**: **~1 TPa**; **Tensile strength**: **30 – 100 GPa**.
- **Density**: **1.3 – 2.1 g/cm³** (MWCNT ≈ 1.75).

---

## 2.3 Hybrid Composites — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 The Three ROM Variants

For a hybrid composite with reinforcements "1" and "2" in a matrix "m":

**1. Simple Rule of Mixtures (lower bound, $E_c$ parallel loading):**

$$E_c = E_1 V_1 + E_2 V_2 + E_m V_m$$

**2. Inverse Rule of Mixtures (lower bound, $E_c$ transverse loading):**

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_1}{E_1} + \frac{V_2}{E_2} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

**3. Modified Rule of Mixtures (Halpin-Tsai for hybrids):**

$$E_c = \eta_1 E_1 V_1 + \eta_2 E_2 V_2 + E_m V_m$$

where $\eta_1, \eta_2$ are **Krenchel fibre-efficiency factors** ($\eta \leq 1$).

### 2.3.2 Laminate Analogy (Fibre–Fibre Hybrid)

Consider an interlayer hybrid laminate with stacking sequence $[0_\text{G}/0_\text{C}]_s$ (G = glass, C = carbon). Classical Laminate Theory (CLT) gives the laminate stiffness:

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \, t_k$$

where $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$ is the transformed reduced stiffness of ply $k$, and $t_k$ is its thickness.

### 2.3.3 The "Hybrid Effect" — Physical Origin

> **Why is there a hybrid effect?**
> 
> 1. **Crack-arresting:** When low-elongation fibres (e.g., carbon) fracture first, the high-elongation partner (e.g., glass) holds the composite together and bridges cracks.
> 2. **Stress redistribution:** Damage in one fibre type forces load into the other.
> 3. **Thermal-residual stress management:** Difference in $\alpha$ (CTE) between two fibres can produce compressive clamping — beneficial.
> 4. **Fibre-fibre interaction:** Reduces effective gauge length for fragmentation tests.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Master these eight equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE module-4 question.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus) | GPa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse ROM (transverse modulus) | GPa |
| 3 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume conservation | — |
| 4 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density rule of mixtures | g/cm³ |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Strength rule of mixtures (parallel) | MPa |
| 6 | $\lambda = 2 d_{001} \sin\theta$ | Bragg's law — clay gallery spacing | nm |
| 7 | $A_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{2}{\rho_f t}$ | Specific surface area of a platelet | m²/g |
| 8 | $\Delta_{\text{hybrid}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{exp}} - P_{\text{ROM}}}{P_{\text{ROM}}} \times 100$ | Hybrid effect percentage | % |

### Supplementary Quick-Reference

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Fibre weight fraction $\rightarrow$ volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ |
| Theoretical composite density | $\rho_{ct} = \dfrac{1}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ |
| Void content (ASTM D2734) | $V_{\text{void}} = 1 - \rho_{\text{exp}}/\rho_{ct}$ |
| Tortuosity factor (barrier) | $\tau = 1 + \dfrac{L}{2 W_c} V_f$ (Nielsen) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Class | Application Sector | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Green composites** | Automotive | Ford Model U (soy-foam), Toyota Raum (kenaf/PLA), Mercedes A-Class (flax/PP) |
| **Green composites** | Construction | Bamboo-PLA decking, jute-epoxy partition panels |
| **Green composites** | Biomedical | PLA/PCL scaffolds with chitosan |
| **Nanocomposites** | Packaging | Nylon-6/MMT (e.g., Aegis™ OXCE) — 40% $\downarrow$ O₂ permeability |
| **Nanocomposites** | Aerospace | CNT/epoxy lightning-strike protection on Boeing 787 skins |
| **Nanocomposites** | Electronics | Graphene/PDMS flexible electrodes, AgNW transparent conductors |
| **Hybrid composites** | Wind energy | Glass/carbon hybrid spar-caps in 80-m blades |
| **Hybrid composites** | Defence | Kevlar/carbon ballistic helmets |
| **Hybrid composites** | Sports | Tennis racquets (carbon/aramid hybrid), F1 monocoques |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal Modulus)

We derive $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ from first principles using an **iso-strain** assumption.

> **Assumptions (KTU standard):**
> - Fibres are **continuous, aligned, and parallel** to the load.
> - Fibre-matrix interface is **perfectly bonded** (no slip).
> - Both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the geometric model.**
The composite is taken as a slab of unit cross-sectional area $A_c = 1$. The fibre and matrix share this area:

$$A_f + A_m = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_f + V_m = 1$$

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium.**
Total load on composite = sum of loads carried by each phase:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

Since $F = \sigma A$ and all areas are taken as fractions of $A_c = 1$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law.**
Within the elastic regime:

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_f, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_m, \quad \sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon_c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into equilibrium.**

$$E_c \varepsilon_c = E_f \varepsilon_f V_f + E_m \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 5 — Apply iso-strain condition** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$:

$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$

Divide both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**This is the upper-bound (Voigt) modulus** — valid for fibres aligned with the load. The fibre efficiency is $\eta_f = 1$ in this idealised case.

> **Why this matters:** It is the *ideal* property. Real composites lie **below** this line because of fibre misalignment, finite length, imperfect bonding, and porosity. The KTU exam frequently asks to compute $E_c$ given $V_f$, $E_f$, $E_m$ and then to compare with an experimental value to **deduce the Krenchel factor** $\eta_f = E_{\text{exp}}/E_{\text{ROM}}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ROM for Flax/PLA Green Composite

> **Problem:** A unidirectional flax-fibre/PLA green composite has fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.35$. Given $E_f = 50 \, \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \, \text{GPa}$, predict the longitudinal Young's modulus and density. Compare with rule-of-mixtures for strength.

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.35 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_m = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$
- $E_f = 50 \, \text{GPa}$ (flax, longitudinal)
- $E_m = 3.5 \, \text{GPa}$ (PLA)
- $\rho_f = 1.45 \, \text{g/cm}^3$ (flax)
- $\rho_m = 1.24 \, \text{g/cm}^3$ (PLA)
- $\sigma_f = 600 \, \text{MPa}$ (flax tensile strength)
- $\sigma_m = 50 \, \text{MPa}$ (PLA tensile strength)

### Step 1 — Longitudinal modulus $E_c$ (iso-strain):

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (50)(0.35) + (3.5)(0.65)$$

$$E_c = 17.50 + 2.275 = \mathbf{19.78 \, GPa}$$

### Step 2 — Composite density $\rho_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.45)(0.35) + (1.24)(0.65)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.5075 + 0.806 = \mathbf{1.314 \, g/cm}^3}$$

### Step 3 — Specific modulus (engineering utility metric):

$$\frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{19.78}{1.314} = \mathbf{15.06 \, \text{GPa}\cdot\text{cm}^3/\text{g}}$$

For comparison, **E-glass/epoxy** typically has $E_c/\rho_c \approx 30 \, \text{GPa}\cdot\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ — so flax/PLA is about **half** the specific stiffness of glass/epoxy but at **lower cost** and **biodegradable**.

### Step 4 — Longitudinal tensile strength (parallel/iso-strain ROM):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = (600)(0.35) + (50)(0.65)$$

$$\sigma_c = 210 + 32.5 = \mathbf{242.5 \, MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Discussion:
- Predicted $\sigma_c$ matches experiment only if fibre-matrix bonding is excellent.
- Real values typically 50–70 % of ROM because fibres fracture *first* (low strain), and once they crack, all load transfers to the matrix. The correct ROM for strength is therefore based on the **smaller of** $V_f \sigma_f$ **or** $\sigma_m$ at fibre-failure strain (Kelly-Tyson model).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Intercalated d-spacing from Bragg's Law

For an intercalated polymer-clay nanocomposite, XRD gives a peak at $2\theta_1$, while the pristine clay peaks at $2\theta_0$. We derive the **gallery expansion** $\Delta d$.

Bragg's law:

$$\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$$

For pristine clay:

$$\lambda = 2 d_0 \sin\theta_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_0 = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta_0}$$

For intercalated nanocomposite:

$$d_1 = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta_1}$$

**Gallery expansion:**

$$\boxed{\Delta d = d_1 - d_0 = \frac{\lambda}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sin\theta_1} - \frac{1}{\sin\theta_0}\right)}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** Cu K$\alpha$ radiation $\lambda = 1.5406 \, \text{Å}$. Pristine Na-MMT peak at $2\theta_0 = 9.0°$. After polymer intercalation, the (001) peak shifts to $2\theta_1 = 4.5°$. Compute $d_0$, $d_1$, and $\Delta d$.

**Step 1 — Compute $d_0$:**

$$d_0 = \frac{1.5406}{2 \sin(4.5°)} = \frac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.0785} = \frac{1.5406}{0.157} = \mathbf{9.81 \, \text{Å}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_1$:**

$$d_1 = \frac{1.5406}{2 \sin(2.25°)} = \frac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.0393} = \frac{1.5406}{0.0785} = \mathbf{19.62 \, \text{Å}}$$

**Step 3 — Gallery expansion:**

$$\Delta d = 19.62 - 9.81 = \mathbf{9.81 \, \text{Å} \approx 1 \, nm}$$

**Interpretation:** The polymer chain has inserted approximately **1 nm** between the silicate layers. Original gallery height in MMT is about **0.3 nm free space + 0.96 nm silicate = ~ 1.26 nm**, so the polymer has effectively **doubled the gallery height** — confirming intercalation. A peak that **disappears** (no peak in the range $2\theta = 1°$–$10°$) would indicate **exfoliation**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Specific Surface Area of a Platelet Nanofiller

For a single platelet of dimensions $L \times L \times t$ (square platelet, $L$ = lateral, $t$ = thickness):

### Step 1 — Volume of one platelet:

$$V_p = L^2 \, t$$

### Step 2 — Mass of one platelet:

$$m_p = V_p \, \rho_f = L^2 t \rho_f$$

### Step 3 — Surface area of one platelet (both faces + 4 edges — edges negligible for $t \ll L$):

$$A_p = 2 L^2$$

### Step 4 — Surface area per unit mass:

$$\frac{A_p}{m_p} = \frac{2 L^2}{L^2 t \rho_f} = \frac{2}{t \, \rho_f}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{sp}} = \frac{2}{\rho_f t}}$$

### Numerical Example — MMT Clay

> Given: $\rho_f = 2.6 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 2600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $t = 1 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$.

$$A_{\text{sp}} = \frac{2}{2600 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2}{2.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.69 \times 10^{5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{sp}} = 769 \, \text{m}^2/\text{g}}$$

This matches the standard literature value for MMT.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Hybrid Composite Modulus — Modified ROM

For a fibre-fibre hybrid with reinforcements "1" (carbon) and "2" (glass) in matrix "m", the **modified ROM** is:

$$E_c = \eta_1 E_1 V_1 + \eta_2 E_2 V_2 + E_m V_m$$

where $\eta_1, \eta_2 \leq 1$ are **fibre-efficiency factors** (Krenchel).

The **hybrid effect** is defined as:

$$\Delta_{\text{hybrid}}(\%) = \left[ \frac{E_{\text{exp}} - E_{\text{ROM}}}{E_{\text{ROM}}} \right] \times 100$$

### Worked Example — Carbon/Glass/Epoxy Hybrid

> **Given:**
> - Carbon fibre: $E_1 = 230 \, \text{GPa}$, $V_1 = 0.20$, $\eta_1 = 0.95$
> - Glass fibre: $E_2 = 72 \, \text{GPa}$, $V_2 = 0.20$, $\eta_2 = 0.90$
> - Epoxy: $E_m = 3.5 \, \text{GPa}$, $V_m = 0.60$
> - Experimental: $E_{\text{exp}} = 75.5 \, \text{GPa}$

**Step 1 — Compute ROM prediction:**

$$E_{\text{ROM}} = (0.95)(230)(0.20) + (0.90)(72)(0.20) + (3.5)(0.60)$$

$$E_{\text{ROM}} = 43.7 + 12.96 + 2.1 = \mathbf{58.76 \, GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute hybrid effect:**

$$\Delta_{\text{hybrid}} = \frac{75.5 - 58.76}{58.76} \times 100 = \mathbf{+28.5\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A **positive** hybrid effect of $+28.5\%$ — the hybrid composite is **28.5 % stiffer** than the rule-of-mixtures prediction. This is because **glass fibres bridge cracks** initiated by brittle carbon fibres, delaying catastrophic failure and increasing the effective modulus contribution.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property Map of the Three Families

| Property | Green Composite (Flax/PLA) | Nanocomposite (Epoxy + 3 wt% CNT) | Hybrid (Carbon/Glass/Epoxy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_c$ (GPa) | 15 – 20 | 4 – 6 | 60 – 80 |
| $\sigma_c$ (MPa) | 100 – 250 | 60 – 90 | 500 – 900 |
| Density (g/cm³) | **1.30** | **1.18** | **1.65** |
| Specific modulus | 11 – 15 | 3.4 – 5.1 | 36 – 49 |
| Cost (USD/kg) | **1.5 – 3** | 50 – 200 | 25 – 80 |
| Biodegradability | **High** | Low | None |
| Recyclability | Composting | Energy recovery | Pyrolysis |
| Main bottleneck | Moisture absorption | Nano-dispersion, cost | Fibre-fibre interface |
