# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define a green composite. List any two natural fibres and two biopolymer resins used in green composites.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *green composite* is a composite in which **either the reinforcement (natural fibre) or the matrix (biopolymer resin) or both** are sourced from renewable, bio-based, and/or biodegradable feedstocks.

**Natural fibres (any two):** jute, flax, hemp, sisal, kenaf, banana, coir, bamboo, ramie, abaca. **(1 mark)**

**Biopolymer resins (any two):** poly(lactic acid) PLA, poly(hydroxybutyrate) PHB, starch, soy protein, bio-based epoxy, cellulose acetate, chitosan, natural rubber. **(1 mark)**

**Justification (1 mark):** Such composites offer reduced carbon footprint, end-of-life biodegradability, and sustainable life-cycle performance, making them suitable for automotive interiors, packaging, and biomedical applications.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between intercalated and exfoliated polymer-clay nanocomposite morphologies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Intercalated | Exfoliated |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer insertion | Polymer chains insert into clay galleries in a *regular, ordered* manner | Individual 1-nm silicate layers are *randomly* dispersed in matrix |
| d-spacing | d(001) increases by 1–4 nm; XRD shows one/two peaks shifted to lower 2θ | No (001) peak in XRD; TEM shows single layers |
| Polymer–clay interaction | Moderate | Maximum interfacial contact |
| Property enhancement | Moderate | Maximum strength, modulus, barrier improvement |
| Synthesis ease | Easier (melt intercalation sufficient) | Harder (requires in-situ polymerisation or high-shear mixing) |

**(3 marks for clear distinction with one figure/diagram or table.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the synthesis, properties, and applications of **green composites** with reference to natural fibre reinforcements and biopolymer matrices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Definition & motivation (1 mark):** Green composites are composites where at least one constituent — reinforcement or matrix — is bio-derived and/or biodegradable. Motivation: depleting petroleum reserves, microplastic pollution, EU end-of-life vehicle directive (95 % recovery), and corporate sustainability targets.

**(ii) Natural fibres (2 marks):**
- **Reinforcement candidates:** jute, flax, hemp, sisal, kenaf, banana, coir, bamboo, ramie, abaca.
- **Composition:** cellulose (microfibrils, load-bearing), hemicellulose (matrix, hydrophilic), lignin (UV/moisture resistance), pectin/wax (outer).
- **Key properties:** low density (1.2 – 1.5 g/cm³), high specific modulus, renewability, biodegradability, low cost (USD 0.5–2/kg), low energy to process.
- **Drawbacks:** high moisture absorption (10–15 %), poor fire resistance, fibre–matrix polarity mismatch, dimensional instability.

**(iii) Biopolymer matrices (1 mark):**
- PLA (Tg ≈ 60 °C, Tm ≈ 150–170 °C, tensile strength 50–70 MPa)
- PHB (biodegradable polyester from bacterial fermentation)
- Starch-based plastics (cheap, water-sensitive)
- Bio-epoxy (e.g., epoxidised linseed oil, soy-based)
- Cellulose acetate, chitosan.

**(iv) Synthesis routes (1 mark):** Hand lay-up, compression moulding, injection moulding, extrusion + calendering, RTM, filament winding. Surface treatment of fibres (alkali, silane, acetylation, plasma) is essential.

**(v) Applications (1 mark):** Automotive interior door-trim panels (Ford, Toyota, Mercedes), construction (bamboo-PLA decking), biomedical scaffolds (PLA/PCL), consumer packaging, and electronic casings.

**(vi) Engineering example (1 mark):** Mercedes-Benz A-Class uses **flax/PP** door-trim panels (~ 30 wt% flax), reducing panel weight by 10 % and life-cycle energy by 30 % versus glass-fibre/PP.

---

**Q. (b)** With neat sketches, explain the **three morphologies of polymer-clay nanocomposites**. Discuss how **Bragg's law** is used to differentiate them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Bragg's law (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta}$$

- $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength (e.g., Cu K$\alpha$ = 1.5406 Å).
- $d$ = gallery spacing (distance between silicate layers).
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle.

**(ii) Three morphologies (3.5 marks — sketch + explanation):**

1. **Immисible (microcomposite):** Clay tactoids exist as aggregates; polymer does not enter gallery; $d_{001}$ unchanged from pristine clay (≈ 1.2 nm for Na-MMT). XRD shows the same peak as pristine. No nano-effect; properties are like a conventional particulate composite.

2. **Intercalated:** Polymer chains insert into gallery in a regular, repeating manner; $d_{001}$ increases by 1–4 nm (e.g., to 3–5 nm). XRD peak shifts to lower $2\theta$, often with multiple orders (001, 002, 003). Polymer is *confined* between silicate layers.

3. **Exfoliated (delaminated):** Individual 1-nm silicate platelets are randomly dispersed in polymer matrix; gallery order is destroyed. **No (001) peak** in XRD range 1°–10°; TEM is needed to confirm single-layer dispersion. Maximum property enhancement.

**(iii) XRD comparison table (1 mark):**

| Morphology | $d_{001}$ shift | Peak in 2θ = 1°–10° |
|---|---|---|
| Immисible | None | Same as pristine |
| Intercalated | Increases | Shifted to lower 2θ |
| Exfoliated | Disordered | Absent |

**(iv) Engineering significance (1 mark):** Exfoliation is the goal in barrier packaging (Nylon-6/MMT by Honeywell Aegis™ OXCE shows 40 % reduction in O₂ permeability at 2 wt% clay) and in structural components where modulus jumps from 2.8 GPa (PA6) to 4.5 GPa (PA6 + 4 wt% exfoliated MMT).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Alternative]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **synthesis routes** for **nanocomposites**. Discuss the role of **organophilisation** and **cation exchange capacity (CEC)** in clay modification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Definition (0.5 mark):** A nanocomposite is a multiphase material with at least one phase having a dimension < 100 nm. Three categories: 0-D (particles), 1-D (fibres/tubes), 2-D (platelets).

**(ii) Synthesis routes (3.5 marks):**

1. **In-situ polymerisation:** Monomer + modified clay are mixed; monomer diffuses into gallery and is then polymerised by heat/radiation/catalyst. *Best for:* Nylon-6/MMT (Toyota's pioneering 1990s work), polyimide/clay. Gives highest exfoliation. (1 mark)

2. **Melt intercalation:** Polymer melt blended with organoclay above its $T_m$ or $T_g$ at high shear (twin-screw extruder, 3-roll mill). Polymer chains diffuse into gallery. Industrially scalable. *Best for:* polypropylene, polyethylene, PET, PLA + clay. (1 mark)

3. **Solution intercalation:** Polymer dissolved in solvent; clay dispersed; solvent evaporated. *Best for:* water-soluble polymers (PVA, PEO). Energy-intensive, solvent recovery needed. (0.5 mark)

4. **Sol-gel:** Hydrolysis + condensation of metal alkoxides (TEOS, Ti(OBu)₄) inside polymer matrix. *Best for:* $\text{SiO}_2$ / polymer, $\text{TiO}_2$ / polymer hybrids for optical/photocatalytic applications. (0.5 mark)

5. **High-shear dispersion (3-roll mill / ultrasonication):** For CNT, graphene; de-bundles and exfoliates. (0.5 mark)

**(iii) Organophilisation & CEC (2 marks):**

- Natural **Na⁺-MMT** has hydrophilic, inorganic galleries; polymers cannot penetrate.
- **Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC)** is the measure of a clay's ability to retain exchangeable cations, expressed in **meq/100 g** (MMT typically 80–120).
- **Organophilisation:** Na⁺ ions are exchanged with organic **onium ions** (e.g., dimethyl dihydrogenated tallow quaternary ammonium, DMDHT) via:

$$\text{Na}^+\text{-MMT} + [\text{R}_4\text{N}^+]\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow [\text{R}_4\text{N}^+]\text{-MMT} + \text{NaCl}$$

- This **expands the gallery** from ~ 1.2 nm to 2–5 nm and makes the surface **organophilic**, allowing polymer chains to wet the silicate surface.
- The amount of surfactant is chosen such that its mass is ≈ **CEC × molecular mass** of the surfactant (per 100 g clay).

**(iv) Outcome (1 mark):** After organophilisation, $d_{001}$ measured by XRD increases, confirming gallery expansion; subsequent melt or in-situ polymerisation produces intercalated or exfoliated nanocomposites with **40–80 % improvement in tensile properties and 30–50 % reduction in gas permeability** at only 2–5 wt% clay loading.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **hybrid effect** in fibre-reinforced polymer composites. A hybrid composite contains **carbon fibre** ($E_1 = 230$ GPa, $V_1 = 0.25$, $\eta_1 = 0.92$) and **glass fibre** ($E_2 = 72$ GPa, $V_2 = 0.20$, $\eta_2 = 0.85$) in **epoxy** ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $V_m = 0.55$). An experimental value of $E_{\text{exp}} = 73$ GPa was measured. Compute the **ROM modulus** and the **hybrid effect percentage**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Concept of hybrid effect (2 marks):**

The **hybrid effect** is defined as the **positive (or negative) deviation** of an experimentally measured hybrid composite property from the value predicted by the **rule of mixtures (ROM)**. It arises because:

1. The **low-elongation fibre** (e.g., carbon, ε_f ≈ 1.5 %) fractures first.
2. The **high-elongation fibre** (e.g., glass, ε_f ≈ 4.5 %) bridges the cracks, redistributes load, and prevents catastrophic failure.
3. **Thermal-residual stresses** (from CTE mismatch) can put the matrix in beneficial compression.
4. **Fibre–fibre friction and crack deflection** contribute to apparent modulus increase.

A **positive hybrid effect** indicates synergistic load-sharing; a **negative** one indicates poor interfacial coupling.

**(ii) Calculation setup (1 mark):**

Given:
- $E_1 = 230$ GPa, $V_1 = 0.25$, $\eta_1 = 0.92$
- $E_2 = 72$ GPa, $V_2 = 0.20$, $\eta_2 = 0.85$
- $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $V_m = 0.55$
- $E_{\text{exp}} = 73$ GPa

Check: $V_1 + V_2 + V_m = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.55 = 1.00$ ✓

**(iii) Compute ROM modulus (2 marks):**

$$E_{\text{ROM}} = \eta_1 E_1 V_1 + \eta_2 E_2 V_2 + E_m V_m$$

Term 1 (carbon contribution): $\eta_1 E_1 V_1 = (0.92)(230)(0.25) = 52.90$ GPa

Term 2 (glass contribution): $\eta_2 E_2 V_2 = (0.85)(72)(0.20) = 12.24$ GPa

Term 3 (matrix contribution): $E_m V_m = (3.5)(0.55) = 1.925$ GPa

$$E_{\text{ROM}} = 52.90 + 12.24 + 1.925 = \mathbf{67.07 \, GPa}$$

**(iv) Compute hybrid effect (2 marks):**

$$\Delta_{\text{hybrid}}(\%) = \frac{E_{\text{exp}} - E_{\text{ROM}}}{E_{\text{ROM}}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta_{\text{hybrid}} = \frac{73 - 67.07}{67.07} \times 100 = \frac{5.93}{67.07} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{hybrid}} \approx +8.84\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A **positive hybrid effect** of ~ 8.8 % indicates synergistic load-sharing between carbon and glass fibres — the hybrid composite is stiffer than the ROM prediction. This justifies using **carbon/glass hybrids** in wind-turbine blade spar-caps and aerospace primary structures where weight-saving and damage tolerance are simultaneously required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalise (Module 4):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "natural fibre composite" with "green composite."** A natural-fibre composite is *not automatically* green unless the matrix is also bio-based. Use precise wording: "green composite = bio-fibre + bio-matrix."
> 2. **Writing 'nanocomposite = nanoparticle composite.'** A nanocomposite may have 0-D, 1-D, **or** 2-D nano-fillers. Examiners specifically test this in Part A.
> 3. **Confusing intercalated vs exfoliated.** If you cannot sketch the gallery structure and identify the XRD peak position, you will lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mass fraction to volume fraction** in numerical problems. Always use $V_f = \dfrac{w_f/\rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ for green composites.
> 5. **Skipping units in ROM problems.** Each term must carry GPa; density g/cm³; final answer boxed with correct units.
> 6. **Mishandling the hybrid-effect sign.** A **positive** Δ indicates synergy; **negative** indicates poor coupling. Examiners will dock 1 mark if you do not comment on the sign's physical meaning.
> 7. **Writing 'CNT = graphene.'** CNT = 1-D rolled graphene; graphene = 2-D planar sheet. Examiners frequently ask this distinction.
> 8. **Not mentioning the role of organophilisation/CEC** in clay-based nanocomposite synthesis. This is the **most-tested sub-topic** of Module 4 — appears in every KTU ESE since 2022.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 in 60 seconds:**

**Green Composites** 🍃
- Reinforcement: natural fibres (cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin + pectin); jute, flax, hemp, sisal, kenaf, coir.
- Matrix: PLA, PHB, starch, bio-epoxy, natural rubber.
- Problem: hydrophilic fibre vs hydrophobic matrix → **must surface-treat** (alkali, silane, acetylation, plasma).
- Synthesis: hand lay-up, compression moulding, RTM, injection moulding.
- Density 1.1–1.4 g/cm³; tensile 30–90 MPa; **biodegradable**.
- Applications: automotive interiors (Mercedes A-Class), packaging, biomedical scaffolds.

**Nanocomposites** 🔬
- Reinforcement has **at least one dimension < 100 nm**.
- Three geometries: **0-D** (SiO₂, TiO₂), **1-D** (CNT, halloysite, CNC), **2-D** (graphene, MMT clay, MXene).
- Three morphologies: **immисible / intercalated / exfoliated** — distinguished by XRD peak shift.
- Bragg's law: $d = \lambda / (2 \sin\theta)$.
- Clay must be **organophilised** via cation exchange (CEC, onium ions).
- Synthesis: in-situ polymerisation, melt intercalation, solution, sol-gel.
- Property boost with only 2–5 wt% filler: +40 % modulus, +50 % barrier, +30 % strength.

**Hybrid Composites** 🧩
- Two or more **different** reinforcements in one matrix.
- Types: fibre–fibre, fibre–particle, nano–nano, natural–synthetic.
- ROM: $E_c = \eta_1 E_1 V_1 + \eta_2 E_2 V_2 + E_m V_m$.
- **Hybrid effect** = $(P_{\text{exp}} - P_{\text{ROM}}) / P_{\text{ROM}} \times 100$; **positive** = synergy (crack bridging by ductile partner).
- Applications: wind-turbine blades (carbon/glass), F1 monocoques (carbon/Kevlar), helmets (Kevlar/carbon).

**Key Numbers to Memorise:**
- Bragg's wavelength (Cu K$\alpha$): **1.5406 Å**.
- MMT CEC: **80 – 120 meq/100 g**.
- MMT $d_{001}$ pristine: **~ 1.2 nm**; after organophilisation: **2 – 5 nm**.
- MMT specific surface area: **~ 770 m²/g**.
- CNT Young's modulus: **~ 1 TPa**.
- Flax/PLA density: **~ 1.30 g/cm³**.

**Master these 8 equations (Section 2.4 cheat sheet) and you will score 90 %+ on any Module 4 KTU question.**
